flashcards.front: What is the composition of local anesthetic solution? Describe the role of each component.
flashcards.back: **Local anesthetic (LA) solution** contains: **1. Anesthetic agent**: **Amide type** (metabolized in liver): **Lignocaine (Lidocaine) 2%** - most common, onset 2-3 min, duration 60-90 min. **Bupivacaine 0.5%** - long-acting (4-8 hrs), slower onset. **Articaine 4%** - higher concentration, better tissue penetration. **Ester type** (metabolized in blood): **Procaine**, **Benzocaine** (topical) - rarely used now due to allergy risk. **2. Vasoconstrictor**: **Adrenaline (Epinephrine)** - most common...

flashcards.back: **Ester type** (metabolized in blood): **Procaine**, **Benzocaine** (topical) - rarely used now due to allergy risk

flashcards.front: Describe the technique of inferior alveolar nerve block.
flashcards.back: **Inferior Alveolar Nerve (IAN) block** anesthetizes **mandibular teeth** (one side), **lower lip**, **chin**, **mucosa**, **bone**. **Nerve anesthetized**: Inferior alveolar nerve (branch of posterior division of mandibular nerve V3). **Landmarks**: (1) **Pterygomandibular raphe** - fold of tissue between pterygoid muscles and mandible, (2) **Coronoid notch** - depression anterior to anterior border of ramus, (3) **Occlusal plane** of mandibular molars. **Injection site**: **1 cm above occlusal...

flashcards.back: **Inferior Alveolar Nerve (IAN) block** anesthetizes **mandibular teeth** (one side), **lower lip**, **chin**, **mucosa**, **bone**

flashcards.front: Define: Landmarks
flashcards.back: **Landmarks**: (1) **Pterygomandibular raphe** - fold of tissue between pterygoid muscles and mandible, (2) **Coronoid notch** - depression anterior to anterior border of ramus, (3) **Occlusal plane** of mandibular molars

flashcards.front: Define: Inferior Alveolar Nerve (IAN) block
flashcards.back: **Inferior Alveolar Nerve (IAN) block** anesthetizes **mandibular teeth** (one side), **lower lip**, **chin**, **mucosa**, **bone**

flashcards.front: What are the complications of local anesthesia?
flashcards.back: **Complications** categorized as: **LOCAL**: **1. Pain on injection**: Rapid injection, cold solution, low pH solution. **Prevention**: Warm cartridge, inject slowly. **2. Hematoma**: Needle trauma to blood vessel. Common in **PSA block** (pterygoid plexus). **Management**: Pressure, ice pack. **3. Trismus**: Muscle spasm post-injection (pterygoid muscles). Common after **IAN block**. **Management**: Heat, analgesics, jaw exercises, antibiotics if infected. **4. Needle breakage**: Rare with mode...

flashcards.front: Define: anaphylaxis
flashcards.back: **Symptoms**: Urticaria, bronchospasm, **anaphylaxis** (rare)

flashcards.front: Define: esters
flashcards.back: More common with **esters** than **amides**

flashcards.front: Define: Lingual nerve
flashcards.back: **Lingual nerve** and **IAN** most common

flashcards.front: Describe the posterior superior alveolar (PSA) nerve block technique.
flashcards.back: **PSA nerve block** anesthetizes **maxillary molars** (except mesiobuccal root of 1st molar supplied by MSA). **Nerve**: Posterior superior alveolar nerve (branch of maxillary nerve V2). **Injection site**: **Height of mucobuccal fold** opposite **2nd maxillary molar**, **45° angle** to occlusal plane, directed **upward, inward, backward** toward **pterygomaxillary fissure**. **Technique**: (1) **Retract cheek** laterally, (2) **Needle**: 25 mm short needle, (3) **Insert** at mucobuccal fold abo...

flashcards.front: Define: PSA nerve block
flashcards.back: **PSA nerve block** anesthetizes **maxillary molars** (except mesiobuccal root of 1st molar supplied by MSA)

flashcards.front: Define: Needle
flashcards.back: **Technique**: (1) **Retract cheek** laterally, (2) **Needle**: 25 mm short needle, (3) **Insert** at mucobuccal fold above 2nd molar, bevel toward bone, (4) **Advance** 16 mm at **45° angle** (upward, medial, posterior), **STOP** before 16 mm if bone contacted (risk of pterygoid plexus puncture), (5) **Aspirate** carefully (pterygoid plexus proximity), (6) **Deposit** 1

flashcards.front: What are the contraindications to use of vasoconstrictors in local anesthesia?
flashcards.back: **Absolute contraindications**: **1. Recent myocardial infarction** (within 6 months) - risk of arrhythmias, re-infarction. **2. Recent cerebrovascular accident** (stroke) - risk of hypertension, re-bleeding. **3. Severe uncontrolled hypertension** (>180/110 mmHg) - risk of hypertensive crisis, stroke. **4. Severe coronary insufficiency** - unstable angina. **5. Uncontrolled hyperthyroidism** - potentiates adrenaline effects (tachycardia, arrhythmias). **6. Uncontrolled diabetes** - adrenaline c...

flashcards.front: Define: Non-selective beta-blockers
flashcards.back: Concurrent medication**: **Non-selective beta-blockers** (propranolol) - unopposed alpha effects cause severe hypertension

flashcards.front: What are the indications and contraindications for tooth extraction?
flashcards.back: **INDICATIONS**: **1. Dental caries**: Grossly carious tooth **not restorable**, extensive destruction below gingival margin. **2. Periodontal disease**: Advanced periodontitis with **grade III mobility**, bone loss >2/3 root length, poor prognosis. **3. Periapical pathology**: Chronic periapical abscess **not amenable to RCT** (curved roots, calcified canals, perforations). **4. Impacted teeth**: **Symptomatic** impaction (pain, infection, cyst formation), **prophylactic** removal (orthodontic ...

flashcards.front: Define: Severe cardiac disease
flashcards.back: Uncontrolled systemic disease**: **Uncontrolled diabetes** (hyperglycemia >200 mg/dL, ketoacidosis), **Uncontrolled hypertension** (>180/110), **Recent MI** (<6 months), **Unstable angina**, **Severe cardiac disease**

flashcards.front: Define: Cellulitis
flashcards.back: Acute infections**: **Acute pericoronitis**, **Cellulitis** - stabilize with antibiotics first

flashcards.front: Define: source of infection
flashcards.back: **Exception**: If tooth is **source of infection**, extract (with antibiotic cover)

flashcards.front: Describe the principles and steps of tooth extraction using forceps.
flashcards.back: **Principles of forceps extraction**: **1. Expansion of alveolar bone**: Controlled force expands socket. **2. Luxation**: Loosen tooth by breaking PDL fibers, dilating socket. **3. Delivery**: Remove tooth from socket. **STEPS**: **1. Pre-operative**: (a) **Consent** - explain procedure, risks, (b) **Radiograph** - assess root morphology, bone, pathology, (c) **Local anesthesia** - adequate. **2. Tissue reflection**: **Mucoperiosteal flap** if surgical extraction needed. Simple extraction - no ...

flashcards.front: Define: Local anesthesia
flashcards.back: Pre-operative**: (a) **Consent** - explain procedure, risks, (b) **Radiograph** - assess root morphology, bone, pathology, (c) **Local anesthesia** - adequate

flashcards.front: Define: parallel to long axis
flashcards.back: Grasping**: Apply forceps **parallel to long axis**, beaks engage tooth **below gingival margin** on **cementum** (not crown - fracture risk)

flashcards.front: Define: below gingival margin
flashcards.back: Grasping**: Apply forceps **parallel to long axis**, beaks engage tooth **below gingival margin** on **cementum** (not crown - fracture risk)

flashcards.front: What are the complications during and after tooth extraction? How do you manage them?
flashcards.back: **INTRA-OPERATIVE**: **1. Fracture of tooth/root**: **Common**. **Prevention**: Proper forceps selection, luxation before forceps, controlled force. **Management**: (a) **Visible root**: Remove with elevator/forceps/surgical extraction, (b) **Small root tip** (<3mm, no infection/pathology, difficult access) - **leave**, inform patient, monitor radiographically. **2. Fracture of alveolar bone**: Thin labial/lingual plate fractures. **Management**: Remove loose fragment, smooth edges, compress soc...

flashcards.front: Define: Small root tip
flashcards.back: **Management**: (a) **Visible root**: Remove with elevator/forceps/surgical extraction, (b) **Small root tip** (<3mm, no infection/pathology, difficult access) - **leave**, inform patient, monitor radiographically

flashcards.back: **INTRA-OPERATIVE**: **1

flashcards.front: Define: Large fragment with tooth attached
flashcards.back: **Large fragment with tooth attached** - **replace** and splint (may retain vitality), refer

flashcards.front: Classify post-extraction hemorrhage. How do you manage it?
flashcards.back: **Post-extraction hemorrhage** classified by **timing**: **1. Primary (Immediate)**: During/immediately after extraction (within hours). **Causes**: Trauma to blood vessels, inadequate compression. **2. Reactionary**: Within **24 hours**. **Causes**: Vasodilator effect (anesthesia wears off), clot dislodgement (rinsing, sucking), hypertension. **3. Secondary**: **After 24 hours** (2-7 days). **Causes**: **Infection** (clot breakdown), local factors (sharp bone), systemic factors. **MANAGEMENT (S...

flashcards.front: Define: 2. Examination
flashcards.back: Key concept in Exodontia

flashcards.front: Define: 24 hours
flashcards.back: Reactionary**: Within **24 hours**

flashcards.front: Define: Remove clot
flashcards.back: Local measures** (FIRST-LINE): (a) **Remove clot** - irrigate socket with saline, suction (expose bleeding point), (b) **Identify source** - socket (bone/soft tissue), torn gingiva, (c) **Gauze pressure** - fold **moistened gauze**, place over socket, patient bites firmly for **20-30 minutes**

flashcards.front: What is dry socket (alveolar osteitis)? Describe etiology, clinical features, and management.
flashcards.back: **Dry socket (Alveolar osteitis)** is a **post-extraction complication** characterized by **severe pain** due to **loss/disintegration of blood clot**, exposing **bare bone**. **Incidence**: 1-5% (all extractions), **20-30%** (mandibular 3rd molars - highest risk). **Etiology (Multifactorial)**: **1. Fibrinolysis**: Excessive **plasmin activity** (dissolves clot). **Bacteria** release fibrinolytic enzymes. **2. Trauma**: **Difficult extraction**, excessive curettage (damages bone blood supply). ...

flashcards.front: Define: Warm saline
flashcards.back: Irrigation**: **Warm saline** (gentle irrigation removes debris, necrotic tissue)

flashcards.front: Define: Socket heals by secondary intention
flashcards.back: **Socket heals by secondary intention**

flashcards.front: Define: 4. Bacterial infection
flashcards.back: Key concept in Exodontia

flashcards.front: What are the indications for surgical extraction? Outline the procedure.
flashcards.back: **Surgical (Transalveolar/Open) extraction** requires **mucoperiosteal flap**, **bone removal**, ± **tooth sectioning**. **Indications**: **1. Fractured root** during extraction (not retrievable with forceps), **2. Root tips** (curved, divergent, ankylosed), **3. Impacted teeth**, **4. Ankylosed teeth**, **5. Hypercementosis**, **6. Dense surrounding bone** (sclerotic), **7. Teeth with abnormal anatomy** (dilacerated roots), **8. Grossly carious teeth** (insufficient crown for forceps), **9. Pla...

flashcards.front: Define: Buccal bone
flashcards.back: Bone removal**: (a) **Buccal bone** - surgical bur (round/fissure) with **copious irrigation** (prevent bone necrosis), (b) Remove **minimum bone** necessary (preserve ridge height), (c) Create **gutter** around root (buccal + interdental bone)

flashcards.front: Define: furcation
flashcards.back: Tooth sectioning** (if needed): **Multirooted teeth**: Separate at **furcation** (remove roots individually)

flashcards.front: Define: tooth sectioning
flashcards.back: **Surgical (Transalveolar/Open) extraction** requires **mucoperiosteal flap**, **bone removal**, ± **tooth sectioning**

flashcards.front: What are the special considerations for tooth extraction in medically compromised patients?
flashcards.back: **CARDIAC PATIENTS**: **Recent MI** (<6 months) - defer elective extraction. **Unstable angina** - defer. **Stable cardiac disease** - proceed with caution. **Prophylactic antibiotics**: **Infective endocarditis (IE) risk**: **High-risk** (prosthetic valves, previous IE, congenital heart disease, cardiac transplant with valvulopathy) - **Amoxicillin 2g** oral **1 hour before** procedure. **Moderate-risk** - no prophylaxis needed (AHA 2007 guidelines). **Anticoagulant therapy**: **Warfarin** - ch...

flashcards.front: Define: Amoxicillin/Penicillin
flashcards.back: **Antibiotics**: **Amoxicillin/Penicillin** safe

flashcards.front: Define: RADIOTHERAPY
flashcards.back: **RADIOTHERAPY**: Extract diseased teeth **2 weeks before** radiotherapy (in radiation field)

flashcards.front: Define: Oral bisphosphonates
flashcards.back: **Assessment**: (a) **IV bisphosphonates** (cancer patients - Zoledronic acid) - **high risk**, (b) **Oral bisphosphonates** (osteoporosis - Alendronate) - low risk if <4 years use

flashcards.front: What post-extraction instructions should be given to patients?
flashcards.back: **Post-extraction care instructions**: **IMMEDIATE (First 24 hours)**: **1. Gauze pack**: Bite firmly on **folded gauze** for **30 minutes** (promotes clot formation). Replace if needed. **2. No rinsing/spitting** for **24 hours** - dislodges clot (dry socket risk). **3. No drinking through straw** - negative pressure dislodges clot. **4. No smoking** for **48 hours** - vasoconstriction, negative pressure, delayed healing, dry socket risk (5x increase). **5. Diet**: **Soft, cool diet** (ice crea...

flashcards.front: Define: Heat packs
flashcards.back: **Heat packs** (after 24 hrs) if swelling persists

flashcards.front: Define: Severe increasing pain
flashcards.back: **Severe increasing pain** after 3 days - **dry socket**, contact dentist

flashcards.front: Define impacted tooth. Classify impacted mandibular third molars.
flashcards.back: **Impacted tooth**: A tooth that **fails to erupt** into normal functional position within expected time, **completely/partially blocked** by bone, soft tissue, or adjacent tooth. **Most commonly impacted**: **Mandibular 3rd molars** (48%), Maxillary 3rd molars (38%), Maxillary canines (10%). **CLASSIFICATIONS OF MANDIBULAR 3rd MOLAR**: **1. WINTER'S CLASSIFICATION** (based on **angulation** of long axis to 2nd molar): **a) Mesioangular** (43%) - most common, crown angulated mesially toward 2nd ...

flashcards.front: Define: f) Buccoangular/Linguoangular
flashcards.back: **f) Buccoangular/Linguoangular** - buccal/lingual tilt

flashcards.front: Define: 1. WINTER'S CLASSIFICATION
flashcards.back: Key concept in Impacted Teeth

flashcards.front: Define: Easiest
flashcards.back: **Easiest** to remove

flashcards.front: What are the indications for removal of impacted third molars? Describe the surgical procedure.
flashcards.back: **INDICATIONS FOR REMOVAL**: **Therapeutic** (symptomatic impactions): **1. Pericoronitis** - recurrent infection of soft tissue covering partially erupted tooth. **2. Dental caries** - impacted tooth or adjacent 2nd molar (distal caries - hard to restore). **3. Periodontal disease** - bone loss distal to 2nd molar. **4. Cyst/tumor formation** - **dentigerous cyst** (around crown), odontogenic tumors. **5. Root resorption** - of adjacent 2nd molar. **6. Pain** - unexplained pain from impacted to...

flashcards.front: Define: Retract
flashcards.back: **Retract** with **Howarth's elevator**

flashcards.front: Define: primary tension-free closure
flashcards.back: Flap closure**: **Interrupted 3-0 silk sutures** - **primary tension-free closure**

flashcards.front: What is pericoronitis? Describe clinical features and management.
flashcards.back: **Pericoronitis** is **acute/chronic inflammation** of **soft tissue (operculum)** overlying **partially erupted tooth**, most commonly **mandibular 3rd molar**. **Etiology**: **1. Trauma**: **Opposing maxillary 3rd molar** bites on inflamed operculum → ulceration → secondary infection. **2. Bacterial**: Food/debris accumulation under operculum → **bacterial proliferation** (mainly **anaerobes**). **3. Insufficient space**: Tooth partially erupted but impacted against 2nd molar or ramus. **CLINI...

flashcards.front: Define: 7. Warm saline rinses
flashcards.back: Key concept in Impacted Teeth

flashcards.front: Define: 1. Pericoronal abscess
flashcards.back: Key concept in Impacted Teeth

flashcards.front: What are the treatment options for impacted maxillary canine?
flashcards.back: **Impacted maxillary canine** - 2nd most common impaction (after 3rd molars). **Location**: **Palatal** (60%), **Labial** (30%), **Mid-alveolar** (10%). **TREATMENT OPTIONS**: **1. Observation**: If **asymptomatic**, adequate space, no pathology, patient refuses treatment. **Monitor** radiographically (3-5 years) for cyst formation, root resorption of adjacent teeth. **2. Extraction**: **Indications**: (a) **Ankylosed** canine (poor prognosis for alignment), (b) **Severe root resorption** of adj...

flashcards.front: Define: 4. Autotransplantation
flashcards.back: Key concept in Impacted Teeth

flashcards.front: Define: Severe root resorption
flashcards.back: Extraction**: **Indications**: (a) **Ankylosed** canine (poor prognosis for alignment), (b) **Severe root resorption** of adjacent incisors, (c) **Advanced age** (poor bone remodeling), (d) **Patient refusal** of orthodontics, (e) **Ectopic position** (unreachable/unfavorable)

flashcards.front: Define: Location
flashcards.back: **Location**: **Palatal** (60%), **Labial** (30%), **Mid-alveolar** (10%)

flashcards.front: What are supernumerary teeth? Describe types and management.
flashcards.back: **Supernumerary teeth** are **extra teeth** beyond normal dentition (32 permanent). **Prevalence**: 1-3% (permanent dentition), males > females (2:1). **Etiology**: **Genetic** (hyperactivity of dental lamina), **cleidocranial dysplasia** (multiple supernumeraries). **TYPES**: **1. Mesiodens**: Most common supernumerary. Located **between maxillary central incisors** (midline). Usually **conical/peg-shaped**, **inverted** position. **Complications**: Prevents eruption of permanent incisors, dias...

flashcards.front: Define: Associated pathology
flashcards.back: **MANAGEMENT**: **INDICATIONS FOR REMOVAL**: (1) **Prevents eruption** of permanent teeth, (2) **Causes displacement/rotation**, (3) **Associated pathology** (cyst, resorption), (4) **Interferes with orthodontic treatment**, (5) **Impedes prosthetic rehabilitation**

flashcards.front: Define: cleidocranial dysplasia
flashcards.back: **Etiology**: **Genetic** (hyperactivity of dental lamina), **cleidocranial dysplasia** (multiple supernumeraries)

flashcards.front: Define: Supplemental
flashcards.back: Supplemental (Eumorphic)**: Resembles **normal tooth** morphology

flashcards.front: How do you prevent and manage inferior alveolar nerve injury during third molar surgery?
flashcards.back: **IAN injury** during mandibular 3rd molar surgery - serious complication causing **paresthesia** (altered sensation) of lower lip, chin, teeth. **Incidence**: **Temporary** 0.4-8%, **Permanent** 0.4-0.8%. **PREVENTION**: **1. Pre-operative assessment**: **Radiographic evaluation** - **OPG** (shows IAN canal proximity). **High-risk signs**: (a) **Darkening of root** (canal superimposition), (b) **Deflection of canal**, (c) **Loss of cortication** of canal, (d) **Root narrowing**. **CBCT** if hig...

flashcards.front: Define: Inform patient
flashcards.back: **MANAGEMENT OF NERVE INJURY**: **IMMEDIATE** (if suspected): (1) **Inform patient**, document, (2) **Reassure** - most recover (70-90% temporary injuries recover in 3-6 months), (3) **Map sensory deficit** - test light touch, pin-prick, two-point discrimination (lower lip, chin), compare sides

flashcards.front: Define: Map sensory deficit
flashcards.back: **MANAGEMENT OF NERVE INJURY**: **IMMEDIATE** (if suspected): (1) **Inform patient**, document, (2) **Reassure** - most recover (70-90% temporary injuries recover in 3-6 months), (3) **Map sensory deficit** - test light touch, pin-prick, two-point discrimination (lower lip, chin), compare sides

flashcards.front: Define: Radiographic evaluation
flashcards.back: Pre-operative assessment**: **Radiographic evaluation** - **OPG** (shows IAN canal proximity)

flashcards.front: Describe the spread of odontogenic infections. What are the determinants?
flashcards.back: **Odontogenic infection** spreads from **tooth apex** through **alveolar bone** into surrounding **soft tissues** (fascial spaces). **PATH OF SPREAD**: **Tooth → Periapical bone → Cortical bone perforation → Soft tissue spaces**. **DETERMINANTS OF SPREAD**: **1. Muscle attachments** (PRIMARY determinant): **a) Above muscle attachments**: Infection points **buccally/labially** → **buccal/vestibular space**. **b) Below muscle attachments**: Infection → **deeper fascial spaces**. **Maxillary teeth*...

flashcards.front: Define: Vestibular, Buccal space, Canine (infraorbital), Submental, Sublingual, Submandibular
flashcards.back: **PRIMARY FASCIAL SPACES** (directly connected to jaw): **Vestibular, Buccal space, Canine (infraorbital), Submental, Sublingual, Submandibular**

flashcards.front: Define: buccal/vestibular space
flashcards.back: Muscle attachments** (PRIMARY determinant): **a) Above muscle attachments**: Infection points **buccally/labially** → **buccal/vestibular space**

flashcards.front: Define: Palatal/Lingual
flashcards.back: Location of perforation**: **Buccal** > **Palatal/Lingual** perforation (due to bone thickness)

flashcards.front: What is Ludwig's angina? Describe clinical features and management.
flashcards.back: **Ludwig's angina** is a **rapidly progressive, life-threatening cellulitis** involving **bilateral submandibular, sublingual, and submental spaces** simultaneously. **BILATERALITY** is key diagnostic feature. **Etiology**: **Odontogenic** (80%) - infection from **mandibular molars** (apices below mylohyoid line). **Precipitating factors**: Poor oral hygiene, immunosuppression (diabetes, HIV), trauma. **Microbiology**: **Mixed aerobic-anaerobic** flora - *Streptococcus viridans, Staphylococcus, ...

flashcards.front: Define: Blunt dissection
flashcards.back: **Multiple incisions**: (a) **Bilateral submandibular** (along lower border of mandible), (b) **Submental** (midline), (c) **Blunt dissection** through spaces (break loculations), (d) **Corrugated rubber drains** placed

flashcards.front: Define: Clindamycin 600-900mg IV q8h
flashcards.back: **Alternative** (penicillin allergy): **Clindamycin 600-900mg IV q8h**

flashcards.front: Define: Mixed aerobic-anaerobic
flashcards.back: **Microbiology**: **Mixed aerobic-anaerobic** flora - *Streptococcus viridans, Staphylococcus, Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus*

flashcards.front: What is cavernous sinus thrombosis? How does odontogenic infection lead to it?
flashcards.back: **Cavernous sinus thrombosis (CST)** is **septic thrombosis** of **cavernous venous sinus** (intracranial venous channel at skull base). **Life-threatening** complication of **maxillary dental infections**. **Mortality**: 20-30% (even with treatment). **PATHWAY OF SPREAD**: **Maxillary teeth** (especially **canine region - "danger triangle of face"**) → **Facial vein/Angular vein** → **Superior/Inferior ophthalmic veins** (valveless) → **Cavernous sinus** → **Septic thrombosis**. **Danger triang...

flashcards.front: Define: Early treatment
flashcards.back: **PREVENTION**: **Early treatment** of maxillary dental infections, **avoid manipulation** of infections in danger triangle (squeezing furuncles/pimples)

flashcards.front: What are the principles of antibiotic therapy in odontogenic infections?
flashcards.back: **Principles**: **1. REMOVE SOURCE**: **Primary treatment** - extraction/RCT of offending tooth, drainage of abscess. **Antibiotics ADJUNCT**, not substitute. **2. EMPIRICAL THERAPY**: Start antibiotics **before culture results** (based on likely organisms). **Modify** when culture/sensitivity available. **3. APPROPRIATE SELECTION**: Based on **microbiology** (mixed aerobic-anaerobic), **severity**, **patient factors**. **ODONTOGENIC FLORA**: **Aerobic**: *Streptococcus viridans* (alpha-hemolyti...

flashcards.front: Define: 3. APPROPRIATE SELECTION
flashcards.back: Key concept in Odontogenic Infections

flashcards.front: Define: 48-72 hours
flashcards.back: **MONITORING**: Reassess **48-72 hours** - if **no improvement**, consider: **Inadequate drainage**, **Resistant organism** (change antibiotic), **Wrong diagnosis**, **Immunosuppression**

flashcards.front: Define: PLUS Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h
flashcards.back: 5-3g IV q6h** **PLUS Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h**

flashcards.front: Describe the principles and technique of incision and drainage of abscess.
flashcards.back: **Incision and drainage (I&D)** - **surgical drainage** of purulent collection (abscess). **PRIMARY treatment** for abscesses (antibiotics adjunct). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Fluctuant abscess** - pus collection present. **2. Spreading cellulitis** - prevent abscess formation. **3. Severe infection** - drain to reduce bacterial load. **PRINCIPLES**: **1. "Pus wherever it is, must be let out"** (Hilton's method). **2. Incision** at **point of maximum fluctuance** (where abscess points). **3. Incision...

flashcards.front: Define: Finger dissection
flashcards.back: **Open** widely (break loculations, explore cavity), **Finger dissection** if large cavity (submandibular space)

flashcards.front: Define: 2. Spreading cellulitis
flashcards.back: Key concept in Odontogenic Infections

flashcards.front: Define: 2-3 days
flashcards.back: Drain placement**: (a) **Corrugated rubber drain** (Penrose) or **iodoform gauze** - prevents premature closure, continued drainage, (b) Insert through incision, **secure** with silk suture to prevent displacement, (c) **Leave** 2-3 days (remove when drainage minimal)

flashcards.front: What is osteomyelitis of the jaw? Describe types and management.
flashcards.back: **Osteomyelitis** is **infection of bone and bone marrow**. **Mandible** more common than maxilla (less vascularity, denser cortical bone). **TYPES**: **1. ACUTE OSTEOMYELITIS**: **Sudden onset**, **severe pain**, **fever**, **swelling**. **Cause**: Odontogenic infection spreads to bone (periapical abscess, fracture, surgery). **Features**: **Pus formation**, **sequestrum** formation (dead bone), **Involucrum** (new bone shell around dead bone). **2. CHRONIC OSTEOMYELITIS**: **Persistent** (>1 m...

flashcards.front: Define: Chronic
flashcards.back: CHRONIC OSTEOMYELITIS**: **Persistent** (>1 month), **low-grade**, **intermittent** symptoms

flashcards.front: Define: Decortication
flashcards.back: Surgical**: (a) **Remove source** - extract infected teeth, (b) **Incision & drainage** - if abscess, (c) **Sequestrectomy** - remove dead bone (sequestra), (d) **Saucerization** - create bony cavity for drainage/granulation, (e) **Decortication** - remove sclerotic bone (chronic), (f) **Resection** - if severe/refractory (rare)

flashcards.front: What is alveoloplasty? Describe indications and procedure.
flashcards.back: **Alveoloplasty** is **surgical reshaping/recontouring** of alveolar ridge to create **optimal foundation** for prosthesis (denture). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Irregular/sharp bony ridge** - causes denture discomfort, sore spots. **2. Undercuts** - prevent denture insertion/seating. **3. Post-extraction** (multiple teeth) - smooth sharp interdental/interradicular septa. **4. Protruding/flared labial plate** - improper denture support. **5. Prominent/sharp mylohyoid ridge** - interferes with denture ...

flashcards.front: Define: Immediate denture
flashcards.back: **PROCEDURE (Radical - separate surgery)**: (1) **Incision**: **Crestal incision** along ridge ± **vertical releasing incisions**, (2) **Flap elevation**: Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap (buccal and lingual/palatal), expose bone, (3) **Bone recontouring**: (a) **Rongeur** - remove irregular bone, sharp projections, (b) **Bone file** - smooth surface, (c) **Surgical bur** (with irrigation) - remove large undercuts, excessive bone, (d) Create **gradual, rounded contour**, (4) **Trim excess soft tissue**: Fibrous/redundant tissue excision, (5) **Flap repositioning**: Approximate flaps (tension-free), (6) **Sutures**: Interrupted 3-0 silk, primary closure, (7) **Immediate denture** or **surgical stent** (protects, applies pressure)

flashcards.front: Define: 3. Post-extraction
flashcards.back: Key concept in Pre-prosthetic Surgery

flashcards.front: Define: surgical reshaping/recontouring
flashcards.back: **Alveoloplasty** is **surgical reshaping/recontouring** of alveolar ridge to create **optimal foundation** for prosthesis (denture)

flashcards.front: What is frenectomy? Describe types and indications.
flashcards.back: **Frenectomy** is **surgical removal** of **frenum** (fold of mucous membrane attaching lip/tongue to underlying structures). **TYPES**: **1. LABIAL FRENECTOMY**: **Maxillary labial** (most common) - between **central incisors** (midline). **Indications**: (a) **Midline diastema** - frenum attachment on incisive papilla prevents closure, (b) **Denture instability** - thick, prominent frenum dislodges denture during lip movement, (c) **Periodontal problem** - frenum pull causes recession. **Proce...

flashcards.front: Define: Ankyloglossia (tongue-tie)
flashcards.back: LINGUAL FRENECTOMY** (Frenuloplasty): **Indication**: **Ankyloglossia (tongue-tie)** - restricted tongue movement

flashcards.front: Define: central incisors
flashcards.back: LABIAL FRENECTOMY**: **Maxillary labial** (most common) - between **central incisors** (midline)

flashcards.front: What is vestibuloplasty? When is it indicated?
flashcards.back: **Vestibuloplasty** is **surgical deepening** of **vestibular sulcus** (space between alveolar ridge and lip/cheek) to **increase denture-bearing area** and improve **denture retention/stability**. **INDICATIONS**: **1. Resorbed alveolar ridge** - severe resorption (long-standing edentulous patient) causes **shallow vestibule**, (2) **Inadequate denture retention** - insufficient sulcus depth for peripheral seal, (3) **High muscle attachments** - muscles attached close to ridge crest (reduce den...

flashcards.front: Define: dissect
flashcards.back: Submucosal vestibuloplasty**: **Incision** at depth of vestibule, **dissect** muscle attachments from bone, **reposition** apically, **secure** with sutures

flashcards.front: Define: away from ridge crest
flashcards.back: **PRINCIPLES**: **Reposition muscles** (mentalis, buccinator, mylohyoid) **away from ridge crest** (apically/laterally)

flashcards.front: Define: Remove
flashcards.back: Secondary epithelialization**: **Remove** mucosa from ridge area, expose bone, allow **spontaneous epithelialization** (6-8 weeks)

flashcards.front: What is maxillary tuberosity reduction? When is it needed?
flashcards.back: **Maxillary tuberosity reduction** is **surgical removal** of **excess bone/soft tissue** from **maxillary tuberosity** (posterior maxilla - distal to last molar). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Enlarged tuberosity**: Excessive vertical height. **2. Insufficient interarch space** - between maxillary tuberosity and mandibular ridge (prevents proper denture fabrication). **Minimum required**: **12-15 mm** for complete dentures. **3. Fibrous hyperplastic tissue** - redundant soft tissue. **4. Unilateral enl...

flashcards.front: Define: 6. Sutures
flashcards.back: Key concept in Pre-prosthetic Surgery

flashcards.front: Define: b) Bone involvement
flashcards.back: **b) Bone involvement**: (i) **Elevate mucoperiosteal flap**, (ii) **Rongeur/bone file** - remove excess bone, (iii) **Smooth** sharp edges, (iv) **Replace flap**

flashcards.front: Define: Measure interarch space
flashcards.back: **Measure interarch space** (calipers/models)

flashcards.front: What is a dentigerous cyst? Describe clinical features and management.
flashcards.back: **Dentigerous (Follicular) cyst** is a **developmental odontogenic cyst** arising from **separation** of **follicle** from **crown** of **unerupted tooth**. **2nd most common** odontogenic cyst (after radicular cyst). **PATHOGENESIS**: Fluid accumulation between **reduced enamel epithelium** and **enamel** surface of unerupted tooth crown. **ASSOCIATION**: **Mandibular 3rd molars** (most common), maxillary canines, maxillary 3rd molars, supernumerary teeth, odontomes. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1....

flashcards.front: Define: fibrous connective tissue
flashcards.back: **HISTOPATHOLOGY**: **Cyst lining** - **non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium** (2-3 cells thick), **fibrous connective tissue** wall

flashcards.front: Define: Enucleation
flashcards.back: **MANAGEMENT**: **SMALL CYSTS** (<2cm): **Enucleation** + **Extraction** of involved tooth

flashcards.front: Define: enamel
flashcards.back: **PATHOGENESIS**: Fluid accumulation between **reduced enamel epithelium** and **enamel** surface of unerupted tooth crown

flashcards.front: What is a radicular cyst? How does it differ from a dentigerous cyst?
flashcards.back: **Radicular (Periapical/Apical) cyst** is an **inflammatory odontogenic cyst** arising from **chronic periapical granuloma** due to **pulp necrosis**. **Most common odontogenic cyst** (60%). **PATHOGENESIS**: **Chronic pulpitis** → **Pulp necrosis** → **Chronic periapical inflammation** → **Stimulation of epithelial rests of Malassez** (in PDL) → **Cyst formation**. **Associated with NON-VITAL tooth** (key difference from dentigerous). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Age**: **3rd-5th decade** (peak)...

flashcards.front: Define: Enucleation + apicoectomy
flashcards.back: Large cysts** (>1 cm - true cyst): **Enucleation + apicoectomy** ± **RCT** (if tooth retained) OR **Extraction + enucleation** (if tooth non-restorable)

flashcards.front: Define: Histology
flashcards.back: **HISTOPATHOLOGY**: **Non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium** lining (thicker than dentigerous - 5-20 cells), **inflamed fibrous wall**, **cholesterol clefts** common (shiny crystals in histology)

flashcards.front: What is odontogenic keratocyst (KCOT)? Why is it clinically significant?
flashcards.back: **Odontogenic keratocyst (OKC)** / **Keratocystic odontogenic tumor (KCOT)** - **aggressive developmental** odontogenic cyst. **Reclassified** as **tumor** by WHO (2005) due to aggressive behavior (changed back to cyst 2017). **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **1. HIGH RECURRENCE** (25-60%) - **most aggressive** odontogenic cyst. **2. Local aggressiveness** - invades marrow spaces, difficult complete removal. **3. Association with syndromes** - **Gorlin-Goltz syndrome** (Nevoid basal cell carcinoma syn...

flashcards.back: **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1

flashcards.front: What are the types of biopsy? When is each indicated?
flashcards.back: **Biopsy** - removal of **tissue** for **histopathological examination** to establish definitive diagnosis. **TYPES**: **1. EXCISIONAL BIOPSY**: **Complete removal** of entire lesion with margin of normal tissue. **Indications**: (a) **Small lesions** (<1-1.5 cm), (b) **Pedunculated** lesions (stalked), (c) **Lesions with low malignancy suspicion** (clinical diagnosis benign), (d) **Therapeutic** - removes lesion completely (diagnosis + treatment). **Advantage**: **Single procedure** (diagnostic...

flashcards.front: Define: Wedge biopsy
flashcards.back: **Technique**: **Wedge biopsy** - elliptical incision (includes normal + abnormal tissue), or **Punch biopsy** (4-6mm punch)

flashcards.front: Define: CONTRAINDICATIONS
flashcards.back: **CONTRAINDICATIONS**: **Vascular lesions** (hemangioma) - risk of severe bleeding (diagnose clinically/radiographically)

flashcards.front: Define: Lesions with low malignancy suspicion
flashcards.back: 5 cm), (b) **Pedunculated** lesions (stalked), (c) **Lesions with low malignancy suspicion** (clinical diagnosis benign), (d) **Therapeutic** - removes lesion completely (diagnosis + treatment)

flashcards.front: Describe the temporomandibular joint (TMJ) anatomy and common disorders.
flashcards.back: **TMJ ANATOMY**: **Ginglymoarthrodial joint** (hinge + gliding). **Components**: **1. Articular surfaces**: **Mandibular condyle** (ellipsoid) + **Glenoid fossa** of temporal bone + **Articular eminence** (tubercle). **2. Articular disc**: **Fibrocartilage** disc divides joint into **upper** (translation/gliding) and **lower** (rotation/hinge) compartments. **Biconcave** shape. **Attachments**: Anteriorly to **lateral pterygoid muscle** (superior head), posteriorly to **retrodiscal tissue** (bil...

flashcards.front: Define: Etiology
flashcards.back: **Etiology**: Stress, bruxism, parafunctional habits

flashcards.front: Define: b) Disc displacement without reduction
flashcards.back: **b) Disc displacement without reduction** (closed lock): Disc **permanently displaced anteriorly**, **cannot reduce**, **limited mouth opening** (<30mm), **deviation** to affected side, **no click** (disc stuck)

flashcards.front: Define: Causes
flashcards.back: **Causes**: Trauma, infection

flashcards.front: What is the management of temporomandibular disorders (TMD)?
flashcards.back: **TMD management** - **conservative first**, surgery **last resort**. **90% improve** with non-surgical therapy. **CONSERVATIVE (First-line)**: **1. Patient education**: Explain condition (usually self-limiting), avoid aggravating factors. **2. Behavior modification**: (a) **Soft diet** - avoid hard, chewy foods, (b) **Avoid wide opening** - no yawning, large bites, (c) **Avoid parafunctional habits** - **no gum chewing**, nail biting, pencil biting, (d) **Stress reduction** - counseling, relaxa...

flashcards.front: Define: Amitriptyline 10-25mg
flashcards.back: Pharmacotherapy**: (a) **NSAIDs** - **Ibuprofen 400mg TDS** (pain, inflammation), (b) **Muscle relaxants** - **Diazepam 5mg** or **Chlorzoxazone** (muscle spasm, especially nighttime bruxism), (c) **Tricyclic antidepressants** - **Amitriptyline 10-25mg** (chronic pain, nocturnal bruxism)

flashcards.front: Define: Muscle relaxation
flashcards.back: Physical therapy**: (a) **Moist heat** - warm compress (20 min, 3-4x/day) - muscle relaxation, (b) **Ice packs** - acute pain (first 24-48 hrs), (c) **Jaw exercises** - gentle stretching (improve mobility, muscle strength), (d) **Massage** - masticatory muscles (masseter, temporalis)

flashcards.front: Define: Lavage
flashcards.back: Arthrocentesis**: **Lavage** of upper joint space (wash out inflammatory mediators, lyse adhesions)

flashcards.front: What is TMJ dislocation? How do you reduce it?
flashcards.back: **TMJ dislocation** - **condyle displaced** anteriorly **beyond articular eminence**, **cannot return** to glenoid fossa. **Bilateral** more common than unilateral. **ETIOLOGY**: **Wide opening** - yawning, laughing, prolonged dental treatment, trauma, seizures. **Predisposing**: **Lax ligaments**, **shallow fossa**, **previous dislocations**. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Mouth wide open** - **cannot close**. **2. Drooling** - cannot swallow saliva. **3. Speech difficulty**. **4. Bilateral**: **P...

flashcards.front: Define: Preauricular depressions
flashcards.back: **Preauricular depressions** (empty fossa palpable)

flashcards.front: Classify mandibular fractures. What is favorable vs unfavorable fracture?
flashcards.back: **ANATOMICAL CLASSIFICATION**: **1. Symphysis**: Midline (between mental foramina). **2. Parasymphysis**: Between midline and canine. **3. Body**: Between canine and angle. **4. Angle**: Junction of body and ramus (most common - 25-30%). **Tooth in fracture line**, thin bone. **5. Ramus**: Ascending vertical portion. **6. Condyle**: Condylar neck/head (2nd most common - 25%). **Subcondylar, condylar neck, intracapsular**. **7. Coronoid process**: Rare (<1%). **DIRECTION OF FRACTURE LINE**: **FAV...

flashcards.front: Define: muscle pull
flashcards.back: **DIRECTION OF FRACTURE LINE**: **FAVORABLE vs UNFAVORABLE** (muscle forces on fracture stability): **FAVORABLE**: Fracture line oriented such that **muscle pull** tends to **CLOSE/APPROXIMATE** fragments (stable)

flashcards.front: Define: closed reduction
flashcards.back: **Favorable** may respond to **closed reduction** (IMF)

flashcards.front: Describe Le Fort classification of maxillary fractures.
flashcards.back: **Le Fort classification** - **midface fractures** involving **maxilla**. Based on **predictable lines of weakness**. **KEY FEATURE**: **ALL Le Fort fractures** involve **pterygoid plates** (posterior maxilla) - **pathognomonic**. **LE FORT I** (Horizontal/Floating palate): **Level**: **Low** (above roots of teeth). **Fracture line**: Transverse across **maxilla above apices** of teeth, through **lateral nasal walls**, across **maxillary sinuses**, through **pterygoid plates** (posterior). **Res...

flashcards.front: Define: Pyramid-shaped
flashcards.back: **Fracture line**: **Pyramid-shaped** - starts at **nasal bridge** (nasofrontal suture), extends **laterally** through **medial orbital walls** (lacrimal bones), **inferior orbital rim**, across **maxillary sinuses**, through **pterygoid plates**

flashcards.back: Key concept in Maxillofacial Trauma

flashcards.front: What are the principles of fracture management? Describe IMF and ORIF.
flashcards.back: **FRACTURE MANAGEMENT PRINCIPLES**: **1. Airway** - ensure patent airway (priority in trauma). **2. Hemorrhage control** - stop bleeding. **3. Soft tissue management** - clean, debride, suture lacerations. **4. Reduction** - restore anatomic position. **5. Fixation/Immobilization** - maintain reduction during healing. **6. Nutrition** - adequate (liquid diet if IMF). **7. Function** - restore occlusion, jaw movement. **8. Esthetics** - restore facial contour. **METHODS OF FIXATION**: **1. CLOSED...

flashcards.front: Define: Displaced
flashcards.back: **Indications**: (a) **Favorable fractures** (stable), (b) **Minimally displaced** fractures, (c) **Condylar fractures** (conservative management), (d) **Adjunct to ORIF** (maintain occlusion during healing)

flashcards.front: Define: Multiple
flashcards.back: **Indications**: (a) **Unfavorable** fractures (unstable), (b) **Displaced** fractures, (c) **Multiple** fractures, (d) **Comminuted** fractures, (e) **Edentulous** mandible (no teeth for IMF), (f) **Bilateral** fractures (both sides), (g) **Condylar fractures** with malocclusion/dislocation

flashcards.front: Define: Advantages
flashcards.back: **Advantages**: (1) **Rigid fixation** - immediate stability, (2) **Early function** - no/minimal IMF needed, (3) **Better alignment** - direct visualization, (4) **Shorter treatment** - faster return to normal

flashcards.front: What is a zygomatic complex (tripod) fracture? How is it diagnosed and managed?
flashcards.back: **Zygomatic complex (ZMC) / Tripod fracture** - fracture of **zygoma** (cheekbone) with **separation** at **3 sutures**: (1) **Zygomaticofrontal** (lateral orbital rim), (2) **Zygomaticomaxillary** (inferior orbital rim), (3) **Zygomaticotemporal** (zygomatic arch). **Etiology**: **Direct trauma** to cheek - assault (fist/blunt object), RTA, sports. **Zygoma prominence** makes it vulnerable. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Flattening of cheek** - **loss of malar prominence** (depressed zygoma). **2....

flashcards.front: Define: 2. Radiographs
flashcards.back: Key concept in Maxillofacial Trauma

flashcards.front: Define: 7. Diplopia

flashcards.front: What is the management of condylar fractures?
flashcards.back: **Condylar fractures** - **2nd most common** mandibular fracture (25%). **Bilateral** common (falls on chin - force transmitted to both condyles). **TYPES**: **1. Intracapsular** - fracture within TMJ capsule (condylar head). **2. Condylar neck** - below head, above sigmoid notch. **3. Subcondylar** - below sigmoid notch (most common). **DISPLACEMENT**: **Non-displaced/minimally displaced** vs **Displaced** vs **Dislocated** (complete loss of contact). **GENERAL PRINCIPLE**: **MOST condylar frac...

flashcards.front: Define: 3. Associated midface fractures
flashcards.back: Key concept in Maxillofacial Trauma

flashcards.back: **SURGICAL APPROACHES**: **Preauricular**, **Submandibular**, **Retromandibular**, **Endoscopic**

flashcards.front: Define: 2. Condylar neck
flashcards.back: Key concept in Maxillofacial Trauma

flashcards.front: What is sialolithiasis? Describe clinical features and management.
flashcards.back: **Sialolithiasis** - **salivary calculus (stone)** in ductal system of salivary gland. **Most common salivary gland disorder**. **LOCATION**: **Submandibular gland/duct** (80-90%) - **Wharton's duct**. **Reasons**: (1) **Upward flow** (against gravity), (2) **Thick alkaline** secretion, (3) **Higher calcium/phosphate** content, (4) **Long tortuous** duct (12 cm). **Parotid** (5-20%), **Sublingual** (rare). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Intermittent painful swelling** - **pathognomonic**. **Swellin...

flashcards.front: Define: 3. Reduced salivary flow
flashcards.back: Key concept in Salivary Gland Disorders

flashcards.front: Define: Occlusal radiograph
flashcards.back: Radiographs**: **Occlusal radiograph** (80% stones radiopaque - calcium)

flashcards.front: What is a ranula? Describe types and management.
flashcards.back: **Ranula** - **mucous extravasation pseudocyst** arising from **sublingual gland** (floor of mouth). **Bluish, translucent swelling** ("frog belly" appearance - Latin *rana* = frog). **TYPES**: **1. SIMPLE (Oral) RANULA**: **Confined** to **floor of mouth** (above mylohyoid muscle). **Clinical features**: (a) **Painless, soft, fluctuant** swelling in **floor of mouth** (one side of frenulum), (b) **Bluish/translucent** color (saliva visible through thin mucosa - pathognomonic), (c) **Displaces t...

flashcards.front: Define: only neck
flashcards.back: **Clinical features**: (a) **Neck swelling** (submandibular region), (b) **Soft, fluctuant**, non-tender, (c) **May** have oral component (floor of mouth swelling) OR **only neck** swelling, (d) **Differential**: Dermoid cyst, lymphangioma, cystic hygroma, branchial cyst

flashcards.front: Define: Wharton's duct injury
flashcards.back: **Risks**: **Lingual nerve injury** (runs close - numbness of tongue), **Wharton's duct injury** (submandibular duct - runs through floor of mouth)

flashcards.front: How do you manage dental extractions in patients with bleeding disorders?
flashcards.back: **PRE-OPERATIVE ASSESSMENT**: **1. History**: **Type of bleeding disorder**, **Severity**, **Previous bleeding episodes** (dental/surgical), **Medications** (anticoagulants, antiplatelets). **2. Laboratory tests**: **Hemophilia**: **Factor VIII** (Hemophilia A) or **Factor IX** (Hemophilia B) levels. **Anticoagulants**: **INR** (warfarin), **aPTT** (heparin). **General**: **Platelet count**, **PT**, **Bleeding time**. **3. Physician consultation**: Coordinate with hematologist/physician. **HEMOP...

flashcards.front: Define: 1 hr before
flashcards.back: 3 mcg/kg IV **1 hr before** procedure

flashcards.front: Define: THROMBOCYTOPENIA
flashcards.back: **THROMBOCYTOPENIA**: **Platelet count <50,000** - **defer** elective extraction

flashcards.front: Define: Desmopressin (DDAVP)
flashcards.back: Mild Hemophilia A** (Factor VIII 5-25%): **Desmopressin (DDAVP)** - releases stored Factor VIII (2-3x increase)

flashcards.front: Describe the phases of wound healing.
flashcards.back: **Wound healing** - complex process restoring tissue integrity. **TYPES**: **Primary intention** - wound edges approximated (sutured) - minimal scarring. **Secondary intention** - wound left open (granulation fills defect) - more scarring. **Tertiary intention** (delayed primary) - initially open, closed later. **PHASES (Overlapping)**: **1. HEMOSTASIS (Immediate - minutes)**: **Vasoconstriction** → **Platelet aggregation** → **Clot formation** (fibrin mesh). **Prevents blood loss**, **scaffoldi...

flashcards.front: Define: Secondary intention
flashcards.back: **Secondary intention** - wound left open (granulation fills defect) - more scarring

flashcards.front: Define: Tertiary intention
flashcards.back: **Tertiary intention** (delayed primary) - initially open, closed later

flashcards.front: Describe principles of suturing and commonly used suture materials.
flashcards.back: **PRINCIPLES OF SUTURING**: **1. Aseptic technique** - sterile instruments, gloves. **2. Tissue handling**: **Gentle** - avoid crushing (tissue forceps with teeth, not hemostats). **3. Hemostasis**: **Control bleeding** before closure (pressure, cautery). **4. Dead space elimination**: **Close in layers** (prevent hematoma/seroma). **5. Eversion**: Wound edges **slightly everted** (not inverted) - better healing, less scar depression. **6. Tension-free**: **No tension** on wound edges (undermini...

flashcards.front: Classify nerve injuries. How do you assess and manage paresthesia?
flashcards.back: **NERVE INJURY CLASSIFICATION (Seddon)**: **1. NEUROPRAXIA**: **Mildest**. **Conduction block** (axon intact, myelin damaged). **No Wallerian degeneration**. **Recovery**: **Complete**, **spontaneous** (days-weeks). **Prognosis**: Excellent. **2. AXONOTMESIS**: **Moderate**. **Axon disrupted**, **endoneurium intact** (nerve sheath preserved). **Wallerian degeneration** occurs distal to injury. **Regeneration**: **Yes** (axons regrow along intact sheath - **1mm/day** / **1 inch/month**). **Recove...

flashcards.front: What are the indications for tracheostomy? Describe the procedure.
flashcards.back: **Tracheostomy** - surgical creation of opening into **trachea** (through anterior neck) for airway access. **Temporary** (tube removable) or **Permanent**. **INDICATIONS**: **1. Upper airway obstruction**: (a) **Maxillofacial trauma** - bilateral mandibular fractures, midface fractures (tongue fall-back, bleeding), (b) **Ludwig's angina** - floor of mouth swelling → airway compromise, (c) **Tumors** - oral/pharyngeal/laryngeal (obstructing airway), (d) **Burns** - inhalational injury, facial bu...

flashcards.front: Describe the extracranial course and branches of the facial nerve.
flashcards.back: **Facial nerve (CN VII)** - **motor nerve** to muscles of **facial expression** + parasympathetic to salivary/lacrimal glands + taste (anterior 2/3 tongue via chorda tympani). **EXTRACRANIAL COURSE**: **1. Exit from skull**: Emerges from **stylomastoid foramen** (between styloid and mastoid processes). **2. Enters parotid gland**: Immediately enters **parotid gland** (runs through gland - **NOT supplies parotid** - supplied by glossopharyngeal CN IX). **3. Division**: Divides into **two main tru...

flashcards.front: Define: Submandibular gland surgery
flashcards.back: **At risk**: **Submandibular gland surgery**, **neck dissections**, **mandibular angle/body fracture surgery**, facelift

flashcards.front: What are the essential elements of informed consent for oral surgery?
flashcards.back: **Informed consent** - **legal and ethical** requirement before any surgical procedure. Patient must **understand** and **voluntarily agree** to treatment after receiving adequate information. **ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS**: **1. COMPETENCE**: Patient must be **legally competent** (adult >18 years, sound mind). **Exceptions**: **Minors** (<18 years) - **parental consent** required. **Emergency** - implied consent (save life). **Incompetent patient** - guardian/next-of-kin consent. **2. DISCLOSURE OF INF...

flashcards.front: Describe General.
flashcards.back:
A suture is a strand or thread of material used to approximate tissues and
also to ligate blood vessels.
Thus, Suturing is the act of sewing or bringing tissues or flap edges together
and holding them in apposition until normal healing takes place.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Monofilament & Multifilament.
flashcards.back:
1> Absorbable Sutures
These are suture materials that are digested or hydrotysed by the enzymes
present in the body. Thus they require no removal from the surgical site.
a> Natural - Catgut
b> Synthetic - Polyglycolic acid
2> Non-Absarbable Sutures
These materials can not be metabolised by the body's natural mechanism
therefore they should be removed by the surgeon at the end of healing.
a> Natural - Silk, linen
b> Synthetic - Nylon, polypropylene

maxillary sinus.
Immediate aro-antral fistula usually donot have any epithelial lining but
chronic aro-antral fistula are epithelialised.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Extraction of teeth.
flashcards.back:
- injudicious instrumentation
- age (sinus cavity enlarges with age)
- surgical removal of impacted maxillary molar
Inan attempt to remove fractured root from socket

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Periapical lesions of teeth.
flashcards.back: Multiple and extensive fractures of facial region

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Malignancy involving maxillary sinus.
flashcards.back:
Removal of a large cyst or resection of large tumor involving maxilla.
CLINICAL FEATURES — ACUTE ORO-ANTRAL FISTULA
Fluid regurgitation and escape of air from nastrils
Due to infection, it results in sinusitis ,so all signs and symptoms of sinusitis
are present.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Enhanced coloumn of air.
flashcards.back:
change in vocal resonance
change in voice
Severe pain in the involved region

flashcards.front: Write a short note on CHRONIC ORO-ANTRAL FISTULA.
flashcards.back:
Continuous unilateral foul smelling nasal discharge
Systemic complications - pyrexia, malaise, headache
Unpleasant taste may result due to discharge of postnasal drip down
the pharynx

flashcards.front: Describe the management of CHRONIC ORO-ANTRAL FISTULA.
flashcards.back:
The blood clot formed at the opening should be preserved to promote healing and
clasure of the opening.

flashcards.front: Describe the procedure for CHRONIC ORO-ANTRAL FISTULA.
flashcards.back:
1. Buccal flap
a> Von Rehrmann flap
b) Moczair flap (sliding buccal flap)
2. Palatal flap
a> Ashley's flap
b> Krugers modification of Ashley's flap
3. Combination of buccal and palatal flap
a> Bridge flap
4. Buccal pad of fat
5. Tongue flap
a> pasteriarly placed darsal tongue flap
b> laterally placed tongue flap
6. Turnover flap
7. Nasolabial flap
8. Gold foil
9. Polyglycolic acid mesh

flashcards.front: Write a short note on SUPPORTIVE MEASURES.
flashcards.back:
1. Antibiotics - For prevention of secondary infection
- Selection of AB should be done on the basis of culture
and sensitivity testing
- Penicillin and its derivatives are the drug of choice.
- Penicillin V 250-500mg 6 hourly is adequate
[initially IV later switched to oral route]
- In case of penicillin resistance, a broad spectrum AB
2. Nasal decongestants - encourage drainage of pus and secretions
- Ephedrine nasal drops (0.5%), instilled intranasally
every 2-3 hours
The correct method of us...

flashcards.front: Describe SUPPORTIVE MEASURES.
flashcards.back:
George Caldwell and Henri Luc described a new method of entering into the
maxillary sinus where 2 separate openings are made, one in the canine fassa
to gain access to the antrum and other in the nasoantral wall for drainage.
Hence. it is called as Caldwell- Luc procedure.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on It is used for.
flashcards.back:
- removal of teeth/fragments of root/impacted teeth in the antrum
- treatment of chronic maxillary sinusitis with polyps and cystic
degeneration of mucosa
- excision of cysts and benign tumours of maxillary sinus
— management of hematoma or haemarrhage in the maxillary sinus
- to treat fractures involving floor of the orbit or anterior maxillary
sinus wall

Incision extends from apex of canine down to near the gingival margin &

In edentulous cases, it is done along the alveolar ridge
The incision should be larger than the window to be cut in the bone
The mucoperiostium is then detached and retracted till the infra orbital
ridge taking care to preserve the integrity of infraorbital nerve.
A small gouge is used to perforate centre of the canine fassa.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on ANAPHYLAXIS.
flashcards.back:
Anaphylaxis is aclinical syndrome of severe hypersensitivity reaction
characterised by cardiovascular system collapse respiratory system
depression, skin reaction and smooth muscle contractions.

Expasure to certain drugs and fareign bodies

clinical features of anaphylaxis

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of Production of IgE antibodies.
flashcards.back:
Cutaneous manifestations — pruritus
- urticaria
- angioedema
Respiratory manifestations — wheezing
- coughing
- stridar
- dyspnoea
CVS manifestations — Tachycardia
- Shock
- Hypotension
- Cardiac dyerhythmia

flashcards.front: What is the treatment for Production of IgE antibodies?
flashcards.back:
If the allergic reaction is confined to the skin, an antihistamine is administered
IV or IM.
If respiratory involvement is seen, position the patient in supine position
with legs elevated, administer O2 and perform CPR if required.
Inject Adr 0.5 mg [ 0.5 ml of 1:1000 solution] IM; repeat every 5-10
min in case the patient does not show improvement or improvement is
transient.
This is the only Life saving measure.
Adr should not be injected IV unless shock is life threatning

flashcards.front: Write a short note on ENUCLEATION.
flashcards.back:
Enucleation involves complete removal of cyst lining and its contents.
Intraaral approach is the method of choice for enucleation although rarely
extraoral approach through the submandibular skin may be excised.
It is also c/a cystectomy or PartischII procedure.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Small cysts.
flashcards.back:
Small or large cysts not endangering vital structures or risk of pathological
fracture
Cysts with high recurrance rate (OKC)

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Pastoperative care is reduced.
flashcards.back: Thorough examination of the entire cystic lining can be done

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of Pastoperative care is reduced?
flashcards.back: Pathological jaw fractures can occur in case of enucleation of large cyst

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Damage to adjacent vital structures.
flashcards.back:
Direct observation of wound healing as in case of marsupialisation is not
passible.
In young people, tooth germ or unerupted teeth involved with the cyst'
are removed with the lining of the cyst.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Mucoperiosteal flap elevation.
flashcards.back:
Prepare bony window, if bone over the cyst is intact
If thin layer of bone is present, it is carefully peeled off with periosteal elevator
cyst should be removed entirely without tearing
cyst Lining is gently separated from cavity wall using
periosteal elevatar or spoon excavator
if lining of cyst is difficult to separate then insert a
rolled gauze b/w cavity & cyst lining.
Or else, the cyst can be aspirated so that the sac shrinks
The cavity is isorigated and inspected and closed with sutures

flashcards.front: Write a short note on ENLICLEATION WITH OPEN PACKING.
flashcards.back:
It is preferred in cases of infected large cysts where primary clasure might lead
to breakdown of the wound and intergere with healing.
So, instead of primary closure, X the flap is turned into the bony cavity.
It is fixed with 1/2 inch medicated gauze pack for 10 days.
A change in pack is followed by construction of an acrylic plug as used in
marsupialisation.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on VASOCONSTRICTORS IN LA.
flashcards.back:
Vasoconstrictors are chemical agents added to local anaesthetic solutions
a> to oppose vasodilation caused by the local anaesthetic agents
b> to achieve hemastasis (constrict the blood vessels and control tissue
perfusion)

flashcards.front: Write a short note on USES OF VASOCONSTRICTORS.
flashcards.back:
The net effects caused by addition of vasoconstrictor to LA are-
a> It decreases the rate of absorption of local anaesthetic agent into
cardiovascular system
b> It lowers the plasma level of local anaesthetic agent, ,and, hence,
decreases the risk of systemic toxicity.
c> It increases the duration and depth of action, as higher volumes of
local araesthetic agent remain in and around the nerve for longer
time
d> It decreases bleeding at the site of injection
The pH of LA solution with vasoconstri...

flashcards.front: What are the indications for USES OF VASOCONSTRICTORS?
flashcards.back:
Patients with significant cardiovascular diseases such as
a> ischemic heart disease b> hypertension and c> cerebral strokes
Patients with thyrotoxicasis or hyperthyroid states & diabetes mellitus
Patients receiving non specific beta-blockers ,monoamine oxidase
inhibitors tricyclic antidepressants

REDUCING AGENTS OR ANTIOXIDANTS FOR VA50CONSTRICTORS
Vasoconstrictors are relatively unstable in solutions, and undergo
oxidation, when exposed to sunlight. The solution appears pink to
brown in color after oxidation.
To ensure a reasonable shelf life, it is necessary to include a
reducing agent or antioxidant in the LA soln.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on A PRINCIPLES OF USE OF ELEVATORS.
flashcards.back:
There are 3 work principles applicable to the dental elevators.
1> Lever principle
2> Wedge principle
3> Wheel and axle principle
flashcards.topic: A PRINCIPLES OF USE OF ELEVATORS

flashcards.front: Write a short note on LEVER PRINCIPLE.
flashcards.back:
most commonly used principle
The elevator is a Lever of first order. In this, the fulcrum is located
between the load and effort. In order to gain mechanical advantage,
the effort arm must be longer than the resistance/ load arm.
When elevators are used, the force is transmitted at Long power arm of the
handle, so that the MA is derived at the short weight arm i.e the working
blade, which engages the tooth. eg - coupland elevator.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on WEDGE PRINCIPLE.
flashcards.back:
It consists of 2 movable inclined planes, which meet and form a sharp
angle. The effort is applied to the base of the plane & the resistance
has its effect on the slant side.
A wedge can be used to split, expand or displace the portion of a
substance that receives force. The sharper the angle of the wedge,
the less effort is required to overcome the resistance.
The MA of wedge is 2.5.
The wedge elevator is forced between the root & bone parallel to the
long axis of the tooth. Eg- A small apexo e...

flashcards.front: Write a short note on LEFORT I FRACTURE.
flashcards.back:
It is also called as Low bevel gractive
- Low level or, Subzygomatic fracture
- Harizontal fracture of maxilla
- Guerin's fracture
- It is also called as floating fracture, because there is a separation of
complete dentoalveolar part of the maxilla, and the fractured fragment
is held only by means of soft tissue.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Fracture Line.
flashcards.back:
Here the harizontal fracture line is seen above the apices of the teeth,
which detaches the tooth-bearing partion of the maxilla from the rest
of the facial skeleton.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Lateral extension of fracture line.
flashcards.back:
commences or the lateral margin of piriform aperture, passes
horizontally above the nasal floor and canine fassa, passes along
the antero lateral wall of maxillary sinus, runs below the zygomatic
and then runs upwards and posteriorly to fracture the pterygoid
& plate at the junction of lower third and upper 2/3 rds.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Medial extension of fracture line.
flashcards.back:
starts on the lateral margin of piriform aperture, passes along the
laterall wall of nase and then joins the lateral wall behind the
maxillary tuberasity.

flashcards.front: What is the etiology of Medial extension of fracture line?
flashcards.back:
This type of fracture results from application of horizontal force just
above the apices of maxillary teeth.

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of Medial extension of fracture line.
flashcards.back:
Slight swelling and edema of the lower part of the face along with
upper Lip swelling.
Ecchymasis in the Labial and buccal vestibule as well as contusion
of the skin of the upper lip.
Laceration of upper Lip and intraoral mucasa may be seen

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Bilateral epistaxis or nasal bleeding.
flashcards.back:
Mobility of upper dentoalveolar partion of jaw, which is frequently
mobile to digital pressure.
Occlusion may be disturbed. Patient may not be able to masticate food
Pain while speaking or moving the jaw
Percussion of the maxillary teeth produces dull "cracked cup "sound.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on FRACTURE OF ZYGOMATIC COMPLEX.
flashcards.back:
The zygomaticomaxillary complex plays a key role in the structure,
function and esthetics of the 100 facial region.
It provides normal check contour and separates the orbital contents
from the temporal fassa and maxillary sinus.
As the zygomatic bone is clasely associated with the maxilla, frontal
and temporal bones, and they are usually involved when a zygomatic
bone fracture occurs, therefore these type of fractures are called
zygomaticomazillary fractures or zygomatic complex fractures or
tri...

flashcards.front: Write a short note on OF ZYGOMATIC COMPLEX.
flashcards.back:
1) ROWE & KILLEY (1968)
Type I - No significant displacement
Type II - Fractures of the zygomatic arch
Type III - Rotation around the vertical axis

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Outward displacement of orbital rim.

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of THAMINM.
flashcards.back:
Flattening of the injured cheek (passibly masked by swelling) - most
common displacement of the complex is inward.
Immediately following injury or after the acute phase of edema is
subsided, then an alteration of contour on the affected side is
apparent.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Unilateral epistaxis may be present.
flashcards.back:
Circumarbital ecchymasis will develop after few hours from
effusion of blood into the surrounding tissues.
Circumarbital edema can be quite grass. The examination of the
eye may not be possible till edema subsides.
Subconjunctival hemarrhage is observed at the outer canthus
Depression of ocular level / limitation of ocular movement may be seen
Proptasis of the eye may be seen due to retrobulbar hemorrhage
Patient may complain of diplopia/ blurring of vision
Anaesthesia of the cheek, nase and Lip...

flashcards.front: What is the treatment for Unilateral epistaxis may be present?
flashcards.back:
In majority of cases, early operation is advisable, provided that there
are no opthalmic or cranial complications.
Whenever there is a grass periorbital edema and ecchymosis,
pastponement of the operation for 3-5 days can be done, but it
should not be prolonged more than 2 weeks.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on STABLE FRACTURES -.
flashcards.back:
Simple elevation will be sufficient, because of high degree of stability
due to integrity of temporal fascia and the interdigitation of the
fracture lines. No additional fixation is required after reduction.
Type 1-No treatment

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Type Unless vertically displaced.
flashcards.back:
Type 3 - and 4 (a) - Open reduction may be required
and transosseous wiring is advisable

flashcards.front: Write a short note on UNSTABLE FRACTURES -.
flashcards.back:
Require open reduction and transasseous wiring or bone plating
- Type 4(b)
- Types 5,6 and 7,8

flashcards.front: Describe the technique for UNSTABLE FRACTURES -.
flashcards.back:
Reduction of zygomatic fracture can be done by following approaches
Temporal fossa approach [Gilles]
Buccal sulcus approach [Keen]
Lateral caronoid approach [Quinn]

flashcards.front: Describe UNSTABLE FRACTURES -.
flashcards.back:
Odontogenic sinusitis is the inflammation of mucasa of any of the paranasal
sinuses.
Inflammation of most or all of the paranasal air sinuses simultaneously is
known as pansinusitis.
The maxillary sinusitis is usually odontogenic in origin, because of its clase
proximity with the maxillary teeth.
Maxillary sixusitis can be classified into <1> Acute and <2> Chronic

flashcards.front: What is the etiology of UNSTABLE FRACTURES -?
flashcards.back:
It may be due to a persistant dental focus,
chronic rhinitis
chronic infection in the frontal or ethmoidal sinus
allergic conditions

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of PATHOPHYSIOLOGY.
flashcards.back:
Severe throbbing pain, when the pus remains confined within bone in apical
region. The intensity reduces after 12-24 hours as the pus is discharged into
antral cavity.
Unilateral foul discharge through pasterior nares
Cacasmia - a fetid odor with bad taste in the mouth.

Anterior rhinascopy - It shows nasal congestion and mucopurulent
material In the middle meatus
Inspection of aropharynx reveals descending pharyngeal exudates
Presence of an obvious aroantral fistila
Prolapse of polypoidal mass into the mouth forming a Lump.

flashcards.back:
IV sedation or deep conscious sedation is a type of sedation where,
medications are administered directly into the bloodstream through
IV route.
First IV sedation was devised by Jargensen, who advocated the use of
diluted solution of pentoborbitone, pethidine and hyascine by slow
IV injection.

fear

flashcards.back:
It is desirable to incise through attached gingiva and over a healthy
bone. The suture line should have adequate bone support underneath
for uneventful healing.
Incisions placed near the teeth for extractions should be made in the
gingival sulcus.
Incisions involving the reflection of mucoperiasteal flap are direct,
straight line or curvilinear taking the shortest distance vertically
through the tissues.
Indirect incisions are used to access the areas like soft palate, tongue,
cheeks, Lips, floo...

flashcards.front: Write a short note on GARRE'S OSTEOMYELITIS.
flashcards.back:
ГИЗМТАТЯ!
Osteomyelitis may be defined as an lammatory condition of bone that
begins as an infection of medullary cavity and haversian systems of the
cartex and extends to involve the periasteum of the affected area.

flashcards.front: Describe the pathogenesis of GARRES OSTEOMYELITIS.
flashcards.back:
The etiological agents can be a carious tooth, or the overlying soft tissue
infection. The infectious process. localises in periasteum or beneath the
periasteal covering of cartex and spreads slightly into the interior dal of bone.
In case of persistence of the causative agent, it causes bony thickening,
visible on radiograph.

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of GARRES OSTEOMYELITIS.
flashcards.back:
Primarilyassects children and young adults, and occasionally in older
individuals.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Mandible is more commonly affected.
flashcards.back:
Localised, hard, nontender bony swelling of lateral and inferior aspects of
mandible
Lymphadenopathy, hyperpyrexia and leukocytasis usually not found

flashcards.front: Write a short note on RADIOGRAPHY.
flashcards.back:
A focal area of well calcified bone proliferation may be seen that is smooth
and that often has a laminated or onion skin appearance.
The radiographic appearance is typical. There is condensation of cortical
bone, and overgrowth of bony Tissue beneath the periasteum.
The increase in mass of bone is due to
- mild Toxic stimulation of fibroblasts by attenuated infection
- compensatory thickening of bone as a mechanical adaptation to
reinforce the area weakened through disease.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on MARSUPIALISATION.
flashcards.back:
Marsupialisation refers To creating a surgical window in the wall of the
cyst, evacuating the contents of the cyst, and maintaining continuity
between the cyst and the oral cavity, maxillary sinus or nasal cavity.
The only partion that is removed is the piece removed to produce the
window. The remaining cystic lining is left in situ.
It is also called decompression or Partisch I technique or cystotomy.
This process decreases intracystic pressure and promotes shrinkage of
the cyst and bone fill.

flashcards.front: What are the indications for MARSUPIALISATION?
flashcards.back:
Age - In a young child, with developing tooth germs, or when development
of displaced Teeth has not progressed enucleation would damage the
tooth buds.
In the elderly, debilitated patient, marsupialisation is less stressful
Proximity to vital structures - when proximity of the cyst to vital
structures could create an aronasal or aroantral fistula, injure
neurovascular bundle or damage vital teeth, then marsupialisation
should be considered.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on SALIVARY FISTULA.
flashcards.back:
Salivary fistula is a chronic communication between the salivary
gland or duct and the skin through which saliva is discharged

flashcards.front: What are the types of SALIVARY FISTULA?
flashcards.back:
Internal -opening into mucasa of buccal cavity
External - opening into the skin, ,saliva is discharged on skin
causing eczema, excoriation, inflammation

flashcards.front: What is the etiology of SALIVARY FISTULA?
flashcards.back:
It is a rare condition caused by accidental trauma, surgery and
infection.
It may occur as a result of actinomycosis, syphillis and cancrum
oris.
Any surgery on the parotid gland -superficial parotidectomy, drainage
of abscess, surgery for carcinoma cheek

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of SALIVARY FISTULA.
flashcards.back:
Discharging watery fluid, exaggerated by keeping time in the mouth
collection of saliva under the skin flap with drainage through the wound
Fistulogram confirms the diagnosis.
Sialography is performed to establish the exact site or origin of fistula.

flashcards.front: What is the treatment for SALIVARY FISTULA?
flashcards.back:
Treatment consists of aspiration of fluid, compression dressings and
removal of salivary stimulants.
Currently the most commonly used treatment is botulinum neurotoxin
type A and systemic hyascine or propantheline bromide.
The problem usually resolves with this treatment regimen.

flashcards.front: Classify SALIVARY FISTULA.
flashcards.back:
Ankylasis means stiff joint or the joint which cant move.
TMJ ankylosis refers to stiffening (immovability) or pathological
intracapsular fusion/union (either of fibrous tissue adhesion or bony
deposition) between the head of the condyle and glenoid fassa articular area.

Intra-articularar true ankylasis is caused by fibrous orbony adhesions
between the articular surfaces of the mandibular condyle and glenoid
fassa.

So an interpasitional material is to be placed in between them to avoid

Materials which can be used are either alloplastic or autogenous materials.

Gry socket is defined as a past-operative pain in and around the dental
alveolus, which increases in severity at some moment between the first
and third day after a dental extraction, , accompanied by partial or
total disintegration of the intraalveolar clot, causing foul smell.
Term dry socket was given by Crawford (1896)
It is also known as - alveolar asteitis
- Fibrinolytic alveolitis
- Fibrinolytic asteitis

Inflammation of most or all of the paranasal sinuses simultaneously is
known as pansinusitis.
The maxillary sinusitis is usually odontogenic in origin, because of its
clase proximity with the maxillary teeth.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on PLEOMORPHIC ADENOMA.
flashcards.back:
Also called as Benign mixed tumors, they are the most common salivary
gland tumors, which predominantly affect the superficial lobe of the
parotid gland.

flashcards.front: Describe the pathogenesis of PLEOMORPHIC ADENOMA.
flashcards.back:
According to Gardick, a neoplastically altered epithelial cell with the
potential for multidirectional differentiation might be histogenetically
responsible for pleomorphic adenoma.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Female predilection.
flashcards.back:
Most common site - parotid glands.
Occurance in sublingual gland is rare.
8% of pleomarphic adenomas involve minor salivary glands.
Among them palate is the most common site. (60-65%)
The tumor in major salivary glands is slow growing, painless and
does not show fixation to deeper tissues or overlying skin.
Though pain is not a common symptom, Local discomfort is always
present.
In intraoral accessory glands, the tumor is 1-2 cm in diameter.
Since this tumor causes the patient difficulties in ma...

flashcards.front: What investigations are done for Female predilection?
flashcards.back:
FNAC - to confirm diagnosis and rule out malignancy
CT scan - done when the tumor is arising from deep lobe.
It helps to define the extraglandular spread & the extent
of parapharyngeal disease.
FNAC of Lymph nodes which are palpable in neck in case of
malignancy of parotid gland.
X ray of bones (mandible and mastoid process) to see for bony
resorption.
MRI is a better investigation but expensive
Slow growing parotid tumors should not be subjected to biopsy for
2 reasons - injury to facial nerve
...

flashcards.front: What is the treatment for Female predilection?
flashcards.back:
A> Conservative superficial parotidectomy.
It is the standerd treatment for benign pleomorphic adenoma.
It means removal of the entire lobe containing the tumor which is
superficial to facial nerve. Facial nerve should always be preserved.
Enucleation should never be done as it causes recurrance.
It is difficult to remove a recurrent tumor.
Imp. points in superficial parotidectomy
Adequate exposure by an incision which starts in front of tragus of
pinna, vertically downwards, curves round the ea...

flashcards.front: What are the complications of Female predilection?
flashcards.back:
After a few years pleomorphic adenoma shows features of transformation
into malignancy. [carcinoma expleomorphic adenoma]

flashcards.front: Write a short note on TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA.
flashcards.back:
A disorder of trigeminal nerve that causes episodes of sharp, stabbing pain
in the cheek, Lips, gums or chin on one side of the face.
People with this pain often wince or twitch, which is where trigeminal neuralgia
gets its French name "Tic douloureux" meaning painful twitch.

flashcards.front: What is the etiology of TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA?
flashcards.back:
Usually caused by contact between a normal blood vessel and the trigeminal
nerve at the base of the brain
This contact puts pressure on the nerve and causes it to malfunction
Can occur as a result of ageing, or it can be related to multiple sclerasis
or a similar disarder that damages the myelin sheath protecting
certain nerves.
Less commonly, TN can be caused by a tumor compressing the nerve.
Anomalies of superior cerebellar artery - most recently blamed cause

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Pain is a characteristic symptom.
flashcards.back:
Occurs in bouts or paroxysms, sharp or lancinating in character
Radiates to the territary of one or more divisions of TN.
The pain is nearly always unilateral, and if bilateral, is successive
rather than concomitant
The mandibular & maxillary divisions are more commonly involved
than the opthatmic.
In some instances ,these 2 divisions may be simultaneously affected.
The pain may be exoked by a touch, a draft of air, any movement of the
face as in talking, chewing or yawning or swallowing.
The fi...
