flashcards.front: Write a short note on OSTIOMYELITIS.
flashcards.back:
OML may be defined as an inflammatory condition of bone that begins
as an infection of medullary cavity and haversian systems of the cartex
and extends to involve the periosteum of the affected area.

flashcards.front: What is the etiology of OSTIOMYELITIS?
flashcards.back:
Odontogenic infections originating from pulpal or periodontal tissues,
pericaronitis, infected socket, infected cyst
Trauma - it is the second leading cause especially compound fracture
and surgery
Infections of orofacial region derived from
- -periastitis following gingival ulceration
- lymph nodes infected from furuncles
- lacerations
- peritonsillar abscess

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Infections derived by hematogenous route.

- furuncle on face
- upper respiratory tract infection
- middle ear infection
- mastoiditis

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Sequestrum formation.
flashcards.back:
Spread of infection to the Volksmann's canal in the cartical
plates to reach external surface of bone below periastium

flashcards.back: Separation of periastium from cartical plate

flashcards.front: Write a short note on PERIAPICAL CYST.
flashcards.back:
Radicular cyst or apical periodontal cyst is an inflammatory odontogenic
cyst that develop in the periapical region of non- vital tooth.

flashcards.front: Describe the pathogenesis of PERIAPICAL CYST.
flashcards.back:
ТИЗМТАЯТ
caries, trauma and periodontal disease
pulpal necrosis [necrotic debris is the inflammatory stimulus]
periapical inflammation
periapical granuloma
[composed of granulation tissue, scar & inflammatory cells]
provide rich vascular area to rests of Malassez
Rest of Malassez proliferate and form large cell mass
inner cell mass deprived of nourishment
undergo Liquifaction necrosis
formation of cavity in the center of granuloma

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of PERIAPICAL CYST.
flashcards.back:
Majority of cases are asymptomatic [smaller cysts]
Age group prevalance 20-60 years

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Seldom associated with primary teeth.
flashcards.back:
Maxillary anteriors are the most commonly affected
Large cysts may produce expansion of the bone, displacement of tooth roots,
and crepitus when palpating the expanded alveolar plate.
The discolaration of the non-vital tooth and negative response of the
affected tooth to electric pulp testing are the presenting signs.
In addition, infected radicular cysts are painful, the infected teeth is
sensitive to percussion, and there may be swelling of overlying soft
tissues and lymphadenopathy.
An intrao...

flashcards.front: What are the radiographic features of Seldom associated with primary teeth?
flashcards.back:
The cyst exhibits' a round, pear or ovoid shaped radiolucency, with
a thin, radiopaque line around the periphery of radiolucent area.
In case of very Large cysts' or infected cysts, the peripheral white line
is occassionally absent.
312 IN 1,0011

flashcards.front: What is the treatment for Seldom associated with primary teeth?
flashcards.back:
Non vital teeth that are associated with the cyst' are either extracted
or retained, by endodontic treatment and apicoectomy.
External sinus tracts should always be excised to prevent epithelial
ingrowth.
The commonly employed surgical procedure for radicular cysts' is
enudeation, with primary clasure.
Very small cysts can be removed through tooth socket
Large periodontal cysts that encroach upon the maxillary antrum
or inferior alveolar neurovascular bundle or the nase may be
treated by marsupi...

flashcards.front: Write a short note on DENTIGEROUS CYST.
flashcards.back:
Dentigerous cyst is a developmental odontogenic cyst that is formed by the
expansion of dental follicle and therefore always attached to the neck of
the impacted or unerupted teeth.
It is also called as follicular cyst.
Paget (1863) coined the term dentigerous cyst.

flashcards.front: Describe the pathogenesis of DENTIGEROUS CYST.
flashcards.back:
A> INTRAFOLLICULAR THEORY
It is caused by fluid accumulation between the REE and the examel
surface, resulting is a cyst in which the crown is located within the lumen.
B> EXTRAFOL.L.ICULAR THEORY
It may also arise by proliferation and custic transformation of islands of
odontogenic epithelium in connective tissue wall of dental follicle or even
outside dental follicle and this transformed epithelium then unite with
lining follicular epithelium farming cystic cavity around tooth crown.

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of DENTIGEROUS CYST.
flashcards.back:
Always associated with crown of an impacted, embedded or unerupted teeth
Most lesions are present in 2nd and 3rd decades with slight male predilec
-tion.
Most common site - mandibular and maxillary 3rd molar area and
maxillary cuspid areas.
Dentigerous cyst has the potential to become an aggressive lesion.
Expansion of bone with subsequent facial asymmetry, extreme displacement
of teeth, severe root resorption of adjacent teeth and pain are consequences
of cyst enlargement.
There is usually no p...

flashcards.front: What are the radiographic features of DENTIGEROUS CYST?
flashcards.back:
Well defined radiolucency around the impacted or unerupted teeth.
Usually unilocular but at times it may be multilocular.
Cysts have a well-defined sclerotic margin, unless when they are infected,
then the margins are poorly defined.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Three radiological variations are observed.
flashcards.back: a> central b> lateral c> circumferential

flashcards.front: What is the treatment for Three radiological variations are observed?
flashcards.back:
Treatment via an intraoral or extraoral approach is decided by the
size of the cyst, adequate access and whether it is desirable to save the
involved teoth.
УЯОЗНТ S/A INDI АЯТИГ
A> MARSUPIALISATION
It is indicated in children if the cyst is very large in size and the
involved teeth/tooth are to be maintained

flashcards.front: Write a short note on INDIA.
flashcards.back:
The tooth may erupt into occlusion as the defect heals with normal bone,
or arthodontic forces may be used to bring the tooth into occlusion.
B> ENLICLEATION
Alternatively. the cyst can be enucleated together with the involved
tooth in adults, as the possibility of the tooth erupting is low

flashcards.front: Write a short note on MUCOCELE.
flashcards.back:
Mucocele is the term used for swelling due to accumulation of saliva, as a
result of obstruction or trauma to the salivary gland ducts.
It is not a true cyst as it lacks an epithelial Lining.
There are 2 types of mucoceles -
A> extravasation type
B> retention type
These two types differ in the pathogenesis. The extravasation type is the
common one.

flashcards.front: Describe the pathogenesis of MUCOCELE.
flashcards.back:
A> EXTRAVASATION TYPE
It is caused by trauma leading to laceration of the duct, commonly of the
minor salivary glands resulting in extravasation of the mucin into the
connective tissue.
The trauma may be caused by biting of the Lip, cheek, pinching of Lip by
extraction forceps etc.
The mucin, which extravasates into the connective tissue, leads to inflamm-
- ation, drawing the neutrophils and macrophages to the site, which in will turn
will result in the formation of the granulation tissue, whic...

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of MUCOCELE.
flashcards.back:
Mucoceles occur as painless swellings, which may be recurrent.
The common sites of occurrence are -
A> extravasation type - Lower Lip and tongue
B> retention type - palate, cheek, floor of mouth and maxillary sinus
A well-demarcated lesion of the upper lip could be a salivary gland
neoplasm than a mucocele.
The extravasation type occurs in the 2nd decade and also in children.
The retention type occurs in the middle-aged persons.
Based on the Location clinically, there are 2 types of mucoceles
a>...

flashcards.front: What is the treatment for MUCOCELE?
flashcards.back:
212 ИЗФОН
Mucoceles are treated by surgical excision. It is common to see the
recurrence after excision. It can be minimised ,if the associated
salivary gland acini are also removed.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on SUBLINGUAL SPACE INFECTION.
flashcards.back:
The sublingual space is a fascial space between the oral mucasa of floor
of the mouth and the myelohyoid muscle.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on BOUNDARIES.
flashcards.back: Superiorly - mucasa of the floor of the mouth

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Inferiarly - myelohyoid muscle.
flashcards.back:
Laterally - medial side of mandible, above the myelohyoid muscle
Medially - Hyoglassus, genioglassus and geniohyoid muscles

flashcards.front: Write a short note on CONTENTS.
flashcards.back:
Majar contents include - geniohyoid and genioglassus muscles, and the
hyoglassus muscle complex.
It also contains - i> deep part of the submandibular salivary gland
and its ducts anteriorly
ii> sublingual salivary gland
iii> Lingual nerve and
iv> hypoglassal nerve

flashcards.front: Write a short note on INVOLVEMENT.
flashcards.back:
The teeth which frequently give rise to involvement of sublingual space
are the mandibular incisors, canines, premolars and sometimes first
molars.
The apices of these teeth are superior to the myelohyoid muscle.
The infection perforates Lingual cartical plate below the Levelo of the mucasa
of the floor of the mouth and passes into the sublingual space.

flashcards.front: Describe the clinical features of INVOLVEMENT.
flashcards.back:
A> EXTRAORAL
- There is Little or no swelling. The lymph nodes may be enlarged and
tender. Pain and discomfart on deglutition. Speech may be affected.
B> INTRAORAL
- Firm, painful swelling seen in the floor of the mouth on the affected
side. The floor of the mouth is raised. The tongue may be pushed
superiorly. It results in airway obstruction.
The ability to protrude the tongue beyond the vermillion border of upper
Lip is affected.

flashcards.front: Describe the management of INVOLVEMENT.
flashcards.back:
A> INTRAORALLY
- An incision is made close to the lingual cartical plate, lateral to the
sublingual plica, as the important structure at this site is the
sublingual nerve which is deeply placed and less likely to be damaged
by this approach.
- The other important structures lie medial to the plica and include
the Wharton's duct, sublingual artery and veins and the lingual
nerve.
- The sinus forceps is then inserted and opened to evacuate the puse
B> EXTRAORALLY
- When both the submental and subl...

flashcards.back:
LA acts on voltage sensitive Na+ channels and block nerve conduction by
preventing /decreasing the entry of Na+ ions.
The Na+ channel has an activation gate near the extracellular mouth and
an inactivation gate at the intracellular mouth. In the resting state, the
activation gate is clased.
The LA receptor is located within the channel in its in atraceltular half.
The LA traverses the membrane in unionised form
reionises in the axoplasm and approaches the LA receptor
The receptor has higher affi...

nerve synapses is assumed to be involved in nerve conduction.
2. CALCIUM AISPLACEMENT THEORY - Depolarisation is prevented by
displacement of calcium from certain membrane sites that control the
permeability to sodium.
3. SURFACE CHARGE [REPULSION] THEORY - Local anaesthetic agents
binds to the nerve membrane and change the electrical potential at
the membrane surface.

flashcards.back:
Dentoalveolar injuries are defined as those in which avulsion, subluxation
or fracture of the teeth occurs in association with a fracture of the
alveolus. This type of injury may occur alone or in conjunction with
some other type of mandibular fracture.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on BELL'S PALSY.
flashcards.back:
Bell's palsy is defined as acute onset of non-suppurative inflammation
of facial nerve above the stylomastoid foramen, producing an unilateral
Lower Motor Neuron facial palsy.

flashcards.front: Describe BELL'S PALSY.
flashcards.back:
It accounts for 60-70% of all cases of unilateral peripheral facial palsy.
Affects with equal frequency on the right and left sides of the face.
Either sex is affected equally.
May occur at any age, the median age is 40 years.
Increased incidence in the elderly diabetics hypertensives, pregnant
women than in the common people.

flashcards.front: What is the etiology of BELL'S PALSY?
flashcards.back:
Cause is unclear, precipitating factors may be -
<a> viral infection
<b> vascular ischaemia
<c> autoimmune inflammatory disarders
<d> Heredity
<e> reactivation of HSV in geniculate ganglion
[Ramsey Hunt syndrome]

flashcards.front: Write a short note on CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS.
flashcards.back:
Onset of Bells palsy is acute
ТИЗМЭРАИАМ
1/2 of the cases attain maximum paralysis in 48 hours
All cases are clinically prominent by 5 days.
Pain behind the ear may precede paralysis by 1-2 days.
Impairment of taste is present to some degree in all cases.
- but rarely beyond 2nd week of paralysis.
Hyperacusis or distartion of sound in ipsilateral ear
- paralysis of stapedius muscle
Patient feels stiffness of face pulled to one side.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Grade I - Normal.
flashcards.back:
Grade II - mild dysfunction, slight weekness on clase inspection,
normal symmetry at rest
Grade III - moderate dysfunction, eye can be completely closed with
effort
Grade IV - moderately severe, normal tone at rest, obvious weakness
or asymmetry with movement, incomplete closureof eye
Grade V - severe dysfunction, only barely perceptible motion,
asymmetry at rest'

UMN - frontalis is spared allowing normal fou furrowing
of eyebrow and eye blinking
LMN - all muscles of facial expression are affected

flashcards.front: Describe the management of Grade I - Normal.
flashcards.back:
A> SYMPTOMATIC
- Lubricating eye drops
- Protectionof the eyes during sleep - eye patches
- Massage of the weakened muscles
- Physiotherapy
B> MEDICOLNAL
- Prednisolone 60-80 mg/be day ^ in divided doses initial 4-5 days.
decreases possibility of permanent paralysis СИА
decreases severe pain

- Vitamins B1, B6, B12 may be administered
- If the patient is seen after 3-4 weeks, then steroid therapy is of
no use

flashcards.front: Write a short note on FRENECTOMY.
flashcards.back:
A frenum is a thin band of fibrous tissue and a few muscle fibres
covered by mucus membrane connecting the Lips, cheek and tonque
to the jawbone.
Maxillary midline frenum is most commonly seen. Lingual frenum
is also seen. Maxillary and mandibular frena are also found in
premolar-molar area.
Frenectomy is the surgical procedure involving the complete surgical excision
of the frenum including its attachments.
A
Frenotomy is the surgical procedure involving incision and relocation
of the frenum.
I...

flashcards.front: What are the indications for FRENECTOMY?
flashcards.back: Abnormal frenal attachment which causes midline diastema

flashcards.front: Write a short note on Frenum causing gingival recession.
flashcards.back:
Prior to denture fabrication where the frenum obstructs the denture
peripheries. Lilcerations at the frenal attachments due to impingement of the denture

flashcards.back:
- no need of sutures
- reduced surgical time
- less postsurgical pain & discomfort
- less intraoperative bleeding

flashcards.front: Describe the procedure for Frenum causing gingival recession.
flashcards.back:
Whenever there is an inadequate vestibular depth present, to increase the
retention and stability of the denture, deepening of the vestibule is considered.
Thus vestibuloplasty is a procedure to increase the depth by uncovering the
existing basal bone of the jaws surgically and by repositioning the overlying
mucasa, muscle attachments to a Lower position in the mandible/superior
position in the maxilla.
Vestibuloplasty can be done in the maxilla or in the mandible or both jaws.
Maxillary vestibu...

flashcards.front: Write a short note on CLOSED SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBILOPLASTY.
flashcards.back:
First described by Macintosh and Obwegeser
Indications - unstable denture because of shallow vestibular depth
with good underlying bone height and contours.
A
vertical midline incision is made in labial vestibule and supra-
periosteal tunnel is made from premolar (R) to premolar (L) area.
In maxilla further incisions may be given in 1st molar region
for further advancement.
The intervening submucasal tissue is then excised or repositioned
superiorly and maintained by placement of preformed dentu...

flashcards.front: Write a short note on OPEN SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBULOPLASTY.
flashcards.back:
Wallenius advocated an open method to overcome the relapse of clased
vestibuloplasty.
A harizontal incision is made in the mucogingival junction.
A thin mucasal flap is elevated by submucasal dissection followed
by supraperiosteal dissection to the desired extent, excising muscle
and subcutaneous tissues.
Stay sutures are used to fix the flap to the periosteum deep in vestibule.

flashcards.front: Describe the technique for OPEN SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBULOPLASTY.
flashcards.back:
Mucasal flap from inner aspect of lower lip is used to increase the
vestibular depth in anterior mandibular labial vestibule.
The mentalis muscle is detached from the periosteum to required
depth and the vestibule is deepened via supraperiesteal dissection.
The flap is then redirected & placed directly against the periasteum
and sutured at the new depth of the vestibule.
Raw area is left on the Lip side to be healed by secondary intention.
Periosteum of bone is left intact.
Drawback - scarring o...

flashcards.front: Write a short note on MODIFICATION.
flashcards.back:
Similar to Kazanjians technique in which a flap of Labial mucasa is raised,
[but the vestibule is deepened by subperiasteal stripping instead of supraperias-
leal dissection]
In this procedure, flap is reflected from the inner aspect of the Lip till the
alveolar crest and periosteum is reflected from crest of the ridge till the
desired depth of the vestibule.
The is periasteum is now sutured to the Lip mucosal margin and then Lip flap
is sutured ^ at the required vestibular depth. biowNalayM ber...

flashcards.front: Describe the technique for X pridut.
flashcards.back:
It is the reverse of Kazanjian's technique.
Here flap is reflected from alveolar crest till vermilion border of the Lip.
Supraperiasteal dissection is done till desired vestibular depth & edge of
the mobilised flap is pushed into vestibular depth.
This flap is held in position with sutures passed through the chin area
extraorally and tied around the rubber catheter.
Here alveolar bone is covered by periosteum which heals quickly by
granulation.

flashcards.back:

Thereare 2 types of autoclaves available

flashcards.back:
It consists of a horizontal or vertical cyclinder of gunmetal or stainless
steel in a supporting iron case.
The lid bears a discharge tap for air and steam, a pressure gauge and a
safety valve.

easily. When steam comes in contact with a cooler surface it condenses

flashcards.front: Write a short note on HYPERBARIC OXYGEN THERAPY.
flashcards.back:
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy for radiation damaged tissue was introduced
in 1973 by Greenwood, Gilchrist and Mainous.
Since there is a decrease in vascularity and cellularity of both hard and
soft tissues following irradiation of the bone, the hypoxic compromised
tissues when challenged by a traumatic episode fail to heal.
Delayed fibroblastic activity in neocollagen formation results in chronic
nonhealing wound. These wounds commonly get injected secondarily.
The goal of treating OML and asteoradi...

Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is administered in a chamber called the
hyperbaric chamber which may be monoplace or multiplace.
Each session ranges from go to 120 min. where patient breathes 100%
oxygen at 2 to 2.4 ATA.
Each session is termed as dive. A total of 20-30 dives spanning 5 days
a week is planned preoperatively.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on TRACHEOSTOMY.
flashcards.back:
Tracheastomy is defined as making an opening in anterior wall of trachea
and converting it into a stoma on skin surface.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on FUNCTIONS.
flashcards.back:
Respiration through alternate pathway in case of obstruction above the stoma
Alveolar ventilation is improved due to reduction in dead space by 30-50%
[normal dead space 150 ml] and reducing the resistance to airflow.
Protection of airway by way of cuffed tracheastomy tube i.e protection
from aspiration of pharyngeal secretions in comatase patients and blood
due to injuries in pharynx, larynx, maxillofacial region.

flashcards.front: What is the treatment for Nerve excitability tests?
flashcards.back:
Open injuries of the facial nerve should be repaired surgically as soon as
possible.
Immediate primary repair at the time of intentional or unintentional
transection [neurotmesis] gives the best result.
Delayed primary repair or early secondary repair is acceptable, when delay T209
is necessary for certain reasons Like contaminated wound ,compromised medical
status, extensive tissue destruction etc.
Because of progressive distal nerve degeneration and muscle atrophy 12
months is often considered...

- Sural nerve is an alternative

flashcards.front: Write a short note on SUPPORTIVE CARE.
flashcards.back:
A tarsarrhaphy or eye patch and methoylcellulose drops are provided
when eyelid closure is inadequate to protect the cornea from injury.
Application of heat to the face, massage to facial muscles and facial
exercises are helpful to maintain muscle tone and prevent muscle
atrophy.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on ODONTOMA.
flashcards.back:
The term odontoma refers to any tumor of odontogenic origin in true
sense. This is the growth in which both epithelial and ectomesenchymal
cells exhibit complete or partial differentiation of tooth formation.
This is considered more as a developmental anomaly, and also as a composite
lesion, as it contains more than one tissue.
Radiographically it is of 2 types-
A> compound Odontoma
B> Complex odontoma

flashcards.front: Write a short note on RIDGE AUGMENTATION.

ridge. In this procedure, augmentation of the bone is achieved by building
up the atrophied jawbone using various materials.

flashcards.front: Write a short note on LOCAL MEASURES TO CONTROL BLEEDING.
flashcards.back:
Hemostasis is the physiological process that stops bleeding at the site of an
injury while maintaining normal blood flow elsewhere in the circulation.
Hemastatic agents are broadly classified as
a> Local
b> Systemic

flashcards.front: Write a short note on LOCAL HEMOSTATIC MEASURES.
flashcards.back:
In a patient with normal coagulative mechanism, the control of hemorrhage
is dependent on vessel contraction, retraction and clot formation.
Direct control of bleeding at the site of injury is the best method to achieve
hemastasis. Surgical bleeding most of the times is caused by ineffective-
local hemastasis.

- Application of pressure counteracts the hydrastatic pressure within the
bleeding vessel, until a dot can farm and occlude the bleeding orifice.
- Pressure should be applied directly over the bleeding site firmly with a
gauze pack for at least 5 min.

see whether bleeding has stopped

maxillofacial trauma can be controlled by nasal packing.

key_points: **Local anesthetic (LA) solution** contains: **1
Anesthetic agent**: **Amide type** (metabolized in liver): **Lignocaine (Lidocaine) 2%** - most common, onset 2-3 min, duration 60-90 min
5%** - long-acting (4-8 hrs), slower onset
**Articaine 4%** - higher concentration, better tissue penetration
**Ester type** (metabolized in blood): **Procaine**, **Benzocaine** (topical) - rarely used now due to allergy risk
Pterygomandibular raphe: fold of tissue between pterygoid muscles and mandible
Coronoid notch: depression anterior to anterior border of ramus
Occlusal plane: of mandibular molars. **Injection site**: **1 cm above occlusal plane** of mandibular molars, **3/4 distance** from anterior border of ramus. **Technique**:
Patient position: : Mouth wide open (relaxes muscles, widens pterygomandibular space)
Barrel of syringe: : Place over **contralateral premolars** (crosses midline), parallel to occlusal plane
Needle insertion: : **25 mm long needle**, insert at pterygomandibular raphe, **1 cm above occlusal plane**, advance **20-25 mm** until bone contacted
Aspirate: : Check for blood (avoid intravascular injection)
Deposit solution: : 1.8 mL slowly over 60 seconds (rapid injection causes pain)

Partially withdraw: needle (5-10 mm), deposit **0.2-0.4 mL** for **lingual nerve** (anesthetizes tongue, lingual gingiva). **Onset**: 3-5 minutes. **Duration**: 90 minutes with adrenaline. **Areas anesthetized**: Mandibular teeth to midline, buccal mucosa of molars, anterior 2/3 tongue (lingual nerve), lower lip/chin (mental nerve branch). **Complications**: Trismus, hematoma, temporary facial nerve paralysis (if LA enters parotid gland), paresthesia (rare).
**Complications** categorized as: **LOCAL**: **1
Pain on injection**: Rapid injection, cold solution, low pH solution
**Prevention**: Warm cartridge, inject slowly
Hematoma**: Needle trauma to blood vessel
Common in **PSA block** (pterygoid plexus)
Retract cheek: laterally
Needle: : 25 mm short needle
Insert: at mucobuccal fold above 2nd molar, bevel toward bone
Advance: 16 mm at **45° angle** (upward, medial, posterior), **STOP** before 16 mm if bone contacted (risk of pterygoid plexus puncture)
Aspirate: carefully (pterygoid plexus proximity)

Deposit: 1.8 mL slowly. **Areas anesthetized**: Maxillary **2nd and 3rd molars** completely, **1st molar** (distobuccal and palatal roots only - mesiobuccal from MSA). **Onset**: 3-5 minutes. **Complications**: **Hematoma** (most common - pterygoid venous plexus puncture) - presents as **swelling, ecchymosis**. **Management**: Pressure, ice, reassure patient.

ictor**: **Adrenaline (Epinephrine)** - most common. **Concentrations**: 1:80,000, **1:100,000**, **1:200,000** (standard). **Funct
trauma). **Lingual nerve** and **IAN** most common. Usually **temporary** (weeks-months), rarely permanent. **6. Facial nerve para

nutes. **Complications**: **Hematoma** (most common - pterygoid venous plexus puncture) - presents as **swelling, ecchymosis**. **M

key_points: Dental caries**: Grossly carious tooth **not restorable**, extensive destruction below gingival margin
Periodontal disease**: Advanced periodontitis with **grade III mobility**, bone loss >2/3 root length, poor prognosis
Periapical pathology**: Chronic periapical abscess **not amenable to RCT** (curved roots, calcified canals, perforations)
Impacted teeth**: **Symptomatic** impaction (pain, infection, cyst formation), **prophylactic** removal (orthodontic reasons, before orthognathic surgery)
Supernumerary teeth**: Interfering with eruption, causing malocclusion
**Principles of forceps extraction**: **1
Expansion of alveolar bone**: Controlled force expands socket
Luxation**: Loosen tooth by breaking PDL fibers, dilating socket
Delivery**: Remove tooth from socket
Pre-operative**: (a) **Consent** - explain procedure, risks, (b) **Radiograph** - assess root morphology, bone, pathology, (c) **Local anesthesia** - adequate
**INTRA-OPERATIVE**: **1
Fracture of tooth/root**: **Common**
**Prevention**: Proper forceps selection, luxation before forceps, controlled force

**Management**: (a) **Visible root**: Remove with elevator/forceps/surgical extraction, (b) **Small root tip** (<3mm, no infection/pathology, difficult access) - **leave**, inform patient, monitor radiographically
Fracture of alveolar bone**: Thin labial/lingual plate fractures
**Post-extraction hemorrhage** classified by **timing**: **1
Primary (Immediate)**: During/immediately after extraction (within hours)
**Causes**: Trauma to blood vessels, inadequate compression
Reactionary**: Within **24 hours**
**Causes**: Vasodilator effect (anesthesia wears off), clot dislodgement (rinsing, sucking), hypertension
**Dry socket (Alveolar osteitis)** is a **post-extraction complication** characterized by **severe pain** due to **loss/disintegration of blood clot**, exposing **bare bone**
**Incidence**: 1-5% (all extractions), **20-30%** (mandibular 3rd molars - highest risk)
**Etiology (Multifactorial)**: **1
Fibrinolysis**: Excessive **plasmin activity** (dissolves clot)
**Bacteria** release fibrinolytic enzymes
**Impacted tooth**: A tooth that **fails to erupt** into normal functional position within expected time, **completely/partially blocked** by bone, soft tissue, or adjacent tooth
**Most commonly impacted**: **Mandibular 3rd molars** (48%), Maxillary 3rd molars (38%), Maxillary canines (10%)
**CLASSIFICATIONS OF MANDIBULAR 3rd MOLAR**: **1
WINTER'S CLASSIFICATION** (based on **angulation** of long axis to 2nd molar): **a) Mesioangular** (43%) - most common, crown angulated mesially toward 2nd molar
**Easiest** to remove
Informed consent: risks (nerve injury, dry socket, pain, swelling)

Local anesthesia: IAN block + long buccal nerve block. **SURGICAL STEPS**: **1. Incision**: **Ward's incision** (most common) - starts on **buccal aspect** of 2nd molar (mid-tooth), extends **posteriorly along external oblique ridge**, then curves **medially** over retromolar area. Creates **triangular flap**. **2. Flap elevation**: Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap using **periosteal elevator**, expose bone overlying impacted tooth. **Retract** with **Howarth's elevator**. **3. Bone removal** (if needed): **Surgical bur** (straight/angled handpiece) with **copious saline irrigation** (prevent bone necrosis). Remove bone **buccally** and **distally** (create gutter around crown). **Remove minimum necessary** bone. **4. Tooth sectioning**: **Horizontal impaction**: Section crown from roots (**horizontal cut** at CEJ), remove crown first, then roots. **Mesioangular**: Section **mesiodistal** (separate mesial portion), remove piece by piece. **Vertical**: May not need sectioning if adequate space. **5. Tooth delivery**: **Elevators** (Coupland's, Cryer's) - carefully luxate and deliver sections. **Avoid excessive force** (IAN injury, fracture). **6. Socket management**: (a) **Debride** - remove follicular sac, granulation tissue, (b) **Irrigate** copiously with saline, (c) **Smooth sharp bone** edges (rongeur/bone file), (d) **Inspect** for root tips, foreign bodies. **7. Hemostasis**: **Pressure** with gauze, **bone wax** if bone bleeding, usually self-limiting. **8. Flap closure**: **Interrupted 3-0 silk sutures** - **primary tension-free closure**. **9. Post-operative**: (a) **Gauze pack** - bite 30 min, (b) **Ice** externally (4-6 hrs), (c) **Analgesics** - Ibuprofen + Paracetamol, (d) **Antibiotics** (controversial - use for difficult surgery/infection) - Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg BD x 5 days, (e) **Chlorhexidine rinses** 0.2% (after 24 hrs). **SUTURE REMOVAL**: **7 days**. **COMPLICATIONS**: **IAN injury** (0.5-5% permanent paresthesia), **Lingual nerve injury** (rare), **Dry socket** (20-30%), **Pain, swelling, trismus**, **Infection**, **Fracture of mandible**, **Hemorrhage**, **Damage to 2nd molar**.
**Pericoronitis** is **acute/chronic inflammation** of **soft tissue (operculum)** overlying **partially erupted tooth**, most commonly **mandibular 3rd molar**
Trauma**: **Opposing maxillary 3rd molar** bites on inflamed operculum → ulceration → secondary infection
Bacterial**: Food/debris accumulation under operculum → **bacterial proliferation** (mainly **anaerobes**)
Insufficient space**: Tooth partially erupted but impacted against 2nd molar or ramus
**CLINICAL FEATURES**: **ACUTE PERICORONITIS**: **1
**Impacted maxillary canine** - 2nd most common impaction (after 3rd molars)
**Location**: **Palatal** (60%), **Labial** (30%), **Mid-alveolar** (10%)
**TREATMENT OPTIONS**: **1
Observation**: If **asymptomatic**, adequate space, no pathology, patient refuses treatment
**Monitor** radiographically (3-5 years) for cyst formation, root resorption of adjacent teeth
Prevents eruption: of permanent teeth
Causes displacement/rotation: ,
Associated pathology: (cyst, resorption)
Interferes with orthodontic treatment: ,

Impedes prosthetic rehabilitation: . **TIMING**: **Early removal** (7-9 years) if interfering with eruption. **Delay** if close to developing roots of adjacent teeth (risk of damage). **Asymptomatic, no complications** - **observe**, extract later if needed. **Procedure**: Usually **surgical extraction** (unerupted) - flap, bone removal, extract, primary closure.
Inform patient: , document
Reassure: most recover (70-90% temporary injuries recover in 3-6 months)
Map sensory deficit: test light touch, pin-prick, two-point discrimination (lower lip, chin), compare sides. **FOLLOW-UP**:

key_points: **Odontogenic infection** spreads from **tooth apex** through **alveolar bone** into surrounding **soft tissues** (fascial spaces)
**PATH OF SPREAD**: **Tooth → Periapical bone → Cortical bone perforation → Soft tissue spaces**
**DETERMINANTS OF SPREAD**: **1
Muscle attachments** (PRIMARY determinant): **a) Above muscle attachments**: Infection points **buccally/labially** → **buccal/vestibular space**
**b) Below muscle attachments**: Infection → **deeper fascial spaces**
**Ludwig's angina** is a **rapidly progressive, life-threatening cellulitis** involving **bilateral submandibular, sublingual, and submental spaces** simultaneously
**BILATERALITY** is key diagnostic feature
**Etiology**: **Odontogenic** (80%) - infection from **mandibular molars** (apices below mylohyoid line)
**Precipitating factors**: Poor oral hygiene, immunosuppression (diabetes, HIV), trauma
**Microbiology**: **Mixed aerobic-anaerobic** flora - *Streptococcus viridans, Staphylococcus, Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus*
**Cavernous sinus thrombosis (CST)** is **septic thrombosis** of **cavernous venous sinus** (intracranial venous channel at skull base)
**Life-threatening** complication of **maxillary dental infections**
**Mortality**: 20-30% (even with treatment)

**PATHWAY OF SPREAD**: **Maxillary teeth** (especially **canine region - "danger triangle of face"**) → **Facial vein/Angular vein** → **Superior/Inferior ophthalmic veins** (valveless) → **Cavernous sinus** → **Septic thrombosis**
**Danger triangle**: Area bounded by **corners of mouth** and **bridge of nose** - venous drainage to cavernous sinus without valves (bidirectional flow)
REMOVE SOURCE**: **Primary treatment** - extraction/RCT of offending tooth, drainage of abscess
**Antibiotics ADJUNCT**, not substitute
EMPIRICAL THERAPY**: Start antibiotics **before culture results** (based on likely organisms)
**Modify** when culture/sensitivity available
APPROPRIATE SELECTION**: Based on **microbiology** (mixed aerobic-anaerobic), **severity**, **patient factors**
Consent: ,
Antibiotics: started (before/with I&D)

Anesthesia: : **Difficult** in infected tissues (low pH inactivates LA). Options: **a) Local infiltration** (around abscess - not into pus), **b) Nerve block** (away from infection), **c) General anesthesia** (severe cases, trismus, children). **PROCEDURE**: **1. Prepare site**: Antiseptic (povidone-iodine). **2. Incision**: **Stab incision** (scalpel #11 or #15 blade) at **point of maximum fluctuance** or most **dependent** area. **Adequate length** (1.5-2 cm minimum - allow drainage). **Intraoral**: Vertical incision (avoid nerves/vessels). **Extraoral**: Along skin tension lines or submandibular/submental approach. **3. Blunt dissection**: Insert **curved hemostat/sinus forceps** into abscess cavity. **Open** widely (break loculations, explore cavity), **Finger dissection** if large cavity (submandibular space). **4. Irrigation**: **Copious saline** irrigation (mechanical debridement). **5. Obtain culture**: Aspirate pus with **syringe** (before contamination) - send for **culture/sensitivity** (anaerobic + aerobic). **6. Drain placement**: (a) **Corrugated rubber drain** (Penrose) or **iodoform gauze** - prevents premature closure, continued drainage, (b) Insert through incision, **secure** with silk suture to prevent displacement, (c) **Leave** 2-3 days (remove when drainage minimal). **7. Dressing**: **Intraoral** - none or loose gauze. **Extraoral** - sterile gauze, pressure dressing (mild). **POST-OPERATIVE CARE**: **1. Antibiotics**: Continue oral antibiotics 5-7 days. **2. Analgesics**: Ibuprofen + Paracetamol. **3. Warm saline rinses** (intraoral) or **hot fomentation** (extraoral) - promotes drainage. **4. Drain removal**: **2-3 days** when drainage minimal/stopped. **5. Follow-up**: Daily initially (assess drainage, change drain if needed). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Inadequate drainage** - recurrence, **Damage to nerves/vessels** (facial nerve, IAN, lingual), **Scarring** (extraoral), **Hemorrhage**.
**Osteomyelitis** is **infection of bone and bone marrow**
**Mandible** more common than maxilla (less vascularity, denser cortical bone)

key_points: Extract teeth: ,
Rongeur/bone file: remove **sharp bone** (interdental septa), smooth labial/lingual plates
Digital compression: compress alveolar bone plates together (reduces ridge width)
Irrigation: ,
Primary closure: if possible (approximate socket margins with sutures). **PROCEDURE (Radical - separate surgery)**:
Incision: : **Crestal incision** along ridge ± **vertical releasing incisions**
Flap elevation: : Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap (buccal and lingual/palatal), expose bone

Bone recontouring: : (a) **Rongeur** - remove irregular bone, sharp projections, (b) **Bone file** - smooth surface, (c) **Surgical bur** (with irrigation) - remove large undercuts, excessive bone, (d) Create **gradual, rounded contour**
Trim excess soft tissue: : Fibrous/redundant tissue excision
Flap repositioning: : Approximate flaps (tension-free)
Sutures: : Interrupted 3-0 silk, primary closure

Immediate denture: or **surgical stent** (protects, applies pressure). **Denture fabrication**: **6-8 weeks** post-surgery (healing, resorption stabilization). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Excessive bone removal** (reduces ridge height - poor retention), **Perforation** into maxillary sinus/nasal cavity, **Nerve injury** (mental, IAN), **Hemorrhage**, **Delayed healing**.
**Frenectomy** is **surgical removal** of **frenum** (fold of mucous membrane attaching lip/tongue to underlying structures)
LABIAL FRENECTOMY**: **Maxillary labial** (most common) - between **central incisors** (midline)

**Indications**: (a) **Midline diastema** - frenum attachment on incisive papilla prevents closure, (b) **Denture instability** - thick, prominent frenum dislodges denture during lip movement, (c) **Periodontal problem** - frenum pull causes recession

**Procedure**: (a) **Anesthesia** - infiltration, (b) **Hemostat** placed along frenum (crush tissue), (c) **Incision** along both sides of hemostat (diamond/rhomboid shape), (d) **Excise** frenum (including fibrous tissue extending to periosteum), (e) **Blunt dissection** to bone if needed, (f) **Suture** - interrupted (approximate margins)
LINGUAL FRENECTOMY** (Frenuloplasty): **Indication**: **Ankyloglossia (tongue-tie)** - restricted tongue movement
Inadequate denture retention: insufficient sulcus depth for peripheral seal
High muscle attachments: muscles attached close to ridge crest (reduce denture stability)

Before implant placement: create adequate soft tissue coverage. **PRINCIPLES**: **Reposition muscles** (mentalis, buccinator, mylohyoid) **away from ridge crest** (apically/laterally). **Cover denuded bone** with **oral mucosa** or allow **epithelialization**. **TECHNIQUES**: **1. Submucosal vestibuloplasty**: **Incision** at depth of vestibule, **dissect** muscle attachments from bone, **reposition** apically, **secure** with sutures. **Surgical stent** maintains depth during healing. **2. Secondary epithelialization**: **Remove** mucosa from ridge area, expose bone, allow **spontaneous epithelialization** (6-8 weeks). **Stent** essential. **3. Skin/mucosal grafts**: **Split-thickness skin graft** (from thigh) or **palatal graft** to cover denuded area. Better stability. **4. Clark's vestibuloplasty** (mandible): Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap reflected to depth, muscles repositioned, **stent** holds position. **POST-OPERATIVE**: **Surgical stent** worn **continuous 7-14 days** (holds soft tissue position, prevents relapse). **Analgesics**, soft diet. **Denture fabrication** after **6-8 weeks** (tissue maturation). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Loss of depth** (relapse - most common), **Nerve injury** (mental nerve), **Hemorrhage**, **Infection**, **Graft failure**. **ALTERNATIVE**: **Ridge augmentation** (bone grafting) - preferred in many cases (restores bone height rather than just sulcus depth).
**Maxillary tuberosity reduction** is **surgical removal** of **excess bone/soft tissue** from **maxillary tuberosity** (posterior maxilla - distal to last molar)
Enlarged tuberosity**: Excessive vertical height
Insufficient interarch space** - between maxillary tuberosity and mandibular ridge (prevents proper denture fabrication)
**Minimum required**: **12-15 mm** for complete dentures
Fibrous hyperplastic tissue** - redundant soft tissue

**Radicular (Periapical/Apical) cyst** is an **inflammatory odontogenic cyst** arising from **chronic periapical granuloma** due to **pulp necrosis**
**Most common odontogenic cyst** (60%)

key_points: Rotation: (0-25mm) - condyle rotates in lower compartment (hinge)

Translation: (>25mm) - condyle + disc glide forward along articular eminence (upper compartment). **Closing**: Reverse. **COMMON TMJ DISORDERS (TMD)**: **1. INTERNAL DERANGEMENT**: **Disc displacement**. **a) Disc displacement with reduction**: Disc **anteriorly displaced** (mouth closed), **clicks/pops** during opening (disc reduces back onto condyle), **full mouth opening**. **Reciprocal click** (opening + closing). **b) Disc displacement without reduction** (closed lock): Disc **permanently displaced anteriorly**, **cannot reduce**, **limited mouth opening** (<30mm), **deviation** to affected side, **no click** (disc stuck). **2. OSTEOARTHRITIS (Degenerative joint disease)**: **Crepitus** (grating sound), pain, limited movement, **radiographic changes** (flattening, osteophytes, sclerosis). **3. MYOFASCIAL PAIN DYSFUNCTION** (MPD): **Muscle pain** (masticatory muscles - masseter, temporalis, pterygoids), **tenderness** on palpation, **limited opening** (muscle spasm), **no joint sounds**. **Etiology**: Stress, bruxism, parafunctional habits. **4. ANKYLOSIS**: **Fusion** of joint - fibrous or bony. **No movement**, severely limited opening. **Causes**: Trauma, infection. **5. TMJ ARTHRITIS**: **Rheumatoid arthritis**, **Septic arthritis**. **DIAGNOSIS**: **Clinical** (history, examination - opening, palpation, sounds), **Radiographs** (OPG, lateral TMJ), **MRI** (gold standard - soft tissue/disc visualization), **CT** (bone changes).
**TMD management** - **conservative first**, surgery **last resort**
**90% improve** with non-surgical therapy
**CONSERVATIVE (First-line)**: **1
Patient education**: Explain condition (usually self-limiting), avoid aggravating factors

Behavior modification**: (a) **Soft diet** - avoid hard, chewy foods, (b) **Avoid wide opening** - no yawning, large bites, (c) **Avoid parafunctional habits** - **no gum chewing**, nail biting, pencil biting, (d) **Stress reduction** - counseling, relaxation techniques
Soft diet: 1 week
Avoid wide opening: 2 weeks
NSAIDs: pain/inflammation

Jaw exercises: prevent stiffness. **PREVENTION OF RECURRENCE**: **1. Patient education** - avoid wide opening, yawning with care. **2. Occlusal splint** (night guard). **3. Sclerosing injections** - into capsule (controversial). **4. Surgical** (recurrent cases): **a) Eminectomy** - flatten/remove articular eminence (condyle cannot lock anteriorly), **b) Capsulorrhaphy** - tighten capsule. **COMPLICATIONS**: **Fracture** (forced reduction), **Nerve injury**, **Recurrence** (most common).

key_points: **ANATOMICAL CLASSIFICATION**: **1
Symphysis**: Midline (between mental foramina)
Parasymphysis**: Between midline and canine
Body**: Between canine and angle
Angle**: Junction of body and ramus (most common - 25-30%)
**Le Fort classification** - **midface fractures** involving **maxilla**
Based on **predictable lines of weakness**
**KEY FEATURE**: **ALL Le Fort fractures** involve **pterygoid plates** (posterior maxilla) - **pathognomonic**
**LE FORT I** (Horizontal/Floating palate): **Level**: **Low** (above roots of teeth)
**Fracture line**: Transverse across **maxilla above apices** of teeth, through **lateral nasal walls**, across **maxillary sinuses**, through **pterygoid plates** (posterior)
Rigid fixation: immediate stability
Early function: no/minimal IMF needed
Better alignment: direct visualization

Shorter treatment: faster return to normal. **Technique**: (a) **Approach** - intraoral or extraoral incision, (b) **Expose fracture** - elevate soft tissue, (c) **Reduce** - align fragments anatomically, (d) **Fix** - **Titanium miniplates + screws** across fracture line. **Champy's principles**: **Load-bearing** (body, angle) - 2 plates. **Tension zone** (superior border), **Compression zone** (inferior border). (e) **Closure** - layer by layer. **Complications**: **Infection** (plate exposure), **Malunion**, **Nerve injury**, **Hardware failure**, **Plate removal** (later if symptomatic). **3. EXTERNAL FIXATION**: **Pins** through skin into bone, connected externally. **Rare** in facial fractures (cosmetic concerns). Used in **comminuted**, **infected**, **gunshot wounds**. **CHOICE**: **Favorable/Simple** → **Closed + IMF**. **Unfavorable/Complex** → **ORIF** (± short IMF).
Zygomaticofrontal: (lateral orbital rim)
Zygomaticomaxillary: (inferior orbital rim)

Zygomaticotemporal: (zygomatic arch). **Etiology**: **Direct trauma** to cheek - assault (fist/blunt object), RTA, sports. **Zygoma prominence** makes it vulnerable. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Flattening of cheek** - **loss of malar prominence** (depressed zygoma). **2. Periorbital edema/ecchymosis** - swelling, black eye. **3. Subconjunctival hemorrhage** - blood in sclera (no posterior limit - differentiates from localized bruise). **4. Step deformity** - palpable at **infraorbital rim** and **lateral orbital rim** (fracture displacement). **5. Infraorbital nerve anesthesia** - **numbness** of cheek, upper lip, nose, upper teeth (nerve injury in infraorbital canal). **Pathognomonic sign**. **6. Enophthalmos** - **sunken eye** (if orbital floor fractured - orbital volume increased). **Late sign** (after edema subsides). **7. Diplopia** - **double vision** (extraocular muscle entrapment in orbital floor fracture, or edema). **8. Trismus** - limited mouth opening (if arch impinges on coronoid process). **9. Epistaxis** - nasal bleeding (maxillary sinus involvement). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic features. **2. Radiographs**: **Waters view** (occipitomental) - best for zygoma. **Shows**: (a) **Opacification** of maxillary sinus (blood), (b) **Fracture lines** at sutures, (c) **Depressed arch**. **3. CT scan** - **gold standard** (3D reconstruction - exact displacement, orbital floor involvement). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Persistent enophthalmos**, **Persistent diplopia** (muscle entrapment), **Facial asymmetry** (malunion), **Infraorbital anesthesia** (may be permanent). **MANAGEMENT**: **OBSERVATION** (No treatment): **Indications**: (a) **Minimal/no displacement**, (b) **No functional impairment** (vision, occlusion), (c) **No esthetic concern**. **Monitor** - most heal uneventfully. **SURGICAL REDUCTION**: **Indications**: (a) **Significant displacement** (cosmetic deformity), (b) **Enophthalmos** (>2mm), (c) **Persistent diplopia** (>2 weeks), (d) **Trismus**, (e) **Large orbital floor defect**. **Timing**: **Early** (within 7-14 days - before healing/fibrosis). **Techniques**: (a) **Closed reduction**: **Gillies approach** - temporal incision, **elevator** under zygomatic arch, **lift** zygoma (pop back into place). **No fixation**. Simple fractures only. (b) **Open reduction**: **Incisions** (intraoral + lower eyelid OR coronal), **expose fracture** sites, **reduce** anatomically, **fix** with **miniplates** at 2-3 points (zygomaticomaxillary buttress, infraorbital rim, zygomaticofrontal). **Orbital floor reconstruction** if large defect (implant - titanium mesh/porous polyethylene). **Post-op**: **Antibiotics** (sinus communication), **Decongestants** (nasal), **Avoid nose blowing**, **Eye care** (if eyelid swelling).
**Condylar fractures** - **2nd most common** mandibular fracture (25%)
**Bilateral** common (falls on chin - force transmitted to both condyles)
Intracapsular** - fracture within TMJ capsule (condylar head)
Condylar neck** - below head, above sigmoid notch
Subcondylar** - below sigmoid notch (most common)

Long tortuous: duct (12 cm). **Parotid** (5-20%), **Sublingual** (rare). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Intermittent painful swelling** - **pathognomonic**. **Swelling** of gland **during meals** (saliva production), **subsides** after meal (1-2 hrs). **"Mealtime syndrome"**. **2. Palpable stone** - **bimanual palpation** (submandibular duct - floor of mouth). **Hard, immobile** mass along duct. **3. Reduced salivary flow** - thick/absent saliva from duct orifice (milk duct). **4. Tenderness** - over gland/duct. **5. Secondary infection** - if complete obstruction → **acute sialadenitis** (fever, swelling, pus from duct). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - mealtime swelling + palpable stone. **2. Radiographs**: **Occlusal radiograph** (80% stones radiopaque - calcium). **Sialography** (contrast in duct - shows stone as **filling defect**) - **AVOID if acute infection** (spread infection). **3. Ultrasound** - detects radiolucent stones, ductal dilation. **4. Sialendoscopy** - endoscopic visualization + removal. **MANAGEMENT**: **MEDICAL** (Small stones <3mm, distal duct): **1. Conservative**: (a) **Hydration** - increase saliva flow, (b) **Sialogogues** - lemon juice, sour candies (stimulate saliva), (c) **Warm compresses** + **Massage** - milk stone distally, (d) **NSAIDs** - pain/inflammation, (e) **Antibiotics** (if infection) - **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate** (covers *S. aureus, Streptococcus*). **2. Stone milking** - **bimanual massage** (push stone anteriorly toward orifice, express). **SURGICAL**: **1. Intraoral removal** (Distal duct stones - palpable in floor of mouth): (a) **Anesthesia** (local), (b) **Dilate duct** orifice, (c) **Incision** over stone (longitudinal - along duct), (d) **Remove stone**, (e) **Marsupialize** duct (suture duct edges to mucosa - create wide opening, prevent stricture). **Do NOT suture duct** closed (stenosis risk). **2. Sialendoscopy** - endoscopic **basket retrieval** or **lithotripsy** (break stone). **Minimally invasive**. **3. Gland excision** - **Submandibular gland removal** if: (a) **Recurrent** sialolithiasis, (b) **Intraglandular** stone (within gland), (c) **Chronic sialadenitis** (gland damaged). **Risks**: **Lingual nerve injury**, **Hypoglossal nerve injury**, **Marginal mandibular nerve** (facial nerve branch - lip droop). **PREVENTION**: Hydration, good oral hygiene.
**Ranula** - **mucous extravasation pseudocyst** arising from **sublingual gland** (floor of mouth)
**Bluish, translucent swelling** ("frog belly" appearance - Latin *rana* = frog)
SIMPLE (Oral) RANULA**: **Confined** to **floor of mouth** (above mylohyoid muscle)

**Clinical features**: (a) **Painless, soft, fluctuant** swelling in **floor of mouth** (one side of frenulum), (b) **Bluish/translucent** color (saliva visible through thin mucosa - pathognomonic), (c) **Displaces tongue** upward/laterally (if large), (d) **No pain** (unless infected)
PLUNGING (Cervical) RANULA**: **Extends** through mylohyoid muscle into **neck** (submandibular/submental space)

key_points: **PRE-OPERATIVE ASSESSMENT**: **1
History**: **Type of bleeding disorder**, **Severity**, **Previous bleeding episodes** (dental/surgical), **Medications** (anticoagulants, antiplatelets)
Laboratory tests**: **Hemophilia**: **Factor VIII** (Hemophilia A) or **Factor IX** (Hemophilia B) levels
**Anticoagulants**: **INR** (warfarin), **aPTT** (heparin)
**General**: **Platelet count**, **PT**, **Bleeding time**
**Wound healing** - complex process restoring tissue integrity
**TYPES**: **Primary intention** - wound edges approximated (sutured) - minimal scarring
**Secondary intention** - wound left open (granulation fills defect) - more scarring
**Tertiary intention** (delayed primary) - initially open, closed later
**PHASES (Overlapping)**: **1
**PRINCIPLES OF SUTURING**: **1
Aseptic technique** - sterile instruments, gloves
Tissue handling**: **Gentle** - avoid crushing (tissue forceps with teeth, not hemostats)
Hemostasis**: **Control bleeding** before closure (pressure, cautery)
Dead space elimination**: **Close in layers** (prevent hematoma/seroma)
**NERVE INJURY CLASSIFICATION (Seddon)**: **1
NEUROPRAXIA**: **Mildest**
**Conduction block** (axon intact, myelin damaged)
**No Wallerian degeneration**
**Recovery**: **Complete**, **spontaneous** (days-weeks)
**Tracheostomy** - surgical creation of opening into **trachea** (through anterior neck) for airway access
**Temporary** (tube removable) or **Permanent**

Upper airway obstruction**: (a) **Maxillofacial trauma** - bilateral mandibular fractures, midface fractures (tongue fall-back, bleeding), (b) **Ludwig's angina** - floor of mouth swelling → airway compromise, (c) **Tumors** - oral/pharyngeal/laryngeal (obstructing airway), (d) **Burns** - inhalational injury, facial burns, (e) **Angioedema** - severe allergic reaction
Prolonged intubation**: **>7-10 days** mechanical ventilation (ICU patients) - **prevents laryngeal damage** from endotracheal tube
Inability to intubate**: **Difficult airway** (limited mouth opening, anatomical anomalies)

key_points:
A suture is a strand or thread of material used to approximate tissues and
also to ligate blood vessels

Thus, Suturing is the act of sewing or bringing tissues or flap edges together
and holding them in apposition until normal healing takes place
Sutures can be conveniently classified into 2 groups-

1> Absorbable Sutures
These are suture materials that are digested or hydrotysed by the enzymes
present in the body
Thus they require no removal from the surgical site

a> Natural - Catgut
b> Synthetic - Polyglycolic acid
2> Non-Absarbable Sutures
These materials can not be metabolised by the body's natural mechanism
therefore they should be removed by the surgeon at the end of healing

a> Natural - Silk, linen
b> Synthetic - Nylon, polypropylene
3> Monofilament
They consist of single strand of suture material
Advantages - monofilament sutures are more smooth and strong
- they do not allow any bacteria to survive

Disadvantages — monofilament sutures can't be handled well like
multifilament sutures
- They have to be handled properly and delicately
without any damage to the strand during surgical
procedures to avoid any breakage past operatively

George Caldwell and Henri Luc described a new method of entering into the
maxillary sinus where 2 separate openings are made, one in the canine fassa
to gain access to the antrum and other in the nasoantral wall for drainage
it is called as Caldwell- Luc procedure

- removal of teeth/fragments of root/impacted teeth in the antrum
- treatment of chronic maxillary sinusitis with polyps and cystic
degeneration of mucosa
- excision of cysts and benign tumours of maxillary sinus
— management of hematoma or haemarrhage in the maxillary sinus
- to treat fractures involving floor of the orbit or anterior maxillary
sinus wall

Incision extends from apex of canine down to near the gingival margin &
posteriorly to the 2nd molar
In edentulous cases, it is done along the alveolar ridge
The incision should be larger than the window to be cut in the bone
The mucoperiostium is then detached and retracted till the infra orbital
ridge taking care to preserve the integrity of infraorbital nerve
A small gouge is used to perforate centre of the canine fassa

The hole is enlarged by means of rongeurs or backbiting forceps until
it is large enough to allow introduction of atleast the index fingers
The finger is used to palpate the lining of the sinus
Blood and pus contained in the sinus are removed by aspirator

Anaphylaxis is aclinical syndrome of severe hypersensitivity reaction
characterised by cardiovascular system collapse respiratory system
depression, skin reaction and smooth muscle contractions

Expasure to certain drugs and fareign bodies
[penicillin, aspirin, lignocaine]

Severe throbbing pain, when the pus remains confined within bone in apical
region

The intensity reduces after 12-24 hours as the pus is discharged into
antral cavity

Unilateral foul discharge through pasterior nares
Cacasmia - a fetid odor with bad taste in the mouth

The antibodies fix to most cells
On exposure to same drug,
antigen-antibody reaction occurs on mast cell surface
Release of mediators [Histamine, Leukotriene, 5-HT]
clinical features of anaphylaxis

Cutaneous manifestations — pruritus
- urticaria
- angioedema
Respiratory manifestations — wheezing
- coughing
- stridar
- dyspnoea
CVS manifestations — Tachycardia
- Shock
- Hypotension
- Cardiac dyerhythmia

If respiratory involvement is seen, position the patient in supine position
with legs elevated, administer O2 and perform CPR if required

Vasoconstrictors are chemical agents added to local anaesthetic solutions
a> to oppose vasodilation caused by the local anaesthetic agents
b> to achieve hemastasis (constrict the blood vessels and control tissue
perfusion)

The net effects caused by addition of vasoconstrictor to LA are-
a> It decreases the rate of absorption of local anaesthetic agent into
cardiovascular system
b> It lowers the plasma level of local anaesthetic agent, ,and, hence,
decreases the risk of systemic toxicity

c> It increases the duration and depth of action, as higher volumes of
local araesthetic agent remain in and around the nerve for longer
time
d> It decreases bleeding at the site of injection
The pH of LA solution with vasoconstrictor is lower/mare acidic than
without vasoconstrictor

3-4)
Because of less acidic property, the plain solutions will have less burning
during injection & have more rapid clinical action

REDUCING AGENTS OR ANTIOXIDANTS FOR VA50CONSTRICTORS
Vasoconstrictors are relatively unstable in solutions, and undergo
oxidation, when exposed to sunlight

The solution appears pink to
brown in color after oxidation

To ensure a reasonable shelf life, it is necessary to include a
reducing agent or antioxidant in the LA soln
The most common antioxidant is sodium metabisuffite

In the
It presence of O2, sodium metabisulfite gets oxidised to sodium bisulfate,
thus prevents oxidation of vasoconstrictors

key_points: There are 3 work principles applicable to the dental elevators

1> Lever principle
2> Wedge principle
3> Wheel and axle principle

most commonly used principle
The elevator is a Lever of first order

In this, the fulcrum is located
between the load and effort

In order to gain mechanical advantage,
the effort arm must be longer than the resistance/ load arm

When elevators are used, the force is transmitted at Long power arm of the
handle, so that the MA is derived at the short weight arm i

e the working
blade, which engages the tooth

The zygomaticomaxillary complex plays a key role in the structure,
function and esthetics of the 100 facial region

It provides normal check contour and separates the orbital contents
from the temporal fassa and maxillary sinus

As the zygomatic bone is clasely associated with the maxilla, frontal
and temporal bones, and they are usually involved when a zygomatic
bone fracture occurs, therefore these type of fractures are called
zygomaticomazillary fractures or zygomatic complex fractures or
tripod fractures

Type II - Fractures of the zygomatic arch
Type III - Rotation around the vertical axis
Type IV - Rotation around longitudinal axis
Type V - Displacement of the complex en bloc
Type VI - Displacement of the arbitoantral partition

Type VII - Displacement of the orbital rim segments
Type VIII - Complex comminuted fractures

Vertical axis - It is an imaginary line running through the frontozygomatic
suture and the first molar teeth

Any displacement of the fracture in this axis becomes stable
after reduction

Harizontal axis - It is a Line running through the infraorbital foramen and
zygomatic arch

When displacement occurs in this axis, it results in separation
of frontozygomatic suture and reduction of these fractures is

Odontogenic sinusitis is the inflammation of mucasa of any of the paranasal

diluted solution of pentoborbitone, pethidine and hyascine by slow
IV injection

Osteomyelitis may be defined as an lammatory condition of bone that
begins as an infection of medullary cavity and haversian systems of the
cartex and extends to involve the periasteum of the affected area

The etiological agents can be a carious tooth, or the overlying soft tissue
infection
The infectious process

localises in periasteum or beneath the
periasteal covering of cartex and spreads slightly into the interior dal of bone

In case of persistence of the causative agent, it causes bony thickening,
visible on radiograph

Primarilyassects children and young adults, and occasionally in older
individuals

Localised, hard, nontender bony swelling of lateral and inferior aspects of
mandible
Lymphadenopathy, hyperpyrexia and leukocytasis usually not found

A focal area of well calcified bone proliferation may be seen that is smooth
and that often has a laminated or onion skin appearance
The radiographic appearance is typical

There is condensation of cortical
bone, and overgrowth of bony Tissue beneath the periasteum

The increase in mass of bone is due to
- mild Toxic stimulation of fibroblasts by attenuated infection
- compensatory thickening of bone as a mechanical adaptation to
reinforce the area weakened through disease

expansion

Marsupialisation refers To creating a surgical window in the wall of the
cyst, evacuating the contents of the cyst, and maintaining continuity
between the cyst and the oral cavity, maxillary sinus or nasal cavity

The only partion that is removed is the piece removed to produce the
window
The remaining cystic lining is left in situ
It is also called decompression or Partisch I technique or cystotomy

This process decreases intracystic pressure and promotes shrinkage of
the cyst and bone fill

Age - In a young child, with developing tooth germs, or when development
of displaced Teeth has not progressed enucleation would damage the
tooth buds

In the elderly, debilitated patient, marsupialisation is less stressful
Proximity to vital structures - when proximity of the cyst to vital
structures could create an aronasal or aroantral fistula, injure
neurovascular bundle or damage vital teeth, then marsupialisation
should be considered

Size of cyst - In very large cyst, where enucleation could result in a
pathological fracture, marsupialisation can be accomplished, through
a more limited bony opening
Allows for endasteal bone formation to take place
Histologic examination of the entire cystic Lining is notdone

Salivary fistula is a chronic communication between the salivary
gland or duct and the skin through which saliva is discharged

Internal -opening into mucasa of buccal cavity
External - opening into the skin, ,saliva is discharged on skin
causing eczema, excoriation, inflammation

It is a rare condition caused by accidental trauma, surgery and
infection

It may occur as a result of actinomycosis, syphillis and cancrum
oris

Any surgery on the parotid gland -superficial parotidectomy, drainage
of abscess, surgery for carcinoma cheek

Discharging watery fluid, exaggerated by keeping time in the mouth
collection of saliva under the skin flap with drainage through the wound
Fistulogram confirms the diagnosis
Sialography is performed to establish the exact site or origin of fistula

Treatment consists of aspiration of fluid, compression dressings and
removal of salivary stimulants

Currently the most commonly used treatment is botulinum neurotoxin
type A and systemic hyascine or propantheline bromide
The problem usually resolves with this treatment regimen

Most surgical procedures can be done through a preauricular incision

A> CONDYLECTOMY
Advocated in cases of fibrous ankylasis, where the joint space is
obliterated with deposition of fibrous bands but anatomy of
condylar head is almost unchanged

Preauricular incision is mostly used to expose the condylar region
A horizontal asteotomy cut is made at the level of condylar neck

Vital structures on the medial surface of condylar neck should be
protected using special condylar retractor

The condylar head is sectioned at the level of the neck, and it is
separated from superior fibrous attachments and removed

a> Lass of vertical height of ramus
b> Bilateral condylectomy may create an anterior open bite
c> Unitateral condylectomy causes deviation of mandible towards
the operated side on oral opening
Therefore, when the site of the fused joint is mobilised via condylec-
tomy, then after recontouring by arthroplasty, an alloplastic
material can be used to maintain the jaw space

B> GAP ARTHROPLASTY
bony
Commonly done in cases of complete ankylosis

A
Gap arthroplasty involves creation of an anatomical gap in the ankylased
segment to form an artificial joint space

A gap in the bone is created to separate the ramus from the ankylased
mass in the glenoid fassa

2 horizontal asteotomy cuts are made and the wedge of the bone between
these 2 cuts is removed
Care should be taken while removing the bone from the medial aspect
as it is clase to the maxillary artery and carotid canal

arthroplasty
c> INTERPOSITIONAL ARTHROPLASTY
When gap arthroplasty is done to release ankylasis, there are chances of
contact between the bone ends to form a reankylasis

So an interpasitional material is to be placed in between them to avoid
contact and minimise chances of reankylosis
Materials which can be used are either alloplastic or autogenous materials

Aggressive total excision of the good ankylotic segment in condylar region
coronoidectomy on affected side to avoid temporalis muscle restriction

Gry socket is defined as a past-operative pain in and around the dental
alveolus, which increases in severity at some moment between the first
and third day after a dental extraction, , accompanied by partial or
total disintegration of the intraalveolar clot, causing foul smell

Term dry socket was given by Crawford (1896)
It is also known as - alveolar asteitis
- Fibrinolytic alveolitis
- Fibrinolytic asteitis

use of oral contraceptives
smoking
Inadequate intrasperatory irrigation of the alveolus

A> BIRN'S FIBRINOLYTIC THEORY
According to Birn's, after the extraction of tooth an inflammatory
process begins that could affect the formation and retention of clot

present in the alveolar bone
Release of activators (XIIa, kallikrein)

B> BACTERIAL THEORY
- This theory relates the highcount of anaerobic bacteria in the
extraction site to the pathogenesis of dry socket

- Actinomyces viscasus and Streptococcus mutans have been related
to dry socket, because it has been demonstrated that they retard
the alveolar pastextraction healing in an animal model

Also fibrinolytic activity has been observed which increases with Treponema
denticola The infection never occurs during childhood a period when
this organism has not yet colonised the mouth

Severe, throbbing pain aggravated during mastication and opening of
mouth
Appear by second or third pastoperative day
Pain may radiate to the ear and neck

Sometimes, associated with low grade fever and ipsilateral Symphaden-
opathy

Exposed bone is necrotic and sequestration of fragments is common
Socket may contain decompased food debris

A disorder of trigeminal nerve that causes episodes of sharp, stabbing pain
in the cheek, Lips, gums or chin on one side of the face

People with this pain often wince or twitch, which is where trigeminal neuralgia
gets its French name "Tic douloureux" meaning painful twitch

Usually caused by contact between a normal blood vessel and the trigeminal
nerve at the base of the brain
This contact puts pressure on the nerve and causes it to malfunction
Can occur as a result of ageing, or it can be related to multiple sclerasis
or a similar disarder that damages the myelin sheath protecting
certain nerves
Less commonly, TN can be caused by a tumor compressing the nerve
Anomalies of superior cerebellar artery - most recently blamed cause

Occurs in bouts or paroxysms, sharp or lancinating in character
Radiates to the territary of one or more divisions of TN

The pain is nearly always unilateral, and if bilateral, is successive
rather than concomitant
The mandibular & maxillary divisions are more commonly involved
than the opthatmic
In some instances ,these 2 divisions may be simultaneously affected

The pain may be exoked by a touch, a draft of air, any movement of the
face as in talking, chewing or yawning or swallowing

The first episodes are usually fairly mild and brief, and it may be
few minutes or hours before the next attack

Radicular cyst or apical periodontal cyst is an inflammatory odontogenic
cyst that develop in the periapical region of non- vital tooth

ТИЗМТАЯТ
caries, trauma and periodontal disease
pulpal necrosis [necrotic debris is the inflammatory stimulus]
periapical inflammation
periapical granuloma
[composed of granulation tissue, scar & inflammatory cells]
provide rich vascular area to rests of Malassez
Rest of Malassez proliferate and form large cell mass
inner cell mass deprived of nourishment
undergo Liquifaction necrosis
formation of cavity in the center of granuloma

Majority of cases are asymptomatic [smaller cysts]
Age group prevalance 20-60 years

Maxillary anteriors are the most commonly affected
Large cysts may produce expansion of the bone, displacement of tooth roots,
and crepitus when palpating the expanded alveolar plate

The discolaration of the non-vital tooth and negative response of the
affected tooth to electric pulp testing are the presenting signs

In addition, infected radicular cysts are painful, the infected teeth is
sensitive to percussion, and there may be swelling of overlying soft
tissues and lymphadenopathy

An intraoral sinus tract may be identified with discharging pus or
brownish fluid, when the cyst is infected

The involved teeth will be found to be non vital, discolared, fractured
or with heavy restorations or a failed root canal B treatment

The cyst exhibits' a round, pear or ovoid shaped radiolucency, with
a thin, radiopaque line around the periphery of radiolucent area

In case of very Large cysts' or infected cysts, the peripheral white line
is occassionally absent

Non vital teeth that are associated with the cyst' are either extracted
or retained, by endodontic treatment and apicoectomy

External sinus tracts should always be excised to prevent epithelial
ingrowth

The commonly employed surgical procedure for radicular cysts' is
enudeation, with primary clasure

Very small cysts can be removed through tooth socket
Large periodontal cysts that encroach upon the maxillary antrum
or inferior alveolar neurovascular bundle or the nase may be
treated by marsupialisation
clinical_pearls:
Maxillary anteriors are the most commonly affected
Large cysts may produce expansion of the bone, displacement of tooth

key_points:
Dentigerous cyst is a developmental odontogenic cyst that is formed by the
expansion of dental follicle and therefore always attached to the neck of
the impacted or unerupted teeth
It is also called as follicular cyst
Paget (1863) coined the term dentigerous cyst

A> INTRAFOLLICULAR THEORY
It is caused by fluid accumulation between the REE and the examel
surface, resulting is a cyst in which the crown is located within the lumen

ICULAR THEORY
It may also arise by proliferation and custic transformation of islands of
odontogenic epithelium in connective tissue wall of dental follicle or even
outside dental follicle and this transformed epithelium then unite with
lining follicular epithelium farming cystic cavity around tooth crown

Always associated with crown of an impacted, embedded or unerupted teeth
Most lesions are present in 2nd and 3rd decades with slight male predilec
-tion

Most common site - mandibular and maxillary 3rd molar area and
maxillary cuspid areas
Dentigerous cyst has the potential to become an aggressive lesion

Expansion of bone with subsequent facial asymmetry, extreme displacement
of teeth, severe root resorption of adjacent teeth and pain are consequences
of cyst enlargement

There is usually no pain or discomfort associated with the cyst unless it
becomes secondarily infected
Well defined radiolucency around the impacted or unerupted teeth
Usually unilocular but at times it may be multilocular

Cysts have a well-defined sclerotic margin, unless when they are infected,
then the margins are poorly defined
clinical_pearls:
ecades with slight male predilec
-tion.
Most common site - mandibular and maxillary 3rd molar area and
maxillary cuspid areas.
Dent

key_points: a> central b> lateral c> circumferential

Treatment via an intraoral or extraoral approach is decided by the
size of the cyst, adequate access and whether it is desirable to save the
involved teoth

УЯОЗНТ S/A INDI АЯТИГ
A> MARSUPIALISATION
It is indicated in children if the cyst is very large in size and the
involved teeth/tooth are to be maintained

The tooth may erupt into occlusion as the defect heals with normal bone,
or arthodontic forces may be used to bring the tooth into occlusion

B> ENLICLEATION
Alternatively

the cyst can be enucleated together with the involved
tooth in adults, as the possibility of the tooth erupting is low

Mucocele is the term used for swelling due to accumulation of saliva, as a
result of obstruction or trauma to the salivary gland ducts
It is not a true cyst as it lacks an epithelial Lining

There are 2 types of mucoceles -
A> extravasation type
B> retention type
These two types differ in the pathogenesis

The extravasation type is the
common one

A> EXTRAVASATION TYPE
It is caused by trauma leading to laceration of the duct, commonly of the
minor salivary glands resulting in extravasation of the mucin into the
connective tissue

The trauma may be caused by biting of the Lip, cheek, pinching of Lip by
extraction forceps etc

The mucin, which extravasates into the connective tissue, leads to inflamm-
- ation, drawing the neutrophils and macrophages to the site, which in will turn
will result in the formation of the granulation tissue, which actually
forms the wall of these extravasation cysts

B> RETENTION TYPE
This occurs due to obstruction of the salivary duct, commonly because of
a siabolith or may be due to periductal scar, ,or impinging tumor, resulting
in the accumulation of the saliva in the duct

This leads to dilatation of the duct resulting in a cyst like lesion, These
cysts may show the presence of ductal epithelium histologically
Mucoceles occur as painless swellings, which may be recurrent

The common sites of occurrence are -
A> extravasation type - Lower Lip and tongue
B> retention type - palate, cheek, floor of mouth and maxillary sinus
A well-demarcated lesion of the upper lip could be a salivary gland
neoplasm than a mucocele
The extravasation type occurs in the 2nd decade and also in children
The retention type occurs in the middle-aged persons

The superficial mucocele resembles a vescicle or bulla

The sublingual space is a fascial space between the oral mucasa of floor
of the mouth and the myelohyoid muscle
Superiorly - mucasa of the floor of the mouth

Laterally - medial side of mandible, above the myelohyoid muscle
Medially - Hyoglassus, genioglassus and geniohyoid muscles

Majar contents include - geniohyoid and genioglassus muscles, and the
hyoglassus muscle complex

It also contains - i> deep part of the submandibular salivary gland
and its ducts anteriorly
ii> sublingual salivary gland
iii> Lingual nerve and
iv> hypoglassal nerve

The teeth which frequently give rise to involvement of sublingual space
are the mandibular incisors, canines, premolars and sometimes first
molars
The apices of these teeth are superior to the myelohyoid muscle

The infection perforates Lingual cartical plate below the Levelo of the mucasa
of the floor of the mouth and passes into the sublingual space

A> EXTRAORAL
- There is Little or no swelling

The lymph nodes may be enlarged and
tender
Pain and discomfart on deglutition
Speech may be affected

B> INTRAORAL
- Firm, painful swelling seen in the floor of the mouth on the affected
side

A> INTRAORALLY
- An incision is made close to the lingual cartical plate, lateral to the
sublingual plica, as the important structure at this site is the
sublingual nerve which is deeply placed and less likely to be damaged
by this approach

- The other important structures lie medial to the plica and include
the Wharton's duct, sublingual artery and veins and the lingual
nerve

- The sinus forceps is then inserted and opened to evacuate the puse
B> EXTRAORALLY
- When both the submental and sublingual spaces contain pus, they
can be drained via a skin incision placed in the submental region,
pushing a closed sinus forceps through the myelohyoid muscle

- Similarly, when the submandibular space is involved, a sublingual
space abscess can be approached and drained through an incision in
the skin overlying the submandibular space, via the submandibular
space

LA acts on voltage sensitive Na+ channels and block nerve conduction by
preventing /decreasing the entry of Na+ ions

The Na+ channel has an activation gate near the extracellular mouth and
an inactivation gate at the intracellular mouth

In the resting state, the
activation gate is clased
The LA receptor is located within the channel in its in atraceltular half

The LA traverses the membrane in unionised form
reionises in the axoplasm and approaches the LA receptor
The receptor has higher affinity to the LA in its activated as
well as inactivedated state compared to resting state
Binding of LA to the receptor closes the Not channel &
prolongs the inactivated state
It prevents the entry of Na+ ions and thus action potential is not generated

key_points:
ACETYLCHOLINE & THEORY - Acetylcholine is a neurotransmitter at the
nerve synapses is assumed to be involved in nerve conduction

CALCIUM AISPLACEMENT THEORY - Depolarisation is prevented by
displacement of calcium from certain membrane sites that control the
permeability to sodium

SURFACE CHARGE [REPULSION] THEORY - Local anaesthetic agents
binds to the nerve membrane and change the electrical potential at
the membrane surface

buco LA molecules with net positive charge make the surface potential
at the nerve membrane more positive and reduce the excitability
of nerve by increasing threshold potential

MEMBRANE EXPANSION THEORY - Local anaesthetic agents diffuse
to hydrophobic regions of excitable membrane and expand the
membrane, preventing the sodium permeability
