Bell's palsy is defined as acute onset of non-suppurative inflammation
of facial nerve above the stylomastoid foramen, producing an unilateral
Lower Motor Neuron facial palsy
It accounts for 60-70% of all cases of unilateral peripheral facial palsy
Affects with equal frequency on the right and left sides of the face
Either sex is affected equally
May occur at any age, the median age is 40 years

Increased incidence in the elderly diabetics hypertensives, pregnant
women than in the common people

Cause is unclear, precipitating factors may be -
<a> viral infection
<b> vascular ischaemia
<c> autoimmune inflammatory disarders
<d> Heredity
<e> reactivation of HSV in geniculate ganglion
[Ramsey Hunt syndrome]

Onset of Bells palsy is acute
ТИЗМЭРАИАМ
1/2 of the cases attain maximum paralysis in 48 hours
All cases are clinically prominent by 5 days
Pain behind the ear may precede paralysis by 1-2 days
Impairment of taste is present to some degree in all cases
- but rarely beyond 2nd week of paralysis

Hyperacusis or distartion of sound in ipsilateral ear
- paralysis of stapedius muscle
Patient feels stiffness of face pulled to one side

your
Eye on the paralysed side roll upward - BELLS PHENOMENON
Lower eyelids sags and falls away from conjunctiva

Grade II - mild dysfunction, slight weekness on clase inspection,
normal symmetry at rest
Grade III - moderate dysfunction, eye can be completely closed with
effort
Grade IV - moderately severe, normal tone at rest, obvious weakness
or asymmetry with movement, incomplete closureof eye
Grade V - severe dysfunction, only barely perceptible motion,
asymmetry at rest'

YAO 10ITE
Differentiate between upper and Lower motor neuron lesion
UMN - frontalis is spared allowing normal fou furrowing
of eyebrow and eye blinking
LMN - all muscles of facial expression are affected

A> SYMPTOMATIC
- Lubricating eye drops
- Protectionof the eyes during sleep - eye patches
- Massage of the weakened muscles
- Physiotherapy
B> MEDICOLNAL
- Prednisolone 60-80 mg/be day ^ in divided doses initial 4-5 days

decreases possibility of permanent paralysis СИА
decreases severe pain
- Acyclovir 400 mg 5times/day
- Vitamins B1, B6, B12 may be administered
- If the patient is seen after 3-4 weeks, then steroid therapy is of
no use
— In hyperkinesia [ excessive restlessness]
- do Clostridium botulinum toxin
C> SURGICAL
- If electroneurography reveals a greater than 90% lass of compound
action potential within first 2 weeks following onset of paralysis, facial
nerve decompression is recommended

After this period, surgery is not
benefecial
- Nerve decompression can be carried out internally or externally

- Internal decompression - The nerve is exposed is exposed in the facial
canal pressure in the canal is relieved by exposing the nerve and the
epineural sheath is opened to visualise the nerve fibres and release adhesions
or re-establish continuity

Whenever there is an inadequate vestibular depth present, to increase the
retention and stability of the denture, deepening of the vestibule is considered

Thus vestibuloplasty is a procedure to increase the depth by uncovering the
existing basal bone of the jaws surgically and by repositioning the overlying
mucasa, muscle attachments to a Lower position in the mandible/superior
position in the maxilla
Vestibuloplasty can be done in the maxilla or in the mandible or both jaws

Maxillary vestibuloplasty are primarily done on labial/buccalside
while mandibular techniques are done via labial or lingual approach

First described by Macintosh and Obwegeser
Indications - unstable denture because of shallow vestibular depth
with good underlying bone height and contours

A
vertical midline incision is made in labial vestibule and supra-
periosteal tunnel is made from premolar (R) to premolar (L) area

In maxilla further incisions may be given in 1st molar region
for further advancement

The intervening submucasal tissue is then excised or repositioned
superiorly and maintained by placement of preformed dentures
which can be fixed to the mandible with circummandibular 239VT wiring
and to the maxilla by per alveolar wiring

Wallenius advocated an open method to overcome the relapse of clased
vestibuloplasty
A harizontal incision is made in the mucogingival junction

A thin mucasal flap is elevated by submucasal dissection followed
by supraperiosteal dissection to the desired extent, excising muscle
and subcutaneous tissues
Stay sutures are used to fix the flap to the periosteum deep in vestibule

Mucasal flap from inner aspect of lower lip is used to increase the
vestibular depth in anterior mandibular labial vestibule

The mentalis muscle is detached from the periosteum to required
depth and the vestibule is deepened via supraperiesteal dissection

The flap is then redirected & placed directly against the periasteum
and sutured at the new depth of the vestibule
Raw area is left on the Lip side to be healed by secondary intention
Periosteum of bone is left intact

Similar to Kazanjians technique in which a flap of Labial mucasa is raised,
[but the vestibule is deepened by subperiasteal stripping instead of supraperias-
leal dissection]
In this procedure, flap is reflected from the inner aspect of the Lip till the
alveolar crest and periosteum is reflected from crest of the ridge till the
desired depth of the vestibule

The is periasteum is now sutured to the Lip mucosal margin and then Lip flap
is sutured ^ at the required vestibular depth

Hyperbaric oxygen therapy for radiation damaged tissue was introduced
in 1973 by Greenwood, Gilchrist and Mainous

Since there is a decrease in vascularity and cellularity of both hard and
soft tissues following irradiation of the bone, the hypoxic compromised
tissues when challenged by a traumatic episode fail to heal

Delayed fibroblastic activity in neocollagen formation results in chronic
nonhealing wound
These wounds commonly get injected secondarily

The goal of treating OML and asteoradionecrosis is to overcome hypoxia
& increase vascularity of tissues

of blood
vessel
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is administered in a chamber called the
hyperbaric chamber which may be monoplace or multiplace
Each session ranges from go to 120 min

where patient breathes 100%
oxygen at 2 to 2
Each session is termed as dive

A total of 20-30 dives spanning 5 days
a week is planned preoperatively

- enhance the recruitment and function of leukocytes
- induces fibroblast growth, increased collagen formation
- stimulate angiogenesis in the healing tissue
- inhibits aerobic and anaerobic bacteria
- inhibit bacterial toxin formation
- hyperbaric oxygen increases the amount of oxygen in tissue fluids by
a 10-fold factor

Immediate primary repair at the time of intentional or unintentional
transection [neurotmesis] gives the best result

Delayed primary repair or early secondary repair is acceptable, when delay T209
is necessary for certain reasons Like contaminated wound ,compromised medical
status, extensive tissue destruction etc

Because of progressive distal nerve degeneration and muscle atrophy 12
months is often considered as the Limit for successful facial nerve
repair

Surgical repaire of the transected facial nerve is done with direct end-to-end

lesion, as it contains more than one tissue

may show associated unerupted or impacted teeth, associated swelling

No bone expansion and facial asymmetry

Multiple small, malformed teeth Like structures, creating a bag of marbles"
appearance

completely calcified compound odontoma is biologically inert and can be left

long_essay.question: What is the composition of local anesthetic solution? Describe the role of each component.
long_essay.model_answer: **Local anesthetic (LA) solution** contains: **1. Anesthetic agent**: **Amide type** (metabolized in liver): **Lignocaine (Lidocaine) 2%** - most common, onset 2-3 min, duration 60-90 min. **Bupivacaine 0.5%** - long-acting (4-8 hrs), slower onset. **Articaine 4%** - higher concentration, better tissue penetration. **Ester type** (metabolized in blood): **Procaine**, **Benzocaine** (topical) - rarely used now due to allergy risk. **2. Vasoconstrictor**: **Adrenaline (Epinephrine)** - most common. **Concentrations**: 1:80,000, **1:100,000**, **1:200,000** (standard). **Functions**: (a) **Prolongs duration** of anesthesia (reduces systemic absorption), (b) **Reduces bleeding** (hemostasis), (c) **Reduces toxicity** (slower absorption), (d) **Deeper anesthesia** (keeps LA at site). **Contraindications**: Uncontrolled hypertension, recent MI, hyperthyroidism, use with caution in cardiac patients. **3. Preservative**: **Sodium metabisulfite** - preserves vasoconstrictor (antioxidant). Can cause allergic reactions in asthmatics. **4. Vehicle/Solvent**: **Sterile water** or **isotonic saline**. **5. pH buffer**: **Sodium hydroxide** - adjusts pH (acidic pH for stability, but delays onset). **Standard cartridge**: **1.8 mL** containing 36 mg lignocaine (2%) + 18 mcg adrenaline (1:100,000). **Maximum safe dose**: Lignocaine **without adrenaline**: 4.4 mg/kg (max 300 mg). Lignocaine **with adrenaline**: 7 mg/kg (max 500 mg). For 70 kg adult: ~10 cartridges max with adrenaline.

long_essay.question: Describe the technique of inferior alveolar nerve block.
long_essay.model_answer: **Inferior Alveolar Nerve (IAN) block** anesthetizes **mandibular teeth** (one side), **lower lip**, **chin**, **mucosa**, **bone**. **Nerve anesthetized**: Inferior alveolar nerve (branch of posterior division of mandibular nerve V3). **Landmarks**: (1) **Pterygomandibular raphe** - fold of tissue between pterygoid muscles and mandible, (2) **Coronoid notch** - depression anterior to anterior border of ramus, (3) **Occlusal plane** of mandibular molars. **Injection site**: **1 cm above occlusal plane** of mandibular molars, **3/4 distance** from anterior border of ramus. **Technique**: (1) **Patient position**: Mouth wide open (relaxes muscles, widens pterygomandibular space), (2) **Barrel of syringe**: Place over **contralateral premolars** (crosses midline), parallel to occlusal plane, (3) **Needle insertion**: **25 mm long needle**, insert at pterygomandibular raphe, **1 cm above occlusal plane**, advance **20-25 mm** until bone contacted, (4) **Aspirate**: Check for blood (avoid intravascular injection), (5) **Deposit solution**: 1.8 mL slowly over 60 seconds (rapid injection causes pain), (6) **Partially withdraw** needle (5-10 mm), deposit **0.2-0.4 mL** for **lingual nerve** (anesthetizes tongue, lingual gingiva). **Onset**: 3-5 minutes. **Duration**: 90 minutes with adrenaline. **Areas anesthetized**: Mandibular teeth to midline, buccal mucosa of molars, anterior 2/3 tongue (lingual nerve), lower lip/chin (mental nerve branch). **Complications**: Trismus, hematoma, temporary facial nerve paralysis (if LA enters parotid gland), paresthesia (rare).

long_essay.question: What are the complications of local anesthesia?
long_essay.model_answer: **Complications** categorized as: **LOCAL**: **1. Pain on injection**: Rapid injection, cold solution, low pH solution. **Prevention**: Warm cartridge, inject slowly. **2. Hematoma**: Needle trauma to blood vessel. Common in **PSA block** (pterygoid plexus). **Management**: Pressure, ice pack. **3. Trismus**: Muscle spasm post-injection (pterygoid muscles). Common after **IAN block**. **Management**: Heat, analgesics, jaw exercises, antibiotics if infected. **4. Needle breakage**: Rare with modern needles. **Prevention**: Don't bend needle, don't insert to hub, change direction slowly. **5. Paresthesia**: Nerve injury (mechanical/chemical trauma). **Lingual nerve** and **IAN** most common. Usually **temporary** (weeks-months), rarely permanent. **6. Facial nerve paralysis**: LA enters **parotid gland** during IAN block. **Temporary** (2-3 hours) - eye cannot close, drooping face. **Management**: Reassure, protect cornea (eye patch), resolves spontaneously. **7. Infection**: Contaminated needle/solution. Rare. **8. Self-inflicted trauma**: Patient bites lip/tongue while numb. **SYSTEMIC**: **1. Syncope (Fainting)**: Most common emergency. **Psychogenic** (fear, anxiety). **Symptoms**: Pallor, sweating, nausea, loss of consciousness. **Management**: Position supine, elevate legs, loosen tight clothing, oxygen, ammonia inhalants. **2. Toxic overdose**: Excessive dose or intravascular injection. **CNS symptoms** (early): Tingling, numbness (perioral), lightheadedness, tinnitus, drowsiness, **seizures**. **CVS symptoms** (late): Bradycardia, hypotension, **cardiac arrest**. **Management**: Stop injection, oxygen, IV fluids, anticonvulsants (diazepam), cardiopulmonary support. **3. Allergic reactions**: Rare (true allergy <1%). More common with **esters** than **amides**. Often reaction to **preservative** (metabisulfite). **Symptoms**: Urticaria, bronchospasm, **anaphylaxis** (rare). **Management**: Stop injection, **adrenaline** (anaphylaxis), antihistamines, corticosteroids, oxygen. **4. Epinephrine overdose**: Tachycardia, palpitations, hypertension, anxiety. Rare with dental concentrations.

long_essay.question: Describe the posterior superior alveolar (PSA) nerve block technique.
long_essay.model_answer: **PSA nerve block** anesthetizes **maxillary molars** (except mesiobuccal root of 1st molar supplied by MSA). **Nerve**: Posterior superior alveolar nerve (branch of maxillary nerve V2). **Injection site**: **Height of mucobuccal fold** opposite **2nd maxillary molar**, **45° angle** to occlusal plane, directed **upward, inward, backward** toward **pterygomaxillary fissure**. **Technique**: (1) **Retract cheek** laterally, (2) **Needle**: 25 mm short needle, (3) **Insert** at mucobuccal fold above 2nd molar, bevel toward bone, (4) **Advance** 16 mm at **45° angle** (upward, medial, posterior), **STOP** before 16 mm if bone contacted (risk of pterygoid plexus puncture), (5) **Aspirate** carefully (pterygoid plexus proximity), (6) **Deposit** 1.8 mL slowly. **Areas anesthetized**: Maxillary **2nd and 3rd molars** completely, **1st molar** (distobuccal and palatal roots only - mesiobuccal from MSA). **Onset**: 3-5 minutes. **Complications**: **Hematoma** (most common - pterygoid venous plexus puncture) - presents as **swelling, ecchymosis**. **Management**: Pressure, ice, reassure patient.

long_essay.question: What are the contraindications to use of vasoconstrictors in local anesthesia?
long_essay.model_answer: **Absolute contraindications**: **1. Recent myocardial infarction** (within 6 months) - risk of arrhythmias, re-infarction. **2. Recent cerebrovascular accident** (stroke) - risk of hypertension, re-bleeding. **3. Severe uncontrolled hypertension** (>180/110 mmHg) - risk of hypertensive crisis, stroke. **4. Severe coronary insufficiency** - unstable angina. **5. Uncontrolled hyperthyroidism** - potentiates adrenaline effects (tachycardia, arrhythmias). **6. Uncontrolled diabetes** - adrenaline causes hyperglycemia. **7. Pheochromocytoma** - catecholamine-secreting tumor, adrenaline worsens hypertension. **8. Sulfite allergy** (severe asthmatics) - preservative in vasoconstrictor solutions. **Relative contraindications** (use with caution, reduce concentration): **1. Controlled hypertension** - use **1:200,000** adrenaline, limit dose. **2. Stable angina pectoris** - use minimal dose. **3. Cardiac arrhythmias** - arrhythmogenic potential. **4. Concurrent medication**: **Non-selective beta-blockers** (propranolol) - unopposed alpha effects cause severe hypertension. **Tricyclic antidepressants** - potentiate adrenaline. **MAO inhibitors** - hypertensive crisis. **Cocaine users** - severe cardiovascular effects. **Safe practice in cardiac patients**: Use **1:200,000 adrenaline**, **limit to 2 cartridges** (36 mcg total), **aspirate carefully** (avoid intravascular), **inject slowly**, **monitor vital signs**, **avoid retraction cord** soaked in adrenaline. **Alternative**: **Mepivacaine 3% (plain)** - provides 20-40 min pulpal anesthesia without vasoconstrictor, suitable for short procedures in high-risk patients.

long_essay.question: What are the indications and contraindications for tooth extraction?
long_essay.model_answer: **INDICATIONS**: **1. Dental caries**: Grossly carious tooth **not restorable**, extensive destruction below gingival margin. **2. Periodontal disease**: Advanced periodontitis with **grade III mobility**, bone loss >2/3 root length, poor prognosis. **3. Periapical pathology**: Chronic periapical abscess **not amenable to RCT** (curved roots, calcified canals, perforations). **4. Impacted teeth**: **Symptomatic** impaction (pain, infection, cyst formation), **prophylactic** removal (orthodontic reasons, before orthognathic surgery). **5. Supernumerary teeth**: Interfering with eruption, causing malocclusion. **6. Orthodontic treatment**: Create space for alignment (commonly premolars). **7. Fractured teeth**: Non-restorable **crown-root fracture**, **vertical root fracture**. **8. Pre-prosthetic**: Before complete denture fabrication, teeth with poor prognosis. **9. Pre-radiotherapy**: Diseased teeth in radiation field (prevent osteoradionecrosis). **10. Malpositioned teeth**: Causing trauma to soft tissues, no functional value. **11. Teeth in fracture line**: Mandibular fracture - tooth in fracture line preventing reduction. **12. Esthetic reasons**: Patient request (after full information). **CONTRAINDICATIONS**: **Absolute**: None (if emergency, all relative contraindications can be managed). **Relative** (defer elective extraction): **1. Uncontrolled systemic disease**: **Uncontrolled diabetes** (hyperglycemia >200 mg/dL, ketoacidosis), **Uncontrolled hypertension** (>180/110), **Recent MI** (<6 months), **Unstable angina**, **Severe cardiac disease**. **2. Blood dyscrasias**: **Uncontrolled bleeding disorders** (hemophilia, severe thrombocytopenia <50,000), **Anticoagulant therapy** (high INR >3-4). **Management**: Reduce warfarin, check INR, local hemostatic measures. **3. Immunosuppression**: **Uncontrolled leukemia**, **Chemotherapy** (defer if neutrophils <1000), **Uncontrolled HIV/AIDS**. **4. Acute infections**: **Acute pericoronitis**, **Cellulitis** - stabilize with antibiotics first. **Exception**: If tooth is **source of infection**, extract (with antibiotic cover). **5. Pregnancy**: **1st trimester** (organogenesis risk), **3rd trimester** (supine hypotension, premature labor risk). **2nd trimester safest**. **Emergency** treatment any trimester. **6. Radiotherapy**: Within **radiation field** - risk of osteoradionecrosis. Extract 2 weeks **before** radiotherapy or defer until 6-12 months **after**. **7. Bisphosphonate therapy**: Risk of **medication-related osteonecrosis of jaw (MRONJ)**. Assess risk, preventive measures.

long_essay.question: Describe the principles and steps of tooth extraction using forceps.
long_essay.model_answer: **Principles of forceps extraction**: **1. Expansion of alveolar bone**: Controlled force expands socket. **2. Luxation**: Loosen tooth by breaking PDL fibers, dilating socket. **3. Delivery**: Remove tooth from socket. **STEPS**: **1. Pre-operative**: (a) **Consent** - explain procedure, risks, (b) **Radiograph** - assess root morphology, bone, pathology, (c) **Local anesthesia** - adequate. **2. Tissue reflection**: **Mucoperiosteal flap** if surgical extraction needed. Simple extraction - no flap. **3. Luxation with elevator**: **Straight elevator** (Coupland's) - insert into **mesial/distal PDL space**, apply **rotational/lever force** (fulcrum on alveolar bone). Loosens tooth, expands socket. **Curved elevator** (Cryer's) - for roots. **4. Forceps application**: Select **appropriate forceps** (matches tooth anatomy). **Upper anterior**: Straight forceps. **Upper premolars**: Upper universal. **Upper molars**: Upper molar (with palatal projection). **Lower anterior/premolars**: Lower universal. **Lower molars**: Lower molar (beaked forceps - fit into furcation). **5. Grasping**: Apply forceps **parallel to long axis**, beaks engage tooth **below gingival margin** on **cementum** (not crown - fracture risk). Firm grasp. **6. Luxation movements**: **Buccal-lingual** (primary) or **mesial-distal** movements. Apply **controlled apical pressure** simultaneously (seats forceps deeper). **Expansion** of socket. **Upper anteriors**: Mainly buccal (thin labial bone). **Upper premolars**: Buccal-palatal. **Upper molars**: Buccal first, then palatal. **Lower molars**: **Figure-of-eight** movement (buccal-lingual), then lift lingually (lingual bone thinner). **7. Delivery**: Once mobile, deliver tooth **occlusally** in arc of rotation. Avoid excessive force. **8. Post-extraction**: (a) **Socket inspection** - ensure complete removal (all roots), check for fracture fragments, (b) **Curettage** - remove granulation tissue, **cyst/granuloma**, (c) **Compress alveolar bone** - digital pressure (restores expanded bone), (d) **Sutures** if indicated (flap closure, large socket, bleeding), (e) **Gauze pack** - patient bites for 30 minutes, (f) **Post-op instructions** - avoid rinsing 24 hrs, soft diet, analgesics. **Complications to avoid**: Fracture (root/crown), luxation into sinus, damage to adjacent teeth, jaw fracture (osteoporotic mandible), nerve injury.

long_essay.question: What are the complications during and after tooth extraction? How do you manage them?
long_essay.model_answer: **INTRA-OPERATIVE**: **1. Fracture of tooth/root**: **Common**. **Prevention**: Proper forceps selection, luxation before forceps, controlled force. **Management**: (a) **Visible root**: Remove with elevator/forceps/surgical extraction, (b) **Small root tip** (<3mm, no infection/pathology, difficult access) - **leave**, inform patient, monitor radiographically. **2. Fracture of alveolar bone**: Thin labial/lingual plate fractures. **Management**: Remove loose fragment, smooth edges, compress socket. **3. Fracture of maxillary tuberosity**: During **upper molar extraction** (especially 3rd molar). **Prevention**: Luxate carefully, surgical extraction if ankylosed. **Management**: **Small fragment** - remove. **Large fragment with tooth attached** - **replace** and splint (may retain vitality), refer. **4. Oro-antral communication (OAC)**: Upper molar/premolar extraction. **Signs**: Bubbling of blood, nose-holding test positive. **Management**: **Small (<2mm)** - encourage clot, no nose blowing, antibiotics. **Large (>2-3mm)** - **buccal advancement flap** closure, antibiotics (Amoxicillin-Clavulanate). **5. Displacement into maxillary sinus**: **Management**: **Do NOT irrigate** (pushes deeper). **Caldwell-Luc** procedure or endoscopic retrieval. **6. Jaw fracture**: **Risk factors**: Atrophic mandible, large cyst, deeply impacted 3rd molar. **Prevention**: Controlled force, support mandible. **Management**: MMF/ORIF. **7. Soft tissue damage**: Laceration, damage to adjacent teeth. **Prevent**: Proper retraction, finger guard. **8. Nerve injury**: **IAN** (mandibular 3rd molar), **Lingual nerve**. **Prevention**: Careful technique. **POST-OPERATIVE**: **1. Hemorrhage** (see omfs009). **2. Dry socket** (see omfs010). **3. Pain**: **Management**: Analgesics (NSAIDs - Ibuprofen 400mg + Paracetamol 500mg). **4. Swelling**: **Management**: Ice packs (24 hrs), elevation, antibiotics if infection. **5. Trismus**: Limited mouth opening. **Management**: Heat, jaw exercises, analgesics. **6. Infection**: **Management**: Antibiotics (Amoxicillin 500mg or Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg), analgesics, drainage if abscess. **7. Paresthesia**: IAN/lingual nerve. Usually **temporary** (3-6 months). **Management**: Reassure, Vitamin B12, observe.

long_essay.question: Classify post-extraction hemorrhage. How do you manage it?
long_essay.model_answer: **Post-extraction hemorrhage** classified by **timing**: **1. Primary (Immediate)**: During/immediately after extraction (within hours). **Causes**: Trauma to blood vessels, inadequate compression. **2. Reactionary**: Within **24 hours**. **Causes**: Vasodilator effect (anesthesia wears off), clot dislodgement (rinsing, sucking), hypertension. **3. Secondary**: **After 24 hours** (2-7 days). **Causes**: **Infection** (clot breakdown), local factors (sharp bone), systemic factors. **MANAGEMENT (Stepwise)**: **1. History**: Onset, duration, amount, **bleeding disorder**, **medications** (aspirin, warfarin, NSAIDs), previous bleeding episodes, **systemic disease** (liver disease, leukemia). **2. Examination**: **Locate source** - socket, gingiva, soft tissue laceration. **Distinguish**: Bleeding vs oozing vs blood-tinged saliva (patient exaggerates). **3. Local measures** (FIRST-LINE): (a) **Remove clot** - irrigate socket with saline, suction (expose bleeding point), (b) **Identify source** - socket (bone/soft tissue), torn gingiva, (c) **Gauze pressure** - fold **moistened gauze**, place over socket, patient bites firmly for **20-30 minutes**. **Success**: 90% controlled. (d) If fails - **Hemostatic agents**: **Oxidized cellulose (Surgicel)**, **Gelfoam** (absorbable gelatin sponge), **Tranexamic acid** soaked gauze - pack into socket. (e) **Sutures** - approximate socket edges (reduces exposed bone), **figure-of-eight suture** over socket. (f) **Bone wax** - if bleeding from bone. **4. Systemic measures** (if local fails): (a) **Bleeding disorder suspected**: **Coagulation profile** (PT, aPTT, INR, platelet count, bleeding time). (b) **Hospitalization** if severe/uncontrolled. (c) **Blood transfusion** if severe blood loss. (d) **Correct coagulopathy**: **High INR** - Vitamin K, FFP. **Thrombocytopenia** - platelet transfusion. **Hemophilia** - Factor VIII/IX replacement. (e) **Antifibrinolytic** - **Tranexamic acid** 500mg orally TID (prevents clot lysis). **5. Prevention** (high-risk patients): Pre-operative coagulation screen, liaise with physician, modify anticoagulant (reduce to therapeutic range INR 2-3), local hemostatic measures, avoid IM injections, avoid aspirin post-op.

long_essay.question: What is dry socket (alveolar osteitis)? Describe etiology, clinical features, and management.
long_essay.model_answer: **Dry socket (Alveolar osteitis)** is a **post-extraction complication** characterized by **severe pain** due to **loss/disintegration of blood clot**, exposing **bare bone**. **Incidence**: 1-5% (all extractions), **20-30%** (mandibular 3rd molars - highest risk). **Etiology (Multifactorial)**: **1. Fibrinolysis**: Excessive **plasmin activity** (dissolves clot). **Bacteria** release fibrinolytic enzymes. **2. Trauma**: **Difficult extraction**, excessive curettage (damages bone blood supply). **3. Poor blood supply**: **Mandible** (dense cortical bone, less vascularity) - 10x more common than maxilla. **4. Bacterial infection**: Pre-existing **pericoronitis**, poor oral hygiene. **PREDISPOSING FACTORS**: **1. Smoking** (most significant) - vasoconstriction, negative pressure (suction dislodges clot). **2. Oral contraceptives** - high estrogen increases fibrinolysis. **3. Trauma during extraction** - difficult surgical extraction. **4. Pre-existing infection** - pericoronitis, periodontitis. **5. Excessive rinsing/spitting** (first 24 hrs) - dislodges clot. **6. Poor oral hygiene**. **7. Mandibular 3rd molar extraction** - #1 site. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Timing**: Presents **2-4 days post-extraction** (delayed onset). **2. Severe throbbing pain** - increasing severity, **radiates** to ear/temple/neck (IAN distribution), **not relieved** by routine analgesics. **3. Foul odor/taste** (halitosis, fetor oris) - from necrotic debris. **4. Socket examination**: **Empty socket** (clot absent or gray necrotic debris), **exposed bone** visible (grayish-white, sensitive), **gingival margin** inflamed. **5. No pus** (not infection - inflammatory condition). **6. Regional lymphadenopathy** - tender submandibular nodes. **MANAGEMENT**: **1. Irrigation**: **Warm saline** (gentle irrigation removes debris, necrotic tissue). **Do NOT curette** (damages bone further). **2. Medicated dressing**: (a) **Eugenol-based** - **Alvogyl** (butyl aminobenzoate + iodoform + eugenol) or **Zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE)** paste on ribbon gauze, (b) Pack **loosely** into socket (not packed tightly), (c) **Analgesic + anti-inflammatory** effect (eugenol). **3. Change dressing**: Every **24-48 hours** until pain subsides (3-7 days). **Socket heals by secondary intention**. **4. Analgesics**: **Strong analgesics** - **Ibuprofen 400mg + Paracetamol 500mg** combination. Tramadol if severe. **5. Antibiotics**: **NOT routinely needed** (not bacterial infection). Use only if **severe infection** suspected (fever, swelling, lymphadenopathy) - **Metronidazole 400mg TDS** or **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg**. **6. Instructions**: Gentle rinsing (warm saline after 24 hrs), soft diet, oral hygiene. **PREVENTION**: Minimize trauma, avoid excessive curettage, **atraumatic technique**, avoid smoking 48 hrs post-extraction, antibiotics (Metronidazole/Amoxicillin) for high-risk (impacted 3rd molars), chlorhexidine rinse post-operatively.

long_essay.question: What are the indications for surgical extraction? Outline the procedure.
long_essay.model_answer: **Surgical (Transalveolar/Open) extraction** requires **mucoperiosteal flap**, **bone removal**, ± **tooth sectioning**. **Indications**: **1. Fractured root** during extraction (not retrievable with forceps), **2. Root tips** (curved, divergent, ankylosed), **3. Impacted teeth**, **4. Ankylosed teeth**, **5. Hypercementosis**, **6. Dense surrounding bone** (sclerotic), **7. Teeth with abnormal anatomy** (dilacerated roots), **8. Grossly carious teeth** (insufficient crown for forceps), **9. Planned ridge augmentation**. **PROCEDURE**: **1. Flap design**: **Envelope flap** (sulcular incision + vertical releasing incisions). **Triangular flap** - one vertical incision. **Two-sided flap** - two vertical incisions (better access). **Principles**: (a) Broad base (blood supply), (b) Include one intact tooth mesial/distal (papilla preservation), (c) Vertical incision away from extraction site (avoid wound over socket). **2. Flap elevation**: **Periosteal elevator** - full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap, expose bone. **3. Bone removal**: (a) **Buccal bone** - surgical bur (round/fissure) with **copious irrigation** (prevent bone necrosis), (b) Remove **minimum bone** necessary (preserve ridge height), (c) Create **gutter** around root (buccal + interdental bone). **4. Tooth sectioning** (if needed): **Multirooted teeth**: Separate at **furcation** (remove roots individually). **Single-rooted**: Vertical section if needed. **5. Tooth/root delivery**: **Elevators** - lever out sections, controlled force. **6. Socket debridement**: Curettage (remove granulation/cyst), irrigation, **smooth sharp bone edges** (bone file/rongeur). **7. Flap closure**: **Primary closure** - **interrupted 3-0 silk sutures**, approximate flap margins (tension-free). **8. Post-op instructions**: Ice (24 hrs), soft diet, oral hygiene, analgesics, antibiotics if indicated. **Suture removal**: **7 days**.

long_essay.question: What are the special considerations for tooth extraction in medically compromised patients?
long_essay.model_answer: **CARDIAC PATIENTS**: **Recent MI** (<6 months) - defer elective extraction. **Unstable angina** - defer. **Stable cardiac disease** - proceed with caution. **Prophylactic antibiotics**: **Infective endocarditis (IE) risk**: **High-risk** (prosthetic valves, previous IE, congenital heart disease, cardiac transplant with valvulopathy) - **Amoxicillin 2g** oral **1 hour before** procedure. **Moderate-risk** - no prophylaxis needed (AHA 2007 guidelines). **Anticoagulant therapy**: **Warfarin** - check **INR** (safe if 2-3.5), do NOT stop warfarin (thromboembolic risk), **local hemostatic measures** (sutures, pressure, tranexamic acid rinse). **Aspirin/Clopidogrel** - continue, local measures. **DIABETIC PATIENTS**: **Controlled diabetes** (FBS <200 mg/dL) - routine extraction. **Uncontrolled** (FBS >200 mg/dL, ketoacidosis) - defer, stabilize first. **Morning appointment** (after breakfast + medication). **Prophylactic antibiotics** if **poor control** or **extensive surgery** - **Amoxicillin 500mg TDS** for 5 days. **Prolonged healing**, infection risk. **BISPHOSPHONATE THERAPY**: **Risk of MRONJ** (medication-related osteonecrosis of jaw). **Assessment**: (a) **IV bisphosphonates** (cancer patients - Zoledronic acid) - **high risk**, (b) **Oral bisphosphonates** (osteoporosis - Alendronate) - low risk if <4 years use. **Management**: **High-risk** - **drug holiday** (stop 3 months before + 3 months after extraction if oncologist permits), **atraumatic technique**, **primary closure**, **antibiotics** (Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 7 days). **Low-risk** - routine extraction, antibiotics, follow-up. **BLEEDING DISORDERS**: **Hemophilia** - Factor VIII/IX replacement pre-operatively, **hospital setting**, local measures, **tranexamic acid** mouthwash. **Thrombocytopenia** - platelet transfusion if <50,000. **PREGNANCY**: **2nd trimester safest**. **Avoid** 1st trimester (organogenesis), 3rd trimester (supine hypotension). **Elective** - defer. **Emergency** - any trimester with precautions. **Avoid X-rays** (lead apron if essential). **Anesthesia**: Lignocaine safe. **Antibiotics**: **Amoxicillin/Penicillin** safe. **Avoid**: Tetracycline, metronidazole (1st trimester). **Analgesics**: **Paracetamol** safe. **Avoid**: NSAIDs (3rd trimester - premature closure of ductus arteriosus), Aspirin. **RADIOTHERAPY**: Extract diseased teeth **2 weeks before** radiotherapy (in radiation field). **After radiotherapy** - risk of osteoradionecrosis (ORN). If extraction needed - **hyperbaric oxygen** (20 sessions before + 10 after), antibiotics, atraumatic technique. **Defer** elective extractions 6-12 months post-RT.

long_essay.question: What post-extraction instructions should be given to patients?
long_essay.model_answer: **Post-extraction care instructions**: **IMMEDIATE (First 24 hours)**: **1. Gauze pack**: Bite firmly on **folded gauze** for **30 minutes** (promotes clot formation). Replace if needed. **2. No rinsing/spitting** for **24 hours** - dislodges clot (dry socket risk). **3. No drinking through straw** - negative pressure dislodges clot. **4. No smoking** for **48 hours** - vasoconstriction, negative pressure, delayed healing, dry socket risk (5x increase). **5. Diet**: **Soft, cool diet** (ice cream, yogurt, soups). **Avoid hot liquids** (24 hrs - causes vasodilation, bleeding). **Chew on opposite side**. **6. Ice packs**: Apply **20 min on/20 min off** for **first 4-6 hours** (reduces swelling). **7. Rest**: Avoid strenuous activity, exercise (24 hrs) - increases BP, bleeding. **8. Sleep**: **Head elevated** (2 pillows) - reduces swelling. **NEXT 2-7 DAYS**: **1. Warm saline rinses**: After **24 hours**, gentle rinsing **after meals** (1 tsp salt in 1 cup warm water). **Do NOT rinse vigorously** - dislodges clot. **2. Oral hygiene**: Resume **gentle brushing** adjacent teeth. **Avoid brushing extraction site** (3-4 days). **Chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash** (if prescribed) - 2x/day. **3. Diet**: Progress from soft to regular diet as tolerated. **4. Pain management**: **Analgesics** - **Ibuprofen 400mg** (every 6-8 hrs) + **Paracetamol 500mg**. Take **before anesthesia wears off**. **Avoid aspirin** (increases bleeding). **5. Antibiotics**: If prescribed - **complete full course** (Amoxicillin 500mg TDS x 5 days). **6. Swelling**: **Peak 48-72 hours**. **Heat packs** (after 24 hrs) if swelling persists. **WHAT TO EXPECT**: **1. Bleeding**: **Slight oozing** normal for 24 hrs (blood-tinged saliva). **Persistent bleeding** - bite gauze 30 min, if continues - contact dentist. **2. Pain**: **Moderate pain** 2-3 days (peaks 12 hrs post-extraction), manageable with analgesics. **Severe increasing pain** after 3 days - **dry socket**, contact dentist. **3. Swelling**: Normal (2-3 days), then gradually reduces. **4. Bruising**: May appear after 2-3 days (ecchymosis) - resolves in 7-10 days. **WHEN TO CALL DENTIST**: **Excessive bleeding** (not controlled with gauze), **Severe pain** (unrelieved by medication, especially increasing after 3-4 days), **Swelling increasing** after 3 days, **Fever** (>100°F), **Difficulty swallowing/breathing**, **Pus discharge**, **Adverse drug reactions**. **SUTURE REMOVAL**: **7 days** post-operatively (if non-resorbable sutures used).

long_essay.question: Define impacted tooth. Classify impacted mandibular third molars.
long_essay.model_answer: **Impacted tooth**: A tooth that **fails to erupt** into normal functional position within expected time, **completely/partially blocked** by bone, soft tissue, or adjacent tooth. **Most commonly impacted**: **Mandibular 3rd molars** (48%), Maxillary 3rd molars (38%), Maxillary canines (10%). **CLASSIFICATIONS OF MANDIBULAR 3rd MOLAR**: **1. WINTER'S CLASSIFICATION** (based on **angulation** of long axis to 2nd molar): **a) Mesioangular** (43%) - most common, crown angulated mesially toward 2nd molar. **Easiest** to remove. **b) Horizontal** (3%) - long axis perpendicular to 2nd molar (90°). **Most difficult**. **c) Vertical** (38%) - long axis parallel to 2nd molar (normal eruption path). **d) Distoangular** (6%) - crown angulated distally. **e) Inverted** (rare) - crown pointing downward. **f) Buccoangular/Linguoangular** - buccal/lingual tilt. **2. PELL & GREGORY CLASSIFICATION**: **Relationship to ascending ramus & 2nd molar**: **PART A - Depth** (relative to occlusal plane of 2nd molar): **Class I**: **Occlusal surface** of 3rd molar at **same level** as occlusal surface of 2nd molar. **Class II**: Occlusal surface **between occlusal plane and CEJ** of 2nd molar. **Class III**: **Completely below CEJ** of 2nd molar (deepest - most difficult). **PART B - Space** (relationship to ramus): **Class A**: **Adequate space** between anterior border of ramus and distal surface of 2nd molar (**>8mm**) - entire tooth anterior to ramus. **Class B**: **Reduced space** (3-8mm) - **part of crown** covered by ramus. **Class C**: **No space** (<3mm) - tooth **completely** within ramus. **Most difficult**: **Class III C** (deep, within ramus) + **Horizontal** angulation. **Clinical significance**: Determines surgical difficulty, approach, bone removal required, complications risk.

long_essay.question: What are the indications for removal of impacted third molars? Describe the surgical procedure.
long_essay.model_answer: **INDICATIONS FOR REMOVAL**: **Therapeutic** (symptomatic impactions): **1. Pericoronitis** - recurrent infection of soft tissue covering partially erupted tooth. **2. Dental caries** - impacted tooth or adjacent 2nd molar (distal caries - hard to restore). **3. Periodontal disease** - bone loss distal to 2nd molar. **4. Cyst/tumor formation** - **dentigerous cyst** (around crown), odontogenic tumors. **5. Root resorption** - of adjacent 2nd molar. **6. Pain** - unexplained pain from impacted tooth. **7. Jaw fracture** - tooth in fracture line or weakens mandible. **Prophylactic** (asymptomatic): **1. Orthodontic treatment** - prevent relapse, create space. **2. Before orthognathic surgery**. **3. Radiotherapy** - remove teeth in radiation field. **4. High risk of complications** if left. **Controversial**: Routine prophylactic removal of asymptomatic impactions **NOT recommended** (NICE guidelines). **SURGICAL PROCEDURE (Mandibular 3rd molar)**: **PRE-OPERATIVE**: (1) Clinical + **Radiographic assessment** - **OPG/CBCT** (anatomy, depth, angulation, relation to **IAN canal**), (2) **Informed consent** - risks (nerve injury, dry socket, pain, swelling), (3) **Local anesthesia** - IAN block + long buccal nerve block. **SURGICAL STEPS**: **1. Incision**: **Ward's incision** (most common) - starts on **buccal aspect** of 2nd molar (mid-tooth), extends **posteriorly along external oblique ridge**, then curves **medially** over retromolar area. Creates **triangular flap**. **2. Flap elevation**: Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap using **periosteal elevator**, expose bone overlying impacted tooth. **Retract** with **Howarth's elevator**. **3. Bone removal** (if needed): **Surgical bur** (straight/angled handpiece) with **copious saline irrigation** (prevent bone necrosis). Remove bone **buccally** and **distally** (create gutter around crown). **Remove minimum necessary** bone. **4. Tooth sectioning**: **Horizontal impaction**: Section crown from roots (**horizontal cut** at CEJ), remove crown first, then roots. **Mesioangular**: Section **mesiodistal** (separate mesial portion), remove piece by piece. **Vertical**: May not need sectioning if adequate space. **5. Tooth delivery**: **Elevators** (Coupland's, Cryer's) - carefully luxate and deliver sections. **Avoid excessive force** (IAN injury, fracture). **6. Socket management**: (a) **Debride** - remove follicular sac, granulation tissue, (b) **Irrigate** copiously with saline, (c) **Smooth sharp bone** edges (rongeur/bone file), (d) **Inspect** for root tips, foreign bodies. **7. Hemostasis**: **Pressure** with gauze, **bone wax** if bone bleeding, usually self-limiting. **8. Flap closure**: **Interrupted 3-0 silk sutures** - **primary tension-free closure**. **9. Post-operative**: (a) **Gauze pack** - bite 30 min, (b) **Ice** externally (4-6 hrs), (c) **Analgesics** - Ibuprofen + Paracetamol, (d) **Antibiotics** (controversial - use for difficult surgery/infection) - Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg BD x 5 days, (e) **Chlorhexidine rinses** 0.2% (after 24 hrs). **SUTURE REMOVAL**: **7 days**. **COMPLICATIONS**: **IAN injury** (0.5-5% permanent paresthesia), **Lingual nerve injury** (rare), **Dry socket** (20-30%), **Pain, swelling, trismus**, **Infection**, **Fracture of mandible**, **Hemorrhage**, **Damage to 2nd molar**.

long_essay.question: What is pericoronitis? Describe clinical features and management.
long_essay.model_answer: **Pericoronitis** is **acute/chronic inflammation** of **soft tissue (operculum)** overlying **partially erupted tooth**, most commonly **mandibular 3rd molar**. **Etiology**: **1. Trauma**: **Opposing maxillary 3rd molar** bites on inflamed operculum → ulceration → secondary infection. **2. Bacterial**: Food/debris accumulation under operculum → **bacterial proliferation** (mainly **anaerobes**). **3. Insufficient space**: Tooth partially erupted but impacted against 2nd molar or ramus. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **ACUTE PERICORONITIS**: **1. Severe pain** - localized to angle of mandible, **radiates** to ear/throat, difficulty swallowing. **2. Swelling** - pericoronal tissue (operculum) **swollen, red, edematous**, may extend to cheek. **3. Trismus** - limited mouth opening (medial pterygoid spasm). **4. Halitosis** - foul odor/taste. **5. Dysphagia** - difficulty swallowing (if infection spreads). **6. Pus discharge** - purulent exudate from under operculum (on pressure). **7. Systemic signs**: **Fever**, malaise, **lymphadenopathy** (submandibular nodes tender). **8. Opposing tooth trauma** - indentation/ulcer on operculum from upper 3rd molar. **CHRONIC PERICORONITIS**: **Mild symptoms** - intermittent discomfort, slight swelling, mild tenderness. **Episodes of acute exacerbation**. **COMPLICATIONS** (if untreated): **1. Pericoronal abscess** - localized collection of pus. **2. Cellulitis** - spread to **masticator spaces**, **submandibular space**. **3. Ludwig's angina** - bilateral submandibular/sublingual space infection (life-threatening). **4. Mediastinitis** - rare, potentially fatal. **MANAGEMENT**: **ACUTE EPISODE**: **1. Irrigation**: **Hydrogen peroxide (1:1)** or **warm saline** under operculum (remove debris/pus). Use **blunt needle syringe**. **2. Operculectomy** (if accessible): **Excise** redundant soft tissue overlying tooth (diathermy/scalpel). **Reduces recurrence**. **Do NOT** if acute infection/trismus (defer). **3. Grind opposing tooth**: Reduce trauma from biting. **Remove cuspal interference**. **4. Antibiotics**: **Amoxicillin 500mg TDS** or **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg BD** x 5-7 days. **Metronidazole 400mg TDS** (excellent for anaerobes). **5. Analgesics**: **Ibuprofen 400mg + Paracetamol 500mg**. **6. Chlorhexidine rinses**: **0.2% CHX** mouthwash BD. **7. Warm saline rinses**: After 24 hours. **DEFINITIVE TREATMENT** (after acute episode resolves - 4-6 weeks): **Extract impacted 3rd molar** - **prevents recurrence**. **Chronic/recurrent pericoronitis** is **absolute indication** for extraction.

long_essay.question: What are the treatment options for impacted maxillary canine?
long_essay.model_answer: **Impacted maxillary canine** - 2nd most common impaction (after 3rd molars). **Location**: **Palatal** (60%), **Labial** (30%), **Mid-alveolar** (10%). **TREATMENT OPTIONS**: **1. Observation**: If **asymptomatic**, adequate space, no pathology, patient refuses treatment. **Monitor** radiographically (3-5 years) for cyst formation, root resorption of adjacent teeth. **2. Extraction**: **Indications**: (a) **Ankylosed** canine (poor prognosis for alignment), (b) **Severe root resorption** of adjacent incisors, (c) **Advanced age** (poor bone remodeling), (d) **Patient refusal** of orthodontics, (e) **Ectopic position** (unreachable/unfavorable). **3. Surgical exposure + Orthodontic alignment**: **GOLD STANDARD** for young patients. **Pre-requisites**: (a) **Adequate space** in arch (orthodontic expansion if needed), (b) **Tooth viable** (no ankylosis, severe dilaceration), (c) **Patient cooperation** (orthodontic treatment 18-24 months). **Procedure**: (a) **Orthodontic preparation** - create space, align adjacent teeth, (b) **Surgical exposure** - **Open technique** (excise tissue, expose crown, pack) or **Closed technique** (expose, bond attachment, replace flap - preferred), (c) **Orthodontic traction** - attach chain/spring, apply force to guide eruption. **Approaches**: **Palatal impaction** - **palatal flap**. **Labial impaction** - **labial/sulcular flap**. **4. Autotransplantation**: Remove impacted canine, place in extraction site of adjacent tooth. **Rarely done** - unpredictable. **Preferred option**: **Surgical exposure + Orthodontics** (preserves canine - esthetic, functional importance).

long_essay.question: What are supernumerary teeth? Describe types and management.
long_essay.model_answer: **Supernumerary teeth** are **extra teeth** beyond normal dentition (32 permanent). **Prevalence**: 1-3% (permanent dentition), males > females (2:1). **Etiology**: **Genetic** (hyperactivity of dental lamina), **cleidocranial dysplasia** (multiple supernumeraries). **TYPES**: **1. Mesiodens**: Most common supernumerary. Located **between maxillary central incisors** (midline). Usually **conical/peg-shaped**, **inverted** position. **Complications**: Prevents eruption of permanent incisors, diastema, rotation of adjacent teeth. **2. Paramolar**: Extra molar **buccal/lingual** to molars (usually maxillary 3rd molar area). **3. Distomolar**: **Distal** to 3rd molar (4th molar). Rare. **4. Supplemental (Eumorphic)**: Resembles **normal tooth** morphology. Often **lateral incisor** region. **CLASSIFICATION by morphology**: **Conical** (most common - mesiodens), **Tuberculate** (barrel-shaped with tubercles), **Supplemental** (normal morphology), **Odontoma** (complex/compound). **COMPLICATIONS**: **1. Delayed/prevented eruption** of permanent teeth, **2. Crowding, rotation, displacement** of adjacent teeth, **3. Diastema** (midline spacing), **4. Root resorption** of adjacent teeth, **5. Cyst formation** (dentigerous cyst), **6. Rarely - erupts** normally. **MANAGEMENT**: **INDICATIONS FOR REMOVAL**: (1) **Prevents eruption** of permanent teeth, (2) **Causes displacement/rotation**, (3) **Associated pathology** (cyst, resorption), (4) **Interferes with orthodontic treatment**, (5) **Impedes prosthetic rehabilitation**. **TIMING**: **Early removal** (7-9 years) if interfering with eruption. **Delay** if close to developing roots of adjacent teeth (risk of damage). **Asymptomatic, no complications** - **observe**, extract later if needed. **Procedure**: Usually **surgical extraction** (unerupted) - flap, bone removal, extract, primary closure.

long_essay.question: How do you prevent and manage inferior alveolar nerve injury during third molar surgery?
long_essay.model_answer: **IAN injury** during mandibular 3rd molar surgery - serious complication causing **paresthesia** (altered sensation) of lower lip, chin, teeth. **Incidence**: **Temporary** 0.4-8%, **Permanent** 0.4-0.8%. **PREVENTION**: **1. Pre-operative assessment**: **Radiographic evaluation** - **OPG** (shows IAN canal proximity). **High-risk signs**: (a) **Darkening of root** (canal superimposition), (b) **Deflection of canal**, (c) **Loss of cortication** of canal, (d) **Root narrowing**. **CBCT** if high-risk (3D evaluation - exact relationship). **2. Careful surgical technique**: (a) **Tooth sectioning** - divide tooth, remove in pieces (less force, better control), (b) **Avoid excessive force** with elevators (luxate gently), (c) **Preserve lingual plate** (lingual nerve protection), (d) **Avoid drilling deep** near IAN canal, (e) **Nerve visualization** if exposed - protect with retractor, (f) **Remove minimum bone**. **3. Alternative approaches**: **Coronectomy** (high-risk cases) - remove **crown only**, leave roots (if vital, no infection, roots below IAN). Roots gradually migrate away from nerve (3-5 years). **MANAGEMENT OF NERVE INJURY**: **IMMEDIATE** (if suspected): (1) **Inform patient**, document, (2) **Reassure** - most recover (70-90% temporary injuries recover in 3-6 months), (3) **Map sensory deficit** - test light touch, pin-prick, two-point discrimination (lower lip, chin), compare sides. **FOLLOW-UP**: (1) **Re-assess** at 1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months. **2. Pharmacotherapy**: **Vitamin B12** (Methylcobalamin) - nerve regeneration (controversial efficacy). **Corticosteroids** (Dexamethasone) - early (within 48 hrs) if severe injury (reduces edema) - controversial. **3. Microsurgical repair** (if complete transection suspected): **Refer** to oral-maxillofacial surgeon within **3 months** (optimal window). **Direct nerve repair** or **grafting**. **Recovery**: **Neuropraxia** (stretching) - full recovery weeks-months. **Axonotmesis** (partial damage) - partial recovery. **Neurotmesis** (complete transection) - poor prognosis without repair.

long_essay.question: Describe the spread of odontogenic infections. What are the determinants?
long_essay.model_answer: **Odontogenic infection** spreads from **tooth apex** through **alveolar bone** into surrounding **soft tissues** (fascial spaces). **PATH OF SPREAD**: **Tooth → Periapical bone → Cortical bone perforation → Soft tissue spaces**. **DETERMINANTS OF SPREAD**: **1. Muscle attachments** (PRIMARY determinant): **a) Above muscle attachments**: Infection points **buccally/labially** → **buccal/vestibular space**. **b) Below muscle attachments**: Infection → **deeper fascial spaces**. **Maxillary teeth**: **Apices above buccinator** attachment → **buccal space/canine fossa**. **Apices below buccinator** → **sublingual/submandibular space** (rare - palatal perforation). **Mandibular teeth**: **Anteriors (incisors)**: Above mentalis → **vestibular**. Below mentalis → **submental space**. **Premolars/Molars**: **Apices above mylohyoid** line → **buccal/vestibular space**. **Apices below mylohyoid** → **submandibular space** (common - 1st/2nd molar). **Lingually**: **Sublingual space** (above mylohyoid). **2. Bone density**: **Thin cortical bone** (maxillary labial, mandibular lingual) - easier perforation. **Thick bone** (mandibular buccal, maxillary palatal) - slow spread, more pain. **3. Location of perforation**: **Buccal** > **Palatal/Lingual** perforation (due to bone thickness). **4. Virulence of organism**: **Aerobic** (early - swelling, cellulitis) vs **Anaerobic** (late - abscess, pus). **Mixed flora** common. **5. Host resistance**: **Immunocompromised** (diabetes, HIV, chemotherapy) - rapid spread, severe infection. **PRIMARY FASCIAL SPACES** (directly connected to jaw): **Vestibular, Buccal space, Canine (infraorbital), Submental, Sublingual, Submandibular**. **SECONDARY SPACES** (infection spreads from primary): **Masticator spaces** (Masseteric, Pterygomandibular, Superficial/Deep temporal), **Lateral pharyngeal, Retropharyngeal, Parotid**. **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Swelling location** indicates **space involved** and **source tooth**. **Severe infections** spread to **multiple spaces** → **Ludwig's angina, Cavernous sinus thrombosis**.

long_essay.question: What is Ludwig's angina? Describe clinical features and management.
long_essay.model_answer: **Ludwig's angina** is a **rapidly progressive, life-threatening cellulitis** involving **bilateral submandibular, sublingual, and submental spaces** simultaneously. **BILATERALITY** is key diagnostic feature. **Etiology**: **Odontogenic** (80%) - infection from **mandibular molars** (apices below mylohyoid line). **Precipitating factors**: Poor oral hygiene, immunosuppression (diabetes, HIV), trauma. **Microbiology**: **Mixed aerobic-anaerobic** flora - *Streptococcus viridans, Staphylococcus, Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus*. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. CARDINAL SIGN**: **Bilateral submandibular swelling** - **board-like hardness** ("woody" induration). **Brawny** edema. **2. Submandibular swelling** extends to **neck** (collar-like). **3. Tongue**: **Elevated and pushed posteriorly** (sublingual space involved) - **"bull neck appearance", "tongue protruding"**. **4. Floor of mouth**: **Raised, indurated** (rock-hard). **5. AIRWAY COMPROMISE**: **Most serious** - dyspnea, **stridor**, dysphagia (difficulty swallowing), **drooling** (cannot swallow saliva), **tripod position** (sitting, leaning forward, mouth open). **Progressive** → respiratory obstruction → **asphyxia**. **6. Systemic signs**: **High fever** (>101°F), tachycardia, tachypnea, toxemia. **7. Trismus**: May be **absent** (distinguishes from masticator space infection). **8. NO fluctuance** - cellulitis (not abscess initially). **Later stages** - abscess formation. **COMPLICATIONS** (life-threatening): **1. Airway obstruction** - **asphyxia** (most common cause of death). **2. Descending mediastinitis** - spread along fascial planes to mediastinum (50% mortality). **3. Septicemia**, **Aspiration pneumonia**, **Jugular vein thrombosis**, **Pericarditis**. **MANAGEMENT** (MEDICAL EMERGENCY - hospitalization): **1. AIRWAY MANAGEMENT** (PRIORITY): **Assessment** - stridor, dyspnea, oxygen saturation. **Intubation** if respiratory distress. **Tracheostomy** (if intubation difficult/failed) - preferred (definitive airway). **Fiber-optic intubation** (if expertise available). **2. Incision & Drainage (I&D)**: **Immediate** if pus/abscess. **Multiple incisions**: (a) **Bilateral submandibular** (along lower border of mandible), (b) **Submental** (midline), (c) **Blunt dissection** through spaces (break loculations), (d) **Corrugated rubber drains** placed. **DO NOT WAIT** for fluctuance (cellulitis can be drained to prevent spread). **3. Source control**: **Extract/RCT** offending tooth (once stabilized). **4. Antibiotics** (HIGH DOSE IV): **Empirical** (before culture): **IV Penicillin G** 2-4 million units q4h or **Ampicillin-Sulbactam 3g q6h**. **PLUS Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h** (anaerobic cover). **Alternative** (penicillin allergy): **Clindamycin 600-900mg IV q8h**. **Adjust** after culture/sensitivity. **Duration**: 10-14 days (IV → oral when improving). **5. Supportive care**: **IV fluids** (dehydration), **Analgesics**, **Antipyretics**, **Nutritional support**. **6. Monitor**: Vital signs, airway patency, WBC count, CRP. **PROGNOSIS**: **Mortality 8-10%** if untreated. **<5%** with aggressive treatment. **Early recognition** and **airway management** crucial.

long_essay.question: What is cavernous sinus thrombosis? How does odontogenic infection lead to it?
long_essay.model_answer: **Cavernous sinus thrombosis (CST)** is **septic thrombosis** of **cavernous venous sinus** (intracranial venous channel at skull base). **Life-threatening** complication of **maxillary dental infections**. **Mortality**: 20-30% (even with treatment). **PATHWAY OF SPREAD**: **Maxillary teeth** (especially **canine region - "danger triangle of face"**) → **Facial vein/Angular vein** → **Superior/Inferior ophthalmic veins** (valveless) → **Cavernous sinus** → **Septic thrombosis**. **Danger triangle**: Area bounded by **corners of mouth** and **bridge of nose** - venous drainage to cavernous sinus without valves (bidirectional flow). **Bacteria**: *Staphylococcus aureus* (most common), *Streptococcus*, anaerobes. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **EARLY (Unilateral)**: **1. Periorbital edema** - marked swelling around eye. **2. Chemosis** - conjunctival edema (red, swollen conjunctiva). **3. Proptosis** - eye protrusion (forward displacement). **4. Ophthalmoplegia** - **paralysis of extraocular muscles** (CN III, IV, VI pass through cavernous sinus) - **fixed, dilated pupil**, inability to move eye. **5. Ptosis** - drooping eyelid (CN III palsy). **6. Loss of corneal reflex** (CN V1 - ophthalmic). **7. Vision loss** - can be permanent. **LATE (Bilateral)**: **Contralateral eye involvement** (infection crosses to opposite cavernous sinus via intercavernous sinuses) - **pathognomonic sign**. **SYSTEMIC**: **High fever** (103-105°F), **rigors**, **altered mental status**, **seizures** (meningeal irritation), **papilledema** (fundoscopy). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic eye signs + source infection. **2. CT/MRI** with contrast - **filling defect** in cavernous sinus, **orbital cellulitis**. **3. Blood culture** - bacteremia. **4. Lumbar puncture** - if meningitis suspected (contraindicated if raised ICP). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Meningitis**, **Brain abscess**, **Stroke** (internal carotid involvement), **Blindness**, **Death**. **MANAGEMENT** (ICU - medical emergency): **1. HIGH-DOSE IV ANTIBIOTICS**: **Empirical**: **Vancomycin 1g IV q12h** (MRSA cover) **PLUS Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h** (broad-spectrum) **PLUS Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h** (anaerobic). Adjust after cultures. **Duration**: 3-4 weeks (IV minimum 2 weeks). **2. Anticoagulation**: **Controversial**. **Heparin** may reduce thrombosis propagation, improve outcome. Risk of hemorrhage. **3. Source control**: **Drain abscess**, **Extract** tooth, **Orbital decompression** if needed. **4. Supportive care**: **Fluids**, **Antipyretics**, **Monitor neurological status**, **Ophthalmology consult**. **5. Corticosteroids** (if cerebral edema) - **Dexamethasone**. **PREVENTION**: **Early treatment** of maxillary dental infections, **avoid manipulation** of infections in danger triangle (squeezing furuncles/pimples).

long_essay.question: What are the principles of antibiotic therapy in odontogenic infections?
long_essay.model_answer: **Principles**: **1. REMOVE SOURCE**: **Primary treatment** - extraction/RCT of offending tooth, drainage of abscess. **Antibiotics ADJUNCT**, not substitute. **2. EMPIRICAL THERAPY**: Start antibiotics **before culture results** (based on likely organisms). **Modify** when culture/sensitivity available. **3. APPROPRIATE SELECTION**: Based on **microbiology** (mixed aerobic-anaerobic), **severity**, **patient factors**. **ODONTOGENIC FLORA**: **Aerobic**: *Streptococcus viridans* (alpha-hemolytic), *S. milleri group*. **Anaerobic**: *Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella, Porphyromonas, Fusobacterium, Bacteroides*. **ANTIBIOTIC CHOICES**: **MILD-MODERATE INFECTIONS** (localized swelling, no systemic signs): **1st line**: **Amoxicillin 500mg TDS** (7 days). **Broad-spectrum**, covers streptococci. **Extended spectrum**: **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Augmentin) 625mg BD/TDS** - covers **beta-lactamase** producing bacteria, **better anaerobic coverage**. **Penicillin allergy**: **Clindamycin 300mg QID** or **Azithromycin 500mg OD** (3-5 days). **Metronidazole 400mg TDS** - **excellent anaerobic** coverage, **synergistic** with penicillin. Often used in **combination**: **Amoxicillin 500mg + Metronidazole 400mg TDS**. **SEVERE INFECTIONS** (cellulitis, spreading, systemic signs, hospitalization): **IV antibiotics**: **Penicillin G 2-4 MU IV q4-6h** or **Ampicillin-Sulbactam 1.5-3g IV q6h** **PLUS Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h**. **Penicillin allergy**: **Clindamycin 600-900mg IV q8h** (excellent bone penetration). **SPECIAL SITUATIONS**: **Penicillin-resistant organisms** (treatment failure): **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate** or **Cefuroxime**. **MRSA suspected** (rare in odontogenic): **Vancomycin**. **DURATION**: **5-7 days** (mild-moderate), **Continue 2-3 days** after signs/symptoms resolve. **Severe**: 10-14 days. **PROPHYLACTIC ANTIBIOTICS**: **Infective endocarditis prevention** (high-risk cardiac patients): **Amoxicillin 2g** oral **1 hour before** invasive dental procedure. **Post-operative infection prevention** (impacted 3rd molar, immunocompromised): **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg BD** starting 1 day before or day of surgery (3-5 days). **CONTRAINDICATIONS TO ANTIBIOTICS ALONE**: **Abscess** requiring drainage, **Spreading infection**, **Systemic involvement**, **Immunocompromised host**. **MONITORING**: Reassess **48-72 hours** - if **no improvement**, consider: **Inadequate drainage**, **Resistant organism** (change antibiotic), **Wrong diagnosis**, **Immunosuppression**.

long_essay.question: Describe the principles and technique of incision and drainage of abscess.
long_essay.model_answer: **Incision and drainage (I&D)** - **surgical drainage** of purulent collection (abscess). **PRIMARY treatment** for abscesses (antibiotics adjunct). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Fluctuant abscess** - pus collection present. **2. Spreading cellulitis** - prevent abscess formation. **3. Severe infection** - drain to reduce bacterial load. **PRINCIPLES**: **1. "Pus wherever it is, must be let out"** (Hilton's method). **2. Incision** at **point of maximum fluctuance** (where abscess points). **3. Incision** along **natural skin creases/lines** (cosmetic), or **dependent** area (gravity drainage). **4. Adequate incision** - allow complete drainage, irrigation, exploration. **5. Break loculations** - blunt dissection separates fibrous septa. **6. Drain placement** - prevent premature closure. **CONTRAINDICATIONS (Relative)**: **Cellulitis without abscess** (no pus - antibiotics first), **Bleeding disorders** (correct first). **TECHNIQUE**: **PRE-OPERATIVE**: (1) **Consent**, (2) **Antibiotics** started (before/with I&D), (3) **Anesthesia**: **Difficult** in infected tissues (low pH inactivates LA). Options: **a) Local infiltration** (around abscess - not into pus), **b) Nerve block** (away from infection), **c) General anesthesia** (severe cases, trismus, children). **PROCEDURE**: **1. Prepare site**: Antiseptic (povidone-iodine). **2. Incision**: **Stab incision** (scalpel #11 or #15 blade) at **point of maximum fluctuance** or most **dependent** area. **Adequate length** (1.5-2 cm minimum - allow drainage). **Intraoral**: Vertical incision (avoid nerves/vessels). **Extraoral**: Along skin tension lines or submandibular/submental approach. **3. Blunt dissection**: Insert **curved hemostat/sinus forceps** into abscess cavity. **Open** widely (break loculations, explore cavity), **Finger dissection** if large cavity (submandibular space). **4. Irrigation**: **Copious saline** irrigation (mechanical debridement). **5. Obtain culture**: Aspirate pus with **syringe** (before contamination) - send for **culture/sensitivity** (anaerobic + aerobic). **6. Drain placement**: (a) **Corrugated rubber drain** (Penrose) or **iodoform gauze** - prevents premature closure, continued drainage, (b) Insert through incision, **secure** with silk suture to prevent displacement, (c) **Leave** 2-3 days (remove when drainage minimal). **7. Dressing**: **Intraoral** - none or loose gauze. **Extraoral** - sterile gauze, pressure dressing (mild). **POST-OPERATIVE CARE**: **1. Antibiotics**: Continue oral antibiotics 5-7 days. **2. Analgesics**: Ibuprofen + Paracetamol. **3. Warm saline rinses** (intraoral) or **hot fomentation** (extraoral) - promotes drainage. **4. Drain removal**: **2-3 days** when drainage minimal/stopped. **5. Follow-up**: Daily initially (assess drainage, change drain if needed). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Inadequate drainage** - recurrence, **Damage to nerves/vessels** (facial nerve, IAN, lingual), **Scarring** (extraoral), **Hemorrhage**.

long_essay.question: What is osteomyelitis of the jaw? Describe types and management.
long_essay.model_answer: **Osteomyelitis** is **infection of bone and bone marrow**. **Mandible** more common than maxilla (less vascularity, denser cortical bone). **TYPES**: **1. ACUTE OSTEOMYELITIS**: **Sudden onset**, **severe pain**, **fever**, **swelling**. **Cause**: Odontogenic infection spreads to bone (periapical abscess, fracture, surgery). **Features**: **Pus formation**, **sequestrum** formation (dead bone), **Involucrum** (new bone shell around dead bone). **2. CHRONIC OSTEOMYELITIS**: **Persistent** (>1 month), **low-grade**, **intermittent** symptoms. **Primary chronic** (direct infection - rare) or **Secondary** (from untreated acute). **Features**: **Draining sinus** (intraoral/extraoral), **Pathological fracture** (weak bone), **Sequestra** in CT/radiograph. **3. CHRONIC SCLEROSING OSTEOMYELITIS** (Garré's): **Non-suppurative** (no pus), **young patients**, mandibular **body/angle**. **Periosteal bone formation** → **cortical thickening** → "**onion-peel**" appearance (radiograph). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **Acute**: Deep boring pain, fever, malaise, swelling, **paresthesia** (IAN involvement), **teeth mobile**, **pus discharge**, **trismus**. **Chronic**: Intermittent pain/swelling, **draining sinus**, **foul odor**, **sequestra** may be palpable/visible. **RADIOGRAPHIC**: **Early** - no changes (10-14 days). **Late**: **Irregular radiolucency**, **moth-eaten** appearance, **sequestra** (radiopaque), **involucrum**. **DIAGNOSIS**: **Clinical + Radiographic + Culture**. **Biopsy** if chronic (rule out malignancy). **MANAGEMENT**: **1. Antibiotics** (prolonged - **6-12 weeks**): **IV initially** (Clindamycin 600-900mg q8h or Ampicillin-Sulbactam), then **oral** (Clindamycin 300mg QID, Amoxicillin-Clavulanate). **Adjust** per culture/sensitivity. **2. Surgical**: (a) **Remove source** - extract infected teeth, (b) **Incision & drainage** - if abscess, (c) **Sequestrectomy** - remove dead bone (sequestra), (d) **Saucerization** - create bony cavity for drainage/granulation, (e) **Decortication** - remove sclerotic bone (chronic), (f) **Resection** - if severe/refractory (rare). **3. Supportive**: **Hyperbaric oxygen** (controversial - improves oxygenation, healing). **4. Prevent**: Good oral hygiene post-extraction, antibiotics for high-risk (irradiated bone, immunosuppressed).

long_essay.question: What is alveoloplasty? Describe indications and procedure.
long_essay.model_answer: **Alveoloplasty** is **surgical reshaping/recontouring** of alveolar ridge to create **optimal foundation** for prosthesis (denture). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Irregular/sharp bony ridge** - causes denture discomfort, sore spots. **2. Undercuts** - prevent denture insertion/seating. **3. Post-extraction** (multiple teeth) - smooth sharp interdental/interradicular septa. **4. Protruding/flared labial plate** - improper denture support. **5. Prominent/sharp mylohyoid ridge** - interferes with denture flange. **6. Genial tubercles enlargement** - mandibular midline prominence. **7. Maxillary tuberosity enlargement** - reduces interarch space. **TYPES**: **1. Simple/Intraseptal**: Removes **interdental/interradicular bone** only. Done **during/immediately after** multiple extractions. **No flap** raised. **2. Radical/Interseptal**: **Extensive reshaping** with **mucoperiosteal flap**. **Removes** labial/lingual cortical plates, sharp ridges. Done **separate procedure** or with extractions. **PROCEDURE (Simple - at time of extraction)**: (1) **Extract teeth**, (2) **Rongeur/bone file** - remove **sharp bone** (interdental septa), smooth labial/lingual plates, (3) **Digital compression** - compress alveolar bone plates together (reduces ridge width), (4) **Irrigation**, (5) **Primary closure** if possible (approximate socket margins with sutures). **PROCEDURE (Radical - separate surgery)**: (1) **Incision**: **Crestal incision** along ridge ± **vertical releasing incisions**, (2) **Flap elevation**: Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap (buccal and lingual/palatal), expose bone, (3) **Bone recontouring**: (a) **Rongeur** - remove irregular bone, sharp projections, (b) **Bone file** - smooth surface, (c) **Surgical bur** (with irrigation) - remove large undercuts, excessive bone, (d) Create **gradual, rounded contour**, (4) **Trim excess soft tissue**: Fibrous/redundant tissue excision, (5) **Flap repositioning**: Approximate flaps (tension-free), (6) **Sutures**: Interrupted 3-0 silk, primary closure, (7) **Immediate denture** or **surgical stent** (protects, applies pressure). **Denture fabrication**: **6-8 weeks** post-surgery (healing, resorption stabilization). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Excessive bone removal** (reduces ridge height - poor retention), **Perforation** into maxillary sinus/nasal cavity, **Nerve injury** (mental, IAN), **Hemorrhage**, **Delayed healing**.

long_essay.question: What is frenectomy? Describe types and indications.
long_essay.model_answer: **Frenectomy** is **surgical removal** of **frenum** (fold of mucous membrane attaching lip/tongue to underlying structures). **TYPES**: **1. LABIAL FRENECTOMY**: **Maxillary labial** (most common) - between **central incisors** (midline). **Indications**: (a) **Midline diastema** - frenum attachment on incisive papilla prevents closure, (b) **Denture instability** - thick, prominent frenum dislodges denture during lip movement, (c) **Periodontal problem** - frenum pull causes recession. **Procedure**: (a) **Anesthesia** - infiltration, (b) **Hemostat** placed along frenum (crush tissue), (c) **Incision** along both sides of hemostat (diamond/rhomboid shape), (d) **Excise** frenum (including fibrous tissue extending to periosteum), (e) **Blunt dissection** to bone if needed, (f) **Suture** - interrupted (approximate margins). **2. LINGUAL FRENECTOMY** (Frenuloplasty): **Indication**: **Ankyloglossia (tongue-tie)** - restricted tongue movement. **Features**: Inability to protrude tongue beyond incisors, V-shaped tongue tip, speech problems ("l", "r" sounds), difficulty breastfeeding (infants). **Procedure**: (a) **Elevate tongue** - retract upward/forward, (b) **Incision** - midline horizontal incision at frenum base, (c) **Release** attachment to floor of mouth, (d) **Suture** - reposition mucosa (prevent reattachment). **Alternative**: **Z-plasty** (more complex) - prevents scarring/reattachment. **3. BUCCAL FRENECTOMY**: Less common. **Indication**: Frenum pull causes denture instability or gingival recession. **TIMING**: **Labial** - after **orthodontic closure** of diastema (prevents recurrence) OR before denture fabrication. **Lingual** - infancy (if severe feeding problems) or childhood (before speech development). **POST-OPERATIVE**: Analgesics, soft diet, chlorhexidine rinses, suture removal 7 days.

long_essay.question: What is vestibuloplasty? When is it indicated?
long_essay.model_answer: **Vestibuloplasty** is **surgical deepening** of **vestibular sulcus** (space between alveolar ridge and lip/cheek) to **increase denture-bearing area** and improve **denture retention/stability**. **INDICATIONS**: **1. Resorbed alveolar ridge** - severe resorption (long-standing edentulous patient) causes **shallow vestibule**, (2) **Inadequate denture retention** - insufficient sulcus depth for peripheral seal, (3) **High muscle attachments** - muscles attached close to ridge crest (reduce denture stability), (4) **Before implant placement** - create adequate soft tissue coverage. **PRINCIPLES**: **Reposition muscles** (mentalis, buccinator, mylohyoid) **away from ridge crest** (apically/laterally). **Cover denuded bone** with **oral mucosa** or allow **epithelialization**. **TECHNIQUES**: **1. Submucosal vestibuloplasty**: **Incision** at depth of vestibule, **dissect** muscle attachments from bone, **reposition** apically, **secure** with sutures. **Surgical stent** maintains depth during healing. **2. Secondary epithelialization**: **Remove** mucosa from ridge area, expose bone, allow **spontaneous epithelialization** (6-8 weeks). **Stent** essential. **3. Skin/mucosal grafts**: **Split-thickness skin graft** (from thigh) or **palatal graft** to cover denuded area. Better stability. **4. Clark's vestibuloplasty** (mandible): Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap reflected to depth, muscles repositioned, **stent** holds position. **POST-OPERATIVE**: **Surgical stent** worn **continuous 7-14 days** (holds soft tissue position, prevents relapse). **Analgesics**, soft diet. **Denture fabrication** after **6-8 weeks** (tissue maturation). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Loss of depth** (relapse - most common), **Nerve injury** (mental nerve), **Hemorrhage**, **Infection**, **Graft failure**. **ALTERNATIVE**: **Ridge augmentation** (bone grafting) - preferred in many cases (restores bone height rather than just sulcus depth).

long_essay.question: What is maxillary tuberosity reduction? When is it needed?
long_essay.model_answer: **Maxillary tuberosity reduction** is **surgical removal** of **excess bone/soft tissue** from **maxillary tuberosity** (posterior maxilla - distal to last molar). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Enlarged tuberosity**: Excessive vertical height. **2. Insufficient interarch space** - between maxillary tuberosity and mandibular ridge (prevents proper denture fabrication). **Minimum required**: **12-15 mm** for complete dentures. **3. Fibrous hyperplastic tissue** - redundant soft tissue. **4. Unilateral enlargement** - causes uneven occlusal plane. **TYPES OF ENLARGEMENT**: **1. Soft tissue hyperplasia** - fibrous tissue only (easier to remove). **2. Bony enlargement** - excess bone (requires bone removal). **3. Combination** - both bone and soft tissue. **PROCEDURE**: **1. Assessment**: **Radiograph** (PA/OPG) - determine if **bone** or **soft tissue** enlargement. **Measure interarch space** (calipers/models). **2. Anesthesia**: **PSA block** + **Greater palatine nerve block**. **3. Incision**: **Elliptical/wedge incision** on **crest** of tuberosity (remove wedge of soft tissue ± bone). OR **Circumferential incision** around tuberosity. **4. Tissue removal**: **a) Soft tissue only**: Excise with scalpel. **b) Bone involvement**: (i) **Elevate mucoperiosteal flap**, (ii) **Rongeur/bone file** - remove excess bone, (iii) **Smooth** sharp edges, (iv) **Replace flap**. **5. Hemostasis**: Pressure, electrocautery if needed. **6. Sutures**: Interrupted 3-0 silk (approximate margins). **7. Immediate denture/surgical stent**: Apply pressure, protect, guide healing. **PRECAUTIONS**: **1. Preserve blood supply**: Maintain **palatal soft tissue** (blood supply from greater palatine artery). **Avoid excessive removal** - risk necrosis. **2. Avoid maxillary sinus**: **Check radiograph** - sinus may extend into tuberosity. **Perforation** - close primarily, antibiotics. **3. Limit bone removal**: Excessive reduction causes **ridge resorption**, poor denture support. **COMPLICATIONS**: **Hemorrhage** (pterygoid plexus proximity), **Oro-antral communication**, **Excessive reduction**, **Flap necrosis**, **Delayed healing**.

long_essay.question: What is a dentigerous cyst? Describe clinical features and management.
long_essay.model_answer: **Dentigerous (Follicular) cyst** is a **developmental odontogenic cyst** arising from **separation** of **follicle** from **crown** of **unerupted tooth**. **2nd most common** odontogenic cyst (after radicular cyst). **PATHOGENESIS**: Fluid accumulation between **reduced enamel epithelium** and **enamel** surface of unerupted tooth crown. **ASSOCIATION**: **Mandibular 3rd molars** (most common), maxillary canines, maxillary 3rd molars, supernumerary teeth, odontomes. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Age**: **2nd-3rd decade** (eruption age). **2. Asymptomatic** - discovered on routine radiograph (unerupted tooth). **3. Painless swelling** - if large (slow-growing). **4. Egg-shell crackling** - thin bone over large cyst. **5. Tooth displacement** - adjacent teeth displaced by expanding cyst. **6. Tooth non-eruption** - involved tooth fails to erupt. **RADIOGRAPHIC**: **1. Well-defined unilocular radiolucency** - **symmetrically surrounding** crown of unerupted tooth. **2. Radiolucency attached** at **CEJ** (cementoenamel junction) - **pathognomonic**. **3. Sclerotic border** (corticated margin). **4. Tooth displacement** - impacted tooth pushed away. **5. Root resorption** - adjacent teeth (if large). **TYPES (by location)**: **Central** (most common - surrounds crown symmetrically), **Lateral** (along tooth root), **Circumferential** (surrounds entire tooth). **DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS**: **Odontogenic keratocyst** (more aggressive, recurrence), **Ameloblastoma** (multilocular, aggressive), **Hyperplastic dental follicle** (<5mm - not cystic). **HISTOPATHOLOGY**: **Cyst lining** - **non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium** (2-3 cells thick), **fibrous connective tissue** wall. **Chronic inflammation** if secondarily infected. **COMPLICATIONS**: **1. Pathological fracture** (large mandibular cysts). **2. Infection** (rare - becomes radicular cyst). **3. Malignant transformation** - **RARE** (ameloblastoma 0.1%, squamous cell carcinoma - extremely rare). **MANAGEMENT**: **SMALL CYSTS** (<2cm): **Enucleation** + **Extraction** of involved tooth. (1) **Flap** - mucoperiosteal, (2) **Bone removal** - expose cyst, (3) **Enucleate** cyst completely (intact if possible - for histopath), (4) **Extract** associated tooth (usually), (5) **Curettage** cyst cavity (remove lining completely), (6) **Irrigation**, (7) **Primary closure**, (8) **Histopathology** - confirm diagnosis. **LARGE CYSTS** (>2cm, extensive bone involvement): **Marsupialization** first (decompress, reduce size), then **Enucleation** later (staged). **Marsupialization**: Create **surgical window** into cyst, **suture** cyst lining to oral mucosa (wide opening), allows **drainage** and **shrinkage** (6-12 months), then enucleate. **PROGNOSIS**: **Excellent** with complete removal. **Recurrence rare** if properly enucleated.

long_essay.question: What is a radicular cyst? How does it differ from a dentigerous cyst?
long_essay.model_answer: **Radicular (Periapical/Apical) cyst** is an **inflammatory odontogenic cyst** arising from **chronic periapical granuloma** due to **pulp necrosis**. **Most common odontogenic cyst** (60%). **PATHOGENESIS**: **Chronic pulpitis** → **Pulp necrosis** → **Chronic periapical inflammation** → **Stimulation of epithelial rests of Malassez** (in PDL) → **Cyst formation**. **Associated with NON-VITAL tooth** (key difference from dentigerous). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Age**: **3rd-5th decade** (peak). **2. Location**: **Anterior maxilla** most common (especially **maxillary lateral incisor** - due to chronic trauma/pulp necrosis). **3. Asymptomatic** - usually discovered on radiograph. **4. Painless swelling** - if large (slow-growing over months/years). **5. Non-vital tooth** - involved tooth does NOT respond to pulp tests. **6. Discolored tooth** - grayish (necrotic pulp). **RADIOGRAPHIC**: **1. Well-defined round/oval radiolucency** at **root apex**. **2. Lamina dura** (white line around root) **lost**. **3.
