Requires rigid fixation. **HORIZONTAL FAVORABLE**: **Anterior fragment** pulled **upward** by **suprahyoid muscles** (digastric, geniohyoid). **Posterior fragment** pulled **upward** by **elevators** (masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid). **Fracture line** runs **anterior-inferior to posterior-superior** - fragments compress together. **Example**: Angle fracture with oblique line. **HORIZONTAL UNFAVORABLE**: **Fracture line** runs **anterior-superior to posterior-inferior** - muscle pull **separates** fragments (gap formation). **Example**: Body fracture with reverse oblique. **VERTICAL FAVORABLE**: **Medial pterygoid** pull (medial) on posterior fragment does NOT displace (fracture line orientation prevents). **VERTICAL UNFAVORABLE**: **Lateral pterygoid** + **medial pterygoid** pull causes **rotation/displacement** of posterior fragment **medially** and **anteriorly**. **OTHER CLASSIFICATIONS**: **1. Dentate vs Edentulous**: **Edentulous** - atrophic bone, higher fracture risk, different fixation. **2. Simple vs Compound**: **Compound** - communicates with external environment (oral cavity/skin) - higher infection risk, needs antibiotics. **Simple** - closed fracture. **3. Complete vs Incomplete** (greenstick). **4. Comminuted**: Multiple fragments. **5. Pathological**: Through diseased bone (cyst, tumor, osteomyelitis). **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Unfavorable** fractures need **ORIF** (open reduction, internal fixation). **Favorable** may respond to **closed reduction** (IMF).

short_answer.question: Describe Le Fort classification of maxillary fractures.
short_answer.model_answer: **Le Fort classification** - **midface fractures** involving **maxilla**. Based on **predictable lines of weakness**. **KEY FEATURE**: **ALL Le Fort fractures** involve **pterygoid plates** (posterior maxilla) - **pathognomonic**. **LE FORT I** (Horizontal/Floating palate): **Level**: **Low** (above roots of teeth). **Fracture line**: Transverse across **maxilla above apices** of teeth, through **lateral nasal walls**, across **maxillary sinuses**, through **pterygoid plates** (posterior). **Result**: **Entire maxilla** (palate + alveolus) **separates** as single unit ("floating palate"). **Mobility**: **Maxilla** mobile (grasp anterior teeth, move up-down - free-floating). **Nasal bones** intact. **LE FORT II** (Pyramidal): **Level**: **Mid-level**. **Fracture line**: **Pyramid-shaped** - starts at **nasal bridge** (nasofrontal suture), extends **laterally** through **medial orbital walls** (lacrimal bones), **inferior orbital rim**, across **maxillary sinuses**, through **pterygoid plates**. **Result**: **Central maxilla** + **nose** detach as **pyramid-shaped segment**. **Mobility**: **Nose** + **maxilla** move together (grasp nose, move - entire central midface mobile). **Orbital rim** fractured. **LE FORT III** (Craniofacial dysjunction): **Level**: **High** (complete separation of facial skeleton from cranium). **Fracture line**: Across **nasofrontal suture**, through **medial** and **lateral orbital walls** (both), across **zygomatic arches**, through **pterygoid plates**, through **ethmoid sinuses**. **Result**: **ENTIRE FACE** separates from skull base (craniofacial separation). **Mobility**: **Entire midface** mobile (grasp face, moves as unit - "dish-face"). **Most severe**. **CLINICAL SIGNS (Common to all)**: **1. Malocclusion** - teeth don't meet properly (open bite, crossbite). **2. Facial swelling/edema** - extensive. **3. Epistaxis** - nasal bleeding. **4. CSF rhinorrhea** (Le Fort II/III - cribriform plate involvement). **5. Ecchymosis** - periorbital ("raccoon eyes"), subconjunctival hemorrhage. **6. Mobility** - grasp maxilla, move (check level). **7. Step deformity** - palpable at fracture sites (infraorbital rim). **8. Anesthesia** - infraorbital nerve (V2) - cheek, upper lip, nose. **SPECIFIC SIGNS**: **Le Fort I**: Mobile maxilla, **nasal bones stable**. **Le Fort II**: **Bilateral periorbital edema**, **CSF leak** (may), **nasal bones mobile**. **Flattened nasal bridge**. **Le Fort III**: **Massive facial edema**, **"Dish-face"** deformity (flattened midface), **CSF leak** (high risk), **entire face mobile**. **Life-threatening** - airway compromise. **DIAGNOSIS**: **Clinical** + **CT scan** (gold standard - 3D reconstruction shows fracture lines). **MANAGEMENT**: **Stabilize airway** (priority - Le Fort III often needs intubation/tracheostomy), **ORIF** (open reduction, internal fixation with plates/screws), **IMF** (inter-maxillary fixation - 4-6 weeks).

short_answer.question: What are the principles of fracture management? Describe IMF and ORIF.
short_answer.model_answer: **FRACTURE MANAGEMENT PRINCIPLES**: **1. Airway** - ensure patent airway (priority in trauma). **2. Hemorrhage control** - stop bleeding. **3. Soft tissue management** - clean, debride, suture lacerations. **4. Reduction** - restore anatomic position. **5. Fixation/Immobilization** - maintain reduction during healing. **6. Nutrition** - adequate (liquid diet if IMF). **7. Function** - restore occlusion, jaw movement. **8. Esthetics** - restore facial contour. **METHODS OF FIXATION**: **1. CLOSED REDUCTION** (Conservative): **No surgical exposure** of fracture. **Indirect manipulation** to reduce, then immobilize. **INTERMAXILLARY FIXATION (IMF / Maxillomandibular Fixation MMF)**: **Principle**: **Wire/elastics** between **maxillary and mandibular teeth** to **immobilize mandible** using maxilla as stable base. **Indications**: (a) **Favorable fractures** (stable), (b) **Minimally displaced** fractures, (c) **Condylar fractures** (conservative management), (d) **Adjunct to ORIF** (maintain occlusion during healing). **Methods**: **a) Arch bars + wires**: **Arch bars** (metal bars with hooks) **bonded/wired** to teeth (maxilla + mandible). **Intermaxillary wires/elastics** between hooks (fixes jaws together). **Standard** method. **b) Ivy loops**: **Individual wire loops** around teeth (instead of arch bars). **Quicker** but less stable. **c) Ernst ligatures, Stout wires**: Variations. **Duration**: **4-6 weeks** (mandible), **6-8 weeks** (maxilla - slower healing). **Complications**: **Aspiration risk** (vomiting - cannot open mouth), **Weight loss**, **Poor oral hygiene**, **TMJ stiffness**, **Airway compromise**. **Wire cutters** kept with patient always (emergency release). **2. OPEN REDUCTION INTERNAL FIXATION (ORIF)**: **Surgical exposure** of fracture, **anatomic reduction**, **rigid fixation** with **plates and screws** (internal). **Indications**: (a) **Unfavorable** fractures (unstable), (b) **Displaced** fractures, (c) **Multiple** fractures, (d) **Comminuted** fractures, (e) **Edentulous** mandible (no teeth for IMF), (f) **Bilateral** fractures (both sides), (g) **Condylar fractures** with malocclusion/dislocation. **Advantages**: (1) **Rigid fixation** - immediate stability, (2) **Early function** - no/minimal IMF needed, (3) **Better alignment** - direct visualization, (4) **Shorter treatment** - faster return to normal. **Technique**: (a) **Approach** - intraoral or extraoral incision, (b) **Expose fracture** - elevate soft tissue, (c) **Reduce** - align fragments anatomically, (d) **Fix** - **Titanium miniplates + screws** across fracture line. **Champy's principles**: **Load-bearing** (body, angle) - 2 plates. **Tension zone** (superior border), **Compression zone** (inferior border). (e) **Closure** - layer by layer. **Complications**: **Infection** (plate exposure), **Malunion**, **Nerve injury**, **Hardware failure**, **Plate removal** (later if symptomatic). **3. EXTERNAL FIXATION**: **Pins** through skin into bone, connected externally. **Rare** in facial fractures (cosmetic concerns). Used in **comminuted**, **infected**, **gunshot wounds**. **CHOICE**: **Favorable/Simple** → **Closed + IMF**. **Unfavorable/Complex** → **ORIF** (± short IMF).

short_answer.question: What is a zygomatic complex (tripod) fracture? How is it diagnosed and managed?
short_answer.model_answer: **Zygomatic complex (ZMC) / Tripod fracture** - fracture of **zygoma** (cheekbone) with **separation** at **3 sutures**: (1) **Zygomaticofrontal** (lateral orbital rim), (2) **Zygomaticomaxillary** (inferior orbital rim), (3) **Zygomaticotemporal** (zygomatic arch). **Etiology**: **Direct trauma** to cheek - assault (fist/blunt object), RTA, sports. **Zygoma prominence** makes it vulnerable. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Flattening of cheek** - **loss of malar prominence** (depressed zygoma). **2. Periorbital edema/ecchymosis** - swelling, black eye. **3. Subconjunctival hemorrhage** - blood in sclera (no posterior limit - differentiates from localized bruise). **4. Step deformity** - palpable at **infraorbital rim** and **lateral orbital rim** (fracture displacement). **5. Infraorbital nerve anesthesia** - **numbness** of cheek, upper lip, nose, upper teeth (nerve injury in infraorbital canal). **Pathognomonic sign**. **6. Enophthalmos** - **sunken eye** (if orbital floor fractured - orbital volume increased). **Late sign** (after edema subsides). **7. Diplopia** - **double vision** (extraocular muscle entrapment in orbital floor fracture, or edema). **8. Trismus** - limited mouth opening (if arch impinges on coronoid process). **9. Epistaxis** - nasal bleeding (maxillary sinus involvement). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic features. **2. Radiographs**: **Waters view** (occipitomental) - best for zygoma. **Shows**: (a) **Opacification** of maxillary sinus (blood), (b) **Fracture lines** at sutures, (c) **Depressed arch**. **3. CT scan** - **gold standard** (3D reconstruction - exact displacement, orbital floor involvement). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Persistent enophthalmos**, **Persistent diplopia** (muscle entrapment), **Facial asymmetry** (malunion), **Infraorbital anesthesia** (may be permanent). **MANAGEMENT**: **OBSERVATION** (No treatment): **Indications**: (a) **Minimal/no displacement**, (b) **No functional impairment** (vision, occlusion), (c) **No esthetic concern**. **Monitor** - most heal uneventfully. **SURGICAL REDUCTION**: **Indications**: (a) **Significant displacement** (cosmetic deformity), (b) **Enophthalmos** (>2mm), (c) **Persistent diplopia** (>2 weeks), (d) **Trismus**, (e) **Large orbital floor defect**. **Timing**: **Early** (within 7-14 days - before healing/fibrosis). **Techniques**: (a) **Closed reduction**: **Gillies approach** - temporal incision, **elevator** under zygomatic arch, **lift** zygoma (pop back into place). **No fixation**. Simple fractures only. (b) **Open reduction**: **Incisions** (intraoral + lower eyelid OR coronal), **expose fracture** sites, **reduce** anatomically, **fix** with **miniplates** at 2-3 points (zygomaticomaxillary buttress, infraorbital rim, zygomaticofrontal). **Orbital floor reconstruction** if large defect (implant - titanium mesh/porous polyethylene). **Post-op**: **Antibiotics** (sinus communication), **Decongestants** (nasal), **Avoid nose blowing**, **Eye care** (if eyelid swelling).

short_answer.question: What is the management of condylar fractures?
short_answer.model_answer: **Condylar fractures** - **2nd most common** mandibular fracture (25%). **Bilateral** common (falls on chin - force transmitted to both condyles). **TYPES**: **1. Intracapsular** - fracture within TMJ capsule (condylar head). **2. Condylar neck** - below head, above sigmoid notch. **3. Subcondylar** - below sigmoid notch (most common). **DISPLACEMENT**: **Non-displaced/minimally displaced** vs **Displaced** vs **Dislocated** (complete loss of contact). **GENERAL PRINCIPLE**: **MOST condylar fractures** managed **CONSERVATIVELY** (non-surgical). **INDICATIONS FOR CONSERVATIVE MANAGEMENT** (90%): **1. Non-displaced/minimally displaced** fractures. **2. Bilateral fractures** with **acceptable occlusion**. **3. Condylar neck fractures** (below head). **4. Children** (excellent remodeling potential - almost always conservative). **5. Edentulous patients**. **6. Unilateral** with normal occlusion. **CONSERVATIVE TREATMENT**: **1. Short-term IMF** (if needed): **7-14 days** only (prevent ankylosis - TMJ stiffness). **Training elastics** (light - guide occlusion, allow some movement). OR **No IMF** if occlusion acceptable. **2. Soft diet**: 4-6 weeks. **3. Early mobilization**: **Jaw exercises** start after **1-2 weeks** (crucial - prevent ankylosis, restore function). **Active** opening/closing, lateral movements. **4. Analgesics**: NSAIDs. **5. Follow-up**: Monitor occlusion, mouth opening, healing (radiographs). **OUTCOMES**: **Excellent** remodeling, functional recovery. **INDICATIONS FOR SURGICAL (ORIF)** (10%): **ABSOLUTE**: **1. Bilateral fractures** with **malocclusion** (anterior open bite - condyles telescoped). **2. Condyle displaced into middle cranial fossa** (intracranial). **3. Foreign body** in joint. **4. Lateral extracapsular dislocation** (condyle outside fossa laterally). **RELATIVE**: **1. Severe displacement** (>45° angulation, >1cm overlap). **2. Inability to achieve occlusion** conservatively. **3. Associated midface fractures** (need stable mandible for MMF). **SURGICAL APPROACHES**: **Preauricular**, **Submandibular**, **Retromandibular**, **Endoscopic**. **Fix** with miniplate. **COMPLICATIONS**: **1. Ankylosis** - especially if prolonged IMF (>2-3 weeks). **Prevention**: Early mobilization. **2. Malocclusion** - managed with occlusal adjustment, orthodontics. **3. Facial nerve injury** (surgical cases - temporary usually). **4. TMJ dysfunction** - pain, clicking (10-20% - usually resolves). **5. Growth disturbance** (children - rare with modern conservative management). **KEY**: **Children** - almost **NEVER** operate (excellent remodeling). **Adults** - **conservative** unless absolute indication.

short_answer.question: What is sialolithiasis? Describe clinical features and management.
short_answer.model_answer: **Sialolithiasis** - **salivary calculus (stone)** in ductal system of salivary gland. **Most common salivary gland disorder**. **LOCATION**: **Submandibular gland/duct** (80-90%) - **Wharton's duct**. **Reasons**: (1) **Upward flow** (against gravity), (2) **Thick alkaline** secretion, (3) **Higher calcium/phosphate** content, (4) **Long tortuous** duct (12 cm). **Parotid** (5-20%), **Sublingual** (rare). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Intermittent painful swelling** - **pathognomonic**. **Swelling** of gland **during meals** (saliva production), **subsides** after meal (1-2 hrs). **"Mealtime syndrome"**. **2. Palpable stone** - **bimanual palpation** (submandibular duct - floor of mouth). **Hard, immobile** mass along duct. **3. Reduced salivary flow** - thick/absent saliva from duct orifice (milk duct). **4. Tenderness** - over gland/duct. **5. Secondary infection** - if complete obstruction → **acute sialadenitis** (fever, swelling, pus from duct). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - mealtime swelling + palpable stone. **2. Radiographs**: **Occlusal radiograph** (80% stones radiopaque - calcium). **Sialography** (contrast in duct - shows stone as **filling defect**) - **AVOID if acute infection** (spread infection). **3. Ultrasound** - detects radiolucent stones, ductal dilation. **4. Sialendoscopy** - endoscopic visualization + removal. **MANAGEMENT**: **MEDICAL** (Small stones <3mm, distal duct): **1. Conservative**: (a) **Hydration** - increase saliva flow, (b) **Sialogogues** - lemon juice, sour candies (stimulate saliva), (c) **Warm compresses** + **Massage** - milk stone distally, (d) **NSAIDs** - pain/inflammation, (e) **Antibiotics** (if infection) - **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate** (covers *S. aureus, Streptococcus*). **2. Stone milking** - **bimanual massage** (push stone anteriorly toward orifice, express). **SURGICAL**: **1. Intraoral removal** (Distal duct stones - palpable in floor of mouth): (a) **Anesthesia** (local), (b) **Dilate duct** orifice, (c) **Incision** over stone (longitudinal - along duct), (d) **Remove stone**, (e) **Marsupialize** duct (suture duct edges to mucosa - create wide opening, prevent stricture). **Do NOT suture duct** closed (stenosis risk). **2. Sialendoscopy** - endoscopic **basket retrieval** or **lithotripsy** (break stone). **Minimally invasive**. **3. Gland excision** - **Submandibular gland removal** if: (a) **Recurrent** sialolithiasis, (b) **Intraglandular** stone (within gland), (c) **Chronic sialadenitis** (gland damaged). **Risks**: **Lingual nerve injury**, **Hypoglossal nerve injury**, **Marginal mandibular nerve** (facial nerve branch - lip droop). **PREVENTION**: Hydration, good oral hygiene.

short_answer.question: What is a ranula? Describe types and management.
short_answer.model_answer: **Ranula** - **mucous extravasation pseudocyst** arising from **sublingual gland** (floor of mouth). **Bluish, translucent swelling** ("frog belly" appearance - Latin *rana* = frog). **TYPES**: **1. SIMPLE (Oral) RANULA**: **Confined** to **floor of mouth** (above mylohyoid muscle). **Clinical features**: (a) **Painless, soft, fluctuant** swelling in **floor of mouth** (one side of frenulum), (b) **Bluish/translucent** color (saliva visible through thin mucosa - pathognomonic), (c) **Displaces tongue** upward/laterally (if large), (d) **No pain** (unless infected). **2. PLUNGING (Cervical) RANULA**: **Extends** through mylohyoid muscle into **neck** (submandibular/submental space). **"Plunges"** below mylohyoid. **Clinical features**: (a) **Neck swelling** (submandibular region), (b) **Soft, fluctuant**, non-tender, (c) **May** have oral component (floor of mouth swelling) OR **only neck** swelling, (d) **Differential**: Dermoid cyst, lymphangioma, cystic hygroma, branchial cyst. **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic bluish oral swelling (floor of mouth). **2. Aspiration** - **thick, ropy, mucoid** fluid (saliva - viscous, egg-white consistency). **Differential from epidermoid** (cheesy material). **3. MRI/CT** - plunging ranula (extent, relation to mylohyoid). **4. NO histopathology** - **pseudocyst** (no epithelial lining - just mucus pool in connective tissue). **MANAGEMENT**: **SIMPLE RANULA**: **1. Observation** - may spontaneously resolve (especially small, recent). **2. Marsupialization**: (a) **Elliptical excision** of cyst roof (create wide opening into mouth), (b) **Suture** cyst lining edges to oral mucosa, (c) **Simple**, outpatient procedure. **Limitation**: **High recurrence** (50%) - gland continues secreting. **3. Excision of sublingual gland**: **Definitive treatment** - **remove entire sublingual gland** (source of saliva). (a) **Intraoral approach**, (b) **Excise sublingual gland** completely (along with ranula), (c) **Low recurrence** (5-10%). **Risks**: **Lingual nerve injury** (runs close - numbness of tongue), **Wharton's duct injury** (submandibular duct - runs through floor of mouth). **Preferred** for recurrent cases. **PLUNGING RANULA**: **Sublingual gland excision** via **intraoral** approach (addresses source). **Neck dissection** usually **not needed** (ranula collapses once gland removed). **Alternative**: **Combined intraoral + neck approach** if large cervical component. **RECURRENCE**: **Marsupialization** 50%, **Gland excision** 5-10%.

short_answer.question: How do you manage dental extractions in patients with bleeding disorders?
short_answer.model_answer: **PRE-OPERATIVE ASSESSMENT**: **1. History**: **Type of bleeding disorder**, **Severity**, **Previous bleeding episodes** (dental/surgical), **Medications** (anticoagulants, antiplatelets). **2. Laboratory tests**: **Hemophilia**: **Factor VIII** (Hemophilia A) or **Factor IX** (Hemophilia B) levels. **Anticoagulants**: **INR** (warfarin), **aPTT** (heparin). **General**: **Platelet count**, **PT**, **Bleeding time**. **3. Physician consultation**: Coordinate with hematologist/physician. **HEMOPHILIA**: **Factor replacement** essential. **1. Mild Hemophilia A** (Factor VIII 5-25%): **Desmopressin (DDAVP)** - releases stored Factor VIII (2-3x increase). **Dose**: 0.3 mcg/kg IV **1 hr before** procedure. **Alternative**: **Tranexamic acid** (antifibrinolytic) alone. **2. Moderate/Severe Hemophilia** (Factor VIII <5%): **Factor VIII concentrate** - raise level to **50-100%** before extraction (target depends on extent). **Dose**: **Loading**: 25-50 IU/kg pre-op, **Maintenance**: Continue post-op (7-10 days). **Hemophilia B**: **Factor IX concentrate** (similar protocol). **3. Local measures** (crucial): (a) **Atraumatic extraction** - minimize trauma, (b) **Sutures** - **primary closure** (figure-of-eight over socket), (c) **Hemostatic agents**: **Oxidized cellulose (Surgicel)**, **Gelfoam**, **Fibrin glue** - pack socket, (d) **Tranexamic acid** mouthwash - **5% TXA** rinse QID x 7 days (prevents clot lysis), (e) **Avoid** aspirin, NSAIDs (use paracetamol). **4. Hospital setting** - NOT outpatient (need monitoring, factor availability). **WARFARIN (Anticoagulation)**: **1. Check INR**: **Therapeutic range** (2-3) - **safe** for simple extractions with local measures. **INR 2.5-3.5** - acceptable for minor oral surgery. **INR >4** - **defer**, adjust warfarin (consult physician). **2. DO NOT STOP warfarin** (risk of thromboembolism - stroke, MI). **Exception**: Major surgery (consult physician - may bridge with heparin). **3. Local hemostatic measures**: (a) **Minimize trauma** - atraumatic technique, **limit extractions** (1-2 teeth per visit), (b) **Sutures** - resorbable, primary closure, (c) **Hemostatic agents** - **Surgicel**, **Gelfoam** pack socket, (d) **Tranexamic acid** mouthwash - **10 mL 5% TXA** rinse **QID x 5-7 days** (do NOT swallow - hold 2 min, spit). **VERY effective**. (e) **Pressure** - bite gauze 30 min. **4. Antibiotics**: **Amoxicillin** (reduce infection - infected socket bleeds more). **5. Post-op instructions**: **No rinsing** 24 hrs, **soft diet**, **avoid hot liquids**, **no alcohol** (interacts with warfarin). **6. Follow-up**: Day 1, day 3 (check bleeding). **ASPIRIN/CLOPIDOGREL**: **Continue medication** (DO NOT STOP - thrombotic risk). **Local measures** sufficient for simple extractions. **Clopidogrel** slightly higher bleeding risk than aspirin. **THROMBOCYTOPENIA**: **Platelet count <50,000** - **defer** elective extraction. **<50,000** - **platelet transfusion** pre-op (raise to >50,000). **LOCAL MEASURES** (universal - all bleeding disorders): Atraumatic technique, sutures, hemostatic packing, **tranexamic acid mouthwash** (CRITICAL), pressure, post-op care.

short_answer.question: Describe the phases of wound healing.
short_answer.model_answer: **Wound healing** - complex process restoring tissue integrity. **TYPES**: **Primary intention** - wound edges approximated (sutured) - minimal scarring. **Secondary intention** - wound left open (granulation fills defect) - more scarring. **Tertiary intention** (delayed primary) - initially open, closed later. **PHASES (Overlapping)**: **1. HEMOSTASIS (Immediate - minutes)**: **Vasoconstriction** → **Platelet aggregation** → **Clot formation** (fibrin mesh). **Prevents blood loss**, **scaffolding** for cell migration. **2. INFLAMMATORY PHASE (0-3 days)**: **a) Early (0-24 hrs)**: **Vasodilation** → **Increased permeability** → **Edema**, **erythema**, **pain**. **Neutrophils** arrive (phagocytose bacteria, debris) - peak 24 hrs. **b) Late (24-72 hrs)**: **Macrophages** arrive (phagocytose debris, **secrete growth factors** - crucial for healing progression). **Lymphocytes** (immune response). **Clinical**: **Swelling, redness, warmth, pain, loss of function** (cardinal signs of inflammation). **Prolonged inflammation** → **chronic wound**, **delayed healing**. **3. PROLIFERATIVE PHASE (3 days - 3 weeks)**: **Tissue formation**. **a) Granulation tissue formation** (3-5 days): **Fibroblasts** proliferate → **collagen synthesis** (Type III initially). **Angiogenesis** → **new capillaries** (pink, granular appearance). **Myofibroblasts** → **wound contraction**. **b) Epithelialization** (24-48 hrs onwards): **Keratinocytes** migrate across wound (from edges) → **cover defect**. **Faster** in **primary intention** (sutured - edges close). **c) Contraction**: **Myofibroblasts** pull wound edges together (reduces defect size - important in **secondary intention**). **4. REMODELING/MATURATION PHASE (3 weeks - 1 year)**: **Collagen remodeling**: **Type III collagen** replaced by **Type I** (stronger). **Collagen** reorganized along **tension lines**. **Scar maturation**: Initially **red, raised** → gradually **pale, flat** (12-18 months). **Tensile strength** increases (plateaus at **80%** of original - never 100%). **Excess collagen** breakdown → **scar softens**. **FACTORS AFFECTING HEALING**: **Local**: Infection, blood supply, foreign body, wound tension, hematoma. **Systemic**: **Diabetes** (impaired), **Immunosuppression** (steroids, chemotherapy), **Malnutrition** (protein, Vitamin C deficiency), **Age** (elderly slower), **Smoking** (vasoconstriction). **NORMAL TIMELINE**: **Epithelialization** complete: **7-10 days** (oral mucosa faster - 3-5 days). **Suture removal**: **7 days** (face/oral), **10-14 days** (skin elsewhere). **Scar maturation**: **12-18 months**.

short_answer.question: Describe principles of suturing and commonly used suture materials.
short_answer.model_answer: **PRINCIPLES OF SUTURING**: **1. Aseptic technique** - sterile instruments, gloves. **2. Tissue handling**: **Gentle** - avoid crushing (tissue forceps with teeth, not hemostats). **3. Hemostasis**: **Control bleeding** before closure (pressure, cautery). **4. Dead space elimination**: **Close in layers** (prevent hematoma/seroma). **5. Eversion**: Wound edges **slightly everted** (not inverted) - better healing, less scar depression. **6. Tension-free**: **No tension** on wound edges (undermining if needed). **7. Accurate approximation**: **Align tissue layers** properly. **8. Knot security**: **Square knots** (surgeon's knot if tension). **SUTURE MATERIALS**: **ABSORBABLE** (degraded by body - no removal needed): **1. Catgut** (Plain/Chromic): **Natural** (collagen from sheep/cattle intestine). **Rapid absorption** (plain 7-10 days, chromic 14-21 days). **High tissue reaction**. **Rarely used** now. **2. Polyglycolic acid (Vicryl)**: **Synthetic**. **Absorption**: 60-90 days. **Moderate tissue reaction**. **Uses**: **Deep layers**, muscle, subcutaneous (where removal difficult). **3. Polyglactin (Vicryl Rapide)**: **Faster absorption** (10-14 days). **Uses**: **Oral mucosa** (intraoral - no removal needed). **NON-ABSORBABLE** (must be removed): **1. Silk**: **Natural** (braided). **Easy handling**, good knot security. **High tissue reaction** (inflammation). **Uses**: **Intraoral/skin** (extraoral) - **MOST COMMON** in oral surgery. **Removal**: **7 days**. **2. Nylon (Ethilon)**: **Synthetic** (monofilament). **Low tissue reaction**. **Less memory** (harder to tie). **Uses**: Skin closure (face). **3. Polypropylene (Prolene)**: **Synthetic** (monofilament). **Very low reaction**. **High memory** (springs back). **Uses**: Skin, cardiovascular. **4. Stainless steel**: **Very strong**, **inert**. **Difficult handling**. **Uses**: Bone wiring, tendon repair. **NEEDLE TYPES**: **Cutting** (triangular - cuts through tissue) - **SKIN, oral mucosa** (tough tissue). **Reverse cutting** (preferred - strength at wound edge). **Round (Tapered)** - muscle, viscera (soft tissue). **SIZE**: **Oral surgery**: **3-0** or **4-0** silk (moderate thickness). **Finer** (5-0, 6-0) for delicate (facial skin). **Thicker** (2-0, 0) for heavy tissue. **SUTURING TECHNIQUES**: **1. Simple interrupted**: **Individual knots** - each suture independent. **Most common**, versatile. **Advantage**: One fails, others hold. **2. Continuous (Running)**: **Single thread**, multiple passes, **one knot** at each end. **Faster**. **Disadvantage**: One break, entire suture fails. **Uses**: Long incisions. **3. Horizontal mattress**: **Everting** suture (good for edges). **Distributes tension**. **4. Vertical mattress**: **Deep + superficial** bites (two-layer closure in one). **Eversion**. **5. Figure-of-eight**: **Hemostatic** (compresses tissue). **Uses**: Extraction sockets, bleeding. **REMOVAL**: **Oral/face**: **7 days**. **Elsewhere**: **10-14 days**. **Absorbable**: **No removal** (falls off/dissolves).

short_answer.question: Classify nerve injuries. How do you assess and manage paresthesia?
short_answer.model_answer: **NERVE INJURY CLASSIFICATION (Seddon)**: **1. NEUROPRAXIA**: **Mildest**. **Conduction block** (axon intact, myelin damaged). **No Wallerian degeneration**. **Recovery**: **Complete**, **spontaneous** (days-weeks). **Prognosis**: Excellent. **2. AXONOTMESIS**: **Moderate**. **Axon disrupted**, **endoneurium intact** (nerve sheath preserved). **Wallerian degeneration** occurs distal to injury. **Regeneration**: **Yes** (axons regrow along intact sheath - **1mm/day** / **1 inch/month**). **Recovery**: **Partial to complete** (weeks-months). **Prognosis**: Good (80-90% recovery). **3. NEUROTMESIS**: **Severe**. **Complete transection** (axon + endoneurium + perineurium). **Wallerian degeneration**. **Regeneration**: **Poor** (axons cannot find pathway - **neuroma** formation, misdirection). **Recovery**: **Minimal** without surgical repair. **Prognosis**: Poor unless **microsurgical repair**. **DENTAL SURGERY NERVE INJURIES**: **Inferior alveolar nerve (IAN)**: 3rd molar extraction, implants, mandibular fractures. **Lingual nerve**: 3rd molar extraction (lingual flap elevation), lingual anesthesia. **Incidence**: **Temporary** 0.5-5%, **Permanent** 0.1-1%. **ASSESSMENT OF PARESTHESIA**: **1. History**: **Onset** (immediate post-op vs delayed), **Duration**, **Progression** (improving/static/worsening), **Nature** (numbness, tingling, dysesthesia - abnormal sensation). **2. Clinical tests**: **a) Two-point discrimination**: **Normal**: 2-4mm (lower lip/tongue). **Impaired**: >6mm. **b) Light touch**: Cotton wisp/brush - **detect sensation**. **c) Pin-prick**: **Sharp/dull** discrimination. **d) Thermal**: Hot/cold discrimination. **3. Map extent**: Mark borders of anesthetic/paresthetic area (compare to normal side). **4. Baseline documentation**: **Immediate post-op** assessment (medico-legal). **5. Serial assessment**: **1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months** (track recovery). **MANAGEMENT**: **IMMEDIATE (If injury suspected intra-op)**: **1. Inform patient** (document consent discussion). **2. Primary repair** (if complete transection visualized - rare) - **microsurgical** epineurial sutures. **POST-OPERATIVE**: **1. Reassurance**: **70-90%** spontaneous recovery (most are neuropraxia/axonotmesis). **Majority recover** in **3-6 months**. **2. Observation**: **First 3 months** - natural recovery expected. **Serial assessments**. **3. Pharmacotherapy** (controversial efficacy): **Vitamin B12** (Methylcobalamin 1500 mcg) - supports nerve regeneration. **Gabapentin/Pregabalin** - if neuropathic pain. **Corticosteroids** (Dexamethasone) - early (within 48 hrs) if severe injury (reduce edema) - **controversial**. **4. Microsurgical repair**: **Indications**: (a) **Complete transection** (known intra-op), (b) **No recovery** at **3-6 months** (likely neurotmesis), (c) **Worsening** paresthesia. **Timing**: **3-6 months** optimal (too early - may recover spontaneously, too late - distal nerve atrophy). **Technique**: **Direct repair** (if gap <5mm) or **Nerve graft** (if larger gap). **Prognosis**: Variable (30-70% improvement). **PREVENTION**: **Pre-operative CBCT** (assess IAN proximity in 3rd molar cases), **Careful technique**, **Lingual plate preservation**, **Avoid excessive force**.

short_answer.question: What are the indications for tracheostomy? Describe the procedure.
short_answer.model_answer: **Tracheostomy** - surgical creation of opening into **trachea** (through anterior neck) for airway access. **Temporary** (tube removable) or **Permanent**. **INDICATIONS**: **1. Upper airway obstruction**: (a) **Maxillofacial trauma** - bilateral mandibular fractures, midface fractures (tongue fall-back, bleeding), (b) **Ludwig's angina** - floor of mouth swelling → airway compromise, (c) **Tumors** - oral/pharyngeal/laryngeal (obstructing airway), (d) **Burns** - inhalational injury, facial burns, (e) **Angioedema** - severe allergic reaction. **2. Prolonged intubation**: **>7-10 days** mechanical ventilation (ICU patients) - **prevents laryngeal damage** from endotracheal tube. **3. Inability to intubate**: **Difficult airway** (limited mouth opening, anatomical anomalies). **4. Pulmonary toilet**: Facilitate **suctioning** of secretions (neuromuscular disorders, coma). **5. Prophylactic**: Before **major head-neck surgery** (airway edema expected post-op). **TYPES**: **Emergency** (cricothyrotomy - faster) vs **Elective** (tracheostomy - preferred if time permits). **PROCEDURE (Elective tracheostomy)**: **1. Position**: **Supine**, **neck extended** (shoulder roll), **head midline**. **2. Anesthesia**: **General** (preferred) or **Local** with sedation (awake tracheostomy). **3. Landmarks**: **Thyroid cartilage** (laryngeal prominence), **Cricoid cartilage** (below thyroid), **Tracheal rings** (below cricoid), **Sternal notch**. **4. Incision**: **Transverse** (2-3 cm) at level of **2nd-3rd tracheal ring** (midway between cricoid and sternal notch). **Alternative**: **Vertical** midline incision (faster - emergency). **5. Dissection**: (a) Divide **subcutaneous tissue**, **platysma** (muscle), (b) **Retract** strap muscles (sternohyoid, sternothyroid) **laterally**, (c) **Divide thyroid isthmus** (suture-ligate or retract superiorly) - exposes trachea. **6. Tracheal incision**: (a) **Identify tracheal rings** (palpate), (b) **Vertical incision** through **2nd-3rd rings** (inferiorly based flap OR cruciate incision OR window excision), (c) **Avoid 1st ring** (subglottic stenosis risk). **7. Tube insertion**: (a) **Dilate** tracheal opening (tracheal dilator), (b) Insert **tracheostomy tube** (cuffed, appropriate size - adult 7-9mm), (c) **Inflate cuff** (seal airway), (d) **Confirm**: Bilateral air entry (auscultation), **Capnography** (CO₂ detection). **8. Secure tube**: **Sutures** to skin (stay sutures through trachea for emergency re-insertion if displaced), **Ties** around neck (snug - 2 fingers space). **9. Dressing**: Sterile gauze around stoma. **COMPLICATIONS**: **Intra-operative**: Hemorrhage (thyroid vessels), **apnea** (hypercapnic patient - loss of respiratory drive), tube misplacement. **Early post-op**: **Tube obstruction** (secretions, blood), **accidental decannulation** (first 3-5 days dangerous - tract not formed, difficult re-insertion), **subcutaneous emphysema**, pneumothorax. **Late**: **Tracheal stenosis** (1st ring injury, high cuff pressure), **tracheoesophageal fistula**, granulation tissue. **POST-OP CARE**: **Humidified oxygen** (prevent secretion drying), **Regular suctioning**, **Tube change** (first change 5-7 days after tract established, then weekly), **Stoma care** (cleaning).

short_answer.question: Describe the extracranial course and branches of the facial nerve.
short_answer.model_answer: **Facial nerve (CN VII)** - **motor nerve** to muscles of **facial expression** + parasympathetic to salivary/lacrimal glands + taste (anterior 2/3 tongue via chorda tympani). **EXTRACRANIAL COURSE**: **1. Exit from skull**: Emerges from **stylomastoid foramen** (between styloid and mastoid processes). **2. Enters parotid gland**: Immediately enters **parotid gland** (runs through gland - **NOT supplies parotid** - supplied by glossopharyngeal CN IX). **3. Division**: Divides into **two main trunks** within parotid: **Temporofacial** (upper) and **Cervicofacial** (lower). **4. Further branching**: Forms **5 main terminal branches** (pes anserinus - "goose foot" pattern). **TERMINAL BRANCHES (memory: "**T**o **Z**anzibar **B**y **M**otor **C**ar")**: **1. TEMPORAL (Frontal)**: **Course**: Crosses **zygomatic arch** (superficial - vulnerable). **Supplies**: **Frontalis** (raises eyebrows), **orbicularis oculi** (upper part - eye closure), corrugator. **Injury**: **Inability to raise eyebrow**, wrinkle forehead. **At risk**: Trauma to temporal region, **parotidectomy**, **facelift surgery**. **2. ZYGOMATIC**: **Course**: Crosses **zygoma**. **Supplies**: **Orbicularis oculi** (lower part - eye closure). **Injury**: **Incomplete eye closure**, lagophthalmos (corneal exposure risk). **3. BUCCAL**: **Course**: Crosses **masseter muscle** (over buccal region). **Multiple branches** (interconnecting). **Supplies**: **Buccinator**, orbicularis oris (upper lip), muscles of nose. **Injury**: **Loss of smile** (upper lip), inability to puff cheeks (blow air). **At risk**: **Parotidectomy**, facial trauma. **LEAST serious** branch injury (multiple interconnections - compensation). **4. MARGINAL MANDIBULAR**: **Course**: Runs along **inferior border of mandible** (may dip **below** mandible up to 1 cm in 20% cases - danger zone). Crosses **facial vessels**. **Supplies**: **Lower lip** depressors (depressor labii inferioris, depressor anguli oris), mentalis. **Injury**: **Most CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT** - **asymmetric smile** (lower lip doesn't move down on affected side - "**lip droop**"), inability to show lower teeth, deviation of mouth to normal side. **At risk**: **Submandibular gland surgery**, **neck dissections**, **mandibular angle/body fracture surgery**, facelift. **5. CERVICAL**: **Course**: Runs **inferiorly** into neck. **Supplies**: **Platysma** (neck muscle). **Injury**: **Minimal clinical significance** (platysma not essential). **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Parotid gland surgery** - nerve runs through gland (risk of injury to all branches). **Mandibular fractures/surgery** - marginal mandibular branch vulnerable. **Temporal artery biopsy** - temporal branch at risk. **PROTECTION during surgery**: **Identify nerve**, gentle tissue handling, **avoid cautery** near nerve.

short_answer.question: What are the essential elements of informed consent for oral surgery?
short_answer.model_answer: **Informed consent** - **legal and ethical** requirement before any surgical procedure. Patient must **understand** and **voluntarily agree** to treatment after receiving adequate information. **ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS**: **1. COMPETENCE**: Patient must be **legally competent** (adult >18 years, sound mind). **Exceptions**: **Minors** (<18 years) - **parental consent** required. **Emergency** - implied consent (save life). **Incompetent patient** - guardian/next-of-kin consent. **2. DISCLOSURE OF INFORMATION**: **a) Diagnosis**: Patient's current condition (e.g., impacted tooth, abscess). **b) Proposed treatment**: Procedure planned (e.g., surgical extraction, I&D), **Alternative treatments** (e.g., observation, antibiotics), **Option of no treatment** (consequences). **c) Risks and complications**: **Common**: Pain, swelling, bleeding, infection, dry socket, trismus. **Serious** (even if rare): **Nerve injury** (IAN/lingual - numbness), **jaw fracture**, **sinus perforation**, **damage to adjacent teeth**, **TMJ problems**. **d) Benefits**: Expected outcome (pain relief, infection control, prevent complications). **e) Prognosis**: Expected recovery time, success rate. **3. UNDERSTANDING**: Patient must **comprehend** information (language appropriate, layman's terms - avoid medical jargon). **Verify understanding**: Ask patient to **repeat** key points. **Written + verbal** explanation (consent form + discussion). **4. VOLUNTARINESS**: Decision made **freely** without coercion, pressure, or undue influence. **5. AUTHORIZATION**: Patient **signs consent form** (written documentation). **DOCUMENTATION**: **1. Consent form**: **Signed**, **dated**, **witnessed**. **Contains**: Procedure name, risks disclosed, patient signature, doctor signature, witness. **2. Medical records**: **Note in records** - "Informed consent obtained, risks explained (nerve injury, bleeding, infection, dry socket, jaw fracture, sinus perforation), patient understood, all questions answered". **Specific to procedure** (e.g., for 3rd molar - mention IAN injury risk with percentage). **SPECIAL SITUATIONS**: **Emergency**: **Implied consent** (unconscious patient, life-threatening - no time for consent). **Document**: "Emergency treatment, unable to obtain consent, acted in patient's best interest". **Minors**: **Parental consent**. **Exception**: **Mature minor** (court-determined competence) or **emancipated minor** (married, self-supporting). **Language barrier**: **Interpreter** (document interpreter used). **WITHDRAWAL OF CONSENT**: Patient can **withdraw consent** at any time (even during procedure if conscious). Must **stop procedure** (unless emergency). **MEDICO-LEGAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Failure** to obtain informed consent = **BATTERY** (illegal touching) - grounds for lawsuit even if treatment successful. **Inadequate disclosure** = **NEGLIGENCE**. **Protection**: **Proper consent** + **documentation** protects against litigation. **WHAT TO DOCUMENT**: "**Discussed**: Procedure, alternatives, risks (bleeding, infection, nerve injury, dry socket), benefits, prognosis. **Patient verbalized understanding**, **all questions answered**, **consent form signed**".

short_answer.question: Describe General.
short_answer.model_answer:
A suture is a strand or thread of material used to approximate tissues and
also to ligate blood vessels.
Thus, Suturing is the act of sewing or bringing tissues or flap edges together
and holding them in apposition until normal healing takes place.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Monofilament & Multifilament.
short_answer.model_answer:
1> Absorbable Sutures
These are suture materials that are digested or hydrotysed by the enzymes
present in the body. Thus they require no removal from the surgical site.
a> Natural - Catgut
b> Synthetic - Polyglycolic acid
2> Non-Absarbable Sutures
These materials can not be metabolised by the body's natural mechanism
therefore they should be removed by the surgeon at the end of healing.
a> Natural - Silk, linen
b> Synthetic - Nylon, polypropylene
3> Monofilament
They consist of single strand of suture material
Advantages - monofilament sutures are more smooth and strong
- they do not allow any bacteria to survive.
Disadvantages — monofilament sutures can't be handled well like
multifilament sutures
- They have to be handled properly and delicately
without any damage to the strand during surgical
procedures to avoid any breakage past operatively.
4> Multifilament
They consist of several filaments braided or twisted together.
Advantages - Multifilament sutures are easier to handle and tie
than monofilament sutures
Disadvantages - They can harbour bacteria and are not suitable
in the presence of contamination and infection.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on PRINCIPLES OF SUTURING.
short_answer.model_answer:
The needle should be grasped with the help of needle holders at
approximately 3/4 th of its distance from the tip of needle.
The needle should never be held at the suture end as it is the weakest
of breakage of the needle.
point of the needle and grasping at this point results in either bending
The needle should pierce the tissue perpendicular to its surface as
piercing the tissue obliquely may result in a tear.
The curved needles should be passed through the tissues following the
curvature of the needle to prevent tearing of the tissue.
The suture should be placed equidistant (2-3 mm) from the incision
line. The depth of the penetration should also be equal on both sides of the
line.
The needle should be passed from mobile tissue to fixed tissue
When one side of the tissue is thinner than the other side, then the needle
should pass from thinner to thicker side.
Similarly, when one side is deeper & other side is superficial, the needle
should pass through the deeper to the superficial line side.
The suture should not be tied so tightly that it results in blanching
of the tissues. The suture should just approximate the wound margins
The knot should not be placed over the wound margins.
Each suture should be placed 3-4 mm apart. The spacing between the
sutures depends upon the type of Tissues which are approximated.
When sutures are placed upon areas of underlying muscular activity
they should be clase to each other.
When length of Tissue on one side of the suture wound is longer than
the other, suturing it would result in 'dog-ear' formation.
In order to avoid it, the excessive Tissue should be undermined and an
incision at 30° to original incision is directed at the undermined
tissue.
The extra tissue is pulled over the incision, the appropriate amount
is excised, and wound is clased.

short_answer.question: Describe Clasure of extraction sites &.
short_answer.model_answer:
Oro-antral fistula is a pathological comminunication between oral cavity and
maxillary sinus.
Immediate aro-antral fistula usually donot have any epithelial lining but
chronic aro-antral fistula are epithelialised.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Malignancy involving maxillary sinus.
short_answer.model_answer:
Removal of a large cyst or resection of large tumor involving maxilla.
CLINICAL FEATURES — ACUTE ORO-ANTRAL FISTULA
Fluid regurgitation and escape of air from nastrils
Due to infection, it results in sinusitis ,so all signs and symptoms of sinusitis
are present.

short_answer.question: Describe the procedure for CHRONIC ORO-ANTRAL FISTULA.
short_answer.model_answer:
1. Buccal flap
a> Von Rehrmann flap
b) Moczair flap (sliding buccal flap)
2. Palatal flap
a> Ashley's flap
b> Krugers modification of Ashley's flap
3. Combination of buccal and palatal flap
a> Bridge flap
4. Buccal pad of fat
5. Tongue flap
a> pasteriarly placed darsal tongue flap
b> laterally placed tongue flap
6. Turnover flap
7. Nasolabial flap
8. Gold foil
9. Polyglycolic acid mesh

short_answer.question: Write a short note on SUPPORTIVE MEASURES.
short_answer.model_answer:
1. Antibiotics - For prevention of secondary infection
- Selection of AB should be done on the basis of culture
and sensitivity testing
- Penicillin and its derivatives are the drug of choice.
- Penicillin V 250-500mg 6 hourly is adequate
[initially IV later switched to oral route]
- In case of penicillin resistance, a broad spectrum AB
2. Nasal decongestants - encourage drainage of pus and secretions
- Ephedrine nasal drops (0.5%), instilled intranasally
every 2-3 hours
The correct method of using this is the recumbent position
with the head inclined to the affected side.
- Benzoin and menthol inhalations
3. Analgesics - Aspirin 500 mg QDS
- Paracetamol 500 mg TDS
- Ibuprofen 400 mg TDS

short_answer.question: Describe SUPPORTIVE MEASURES.
short_answer.model_answer:
George Caldwell and Henri Luc described a new method of entering into the
maxillary sinus where 2 separate openings are made, one in the canine fassa
to gain access to the antrum and other in the nasoantral wall for drainage.
Hence. it is called as Caldwell- Luc procedure.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on It is used for.
short_answer.model_answer:
- removal of teeth/fragments of root/impacted teeth in the antrum
- treatment of chronic maxillary sinusitis with polyps and cystic
degeneration of mucosa
- excision of cysts and benign tumours of maxillary sinus
— management of hematoma or haemarrhage in the maxillary sinus
- to treat fractures involving floor of the orbit or anterior maxillary
sinus wall

short_answer.question: Describe the procedure for It is used for.
short_answer.model_answer:
Incision extends from apex of canine down to near the gingival margin &
posteriorly to the 2nd molar
In edentulous cases, it is done along the alveolar ridge
The incision should be larger than the window to be cut in the bone
The mucoperiostium is then detached and retracted till the infra orbital
ridge taking care to preserve the integrity of infraorbital nerve.
A small gouge is used to perforate centre of the canine fassa.
The hole is enlarged by means of rongeurs or backbiting forceps until
it is large enough to allow introduction of atleast the index fingers.
The finger is used to palpate the lining of the sinus.
Blood and pus contained in the sinus are removed by aspirator.
Bleeding may be arrested by inserting small sponges saturated with
adrenaline.
The antrum is inspected using small light such as laryngoscope to illuminate
the antral walls.
If the entire antral membrane is thickened, hyperplastic or altered by
cystic degeneration and filled with polyps, all the diseased tissue should
be removed.
Granulomatous tissue may be found in the floor of the sinus if an abscessed tooth
has been the cause of infection or if a perforation had been made while extracting
a tooth tissue. A curette may be useful for removing the inflammatory granula-
-tion.
slowing Albance burn
The socket should be allowed to fill with a healthy blood clot and finally
gingival margins may be approximated by a suture.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of Production of IgE antibodies.
short_answer.model_answer:
Cutaneous manifestations — pruritus
- urticaria
- angioedema
Respiratory manifestations — wheezing
- coughing
- stridar
- dyspnoea
CVS manifestations — Tachycardia
- Shock
- Hypotension
- Cardiac dyerhythmia

short_answer.question: What is the treatment for Production of IgE antibodies?
short_answer.model_answer:
If the allergic reaction is confined to the skin, an antihistamine is administered
IV or IM.
If respiratory involvement is seen, position the patient in supine position
with legs elevated, administer O2 and perform CPR if required.
Inject Adr 0.5 mg [ 0.5 ml of 1:1000 solution] IM; repeat every 5-10
min in case the patient does not show improvement or improvement is
transient.
This is the only Life saving measure.
Adr should not be injected IV unless shock is life threatning
If Adr is to be injected IV; it should be diluted to 1:10000 to 1:100000 and
infused slowly with constant monitoring.
Administer a H₁-antihistamine [chlorpheniramine 10-20 mg ] IM/slow IV.
IV glucocorticoid I hydro cartisone sodium succinate 200mg ] should be added
in severe/recurrant cases.
In case of bronchaspasm, administer salbutamol, initiate 8/29/0 BLS and monitor
vital signs.
basic
Prepare the pt. for transport to emergency care.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on ENUCLEATION.
short_answer.model_answer:
Enucleation involves complete removal of cyst lining and its contents.
Intraaral approach is the method of choice for enucleation although rarely
extraoral approach through the submandibular skin may be excised.
It is also c/a cystectomy or PartischII procedure.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Damage to adjacent vital structures.
short_answer.model_answer:
Direct observation of wound healing as in case of marsupialisation is not
passible.
In young people, tooth germ or unerupted teeth involved with the cyst'
are removed with the lining of the cyst.
SURGICAL PROCEDURE : ENLICLEATION WITH PRIMARY CLOSURE

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Mucoperiosteal flap elevation.
short_answer.model_answer:
Prepare bony window, if bone over the cyst is intact
If thin layer of bone is present, it is carefully peeled off with periosteal elevator
cyst should be removed entirely without tearing
cyst Lining is gently separated from cavity wall using
periosteal elevatar or spoon excavator
if lining of cyst is difficult to separate then insert a
rolled gauze b/w cavity & cyst lining.
Or else, the cyst can be aspirated so that the sac shrinks
The cavity is isorigated and inspected and closed with sutures

short_answer.question: Write a short note on ENLICLEATION WITH OPEN PACKING.
short_answer.model_answer:
It is preferred in cases of infected large cysts where primary clasure might lead
to breakdown of the wound and intergere with healing.
So, instead of primary closure, X the flap is turned into the bony cavity.
It is fixed with 1/2 inch medicated gauze pack for 10 days.
A change in pack is followed by construction of an acrylic plug as used in
marsupialisation.
A. PERIAPICAL

short_answer.question: Write a short note on AREAS ANAESTHETISED -.
short_answer.model_answer:
Pulps of maxillary central incisor upto canine on the infected side
In 72% cases, pulps of maxillary premolars and mesiobuccal root of
the first molar
Buccal periodontium and bone of these teeth
Lower eyelid, lateral aspect of nase, upper Lip

short_answer.question: Describe the procedure for ANATOMICAL LANDMARKS Mucobuccal fold.
short_answer.model_answer:
25 gauge long needle preferred
Area of insertion- - height of mucobuccal fold directly over 1st premolar
Position the pt. in such a way that his/her maxillary occlusal plane is
at 45° to the floor.
inferior to
Locate the infraorbital foramen - concavity below the infraorbital notch
Maintain an index finger on this foramen externally, lip is retracted using
the thumb and mucobuccal fold is expased.
BICUSPID APPROACH In this, needle is held parallel to the premolars &
inserted into the height of mucobuccal fold over 1st premolar.
Maximum penetration should be 2 cm; 0.9-1.2 ml soin. is deposited
slowly and the overlying tissues are gently massaged to increased the
diffusion of LA soln.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on VASOCONSTRICTORS IN LA.
short_answer.model_answer:
Vasoconstrictors are chemical agents added to local anaesthetic solutions
a> to oppose vasodilation caused by the local anaesthetic agents
b> to achieve hemastasis (constrict the blood vessels and control tissue
perfusion)

short_answer.question: Write a short note on USES OF VASOCONSTRICTORS.
short_answer.model_answer:
The net effects caused by addition of vasoconstrictor to LA are-
a> It decreases the rate of absorption of local anaesthetic agent into
cardiovascular system
b> It lowers the plasma level of local anaesthetic agent, ,and, hence,
decreases the risk of systemic toxicity.
c> It increases the duration and depth of action, as higher volumes of
local araesthetic agent remain in and around the nerve for longer
time
d> It decreases bleeding at the site of injection
The pH of LA solution with vasoconstrictor is lower/mare acidic than
without vasoconstrictor. (5.5-6)
(3.3-4)
Because of less acidic property, the plain solutions will have less burning
during injection & have more rapid clinical action.

short_answer.question: What are the indications for USES OF VASOCONSTRICTORS?
short_answer.model_answer:
Patients with significant cardiovascular diseases such as
a> ischemic heart disease b> hypertension and c> cerebral strokes
Patients with thyrotoxicasis or hyperthyroid states & diabetes mellitus
Patients receiving non specific beta-blockers ,monoamine oxidase
inhibitors tricyclic antidepressants

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Pregnancy.
short_answer.model_answer:
on
Patients with ^ general anaesthesia with halogenated agents
REDUCING AGENTS OR ANTIOXIDANTS FOR VA50CONSTRICTORS
Vasoconstrictors are relatively unstable in solutions, and undergo
oxidation, when exposed to sunlight. The solution appears pink to
brown in color after oxidation.
To ensure a reasonable shelf life, it is necessary to include a
reducing agent or antioxidant in the LA soln.

thus prevents oxidation of vasoconstrictors. hienira rsval

short_answer.question: Write a short note on LEVER PRINCIPLE.
short_answer.model_answer:
most commonly used principle
The elevator is a Lever of first order. In this, the fulcrum is located
between the load and effort. In order to gain mechanical advantage,
the effort arm must be longer than the resistance/ load arm.
When elevators are used, the force is transmitted at Long power arm of the
handle, so that the MA is derived at the short weight arm i.e the working
blade, which engages the tooth. eg - coupland elevator.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on WEDGE PRINCIPLE.
short_answer.model_answer:
It consists of 2 movable inclined planes, which meet and form a sharp
angle. The effort is applied to the base of the plane & the resistance
has its effect on the slant side.
A wedge can be used to split, expand or displace the portion of a
substance that receives force. The sharper the angle of the wedge,
the less effort is required to overcome the resistance.
The MA of wedge is 2.5.
The wedge elevator is forced between the root & bone parallel to the
long axis of the tooth. Eg- A small apexo elevator can displace the roots
towards the occlusal plane out of socket, similarly a straight elevator is
applied between the tooth and interdental bone to separate periodontal
ligament attachment.

light force applied to a crank handle onto the side of the wheel creates
a torque about the axle centreline to lift a heavy load.
The effort is applied to the circumjerence of a wheel, which turns the
axle to raise the weight.
Greater the diameter of the wheel, more is the mechanical advantage.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on LEFORT I FRACTURE.
short_answer.model_answer:
It is also called as Low bevel gractive
- Low level or, Subzygomatic fracture
- Harizontal fracture of maxilla
- Guerin's fracture
- It is also called as floating fracture, because there is a separation of
complete dentoalveolar part of the maxilla, and the fractured fragment
is held only by means of soft tissue.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Lateral extension of fracture line.
short_answer.model_answer:
commences or the lateral margin of piriform aperture, passes
horizontally above the nasal floor and canine fassa, passes along
the antero lateral wall of maxillary sinus, runs below the zygomatic
and then runs upwards and posteriorly to fracture the pterygoid
& plate at the junction of lower third and upper 2/3 rds.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of Medial extension of fracture line.
short_answer.model_answer:
Slight swelling and edema of the lower part of the face along with
upper Lip swelling.
Ecchymasis in the Labial and buccal vestibule as well as contusion
of the skin of the upper lip.
Laceration of upper Lip and intraoral mucasa may be seen

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Bilateral epistaxis or nasal bleeding.
short_answer.model_answer:
Mobility of upper dentoalveolar partion of jaw, which is frequently
mobile to digital pressure.
Occlusion may be disturbed. Patient may not be able to masticate food
Pain while speaking or moving the jaw
Percussion of the maxillary teeth produces dull "cracked cup "sound.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on FRACTURE OF ZYGOMATIC COMPLEX.
short_answer.model_answer:
The zygomaticomaxillary complex plays a key role in the structure,
function and esthetics of the 100 facial region.
It provides normal check contour and separates the orbital contents
from the temporal fassa and maxillary sinus.
As the zygomatic bone is clasely associated with the maxilla, frontal
and temporal bones, and they are usually involved when a zygomatic
bone fracture occurs, therefore these type of fractures are called
zygomaticomazillary fractures or zygomatic complex fractures or
tripod fractures.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Group C - Stable fracture.
short_answer.model_answer:
other types of zygomatic fractures which
require reduction but no fixation.
3> ROWE AND WILLIAMS CLASSIFICATION
Vertical axis - It is an imaginary line running through the frontozygomatic
suture and the first molar teeth.
Any displacement of the fracture in this axis becomes stable
after reduction.
Harizontal axis - It is a Line running through the infraorbital foramen and
zygomatic arch.
When displacement occurs in this axis, it results in separation
of frontozygomatic suture and reduction of these fractures is
unstable.
a> Fractures stable after elevation
- Arch only (Medially displaced)
- Rotationaround the vertical axis
medially

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of THAMINM.
short_answer.model_answer:
Flattening of the injured cheek (passibly masked by swelling) - most
common displacement of the complex is inward.
Immediately following injury or after the acute phase of edema is
subsided, then an alteration of contour on the affected side is
apparent.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Unilateral epistaxis may be present.
short_answer.model_answer:
Circumarbital ecchymasis will develop after few hours from
effusion of blood into the surrounding tissues.
Circumarbital edema can be quite grass. The examination of the
eye may not be possible till edema subsides.
Subconjunctival hemarrhage is observed at the outer canthus
Depression of ocular level / limitation of ocular movement may be seen
Proptasis of the eye may be seen due to retrobulbar hemorrhage
Patient may complain of diplopia/ blurring of vision
Anaesthesia of the cheek, nase and Lip may be present

short_answer.question: What is the treatment for Unilateral epistaxis may be present?
short_answer.model_answer:
In majority of cases, early operation is advisable, provided that there
are no opthalmic or cranial complications.
Whenever there is a grass periorbital edema and ecchymosis,
pastponement of the operation for 3-5 days can be done, but it
should not be prolonged more than 2 weeks.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on STABLE FRACTURES -.
short_answer.model_answer:
Simple elevation will be sufficient, because of high degree of stability
due to integrity of temporal fascia and the interdigitation of the
fracture lines. No additional fixation is required after reduction.
Type 1-No treatment

short_answer.question: Describe UNSTABLE FRACTURES -.
short_answer.model_answer:
Odontogenic sinusitis is the inflammation of mucasa of any of the paranasal
sinuses.
Inflammation of most or all of the paranasal air sinuses simultaneously is
known as pansinusitis.
The maxillary sinusitis is usually odontogenic in origin, because of its clase
proximity with the maxillary teeth.
Maxillary sixusitis can be classified into <1> Acute and <2> Chronic

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of PATHOPHYSIOLOGY.
short_answer.model_answer:
Severe throbbing pain, when the pus remains confined within bone in apical
region. The intensity reduces after 12-24 hours as the pus is discharged into
antral cavity.
Unilateral foul discharge through pasterior nares
Cacasmia - a fetid odor with bad taste in the mouth.

Presence of an obvious aroantral fistila
Prolapse of polypoidal mass into the mouth forming a Lump.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on INCISIONS IN THE ORAL CAVITY.
short_answer.model_answer:
It is desirable to incise through attached gingiva and over a healthy
bone. The suture line should have adequate bone support underneath
for uneventful healing.
Incisions placed near the teeth for extractions should be made in the
gingival sulcus.
Incisions involving the reflection of mucoperiasteal flap are direct,
straight line or curvilinear taking the shortest distance vertically
through the tissues.
Indirect incisions are used to access the areas like soft palate, tongue,
cheeks, Lips, floor of the mouth etc.
Integrity of the interdental papilla should be maintained as far
as passible
Blood supply to the incision should be adequate
Incisions should be at right angles to mucasa to prevent shelfing
edges that might cause necrosis of the undermined part

short_answer.model_answer:
A soft yests Yeates or carrugated rubber drain is inserted into the depth of the

Drain left for atleast 24 hours.
The purpose of the drain is to allow the discharge of tissue fluids and
pus from the wound by keeping it patent. clean
The drain allows for debridement of abscess cavity by irrigation.
hrs Tissue fluids flow along the external surface of a latex drain.
Hence, it is not always necessary to make perforations in the drain,
which could weaken and perhaps cause fragmentation within the tissues.
Drains should be removed when the drainage has nearly И.Г. аиога completely
ceased. Some form of drains, such as latex drains are Trritating
to the surrounding tissues and may stimulate exudate formation
on their own. Thus, drains are left in infected wounds for 2-7 days

Osteomyelitis may be defined as an lammatory condition of bone that

cartex and extends to involve the periasteum of the affected area.

short_answer.question: Describe the pathogenesis of GARRES OSTEOMYELITIS.
short_answer.model_answer:
The etiological agents can be a carious tooth, or the overlying soft tissue
infection. The infectious process. localises in periasteum or beneath the
periasteal covering of cartex and spreads slightly into the interior dal of bone.
In case of persistence of the causative agent, it causes bony thickening,
visible on radiograph.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on RADIOGRAPHY.
short_answer.model_answer:
A focal area of well calcified bone proliferation may be seen that is smooth
and that often has a laminated or onion skin appearance.
The radiographic appearance is typical. There is condensation of cortical
bone, and overgrowth of bony Tissue beneath the periasteum.
The increase in mass of bone is due to
- mild Toxic stimulation of fibroblasts by attenuated infection
- compensatory thickening of bone as a mechanical adaptation to
reinforce the area weakened through disease.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on MARSUPIALISATION.
short_answer.model_answer:
Marsupialisation refers To creating a surgical window in the wall of the
cyst, evacuating the contents of the cyst, and maintaining continuity
between the cyst and the oral cavity, maxillary sinus or nasal cavity.
The only partion that is removed is the piece removed to produce the
window. The remaining cystic lining is left in situ.
It is also called decompression or Partisch I technique or cystotomy.
This process decreases intracystic pressure and promotes shrinkage of
the cyst and bone fill.

short_answer.question: What are the indications for MARSUPIALISATION?
short_answer.model_answer:
Age - In a young child, with developing tooth germs, or when development
of displaced Teeth has not progressed enucleation would damage the
tooth buds.
In the elderly, debilitated patient, marsupialisation is less stressful
Proximity to vital structures - when proximity of the cyst to vital
structures could create an aronasal or aroantral fistula, injure
neurovascular bundle or damage vital teeth, then marsupialisation
should be considered.
Size of cyst - In very large cyst, where enucleation could result in a
pathological fracture, marsupialisation can be accomplished, through
a more limited bony opening.

short_answer.question: What is the etiology of SALIVARY FISTULA?
short_answer.model_answer:
It is a rare condition caused by accidental trauma, surgery and
infection.
It may occur as a result of actinomycosis, syphillis and cancrum
oris.
Any surgery on the parotid gland -superficial parotidectomy, drainage
of abscess, surgery for carcinoma cheek

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of SALIVARY FISTULA.
short_answer.model_answer:
Discharging watery fluid, exaggerated by keeping time in the mouth
collection of saliva under the skin flap with drainage through the wound
Fistulogram confirms the diagnosis.
Sialography is performed to establish the exact site or origin of fistula.

short_answer.question: What is the treatment for SALIVARY FISTULA?
short_answer.model_answer:
Treatment consists of aspiration of fluid, compression dressings and
removal of salivary stimulants.
Currently the most commonly used treatment is botulinum neurotoxin
type A and systemic hyascine or propantheline bromide.
The problem usually resolves with this treatment regimen.

short_answer.question: Describe the technique for To prevent recurrence - reankylasis.
short_answer.model_answer:
A> Condylectomy
B> Gap arthroplasty
c> Interpositional arthroplasty
Most surgical procedures can be done through a preauricular incision
alone.
A> CONDYLECTOMY
Advocated in cases of fibrous ankylasis, where the joint space is
obliterated with deposition of fibrous bands but anatomy of
condylar head is almost unchanged.
Preauricular incision is mostly used to expose the condylar region
A horizontal asteotomy cut is made at the level of condylar neck.
Vital structures on the medial surface of condylar neck should be
protected using special condylar retractor.
The condylar head is sectioned at the level of the neck, and it is
separated from superior fibrous attachments and removed.
The stump of the condyle at the neck is smoothened and thus a new
joint is created here.

short_answer.question: What are the complications of To prevent recurrence - reankylasis?
short_answer.model_answer:
a> Lass of vertical height of ramus
b> Bilateral condylectomy may create an anterior open bite
c> Unitateral condylectomy causes deviation of mandible towards
the operated side on oral opening
Therefore, when the site of the fused joint is mobilised via condylec-
tomy, then after recontouring by arthroplasty, an alloplastic
material can be used to maintain the jaw space.
B> GAP ARTHROPLASTY
bony
Commonly done in cases of complete ankylosis.
A
Gap arthroplasty involves creation of an anatomical gap in the ankylased
segment to form an artificial joint space.
A gap in the bone is created to separate the ramus from the ankylased
mass in the glenoid fassa.
2 horizontal asteotomy cuts are made and the wedge of the bone between
these 2 cuts is removed
Care should be taken while removing the bone from the medial aspect
as it is clase to the maxillary artery and carotid canal.
A gap of 1-1.5cm is created to prevent reankylosis and the gap should be
maintained by active physiotherapy of the joint.

When gap arthroplasty is done to release ankylasis, there are chances of

So an interpasitional material is to be placed in between them to avoid
contact and minimise chances of reankylosis
Materials which can be used are either alloplastic or autogenous materials.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on DRY SOCKET.
short_answer.model_answer:
Gry socket is defined as a past-operative pain in and around the dental
alveolus, which increases in severity at some moment between the first
and third day after a dental extraction, , accompanied by partial or
total disintegration of the intraalveolar clot, causing foul smell.
Term dry socket was given by Crawford (1896)
It is also known as - alveolar asteitis
- Fibrinolytic alveolitis
- Fibrinolytic asteitis

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Pain in the socket.
short_answer.model_answer:
B> BACTERIAL THEORY
- This theory relates the highcount of anaerobic bacteria in the
extraction site to the pathogenesis of dry socket.
- Actinomyces viscasus and Streptococcus mutans have been related
to dry socket, because it has been demonstrated that they retard
the alveolar pastextraction healing in an animal model.
Also fibrinolytic activity has been observed which increases with Treponema
denticola The infection never occurs during childhood a period when
this organism has not yet colonised the mouth.

mouth
Appear by second or third pastoperative day
Pain may radiate to the ear and neck.
Sometimes, associated with low grade fever and ipsilateral Symphaden-
opathy.

Class IV - Impacted canines located in the alveolar process between

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Female predilection.
short_answer.model_answer:
Most common site - parotid glands.
Occurance in sublingual gland is rare.
8% of pleomarphic adenomas involve minor salivary glands.
Among them palate is the most common site. (60-65%)
The tumor in major salivary glands is slow growing, painless and
does not show fixation to deeper tissues or overlying skin.
Though pain is not a common symptom, Local discomfort is always
present.
In intraoral accessory glands, the tumor is 1-2 cm in diameter.
Since this tumor causes the patient difficulties in mastication, talking
and breathing, ,it is detected and treated earlier than tumor of
major glands.
Recurrent lesions occur as multiple nodules and are less mobile
than original Tumor.
Deep Lobe tumors may not show grass swelling on the outer aspect
but as they grow, they pass through the stylomand ibular tunnel of
Patey and push the pharyngeal wall, tonsil & soft palate. Thus, these
tumors are called dumb bell tumors.

short_answer.question: What investigations are done for Female predilection?
short_answer.model_answer:
FNAC - to confirm diagnosis and rule out malignancy
CT scan - done when the tumor is arising from deep lobe.
It helps to define the extraglandular spread & the extent
of parapharyngeal disease.
FNAC of Lymph nodes which are palpable in neck in case of
malignancy of parotid gland.
X ray of bones (mandible and mastoid process) to see for bony
resorption.
MRI is a better investigation but expensive
Slow growing parotid tumors should not be subjected to biopsy for
2 reasons - injury to facial nerve
- seeding of tumor cells in the subcutaneous plane

short_answer.question: What is the treatment for Female predilection?
short_answer.model_answer:
A> Conservative superficial parotidectomy.
It is the standerd treatment for benign pleomorphic adenoma.
It means removal of the entire lobe containing the tumor which is
superficial to facial nerve. Facial nerve should always be preserved.
Enucleation should never be done as it causes recurrance.
It is difficult to remove a recurrent tumor.
Imp. points in superficial parotidectomy
Adequate exposure by an incision which starts in front of tragus of
pinna, vertically downwards, curves round the ear lobule up to the
mastoid process and is carried downwards in the neck [Lazy incision]
Recognising the facial nerve - Facial nerve lies 1 cm inferomedial to
the pointed end of the tragal cartilage of external ear.
Trace the pasterior belly of digastric up to the mastoid process.
Facial nerve is in between the muscle and tympanic plate.
Gentle handling good suction & perfect hemostasis helps in clear
with
recognition of the nerve.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on SALIVARY GLAND TUMORS.
short_answer.model_answer:
Salivary gland tumors are tumors that arise from the tissues of salivary
glands.
They make up 6% of all head and neck tumors.
They are known to arise from both major as well as intraoral minor
salivary glands.
lainiperqus 41

short_answer.question: Write a short note on TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA.
short_answer.model_answer:
A disorder of trigeminal nerve that causes episodes of sharp, stabbing pain
in the cheek, Lips, gums or chin on one side of the face.
People with this pain often wince or twitch, which is where trigeminal neuralgia
gets its French name "Tic douloureux" meaning painful twitch.

short_answer.question: What is the etiology of TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA?
short_answer.model_answer:
Usually caused by contact between a normal blood vessel and the trigeminal
nerve at the base of the brain
This contact puts pressure on the nerve and causes it to malfunction
Can occur as a result of ageing, or it can be related to multiple sclerasis
or a similar disarder that damages the myelin sheath protecting
certain nerves.
Less commonly, TN can be caused by a tumor compressing the nerve.
Anomalies of superior cerebellar artery - most recently blamed cause

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Pain is a characteristic symptom.
short_answer.model_answer:
Occurs in bouts or paroxysms, sharp or lancinating in character
Radiates to the territary of one or more divisions of TN.
The pain is nearly always unilateral, and if bilateral, is successive
rather than concomitant
The mandibular & maxillary divisions are more commonly involved
than the opthatmic.
In some instances ,these 2 divisions may be simultaneously affected.
The pain may be exoked by a touch, a draft of air, any movement of the
face as in talking, chewing or yawning or swallowing.
The first episodes are usually fairly mild and brief, and it may be
few minutes or hours before the next attack.
With age the episodes become more frequent and painful, until the
patient begins to live in constant fear of next attack.
The momentary bursts of pain usually begins from the same spot on
the face each time. They are called trigger zones.
They are common on the vermillion barder of the lips, ala of the nase,
the cheeks and around the eyes.
The pt. learns to avoid touching the skin over the trigger zones which
frequently makes him go unwashed or unshaven for days.
