Large cysts may produce expansion of the bone, displacement of tooth roots,
and crepitus when palpating the expanded alveolar plate.
The discolaration of the non-vital tooth and negative response of the
affected tooth to electric pulp testing are the presenting signs.
In addition, infected radicular cysts are painful, the infected teeth is
sensitive to percussion, and there may be swelling of overlying soft
tissues and lymphadenopathy.
An intraoral sinus tract may be identified with discharging pus or
brownish fluid, when the cyst is infected.
The involved teeth will be found to be non vital, discolared, fractured
or with heavy restorations or a failed root canal B treatment.

short_answer.question: What are the radiographic features of Seldom associated with primary teeth?
short_answer.model_answer:
The cyst exhibits' a round, pear or ovoid shaped radiolucency, with
a thin, radiopaque line around the periphery of radiolucent area.
In case of very Large cysts' or infected cysts, the peripheral white line
is occassionally absent.
312 IN 1,0011

short_answer.question: What is the treatment for Seldom associated with primary teeth?
short_answer.model_answer:
Non vital teeth that are associated with the cyst' are either extracted
or retained, by endodontic treatment and apicoectomy.
External sinus tracts should always be excised to prevent epithelial
ingrowth.
The commonly employed surgical procedure for radicular cysts' is
enudeation, with primary clasure.
Very small cysts can be removed through tooth socket
Large periodontal cysts that encroach upon the maxillary antrum
or inferior alveolar neurovascular bundle or the nase may be
treated by marsupialisation.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on DENTIGEROUS CYST.
short_answer.model_answer:
Dentigerous cyst is a developmental odontogenic cyst that is formed by the
expansion of dental follicle and therefore always attached to the neck of
the impacted or unerupted teeth.
It is also called as follicular cyst.
Paget (1863) coined the term dentigerous cyst.

short_answer.question: Describe the pathogenesis of DENTIGEROUS CYST.
short_answer.model_answer:
A> INTRAFOLLICULAR THEORY
It is caused by fluid accumulation between the REE and the examel
surface, resulting is a cyst in which the crown is located within the lumen.
B> EXTRAFOL.L.ICULAR THEORY
It may also arise by proliferation and custic transformation of islands of
odontogenic epithelium in connective tissue wall of dental follicle or even
outside dental follicle and this transformed epithelium then unite with
lining follicular epithelium farming cystic cavity around tooth crown.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of DENTIGEROUS CYST.
short_answer.model_answer:
Always associated with crown of an impacted, embedded or unerupted teeth
Most lesions are present in 2nd and 3rd decades with slight male predilec
-tion.
Most common site - mandibular and maxillary 3rd molar area and
maxillary cuspid areas.
Dentigerous cyst has the potential to become an aggressive lesion.
Expansion of bone with subsequent facial asymmetry, extreme displacement
of teeth, severe root resorption of adjacent teeth and pain are consequences
of cyst enlargement.
There is usually no pain or discomfort associated with the cyst unless it
becomes secondarily infected.
The thinned out bone covering the center of convexity becomes fragile
and ultimately gets fragmented. As a result, $ oup percussion over
the area produces a typical egg shell crackling sound, which is also
true for other large odontogenic cysts.

short_answer.question: What are the radiographic features of DENTIGEROUS CYST?
short_answer.model_answer:
Well defined radiolucency around the impacted or unerupted teeth.
Usually unilocular but at times it may be multilocular.
Cysts have a well-defined sclerotic margin, unless when they are infected,
then the margins are poorly defined.

short_answer.question: What is the treatment for Three radiological variations are observed?
short_answer.model_answer:
Treatment via an intraoral or extraoral approach is decided by the
size of the cyst, adequate access and whether it is desirable to save the
involved teoth.
УЯОЗНТ S/A INDI АЯТИГ
A> MARSUPIALISATION
It is indicated in children if the cyst is very large in size and the
involved teeth/tooth are to be maintained

short_answer.question: Write a short note on INDIA.
short_answer.model_answer:
The tooth may erupt into occlusion as the defect heals with normal bone,
or arthodontic forces may be used to bring the tooth into occlusion.
B> ENLICLEATION
Alternatively. the cyst can be enucleated together with the involved
tooth in adults, as the possibility of the tooth erupting is low

short_answer.question: Write a short note on MUCOCELE.
short_answer.model_answer:
Mucocele is the term used for swelling due to accumulation of saliva, as a
result of obstruction or trauma to the salivary gland ducts.
It is not a true cyst as it lacks an epithelial Lining.
There are 2 types of mucoceles -
A> extravasation type
B> retention type
These two types differ in the pathogenesis. The extravasation type is the
common one.

short_answer.question: Describe the pathogenesis of MUCOCELE.
short_answer.model_answer:
A> EXTRAVASATION TYPE
It is caused by trauma leading to laceration of the duct, commonly of the
minor salivary glands resulting in extravasation of the mucin into the
connective tissue.
The trauma may be caused by biting of the Lip, cheek, pinching of Lip by
extraction forceps etc.
The mucin, which extravasates into the connective tissue, leads to inflamm-
- ation, drawing the neutrophils and macrophages to the site, which in will turn
will result in the formation of the granulation tissue, which actually
forms the wall of these extravasation cysts.
B> RETENTION TYPE
This occurs due to obstruction of the salivary duct, commonly because of
a siabolith or may be due to periductal scar, ,or impinging tumor, resulting
in the accumulation of the saliva in the duct.
This leads to dilatation of the duct resulting in a cyst like lesion, These
cysts may show the presence of ductal epithelium histologically.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of MUCOCELE.
short_answer.model_answer:
Mucoceles occur as painless swellings, which may be recurrent.
The common sites of occurrence are -
A> extravasation type - Lower Lip and tongue
B> retention type - palate, cheek, floor of mouth and maxillary sinus
A well-demarcated lesion of the upper lip could be a salivary gland
neoplasm than a mucocele.
The extravasation type occurs in the 2nd decade and also in children.
The retention type occurs in the middle-aged persons.
Based on the Location clinically, there are 2 types of mucoceles
a> Superficial
b> Deep type
The superficial mucocele resembles a vescicle or bulla.
Since they are placed superficially, the bluish translucency is seen.
They are fluctuant. Usually, these cysts' are 4mm to 1cm in size.
The wall is very thin and they rupture easily.
The deep mucoceles are covered by normal appearing mucasa thus
they are different from the superficial ones in the color.
They are firm, well- circumscribed lesions and are present for a
longer period.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on CONTENTS.
short_answer.model_answer:
Majar contents include - geniohyoid and genioglassus muscles, and the
hyoglassus muscle complex.
It also contains - i> deep part of the submandibular salivary gland
and its ducts anteriorly
ii> sublingual salivary gland
iii> Lingual nerve and
iv> hypoglassal nerve

short_answer.question: Write a short note on INVOLVEMENT.
short_answer.model_answer:
The teeth which frequently give rise to involvement of sublingual space
are the mandibular incisors, canines, premolars and sometimes first
molars.
The apices of these teeth are superior to the myelohyoid muscle.
The infection perforates Lingual cartical plate below the Levelo of the mucasa
of the floor of the mouth and passes into the sublingual space.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of INVOLVEMENT.
short_answer.model_answer:
A> EXTRAORAL
- There is Little or no swelling. The lymph nodes may be enlarged and
tender. Pain and discomfart on deglutition. Speech may be affected.
B> INTRAORAL
- Firm, painful swelling seen in the floor of the mouth on the affected
side. The floor of the mouth is raised. The tongue may be pushed
superiorly. It results in airway obstruction.
The ability to protrude the tongue beyond the vermillion border of upper
Lip is affected.

short_answer.question: Describe the management of INVOLVEMENT.
short_answer.model_answer:
A> INTRAORALLY
- An incision is made close to the lingual cartical plate, lateral to the
sublingual plica, as the important structure at this site is the
sublingual nerve which is deeply placed and less likely to be damaged
by this approach.
- The other important structures lie medial to the plica and include
the Wharton's duct, sublingual artery and veins and the lingual
nerve.
- The sinus forceps is then inserted and opened to evacuate the puse
B> EXTRAORALLY
- When both the submental and sublingual spaces contain pus, they
can be drained via a skin incision placed in the submental region,
pushing a closed sinus forceps through the myelohyoid muscle.
- Similarly, when the submandibular space is involved, a sublingual
space abscess can be approached and drained through an incision in
the skin overlying the submandibular space, via the submandibular
space.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on MODE OF ACTION OF LA.
short_answer.model_answer:
LA acts on voltage sensitive Na+ channels and block nerve conduction by
preventing /decreasing the entry of Na+ ions.
The Na+ channel has an activation gate near the extracellular mouth and
an inactivation gate at the intracellular mouth. In the resting state, the
activation gate is clased.
The LA receptor is located within the channel in its in atraceltular half.
The LA traverses the membrane in unionised form
reionises in the axoplasm and approaches the LA receptor
The receptor has higher affinity to the LA in its activated as
well as inactivedated state compared to resting state
Binding of LA to the receptor closes the Not channel &
prolongs the inactivated state
It prevents the entry of Na+ ions and thus action potential is not generated
conduction blocade k

short_answer.question: Write a short note on THERO THEORIES FOR MODE OF ACTION OF LA.
short_answer.model_answer:
1. ACETYLCHOLINE & THEORY - Acetylcholine is a neurotransmitter at the
nerve synapses is assumed to be involved in nerve conduction.
2. CALCIUM AISPLACEMENT THEORY - Depolarisation is prevented by
displacement of calcium from certain membrane sites that control the
permeability to sodium.
3. SURFACE CHARGE [REPULSION] THEORY - Local anaesthetic agents
binds to the nerve membrane and change the electrical potential at
the membrane surface.
buco LA molecules with net positive charge make the surface potential
at the nerve membrane more positive and reduce the excitability
of nerve by increasing threshold potential.
4. MEMBRANE EXPANSION THEORY - Local anaesthetic agents diffuse
to hydrophobic regions of excitable membrane and expand the
membrane, preventing the sodium permeability.
5. SPECIFIC RECEPTOR THEORY - LA agents act by binding to specific
receptors on sodium channels in the nerve membrane.
Once the receptor access is gained either on its external surface or
on the internal axoplasmic surface ,Nat permeability is decreased or
eliminated, interrupting the nerve conduction.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on VPOJOITE.
short_answer.model_answer:
Dentoalveolar injuries are defined as those in which avulsion, subluxation
or fracture of the teeth occurs in association with a fracture of the
alveolus. This type of injury may occur alone or in conjunction with
some other type of mandibular fracture.

short_answer.question: Describe BELL'S PALSY.
short_answer.model_answer:
It accounts for 60-70% of all cases of unilateral peripheral facial palsy.
Affects with equal frequency on the right and left sides of the face.
Either sex is affected equally.
May occur at any age, the median age is 40 years.
Increased incidence in the elderly diabetics hypertensives, pregnant
women than in the common people.

short_answer.question: What is the etiology of BELL'S PALSY?
short_answer.model_answer:
Cause is unclear, precipitating factors may be -
<a> viral infection
<b> vascular ischaemia
<c> autoimmune inflammatory disarders
<d> Heredity
<e> reactivation of HSV in geniculate ganglion
[Ramsey Hunt syndrome]

short_answer.question: Write a short note on CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS.
short_answer.model_answer:
Onset of Bells palsy is acute
ТИЗМЭРАИАМ
1/2 of the cases attain maximum paralysis in 48 hours
All cases are clinically prominent by 5 days.
Pain behind the ear may precede paralysis by 1-2 days.
Impairment of taste is present to some degree in all cases.
- but rarely beyond 2nd week of paralysis.
Hyperacusis or distartion of sound in ipsilateral ear
- paralysis of stapedius muscle
Patient feels stiffness of face pulled to one side.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on Grade I - Normal.
short_answer.model_answer:
Grade II - mild dysfunction, slight weekness on clase inspection,
normal symmetry at rest
Grade III - moderate dysfunction, eye can be completely closed with
effort
Grade IV - moderately severe, normal tone at rest, obvious weakness
or asymmetry with movement, incomplete closureof eye
Grade V - severe dysfunction, only barely perceptible motion,
asymmetry at rest'

short_answer.question: What investigations are done for Grade I - Normal?
short_answer.model_answer:
YAO 10ITE
Differentiate between upper and Lower motor neuron lesion
UMN - frontalis is spared allowing normal fou furrowing
of eyebrow and eye blinking
LMN - all muscles of facial expression are affected

short_answer.question: Describe the management of Grade I - Normal.
short_answer.model_answer:
A> SYMPTOMATIC
- Lubricating eye drops
- Protectionof the eyes during sleep - eye patches
- Massage of the weakened muscles
- Physiotherapy
B> MEDICOLNAL
- Prednisolone 60-80 mg/be day ^ in divided doses initial 4-5 days.
decreases possibility of permanent paralysis СИА
decreases severe pain
- Acyclovir 400 mg 5times/day
- Vitamins B1, B6, B12 may be administered
- If the patient is seen after 3-4 weeks, then steroid therapy is of
no use
— In hyperkinesia [ excessive restlessness]
- do Clostridium botulinum toxin
C> SURGICAL
- If electroneurography reveals a greater than 90% lass of compound
action potential within first 2 weeks following onset of paralysis, facial
nerve decompression is recommended. After this period, surgery is not
benefecial.
- Nerve decompression can be carried out internally or externally.
- Internal decompression - The nerve is exposed is exposed in the facial
canal pressure in the canal is relieved by exposing the nerve and the
epineural sheath is opened to visualise the nerve fibres and release adhesions
or re-establish continuity.
- External decompression - is done by releasing the epineural sheath
from surrounding scar tissue, bone or a foreign body.
- Nerve anastomosis - reanimation
- anastomasis of the central end of hypoglassal or spinal accessary
nerve with the distal end of the facial nerve is done
- Nerve grafting - whenever there is evidence of пеичота or loss of
portion of a nerve, nerve grafting can be considered.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on FRENECTOMY.
short_answer.model_answer:
A frenum is a thin band of fibrous tissue and a few muscle fibres
covered by mucus membrane connecting the Lips, cheek and tonque
to the jawbone.
Maxillary midline frenum is most commonly seen. Lingual frenum
is also seen. Maxillary and mandibular frena are also found in
premolar-molar area.
Frenectomy is the surgical procedure involving the complete surgical excision
of the frenum including its attachments.
A
Frenotomy is the surgical procedure involving incision and relocation
of the frenum.
It is mainly of 2 types - a> Labial Frenectomy
b> Lingual Frenectomy

short_answer.question: Describe the procedure for Frenum causing gingival recession.
short_answer.model_answer:
Whenever there is an inadequate vestibular depth present, to increase the
retention and stability of the denture, deepening of the vestibule is considered.
Thus vestibuloplasty is a procedure to increase the depth by uncovering the
existing basal bone of the jaws surgically and by repositioning the overlying
mucasa, muscle attachments to a Lower position in the mandible/superior
position in the maxilla.
Vestibuloplasty can be done in the maxilla or in the mandible or both jaws.
Maxillary vestibuloplasty are primarily done on labial/buccalside
while mandibular techniques are done via labial or lingual approach.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on CLOSED SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBILOPLASTY.
short_answer.model_answer:
First described by Macintosh and Obwegeser
Indications - unstable denture because of shallow vestibular depth
with good underlying bone height and contours.
A
vertical midline incision is made in labial vestibule and supra-
periosteal tunnel is made from premolar (R) to premolar (L) area.
In maxilla further incisions may be given in 1st molar region
for further advancement.
The intervening submucasal tissue is then excised or repositioned
superiorly and maintained by placement of preformed dentures
which can be fixed to the mandible with circummandibular 239VT wiring
and to the maxilla by per alveolar wiring.

TO

short_answer.question: Write a short note on OPEN SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBULOPLASTY.
short_answer.model_answer:
Wallenius advocated an open method to overcome the relapse of clased
vestibuloplasty.
A harizontal incision is made in the mucogingival junction.
A thin mucasal flap is elevated by submucasal dissection followed
by supraperiosteal dissection to the desired extent, excising muscle
and subcutaneous tissues.
Stay sutures are used to fix the flap to the periosteum deep in vestibule.

short_answer.question: Describe the technique for OPEN SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBULOPLASTY.
short_answer.model_answer:
Mucasal flap from inner aspect of lower lip is used to increase the
vestibular depth in anterior mandibular labial vestibule.
The mentalis muscle is detached from the periosteum to required
depth and the vestibule is deepened via supraperiesteal dissection.
The flap is then redirected & placed directly against the periasteum
and sutured at the new depth of the vestibule.
Raw area is left on the Lip side to be healed by secondary intention.
Periosteum of bone is left intact.
Drawback - scarring of mucosa with subsequent decreased flexibility
of lower lip.
ЭЦОГИНОНТ 29 ГИЦАЯТ

short_answer.question: Write a short note on MODIFICATION.
short_answer.model_answer:
Similar to Kazanjians technique in which a flap of Labial mucasa is raised,
[but the vestibule is deepened by subperiasteal stripping instead of supraperias-
leal dissection]
In this procedure, flap is reflected from the inner aspect of the Lip till the
alveolar crest and periosteum is reflected from crest of the ridge till the
desired depth of the vestibule.
The is periasteum is now sutured to the Lip mucosal margin and then Lip flap
is sutured ^ at the required vestibular depth. biowNalayM beritus
art
at
A stent or splint is used for adaptation.
Advantage less scaring of the lip mucasa

short_answer.question: Describe the technique for X pridut.
short_answer.model_answer:
It is the reverse of Kazanjian's technique.
Here flap is reflected from alveolar crest till vermilion border of the Lip.
Supraperiasteal dissection is done till desired vestibular depth & edge of
the mobilised flap is pushed into vestibular depth.
This flap is held in position with sutures passed through the chin area
extraorally and tied around the rubber catheter.
Here alveolar bone is covered by periosteum which heals quickly by
granulation.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on AUTOCLAVE.
short_answer.model_answer:
It is one of the most common methods of moist heat sterilisation.
Thereare 2 types of autoclaves available
a> Downward displacement steriliser - this is nonvacuum type autoclave.
b> Steam sterilisers with pre and past-vacuum processes

short_answer.question: Write a short note on WORKING PRINCIPLE.
short_answer.model_answer:
Water boils when its vapour pressure equals to that of surrounding atmas-
- phere. Hence, when pressure inside a closed ЮЯТИОЭ vessel no increases the temp.
at which water boils also increases.
This principle has been applied in autoclave.
Bacteria are more susceptible to moist heat as bacterial protein
coagulates rapidly. Saturated steam can penetrate porous material
easily. When steam comes in contact with a cooler surface it condenses
to water and liberates it Patent heat to that surface.
The Large reduction in volume sucks in more steam to the same site and
the process continues till the temperature of the article is raised to that of
steam. The condensed water produces moist conditions for killing the
microbes present.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on STERILISATION PROCESS AND CONDITIONS.
short_answer.model_answer:
The sterilisation process is composed of 3 main phases -
a> pretreatment phase or heat up cycle
b> sterilising phase or sterilisation cycle
c> post treatment phase or depressurisation cycle
Temp 121°C, chamber pressure 15 lbs persquare inch,
holding time 15 min [generally used]
Sterilisation can also be done at higher temperatures,
126°C, 20 lbs psi for 10 min
or

The advantages of using steam sterilisation AVA are -
a> high penetrating capacity
b> time efficient

short_answer.question: Write a short note on HYPERBARIC OXYGEN THERAPY.
short_answer.model_answer:
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy for radiation damaged tissue was introduced
in 1973 by Greenwood, Gilchrist and Mainous.
Since there is a decrease in vascularity and cellularity of both hard and
soft tissues following irradiation of the bone, the hypoxic compromised
tissues when challenged by a traumatic episode fail to heal.
Delayed fibroblastic activity in neocollagen formation results in chronic
nonhealing wound. These wounds commonly get injected secondarily.
The goal of treating OML and asteoradionecrosis is to overcome hypoxia
& increase vascularity of tissues.
In order to increase tissue oxygenation, the patient is subjected to increa-
sed oxygen concentration and pressure.
HBO therapy consists of 100% oxygen administered at more than
1 absolute ospheric pressure. [ATA]
This 100% oxygen is a means of increasing the oxygen dissolved in plasma
and tissues that inturn induces angiogenesis in the compromised tissues.
formation

short_answer.question: Write a short note on METHODS OF DELIVERY.
short_answer.model_answer:
of blood
vessel
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is administered in a chamber called the
hyperbaric chamber which may be monoplace or multiplace.
Each session ranges from go to 120 min. where patient breathes 100%
oxygen at 2 to 2.4 ATA.
Each session is termed as dive. A total of 20-30 dives spanning 5 days
a week is planned preoperatively.

The most common site is submandibular gland and Whartons duct,
because its secretionis more alkaline, thicker, and the duct has a
tortuous course and a smaller orifice.
It usually occursin 3rd-5th decades, an with slight male predominance.
Simuttaneous sialdlithiasis in more than 1 salivary gland is rare.
Bilateral occurance of the same gland is also rare.
However, multiple sialoliths in the same duct or gland is common.

short_answer.question: Describe the clinical features of PREVALENCE.
short_answer.model_answer:
Most common salivary gland disease in adults and the second most
common salivary gland disease in children [after mumps]
gland swelling.
It is the major cause of unilateral diffuse widespread parotid or submandibular
Patients complain of pain and swelling during and after eating the food,
when saliva production is at its peak. There is gradual reduction of
pain and swelling, which occurs at the resting period.
It recurs again repeatedly, when flow is stimulated.
In some cases, the swelling is diffuse and resembles cellulitis.
Secondary infection often supervenes with fever
Tenderness, trismus, decreased or absence of salivary flow or purulent
discharge at the orifice of the duct may be present.

short_answer.question: What is the treatment for Nerve excitability tests?
short_answer.model_answer:
Open injuries of the facial nerve should be repaired surgically as soon as
possible.
Immediate primary repair at the time of intentional or unintentional
transection [neurotmesis] gives the best result.
Delayed primary repair or early secondary repair is acceptable, when delay T209
is necessary for certain reasons Like contaminated wound ,compromised medical
status, extensive tissue destruction etc.
Because of progressive distal nerve degeneration and muscle atrophy 12
months is often considered as the Limit for successful facial nerve
repair.

short_answer.model_answer:

FAN EVITRO9948

If there is a gap between proximal and distal part of the nerve with some
part of the nerve loss then autogenous nerve grafting is done
- Hypoglassal nerve is the most commonly used nerve graft
- Sural nerve is an alternative

short_answer.question: Describe the technique for Nerve excitability tests.
short_answer.model_answer:
A> MICROSURGICAL TECHNIQUE
B> REANIMATION
On the non-paralysed side intact buccal branch can be connected to
the paralysed stem of the facial nerve with a sural nerve graft.
The graft is passed over the upperlip.
c> MASKING PROCEDURES
In long standing facial nerve palsy ,the results of micronerve
reconstruction are unfavorable due to atrophy of the facial muscles.
In these patients, regional muscle transpositions with temporal,
massetar or platysma muscles may be considered.
These muscles are transpased and passed through the incisions in the
Lip /cheek groove and then sutured to the dermis.
ТИЗМТАЗЯТ
Alternatively, the strips of fascia lata may be looped through the
cheek and anchored to the temporal and parotid fascia at one
end and to the angle of the mouth and lips at the other end.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on SUPPORTIVE CARE.
short_answer.model_answer:
A tarsarrhaphy or eye patch and methoylcellulose drops are provided
when eyelid closure is inadequate to protect the cornea from injury.
Application of heat to the face, massage to facial muscles and facial
exercises are helpful to maintain muscle tone and prevent muscle
atrophy.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on ODONTOMA.
short_answer.model_answer:
The term odontoma refers to any tumor of odontogenic origin in true
sense. This is the growth in which both epithelial and ectomesenchymal
cells exhibit complete or partial differentiation of tooth formation.
This is considered more as a developmental anomaly, and also as a composite
lesion, as it contains more than one tissue.
Radiographically it is of 2 types-
A> compound Odontoma
B> Complex odontoma
A>

short_answer.question: Describe the management of Generally asymptomatic.
short_answer.model_answer:
alone. completely calcified compound odontoma is biologically inert and can be left
Adequate amount of overlying bone removal should be done to access the
lesion.
Compound odontoma is enuclerated if the capsule is intact.
If the capsule is disrupted then the individual tooth forms are removed
carefully.
B> COMPLEX ODONTOMA
It is a malformation in which all the individual calcified dental tissues are
well formed but are arranged in a disordered pattern.
As a result ,the mass bears no resemblance to enamel and denti normal
tooth structure.

It will range from complete radiolucency In the initial stage to the stage
of maturation , i.e complete calcified structure.
It appears as a densely radiopaque mass, often surrounded by a thin

short_answer.question: Write a short note on LOCAL MEASURES TO CONTROL BLEEDING.
short_answer.model_answer:
Hemostasis is the physiological process that stops bleeding at the site of an
injury while maintaining normal blood flow elsewhere in the circulation.
Hemastatic agents are broadly classified as
a> Local
b> Systemic

short_answer.question: Write a short note on LOCAL HEMOSTATIC MEASURES.
short_answer.model_answer:
In a patient with normal coagulative mechanism, the control of hemorrhage
is dependent on vessel contraction, retraction and clot formation.
Direct control of bleeding at the site of injury is the best method to achieve
hemastasis. Surgical bleeding most of the times is caused by ineffective-
local hemastasis.

short_answer.question: Write a short note on CRYOSURGERY.
short_answer.model_answer:

- It is specially used to treat superficial hemangiomas.

viva.question: What is the composition of local anesthetic solution? Describe the role of each component.
viva.model_answer: **Local anesthetic (LA) solution** contains: **1. Anesthetic agent**: **Amide type** (metabolized in liver): **Lignocaine (Lidocaine) 2%** - most common, onset 2-3 min, duration 60-90 min. **Bupivacaine 0.5%** - long-acting (4-8 hrs), slower onset. **Articaine 4%** - higher concentration, better tissue penetration. **Ester type** (metabolized in blood): **Procaine**, **Benzocaine** (topical) - rarely used now due to allergy risk. **2. Vasoconstrictor**: **Adrenaline (Epinephrine)** - most common. **Concentrations**: 1:80,000, **1:100,000**, **1:200,000** (standard). **Functions**: (a) **Prolongs duration** of anesthesia (reduces systemic absorption), (b) **Reduces bleeding** (hemostasis), (c) **Reduces toxicity** (slower absorption), (d) **Deeper anesthesia** (keeps LA at site). **Contraindications**: Uncontrolled hypertension, recent MI, hyperthyroidism, use with caution in cardiac patients. **3. Preservative**: **Sodium metabisulfite** - preserves vasoconstrictor (antioxidant). Can cause allergic reactions in asthmatics. **4. Vehicle/Solvent**: **Sterile water** or **isotonic saline**. **5. pH buffer**: **Sodium hydroxide** - adjusts pH (acidic pH for stability, but delays onset). **Standard cartridge**: **1.8 mL** containing 36 mg lignocaine (2%) + 18 mcg adrenaline (1:100,000). **Maximum safe dose**: Lignocaine **without adrenaline**: 4.4 mg/kg (max 300 mg). Lignocaine **with adrenaline**: 7 mg/kg (max 500 mg). For 70 kg adult: ~10 cartridges max with adrenaline.

viva.question: Describe the technique of inferior alveolar nerve block.
viva.model_answer: **Inferior Alveolar Nerve (IAN) block** anesthetizes **mandibular teeth** (one side), **lower lip**, **chin**, **mucosa**, **bone**. **Nerve anesthetized**: Inferior alveolar nerve (branch of posterior division of mandibular nerve V3). **Landmarks**: (1) **Pterygomandibular raphe** - fold of tissue between pterygoid muscles and mandible, (2) **Coronoid notch** - depression anterior to anterior border of ramus, (3) **Occlusal plane** of mandibular molars. **Injection site**: **1 cm above occlusal plane** of mandibular molars, **3/4 distance** from anterior border of ramus. **Technique**: (1) **Patient position**: Mouth wide open (relaxes muscles, widens pterygomandibular space), (2) **Barrel of syringe**: Place over **contralateral premolars** (crosses midline), parallel to occlusal plane, (3) **Needle insertion**: **25 mm long needle**, insert at pterygomandibular raphe, **1 cm above occlusal plane**, advance **20-25 mm** until bone contacted, (4) **Aspirate**: Check for blood (avoid intravascular injection), (5) **Deposit solution**: 1.8 mL slowly over 60 seconds (rapid injection causes pain), (6) **Partially withdraw** needle (5-10 mm), deposit **0.2-0.4 mL** for **lingual nerve** (anesthetizes tongue, lingual gingiva). **Onset**: 3-5 minutes. **Duration**: 90 minutes with adrenaline. **Areas anesthetized**: Mandibular teeth to midline, buccal mucosa of molars, anterior 2/3 tongue (lingual nerve), lower lip/chin (mental nerve branch). **Complications**: Trismus, hematoma, temporary facial nerve paralysis (if LA enters parotid gland), paresthesia (rare).

viva.question: What are the complications of local anesthesia?
viva.model_answer: **Complications** categorized as: **LOCAL**: **1. Pain on injection**: Rapid injection, cold solution, low pH solution. **Prevention**: Warm cartridge, inject slowly. **2. Hematoma**: Needle trauma to blood vessel. Common in **PSA block** (pterygoid plexus). **Management**: Pressure, ice pack. **3. Trismus**: Muscle spasm post-injection (pterygoid muscles). Common after **IAN block**. **Management**: Heat, analgesics, jaw exercises, antibiotics if infected. **4. Needle breakage**: Rare with modern needles. **Prevention**: Don't bend needle, don't insert to hub, change direction slowly. **5. Paresthesia**: Nerve injury (mechanical/chemical trauma). **Lingual nerve** and **IAN** most common. Usually **temporary** (weeks-months), rarely permanent. **6. Facial nerve paralysis**: LA enters **parotid gland** during IAN block. **Temporary** (2-3 hours) - eye cannot close, drooping face. **Management**: Reassure, protect cornea (eye patch), resolves spontaneously. **7. Infection**: Contaminated needle/solution. Rare. **8. Self-inflicted trauma**: Patient bites lip/tongue while numb. **SYSTEMIC**: **1. Syncope (Fainting)**: Most common emergency. **Psychogenic** (fear, anxiety). **Symptoms**: Pallor, sweating, nausea, loss of consciousness. **Management**: Position supine, elevate legs, loosen tight clothing, oxygen, ammonia inhalants. **2. Toxic overdose**: Excessive dose or intravascular injection. **CNS symptoms** (early): Tingling, numbness (perioral), lightheadedness, tinnitus, drowsiness, **seizures**. **CVS symptoms** (late): Bradycardia, hypotension, **cardiac arrest**. **Management**: Stop injection, oxygen, IV fluids, anticonvulsants (diazepam), cardiopulmonary support. **3. Allergic reactions**: Rare (true allergy <1%). More common with **esters** than **amides**. Often reaction to **preservative** (metabisulfite). **Symptoms**: Urticaria, bronchospasm, **anaphylaxis** (rare). **Management**: Stop injection, **adrenaline** (anaphylaxis), antihistamines, corticosteroids, oxygen. **4. Epinephrine overdose**: Tachycardia, palpitations, hypertension, anxiety. Rare with dental concentrations.

viva.question: Describe the posterior superior alveolar (PSA) nerve block technique.
viva.model_answer: **PSA nerve block** anesthetizes **maxillary molars** (except mesiobuccal root of 1st molar supplied by MSA). **Nerve**: Posterior superior alveolar nerve (branch of maxillary nerve V2). **Injection site**: **Height of mucobuccal fold** opposite **2nd maxillary molar**, **45° angle** to occlusal plane, directed **upward, inward, backward** toward **pterygomaxillary fissure**. **Technique**: (1) **Retract cheek** laterally, (2) **Needle**: 25 mm short needle, (3) **Insert** at mucobuccal fold above 2nd molar, bevel toward bone, (4) **Advance** 16 mm at **45° angle** (upward, medial, posterior), **STOP** before 16 mm if bone contacted (risk of pterygoid plexus puncture), (5) **Aspirate** carefully (pterygoid plexus proximity), (6) **Deposit** 1.8 mL slowly. **Areas anesthetized**: Maxillary **2nd and 3rd molars** completely, **1st molar** (distobuccal and palatal roots only - mesiobuccal from MSA). **Onset**: 3-5 minutes. **Complications**: **Hematoma** (most common - pterygoid venous plexus puncture) - presents as **swelling, ecchymosis**. **Management**: Pressure, ice, reassure patient.

viva.question: What are the contraindications to use of vasoconstrictors in local anesthesia?
viva.model_answer: **Absolute contraindications**: **1. Recent myocardial infarction** (within 6 months) - risk of arrhythmias, re-infarction. **2. Recent cerebrovascular accident** (stroke) - risk of hypertension, re-bleeding. **3. Severe uncontrolled hypertension** (>180/110 mmHg) - risk of hypertensive crisis, stroke. **4. Severe coronary insufficiency** - unstable angina. **5. Uncontrolled hyperthyroidism** - potentiates adrenaline effects (tachycardia, arrhythmias). **6. Uncontrolled diabetes** - adrenaline causes hyperglycemia. **7. Pheochromocytoma** - catecholamine-secreting tumor, adrenaline worsens hypertension. **8. Sulfite allergy** (severe asthmatics) - preservative in vasoconstrictor solutions. **Relative contraindications** (use with caution, reduce concentration): **1. Controlled hypertension** - use **1:200,000** adrenaline, limit dose. **2. Stable angina pectoris** - use minimal dose. **3. Cardiac arrhythmias** - arrhythmogenic potential. **4. Concurrent medication**: **Non-selective beta-blockers** (propranolol) - unopposed alpha effects cause severe hypertension. **Tricyclic antidepressants** - potentiate adrenaline. **MAO inhibitors** - hypertensive crisis. **Cocaine users** - severe cardiovascular effects. **Safe practice in cardiac patients**: Use **1:200,000 adrenaline**, **limit to 2 cartridges** (36 mcg total), **aspirate carefully** (avoid intravascular), **inject slowly**, **monitor vital signs**, **avoid retraction cord** soaked in adrenaline. **Alternative**: **Mepivacaine 3% (plain)** - provides 20-40 min pulpal anesthesia without vasoconstrictor, suitable for short procedures in high-risk patients.

viva.question: What are the indications and contraindications for tooth extraction?
viva.model_answer: **INDICATIONS**: **1. Dental caries**: Grossly carious tooth **not restorable**, extensive destruction below gingival margin. **2. Periodontal disease**: Advanced periodontitis with **grade III mobility**, bone loss >2/3 root length, poor prognosis. **3. Periapical pathology**: Chronic periapical abscess **not amenable to RCT** (curved roots, calcified canals, perforations). **4. Impacted teeth**: **Symptomatic** impaction (pain, infection, cyst formation), **prophylactic** removal (orthodontic reasons, before orthognathic surgery). **5. Supernumerary teeth**: Interfering with eruption, causing malocclusion. **6. Orthodontic treatment**: Create space for alignment (commonly premolars). **7. Fractured teeth**: Non-restorable **crown-root fracture**, **vertical root fracture**. **8. Pre-prosthetic**: Before complete denture fabrication, teeth with poor prognosis. **9. Pre-radiotherapy**: Diseased teeth in radiation field (prevent osteoradionecrosis). **10. Malpositioned teeth**: Causing trauma to soft tissues, no functional value. **11. Teeth in fracture line**: Mandibular fracture - tooth in fracture line preventing reduction. **12. Esthetic reasons**: Patient request (after full information). **CONTRAINDICATIONS**: **Absolute**: None (if emergency, all relative contraindications can be managed). **Relative** (defer elective extraction): **1. Uncontrolled systemic disease**: **Uncontrolled diabetes** (hyperglycemia >200 mg/dL, ketoacidosis), **Uncontrolled hypertension** (>180/110), **Recent MI** (<6 months), **Unstable angina**, **Severe cardiac disease**. **2. Blood dyscrasias**: **Uncontrolled bleeding disorders** (hemophilia, severe thrombocytopenia <50,000), **Anticoagulant therapy** (high INR >3-4). **Management**: Reduce warfarin, check INR, local hemostatic measures. **3. Immunosuppression**: **Uncontrolled leukemia**, **Chemotherapy** (defer if neutrophils <1000), **Uncontrolled HIV/AIDS**. **4. Acute infections**: **Acute pericoronitis**, **Cellulitis** - stabilize with antibiotics first. **Exception**: If tooth is **source of infection**, extract (with antibiotic cover). **5. Pregnancy**: **1st trimester** (organogenesis risk), **3rd trimester** (supine hypotension, premature labor risk). **2nd trimester safest**. **Emergency** treatment any trimester. **6. Radiotherapy**: Within **radiation field** - risk of osteoradionecrosis. Extract 2 weeks **before** radiotherapy or defer until 6-12 months **after**. **7. Bisphosphonate therapy**: Risk of **medication-related osteonecrosis of jaw (MRONJ)**. Assess risk, preventive measures.

viva.question: Describe the principles and steps of tooth extraction using forceps.
viva.model_answer: **Principles of forceps extraction**: **1. Expansion of alveolar bone**: Controlled force expands socket. **2. Luxation**: Loosen tooth by breaking PDL fibers, dilating socket. **3. Delivery**: Remove tooth from socket. **STEPS**: **1. Pre-operative**: (a) **Consent** - explain procedure, risks, (b) **Radiograph** - assess root morphology, bone, pathology, (c) **Local anesthesia** - adequate. **2. Tissue reflection**: **Mucoperiosteal flap** if surgical extraction needed. Simple extraction - no flap. **3. Luxation with elevator**: **Straight elevator** (Coupland's) - insert into **mesial/distal PDL space**, apply **rotational/lever force** (fulcrum on alveolar bone). Loosens tooth, expands socket. **Curved elevator** (Cryer's) - for roots. **4. Forceps application**: Select **appropriate forceps** (matches tooth anatomy). **Upper anterior**: Straight forceps. **Upper premolars**: Upper universal. **Upper molars**: Upper molar (with palatal projection). **Lower anterior/premolars**: Lower universal. **Lower molars**: Lower molar (beaked forceps - fit into furcation). **5. Grasping**: Apply forceps **parallel to long axis**, beaks engage tooth **below gingival margin** on **cementum** (not crown - fracture risk). Firm grasp. **6. Luxation movements**: **Buccal-lingual** (primary) or **mesial-distal** movements. Apply **controlled apical pressure** simultaneously (seats forceps deeper). **Expansion** of socket. **Upper anteriors**: Mainly buccal (thin labial bone). **Upper premolars**: Buccal-palatal. **Upper molars**: Buccal first, then palatal. **Lower molars**: **Figure-of-eight** movement (buccal-lingual), then lift lingually (lingual bone thinner). **7. Delivery**: Once mobile, deliver tooth **occlusally** in arc of rotation. Avoid excessive force. **8. Post-extraction**: (a) **Socket inspection** - ensure complete removal (all roots), check for fracture fragments, (b) **Curettage** - remove granulation tissue, **cyst/granuloma**, (c) **Compress alveolar bone** - digital pressure (restores expanded bone), (d) **Sutures** if indicated (flap closure, large socket, bleeding), (e) **Gauze pack** - patient bites for 30 minutes, (f) **Post-op instructions** - avoid rinsing 24 hrs, soft diet, analgesics. **Complications to avoid**: Fracture (root/crown), luxation into sinus, damage to adjacent teeth, jaw fracture (osteoporotic mandible), nerve injury.

viva.question: What are the complications during and after tooth extraction? How do you manage them?
viva.model_answer: **INTRA-OPERATIVE**: **1. Fracture of tooth/root**: **Common**. **Prevention**: Proper forceps selection, luxation before forceps, controlled force. **Management**: (a) **Visible root**: Remove with elevator/forceps/surgical extraction, (b) **Small root tip** (<3mm, no infection/pathology, difficult access) - **leave**, inform patient, monitor radiographically. **2. Fracture of alveolar bone**: Thin labial/lingual plate fractures. **Management**: Remove loose fragment, smooth edges, compress socket. **3. Fracture of maxillary tuberosity**: During **upper molar extraction** (especially 3rd molar). **Prevention**: Luxate carefully, surgical extraction if ankylosed. **Management**: **Small fragment** - remove. **Large fragment with tooth attached** - **replace** and splint (may retain vitality), refer. **4. Oro-antral communication (OAC)**: Upper molar/premolar extraction. **Signs**: Bubbling of blood, nose-holding test positive. **Management**: **Small (<2mm)** - encourage clot, no nose blowing, antibiotics. **Large (>2-3mm)** - **buccal advancement flap** closure, antibiotics (Amoxicillin-Clavulanate). **5. Displacement into maxillary sinus**: **Management**: **Do NOT irrigate** (pushes deeper). **Caldwell-Luc** procedure or endoscopic retrieval. **6. Jaw fracture**: **Risk factors**: Atrophic mandible, large cyst, deeply impacted 3rd molar. **Prevention**: Controlled force, support mandible. **Management**: MMF/ORIF. **7. Soft tissue damage**: Laceration, damage to adjacent teeth. **Prevent**: Proper retraction, finger guard. **8. Nerve injury**: **IAN** (mandibular 3rd molar), **Lingual nerve**. **Prevention**: Careful technique. **POST-OPERATIVE**: **1. Hemorrhage** (see omfs009). **2. Dry socket** (see omfs010). **3. Pain**: **Management**: Analgesics (NSAIDs - Ibuprofen 400mg + Paracetamol 500mg). **4. Swelling**: **Management**: Ice packs (24 hrs), elevation, antibiotics if infection. **5. Trismus**: Limited mouth opening. **Management**: Heat, jaw exercises, analgesics. **6. Infection**: **Management**: Antibiotics (Amoxicillin 500mg or Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg), analgesics, drainage if abscess. **7. Paresthesia**: IAN/lingual nerve. Usually **temporary** (3-6 months). **Management**: Reassure, Vitamin B12, observe.

viva.question: Classify post-extraction hemorrhage. How do you manage it?
viva.model_answer: **Post-extraction hemorrhage** classified by **timing**: **1. Primary (Immediate)**: During/immediately after extraction (within hours). **Causes**: Trauma to blood vessels, inadequate compression. **2. Reactionary**: Within **24 hours**. **Causes**: Vasodilator effect (anesthesia wears off), clot dislodgement (rinsing, sucking), hypertension. **3. Secondary**: **After 24 hours** (2-7 days). **Causes**: **Infection** (clot breakdown), local factors (sharp bone), systemic factors. **MANAGEMENT (Stepwise)**: **1. History**: Onset, duration, amount, **bleeding disorder**, **medications** (aspirin, warfarin, NSAIDs), previous bleeding episodes, **systemic disease** (liver disease, leukemia). **2. Examination**: **Locate source** - socket, gingiva, soft tissue laceration. **Distinguish**: Bleeding vs oozing vs blood-tinged saliva (patient exaggerates). **3. Local measures** (FIRST-LINE): (a) **Remove clot** - irrigate socket with saline, suction (expose bleeding point), (b) **Identify source** - socket (bone/soft tissue), torn gingiva, (c) **Gauze pressure** - fold **moistened gauze**, place over socket, patient bites firmly for **20-30 minutes**. **Success**: 90% controlled. (d) If fails - **Hemostatic agents**: **Oxidized cellulose (Surgicel)**, **Gelfoam** (absorbable gelatin sponge), **Tranexamic acid** soaked gauze - pack into socket. (e) **Sutures** - approximate socket edges (reduces exposed bone), **figure-of-eight suture** over socket. (f) **Bone wax** - if bleeding from bone. **4. Systemic measures** (if local fails): (a) **Bleeding disorder suspected**: **Coagulation profile** (PT, aPTT, INR, platelet count, bleeding time). (b) **Hospitalization** if severe/uncontrolled. (c) **Blood transfusion** if severe blood loss. (d) **Correct coagulopathy**: **High INR** - Vitamin K, FFP. **Thrombocytopenia** - platelet transfusion. **Hemophilia** - Factor VIII/IX replacement. (e) **Antifibrinolytic** - **Tranexamic acid** 500mg orally TID (prevents clot lysis). **5. Prevention** (high-risk patients): Pre-operative coagulation screen, liaise with physician, modify anticoagulant (reduce to therapeutic range INR 2-3), local hemostatic measures, avoid IM injections, avoid aspirin post-op.

viva.question: What is dry socket (alveolar osteitis)? Describe etiology, clinical features, and management.
viva.model_answer: **Dry socket (Alveolar osteitis)** is a **post-extraction complication** characterized by **severe pain** due to **loss/disintegration of blood clot**, exposing **bare bone**. **Incidence**: 1-5% (all extractions), **20-30%** (mandibular 3rd molars - highest risk). **Etiology (Multifactorial)**: **1. Fibrinolysis**: Excessive **plasmin activity** (dissolves clot). **Bacteria** release fibrinolytic enzymes. **2. Trauma**: **Difficult extraction**, excessive curettage (damages bone blood supply). **3. Poor blood supply**: **Mandible** (dense cortical bone, less vascularity) - 10x more common than maxilla. **4. Bacterial infection**: Pre-existing **pericoronitis**, poor oral hygiene. **PREDISPOSING FACTORS**: **1. Smoking** (most significant) - vasoconstriction, negative pressure (suction dislodges clot). **2. Oral contraceptives** - high estrogen increases fibrinolysis. **3. Trauma during extraction** - difficult surgical extraction. **4. Pre-existing infection** - pericoronitis, periodontitis. **5. Excessive rinsing/spitting** (first 24 hrs) - dislodges clot. **6. Poor oral hygiene**. **7. Mandibular 3rd molar extraction** - #1 site. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Timing**: Presents **2-4 days post-extraction** (delayed onset). **2. Severe throbbing pain** - increasing severity, **radiates** to ear/temple/neck (IAN distribution), **not relieved** by routine analgesics. **3. Foul odor/taste** (halitosis, fetor oris) - from necrotic debris. **4. Socket examination**: **Empty socket** (clot absent or gray necrotic debris), **exposed bone** visible (grayish-white, sensitive), **gingival margin** inflamed. **5. No pus** (not infection - inflammatory condition). **6. Regional lymphadenopathy** - tender submandibular nodes. **MANAGEMENT**: **1. Irrigation**: **Warm saline** (gentle irrigation removes debris, necrotic tissue). **Do NOT curette** (damages bone further). **2. Medicated dressing**: (a) **Eugenol-based** - **Alvogyl** (butyl aminobenzoate + iodoform + eugenol) or **Zinc oxide eugenol (ZOE)** paste on ribbon gauze, (b) Pack **loosely** into socket (not packed tightly), (c) **Analgesic + anti-inflammatory** effect (eugenol). **3. Change dressing**: Every **24-48 hours** until pain subsides (3-7 days). **Socket heals by secondary intention**. **4. Analgesics**: **Strong analgesics** - **Ibuprofen 400mg + Paracetamol 500mg** combination. Tramadol if severe. **5. Antibiotics**: **NOT routinely needed** (not bacterial infection). Use only if **severe infection** suspected (fever, swelling, lymphadenopathy) - **Metronidazole 400mg TDS** or **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg**. **6. Instructions**: Gentle rinsing (warm saline after 24 hrs), soft diet, oral hygiene. **PREVENTION**: Minimize trauma, avoid excessive curettage, **atraumatic technique**, avoid smoking 48 hrs post-extraction, antibiotics (Metronidazole/Amoxicillin) for high-risk (impacted 3rd molars), chlorhexidine rinse post-operatively.

viva.question: What are the indications for surgical extraction? Outline the procedure.
viva.model_answer: **Surgical (Transalveolar/Open) extraction** requires **mucoperiosteal flap**, **bone removal**, ± **tooth sectioning**. **Indications**: **1. Fractured root** during extraction (not retrievable with forceps), **2. Root tips** (curved, divergent, ankylosed), **3. Impacted teeth**, **4. Ankylosed teeth**, **5. Hypercementosis**, **6. Dense surrounding bone** (sclerotic), **7. Teeth with abnormal anatomy** (dilacerated roots), **8. Grossly carious teeth** (insufficient crown for forceps), **9. Planned ridge augmentation**. **PROCEDURE**: **1. Flap design**: **Envelope flap** (sulcular incision + vertical releasing incisions). **Triangular flap** - one vertical incision. **Two-sided flap** - two vertical incisions (better access). **Principles**: (a) Broad base (blood supply), (b) Include one intact tooth mesial/distal (papilla preservation), (c) Vertical incision away from extraction site (avoid wound over socket). **2. Flap elevation**: **Periosteal elevator** - full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap, expose bone. **3. Bone removal**: (a) **Buccal bone** - surgical bur (round/fissure) with **copious irrigation** (prevent bone necrosis), (b) Remove **minimum bone** necessary (preserve ridge height), (c) Create **gutter** around root (buccal + interdental bone). **4. Tooth sectioning** (if needed): **Multirooted teeth**: Separate at **furcation** (remove roots individually). **Single-rooted**: Vertical section if needed. **5. Tooth/root delivery**: **Elevators** - lever out sections, controlled force. **6. Socket debridement**: Curettage (remove granulation/cyst), irrigation, **smooth sharp bone edges** (bone file/rongeur). **7. Flap closure**: **Primary closure** - **interrupted 3-0 silk sutures**, approximate flap margins (tension-free). **8. Post-op instructions**: Ice (24 hrs), soft diet, oral hygiene, analgesics, antibiotics if indicated. **Suture removal**: **7 days**.

viva.question: What are the special considerations for tooth extraction in medically compromised patients?
viva.model_answer: **CARDIAC PATIENTS**: **Recent MI** (<6 months) - defer elective extraction. **Unstable angina** - defer. **Stable cardiac disease** - proceed with caution. **Prophylactic antibiotics**: **Infective endocarditis (IE) risk**: **High-risk** (prosthetic valves, previous IE, congenital heart disease, cardiac transplant with valvulopathy) - **Amoxicillin 2g** oral **1 hour before** procedure. **Moderate-risk** - no prophylaxis needed (AHA 2007 guidelines). **Anticoagulant therapy**: **Warfarin** - check **INR** (safe if 2-3.5), do NOT stop warfarin (thromboembolic risk), **local hemostatic measures** (sutures, pressure, tranexamic acid rinse). **Aspirin/Clopidogrel** - continue, local measures. **DIABETIC PATIENTS**: **Controlled diabetes** (FBS <200 mg/dL) - routine extraction. **Uncontrolled** (FBS >200 mg/dL, ketoacidosis) - defer, stabilize first. **Morning appointment** (after breakfast + medication). **Prophylactic antibiotics** if **poor control** or **extensive surgery** - **Amoxicillin 500mg TDS** for 5 days. **Prolonged healing**, infection risk. **BISPHOSPHONATE THERAPY**: **Risk of MRONJ** (medication-related osteonecrosis of jaw). **Assessment**: (a) **IV bisphosphonates** (cancer patients - Zoledronic acid) - **high risk**, (b) **Oral bisphosphonates** (osteoporosis - Alendronate) - low risk if <4 years use. **Management**: **High-risk** - **drug holiday** (stop 3 months before + 3 months after extraction if oncologist permits), **atraumatic technique**, **primary closure**, **antibiotics** (Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 7 days). **Low-risk** - routine extraction, antibiotics, follow-up. **BLEEDING DISORDERS**: **Hemophilia** - Factor VIII/IX replacement pre-operatively, **hospital setting**, local measures, **tranexamic acid** mouthwash. **Thrombocytopenia** - platelet transfusion if <50,000. **PREGNANCY**: **2nd trimester safest**. **Avoid** 1st trimester (organogenesis), 3rd trimester (supine hypotension). **Elective** - defer. **Emergency** - any trimester with precautions. **Avoid X-rays** (lead apron if essential). **Anesthesia**: Lignocaine safe. **Antibiotics**: **Amoxicillin/Penicillin** safe. **Avoid**: Tetracycline, metronidazole (1st trimester). **Analgesics**: **Paracetamol** safe. **Avoid**: NSAIDs (3rd trimester - premature closure of ductus arteriosus), Aspirin. **RADIOTHERAPY**: Extract diseased teeth **2 weeks before** radiotherapy (in radiation field). **After radiotherapy** - risk of osteoradionecrosis (ORN). If extraction needed - **hyperbaric oxygen** (20 sessions before + 10 after), antibiotics, atraumatic technique. **Defer** elective extractions 6-12 months post-RT.

viva.question: What post-extraction instructions should be given to patients?
viva.model_answer: **Post-extraction care instructions**: **IMMEDIATE (First 24 hours)**: **1. Gauze pack**: Bite firmly on **folded gauze** for **30 minutes** (promotes clot formation). Replace if needed. **2. No rinsing/spitting** for **24 hours** - dislodges clot (dry socket risk). **3. No drinking through straw** - negative pressure dislodges clot. **4. No smoking** for **48 hours** - vasoconstriction, negative pressure, delayed healing, dry socket risk (5x increase). **5. Diet**: **Soft, cool diet** (ice cream, yogurt, soups). **Avoid hot liquids** (24 hrs - causes vasodilation, bleeding). **Chew on opposite side**. **6. Ice packs**: Apply **20 min on/20 min off** for **first 4-6 hours** (reduces swelling). **7. Rest**: Avoid strenuous activity, exercise (24 hrs) - increases BP, bleeding. **8. Sleep**: **Head elevated** (2 pillows) - reduces swelling. **NEXT 2-7 DAYS**: **1. Warm saline rinses**: After **24 hours**, gentle rinsing **after meals** (1 tsp salt in 1 cup warm water). **Do NOT rinse vigorously** - dislodges clot. **2. Oral hygiene**: Resume **gentle brushing** adjacent teeth. **Avoid brushing extraction site** (3-4 days). **Chlorhexidine 0.2% mouthwash** (if prescribed) - 2x/day. **3. Diet**: Progress from soft to regular diet as tolerated. **4. Pain management**: **Analgesics** - **Ibuprofen 400mg** (every 6-8 hrs) + **Paracetamol 500mg**. Take **before anesthesia wears off**. **Avoid aspirin** (increases bleeding). **5. Antibiotics**: If prescribed - **complete full course** (Amoxicillin 500mg TDS x 5 days). **6. Swelling**: **Peak 48-72 hours**. **Heat packs** (after 24 hrs) if swelling persists. **WHAT TO EXPECT**: **1. Bleeding**: **Slight oozing** normal for 24 hrs (blood-tinged saliva). **Persistent bleeding** - bite gauze 30 min, if continues - contact dentist. **2. Pain**: **Moderate pain** 2-3 days (peaks 12 hrs post-extraction), manageable with analgesics. **Severe increasing pain** after 3 days - **dry socket**, contact dentist. **3. Swelling**: Normal (2-3 days), then gradually reduces. **4. Bruising**: May appear after 2-3 days (ecchymosis) - resolves in 7-10 days. **WHEN TO CALL DENTIST**: **Excessive bleeding** (not controlled with gauze), **Severe pain** (unrelieved by medication, especially increasing after 3-4 days), **Swelling increasing** after 3 days, **Fever** (>100°F), **Difficulty swallowing/breathing**, **Pus discharge**, **Adverse drug reactions**. **SUTURE REMOVAL**: **7 days** post-operatively (if non-resorbable sutures used).

viva.question: Define impacted tooth. Classify impacted mandibular third molars.
viva.model_answer: **Impacted tooth**: A tooth that **fails to erupt** into normal functional position within expected time, **completely/partially blocked** by bone, soft tissue, or adjacent tooth. **Most commonly impacted**: **Mandibular 3rd molars** (48%), Maxillary 3rd molars (38%), Maxillary canines (10%). **CLASSIFICATIONS OF MANDIBULAR 3rd MOLAR**: **1. WINTER'S CLASSIFICATION** (based on **angulation** of long axis to 2nd molar): **a) Mesioangular** (43%) - most common, crown angulated mesially toward 2nd molar. **Easiest** to remove. **b) Horizontal** (3%) - long axis perpendicular to 2nd molar (90°). **Most difficult**. **c) Vertical** (38%) - long axis parallel to 2nd molar (normal eruption path). **d) Distoangular** (6%) - crown angulated distally. **e) Inverted** (rare) - crown pointing downward. **f) Buccoangular/Linguoangular** - buccal/lingual tilt. **2. PELL & GREGORY CLASSIFICATION**: **Relationship to ascending ramus & 2nd molar**: **PART A - Depth** (relative to occlusal plane of 2nd molar): **Class I**: **Occlusal surface** of 3rd molar at **same level** as occlusal surface of 2nd molar. **Class II**: Occlusal surface **between occlusal plane and CEJ** of 2nd molar. **Class III**: **Completely below CEJ** of 2nd molar (deepest - most difficult). **PART B - Space** (relationship to ramus): **Class A**: **Adequate space** between anterior border of ramus and distal surface of 2nd molar (**>8mm**) - entire tooth anterior to ramus. **Class B**: **Reduced space** (3-8mm) - **part of crown** covered by ramus. **Class C**: **No space** (<3mm) - tooth **completely** within ramus. **Most difficult**: **Class III C** (deep, within ramus) + **Horizontal** angulation. **Clinical significance**: Determines surgical difficulty, approach, bone removal required, complications risk.

viva.question: What are the indications for removal of impacted third molars? Describe the surgical procedure.
viva.model_answer: **INDICATIONS FOR REMOVAL**: **Therapeutic** (symptomatic impactions): **1. Pericoronitis** - recurrent infection of soft tissue covering partially erupted tooth. **2. Dental caries** - impacted tooth or adjacent 2nd molar (distal caries - hard to restore). **3. Periodontal disease** - bone loss distal to 2nd molar. **4. Cyst/tumor formation** - **dentigerous cyst** (around crown), odontogenic tumors. **5. Root resorption** - of adjacent 2nd molar. **6. Pain** - unexplained pain from impacted tooth. **7. Jaw fracture** - tooth in fracture line or weakens mandible. **Prophylactic** (asymptomatic): **1. Orthodontic treatment** - prevent relapse, create space. **2. Before orthognathic surgery**. **3. Radiotherapy** - remove teeth in radiation field. **4. High risk of complications** if left. **Controversial**: Routine prophylactic removal of asymptomatic impactions **NOT recommended** (NICE guidelines). **SURGICAL PROCEDURE (Mandibular 3rd molar)**: **PRE-OPERATIVE**: (1) Clinical + **Radiographic assessment** - **OPG/CBCT** (anatomy, depth, angulation, relation to **IAN canal**), (2) **Informed consent** - risks (nerve injury, dry socket, pain, swelling), (3) **Local anesthesia** - IAN block + long buccal nerve block. **SURGICAL STEPS**: **1. Incision**: **Ward's incision** (most common) - starts on **buccal aspect** of 2nd molar (mid-tooth), extends **posteriorly along external oblique ridge**, then curves **medially** over retromolar area. Creates **triangular flap**. **2. Flap elevation**: Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap using **periosteal elevator**, expose bone overlying impacted tooth. **Retract** with **Howarth's elevator**. **3. Bone removal** (if needed): **Surgical bur** (straight/angled handpiece) with **copious saline irrigation** (prevent bone necrosis). Remove bone **buccally** and **distally** (create gutter around crown). **Remove minimum necessary** bone. **4. Tooth sectioning**: **Horizontal impaction**: Section crown from roots (**horizontal cut** at CEJ), remove crown first, then roots. **Mesioangular**: Section **mesiodistal** (separate mesial portion), remove piece by piece. **Vertical**: May not need sectioning if adequate space. **5. Tooth delivery**: **Elevators** (Coupland's, Cryer's) - carefully luxate and deliver sections. **Avoid excessive force** (IAN injury, fracture). **6. Socket management**: (a) **Debride** - remove follicular sac, granulation tissue, (b) **Irrigate** copiously with saline, (c) **Smooth sharp bone** edges (rongeur/bone file), (d) **Inspect** for root tips, foreign bodies. **7. Hemostasis**: **Pressure** with gauze, **bone wax** if bone bleeding, usually self-limiting. **8. Flap closure**: **Interrupted 3-0 silk sutures** - **primary tension-free closure**. **9. Post-operative**: (a) **Gauze pack** - bite 30 min, (b) **Ice** externally (4-6 hrs), (c) **Analgesics** - Ibuprofen + Paracetamol, (d) **Antibiotics** (controversial - use for difficult surgery/infection) - Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg BD x 5 days, (e) **Chlorhexidine rinses** 0.2% (after 24 hrs). **SUTURE REMOVAL**: **7 days**. **COMPLICATIONS**: **IAN injury** (0.5-5% permanent paresthesia), **Lingual nerve injury** (rare), **Dry socket** (20-30%), **Pain, swelling, trismus**, **Infection**, **Fracture of mandible**, **Hemorrhage**, **Damage to 2nd molar**.

viva.question: What is pericoronitis? Describe clinical features and management.
viva.model_answer: **Pericoronitis** is **acute/chronic inflammation** of **soft tissue (operculum)** overlying **partially erupted tooth**, most commonly **mandibular 3rd molar**. **Etiology**: **1. Trauma**: **Opposing maxillary 3rd molar** bites on inflamed operculum → ulceration → secondary infection. **2. Bacterial**: Food/debris accumulation under operculum → **bacterial proliferation** (mainly **anaerobes**). **3. Insufficient space**: Tooth partially erupted but impacted against 2nd molar or ramus. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **ACUTE PERICORONITIS**: **1. Severe pain** - localized to angle of mandible, **radiates** to ear/throat, difficulty swallowing. **2. Swelling** - pericoronal tissue (operculum) **swollen, red, edematous**, may extend to cheek. **3. Trismus** - limited mouth opening (medial pterygoid spasm). **4. Halitosis** - foul odor/taste. **5. Dysphagia** - difficulty swallowing (if infection spreads). **6. Pus discharge** - purulent exudate from under operculum (on pressure). **7. Systemic signs**: **Fever**, malaise, **lymphadenopathy** (submandibular nodes tender). **8. Opposing tooth trauma** - indentation/ulcer on operculum from upper 3rd molar. **CHRONIC PERICORONITIS**: **Mild symptoms** - intermittent discomfort, slight swelling, mild tenderness. **Episodes of acute exacerbation**. **COMPLICATIONS** (if untreated): **1. Pericoronal abscess** - localized collection of pus. **2. Cellulitis** - spread to **masticator spaces**, **submandibular space**. **3. Ludwig's angina** - bilateral submandibular/sublingual space infection (life-threatening). **4. Mediastinitis** - rare, potentially fatal. **MANAGEMENT**: **ACUTE EPISODE**: **1. Irrigation**: **Hydrogen peroxide (1:1)** or **warm saline** under operculum (remove debris/pus). Use **blunt needle syringe**. **2. Operculectomy** (if accessible): **Excise** redundant soft tissue overlying tooth (diathermy/scalpel). **Reduces recurrence**. **Do NOT** if acute infection/trismus (defer). **3. Grind opposing tooth**: Reduce trauma from biting. **Remove cuspal interference**. **4. Antibiotics**: **Amoxicillin 500mg TDS** or **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg BD** x 5-7 days. **Metronidazole 400mg TDS** (excellent for anaerobes). **5. Analgesics**: **Ibuprofen 400mg + Paracetamol 500mg**. **6. Chlorhexidine rinses**: **0.2% CHX** mouthwash BD. **7. Warm saline rinses**: After 24 hours. **DEFINITIVE TREATMENT** (after acute episode resolves - 4-6 weeks): **Extract impacted 3rd molar** - **prevents recurrence**. **Chronic/recurrent pericoronitis** is **absolute indication** for extraction.

viva.question: What are the treatment options for impacted maxillary canine?
viva.model_answer: **Impacted maxillary canine** - 2nd most common impaction (after 3rd molars). **Location**: **Palatal** (60%), **Labial** (30%), **Mid-alveolar** (10%). **TREATMENT OPTIONS**: **1. Observation**: If **asymptomatic**, adequate space, no pathology, patient refuses treatment. **Monitor** radiographically (3-5 years) for cyst formation, root resorption of adjacent teeth. **2. Extraction**: **Indications**: (a) **Ankylosed** canine (poor prognosis for alignment), (b) **Severe root resorption** of adjacent incisors, (c) **Advanced age** (poor bone remodeling), (d) **Patient refusal** of orthodontics, (e) **Ectopic position** (unreachable/unfavorable). **3. Surgical exposure + Orthodontic alignment**: **GOLD STANDARD** for young patients. **Pre-requisites**: (a) **Adequate space** in arch (orthodontic expansion if needed), (b) **Tooth viable** (no ankylosis, severe dilaceration), (c) **Patient cooperation** (orthodontic treatment 18-24 months). **Procedure**: (a) **Orthodontic preparation** - create space, align adjacent teeth, (b) **Surgical exposure** - **Open technique** (excise tissue, expose crown, pack) or **Closed technique** (expose, bond attachment, replace flap - preferred), (c) **Orthodontic traction** - attach chain/spring, apply force to guide eruption. **Approaches**: **Palatal impaction** - **palatal flap**. **Labial impaction** - **labial/sulcular flap**. **4. Autotransplantation**: Remove impacted canine, place in extraction site of adjacent tooth. **Rarely done** - unpredictable. **Preferred option**: **Surgical exposure + Orthodontics** (preserves canine - esthetic, functional importance).

viva.question: What are supernumerary teeth? Describe types and management.
viva.model_answer: **Supernumerary teeth** are **extra teeth** beyond normal dentition (32 permanent). **Prevalence**: 1-3% (permanent dentition), males > females (2:1). **Etiology**: **Genetic** (hyperactivity of dental lamina), **cleidocranial dysplasia** (multiple supernumeraries). **TYPES**: **1. Mesiodens**: Most common supernumerary. Located **between maxillary central incisors** (midline). Usually **conical/peg-shaped**, **inverted** position. **Complications**: Prevents eruption of permanent incisors, diastema, rotation of adjacent teeth. **2. Paramolar**: Extra molar **buccal/lingual** to molars (usually maxillary 3rd molar area). **3. Distomolar**: **Distal** to 3rd molar (4th molar). Rare. **4. Supplemental (Eumorphic)**: Resembles **normal tooth** morphology. Often **lateral incisor** region. **CLASSIFICATION by morphology**: **Conical** (most common - mesiodens), **Tuberculate** (barrel-shaped with tubercles), **Supplemental** (normal morphology), **Odontoma** (complex/compound). **COMPLICATIONS**: **1. Delayed/prevented eruption** of permanent teeth, **2. Crowding, rotation, displacement** of adjacent teeth, **3. Diastema** (midline spacing), **4. Root resorption** of adjacent teeth, **5. Cyst formation** (dentigerous cyst), **6. Rarely - erupts** normally. **MANAGEMENT**: **INDICATIONS FOR REMOVAL**: (1) **Prevents eruption** of permanent teeth, (2) **Causes displacement/rotation**, (3) **Associated pathology** (cyst, resorption), (4) **Interferes with orthodontic treatment**, (5) **Impedes prosthetic rehabilitation**. **TIMING**: **Early removal** (7-9 years) if interfering with eruption. **Delay** if close to developing roots of adjacent teeth (risk of damage). **Asymptomatic, no complications** - **observe**, extract later if needed. **Procedure**: Usually **surgical extraction** (unerupted) - flap, bone removal, extract, primary closure.

viva.question: How do you prevent and manage inferior alveolar nerve injury during third molar surgery?
viva.model_answer: **IAN injury** during mandibular 3rd molar surgery - serious complication causing **paresthesia** (altered sensation) of lower lip, chin, teeth. **Incidence**: **Temporary** 0.4-8%, **Permanent** 0.4-0.8%. **PREVENTION**: **1. Pre-operative assessment**: **Radiographic evaluation** - **OPG** (shows IAN canal proximity). **High-risk signs**: (a) **Darkening of root** (canal superimposition), (b) **Deflection of canal**, (c) **Loss of cortication** of canal, (d) **Root narrowing**. **CBCT** if high-risk (3D evaluation - exact relationship). **2. Careful surgical technique**: (a) **Tooth sectioning** - divide tooth, remove in pieces (less force, better control), (b) **Avoid excessive force** with elevators (luxate gently), (c) **Preserve lingual plate** (lingual nerve protection), (d) **Avoid drilling deep** near IAN canal, (e) **Nerve visualization** if exposed - protect with retractor, (f) **Remove minimum bone**. **3. Alternative approaches**: **Coronectomy** (high-risk cases) - remove **crown only**, leave roots (if vital, no infection, roots below IAN). Roots gradually migrate away from nerve (3-5 years). **MANAGEMENT OF NERVE INJURY**: **IMMEDIATE** (if suspected): (1) **Inform patient**, document, (2) **Reassure** - most recover (70-90% temporary injuries recover in 3-6 months), (3) **Map sensory deficit** - test light touch, pin-prick, two-point discrimination (lower lip, chin), compare sides. **FOLLOW-UP**: (1) **Re-assess** at 1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months. **2. Pharmacotherapy**: **Vitamin B12** (Methylcobalamin) - nerve regeneration (controversial efficacy). **Corticosteroids** (Dexamethasone) - early (within 48 hrs) if severe injury (reduces edema) - controversial. **3. Microsurgical repair** (if complete transection suspected): **Refer** to oral-maxillofacial surgeon within **3 months** (optimal window). **Direct nerve repair** or **grafting**. **Recovery**: **Neuropraxia** (stretching) - full recovery weeks-months. **Axonotmesis** (partial damage) - partial recovery. **Neurotmesis** (complete transection) - poor prognosis without repair.

viva.question: Describe the spread of odontogenic infections. What are the determinants?
viva.model_answer: **Odontogenic infection** spreads from **tooth apex** through **alveolar bone** into surrounding **soft tissues** (fascial spaces). **PATH OF SPREAD**: **Tooth → Periapical bone → Cortical bone perforation → Soft tissue spaces**. **DETERMINANTS OF SPREAD**: **1. Muscle attachments** (PRIMARY determinant): **a) Above muscle attachments**: Infection points **buccally/labially** → **buccal/vestibular space**. **b) Below muscle attachments**: Infection → **deeper fascial spaces**. **Maxillary teeth**: **Apices above buccinator** attachment → **buccal space/canine fossa**. **Apices below buccinator** → **sublingual/submandibular space** (rare - palatal perforation). **Mandibular teeth**: **Anteriors (incisors)**: Above mentalis → **vestibular**. Below mentalis → **submental space**. **Premolars/Molars**: **Apices above mylohyoid** line → **buccal/vestibular space**. **Apices below mylohyoid** → **submandibular space** (common - 1st/2nd molar). **Lingually**: **Sublingual space** (above mylohyoid). **2. Bone density**: **Thin cortical bone** (maxillary labial, mandibular lingual) - easier perforation. **Thick bone** (mandibular buccal, maxillary palatal) - slow spread, more pain. **3. Location of perforation**: **Buccal** > **Palatal/Lingual** perforation (due to bone thickness). **4. Virulence of organism**: **Aerobic** (early - swelling, cellulitis) vs **Anaerobic** (late - abscess, pus). **Mixed flora** common. **5. Host resistance**: **Immunocompromised** (diabetes, HIV, chemotherapy) - rapid spread, severe infection. **PRIMARY FASCIAL SPACES** (directly connected to jaw): **Vestibular, Buccal space, Canine (infraorbital), Submental, Sublingual, Submandibular**. **SECONDARY SPACES** (infection spreads from primary): **Masticator spaces** (Masseteric, Pterygomandibular, Superficial/Deep temporal), **Lateral pharyngeal, Retropharyngeal, Parotid**. **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Swelling location** indicates **space involved** and **source tooth**. **Severe infections** spread to **multiple spaces** → **Ludwig's angina, Cavernous sinus thrombosis**.

viva.question: What is Ludwig's angina? Describe clinical features and management.
viva.model_answer: **Ludwig's angina** is a **rapidly progressive, life-threatening cellulitis** involving **bilateral submandibular, sublingual, and submental spaces** simultaneously. **BILATERALITY** is key diagnostic feature. **Etiology**: **Odontogenic** (80%) - infection from **mandibular molars** (apices below mylohyoid line). **Precipitating factors**: Poor oral hygiene, immunosuppression (diabetes, HIV), trauma. **Microbiology**: **Mixed aerobic-anaerobic** flora - *Streptococcus viridans, Staphylococcus, Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus*. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. CARDINAL SIGN**: **Bilateral submandibular swelling** - **board-like hardness** ("woody" induration). **Brawny** edema. **2. Submandibular swelling** extends to **neck** (collar-like). **3. Tongue**: **Elevated and pushed posteriorly** (sublingual space involved) - **"bull neck appearance", "tongue protruding"**. **4. Floor of mouth**: **Raised, indurated** (rock-hard). **5. AIRWAY COMPROMISE**: **Most serious** - dyspnea, **stridor**, dysphagia (difficulty swallowing), **drooling** (cannot swallow saliva), **tripod position** (sitting, leaning forward, mouth open). **Progressive** → respiratory obstruction → **asphyxia**. **6. Systemic signs**: **High fever** (>101°F), tachycardia, tachypnea, toxemia. **7. Trismus**: May be **absent** (distinguishes from masticator space infection). **8. NO fluctuance** - cellulitis (not abscess initially). **Later stages** - abscess formation. **COMPLICATIONS** (life-threatening): **1. Airway obstruction** - **asphyxia** (most common cause of death). **2. Descending mediastinitis** - spread along fascial planes to mediastinum (50% mortality). **3. Septicemia**, **Aspiration pneumonia**, **Jugular vein thrombosis**, **Pericarditis**. **MANAGEMENT** (MEDICAL EMERGENCY - hospitalization): **1. AIRWAY MANAGEMENT** (PRIORITY): **Assessment** - stridor, dyspnea, oxygen saturation. **Intubation** if respiratory distress. **Tracheostomy** (if intubation difficult/failed) - preferred (definitive airway). **Fiber-optic intubation** (if expertise available). **2. Incision & Drainage (I&D)**: **Immediate** if pus/abscess. **Multiple incisions**: (a) **Bilateral submandibular** (along lower border of mandible), (b) **Submental** (midline), (c) **Blunt dissection** through spaces (break loculations), (d) **Corrugated rubber drains** placed. **DO NOT WAIT** for fluctuance (cellulitis can be drained to prevent spread). **3. Source control**: **Extract/RCT** offending tooth (once stabilized). **4. Antibiotics** (HIGH DOSE IV): **Empirical** (before culture): **IV Penicillin G** 2-4 million units q4h or **Ampicillin-Sulbactam 3g q6h**. **PLUS Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h** (anaerobic cover). **Alternative** (penicillin allergy): **Clindamycin 600-900mg IV q8h**. **Adjust** after culture/sensitivity. **Duration**: 10-14 days (IV → oral when improving). **5. Supportive care**: **IV fluids** (dehydration), **Analgesics**, **Antipyretics**, **Nutritional support**. **6. Monitor**: Vital signs, airway patency, WBC count, CRP. **PROGNOSIS**: **Mortality 8-10%** if untreated. **<5%** with aggressive treatment. **Early recognition** and **airway management** crucial.

viva.question: What is cavernous sinus thrombosis? How does odontogenic infection lead to it?
viva.model_answer: **Cavernous sinus thrombosis (CST)** is **septic thrombosis** of **cavernous venous sinus** (intracranial venous channel at skull base). **Life-threatening** complication of **maxillary dental infections**. **Mortality**: 20-30% (even with treatment). **PATHWAY OF SPREAD**: **Maxillary teeth** (especially **canine region - "danger triangle of face"**) → **Facial vein/Angular vein** → **Superior/Inferior ophthalmic veins** (valveless) → **Cavernous sinus** → **Septic thrombosis**. **Danger triangle**: Area bounded by **corners of mouth** and **bridge of nose** - venous drainage to cavernous sinus without valves (bidirectional flow). **Bacteria**: *Staphylococcus aureus* (most common), *Streptococcus*, anaerobes. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **EARLY (Unilateral)**: **1. Periorbital edema** - marked swelling around eye. **2. Chemosis** - conjunctival edema (red, swollen conjunctiva). **3. Proptosis** - eye protrusion (forward displacement). **4. Ophthalmoplegia** - **paralysis of extraocular muscles** (CN III, IV, VI pass through cavernous sinus) - **fixed, dilated pupil**, inability to move eye. **5. Ptosis** - drooping eyelid (CN III palsy). **6. Loss of corneal reflex** (CN V1 - ophthalmic). **7. Vision loss** - can be permanent. **LATE (Bilateral)**: **Contralateral eye involvement** (infection crosses to opposite cavernous sinus via intercavernous sinuses) - **pathognomonic sign**. **SYSTEMIC**: **High fever** (103-105°F), **rigors**, **altered mental status**, **seizures** (meningeal irritation), **papilledema** (fundoscopy). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic eye signs + source infection. **2. CT/MRI** with contrast - **filling defect** in cavernous sinus, **orbital cellulitis**. **3. Blood culture** - bacteremia. **4. Lumbar puncture** - if meningitis suspected (contraindicated if raised ICP). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Meningitis**, **Brain abscess**, **Stroke** (internal carotid involvement), **Blindness**, **Death**. **MANAGEMENT** (ICU - medical emergency): **1. HIGH-DOSE IV ANTIBIOTICS**: **Empirical**: **Vancomycin 1g IV q12h** (MRSA cover) **PLUS Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h** (broad-spectrum) **PLUS Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h** (anaerobic). Adjust after cultures. **Duration**: 3-4 weeks (IV minimum 2 weeks). **2. Anticoagulation**: **Controversial**. **Heparin** may reduce thrombosis propagation, improve outcome. Risk of hemorrhage. **3. Source control**: **Drain abscess**, **Extract** tooth, **Orbital decompression** if needed. **4. Supportive care**: **Fluids**, **Antipyretics**, **Monitor neurological status**, **Ophthalmology consult**. **5. Corticosteroids** (if cerebral edema) - **Dexamethasone**. **PREVENTION**: **Early treatment** of maxillary dental infections, **avoid manipulation** of infections in danger triangle (squeezing furuncles/pimples).
