viva.question: What are the principles of antibiotic therapy in odontogenic infections?
viva.model_answer: **Principles**: **1. REMOVE SOURCE**: **Primary treatment** - extraction/RCT of offending tooth, drainage of abscess. **Antibiotics ADJUNCT**, not substitute. **2. EMPIRICAL THERAPY**: Start antibiotics **before culture results** (based on likely organisms). **Modify** when culture/sensitivity available. **3. APPROPRIATE SELECTION**: Based on **microbiology** (mixed aerobic-anaerobic), **severity**, **patient factors**. **ODONTOGENIC FLORA**: **Aerobic**: *Streptococcus viridans* (alpha-hemolytic), *S. milleri group*. **Anaerobic**: *Peptostreptococcus, Prevotella, Porphyromonas, Fusobacterium, Bacteroides*. **ANTIBIOTIC CHOICES**: **MILD-MODERATE INFECTIONS** (localized swelling, no systemic signs): **1st line**: **Amoxicillin 500mg TDS** (7 days). **Broad-spectrum**, covers streptococci. **Extended spectrum**: **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate (Augmentin) 625mg BD/TDS** - covers **beta-lactamase** producing bacteria, **better anaerobic coverage**. **Penicillin allergy**: **Clindamycin 300mg QID** or **Azithromycin 500mg OD** (3-5 days). **Metronidazole 400mg TDS** - **excellent anaerobic** coverage, **synergistic** with penicillin. Often used in **combination**: **Amoxicillin 500mg + Metronidazole 400mg TDS**. **SEVERE INFECTIONS** (cellulitis, spreading, systemic signs, hospitalization): **IV antibiotics**: **Penicillin G 2-4 MU IV q4-6h** or **Ampicillin-Sulbactam 1.5-3g IV q6h** **PLUS Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h**. **Penicillin allergy**: **Clindamycin 600-900mg IV q8h** (excellent bone penetration). **SPECIAL SITUATIONS**: **Penicillin-resistant organisms** (treatment failure): **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate** or **Cefuroxime**. **MRSA suspected** (rare in odontogenic): **Vancomycin**. **DURATION**: **5-7 days** (mild-moderate), **Continue 2-3 days** after signs/symptoms resolve. **Severe**: 10-14 days. **PROPHYLACTIC ANTIBIOTICS**: **Infective endocarditis prevention** (high-risk cardiac patients): **Amoxicillin 2g** oral **1 hour before** invasive dental procedure. **Post-operative infection prevention** (impacted 3rd molar, immunocompromised): **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 625mg BD** starting 1 day before or day of surgery (3-5 days). **CONTRAINDICATIONS TO ANTIBIOTICS ALONE**: **Abscess** requiring drainage, **Spreading infection**, **Systemic involvement**, **Immunocompromised host**. **MONITORING**: Reassess **48-72 hours** - if **no improvement**, consider: **Inadequate drainage**, **Resistant organism** (change antibiotic), **Wrong diagnosis**, **Immunosuppression**.

viva.question: Describe the principles and technique of incision and drainage of abscess.
viva.model_answer: **Incision and drainage (I&D)** - **surgical drainage** of purulent collection (abscess). **PRIMARY treatment** for abscesses (antibiotics adjunct). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Fluctuant abscess** - pus collection present. **2. Spreading cellulitis** - prevent abscess formation. **3. Severe infection** - drain to reduce bacterial load. **PRINCIPLES**: **1. "Pus wherever it is, must be let out"** (Hilton's method). **2. Incision** at **point of maximum fluctuance** (where abscess points). **3. Incision** along **natural skin creases/lines** (cosmetic), or **dependent** area (gravity drainage). **4. Adequate incision** - allow complete drainage, irrigation, exploration. **5. Break loculations** - blunt dissection separates fibrous septa. **6. Drain placement** - prevent premature closure. **CONTRAINDICATIONS (Relative)**: **Cellulitis without abscess** (no pus - antibiotics first), **Bleeding disorders** (correct first). **TECHNIQUE**: **PRE-OPERATIVE**: (1) **Consent**, (2) **Antibiotics** started (before/with I&D), (3) **Anesthesia**: **Difficult** in infected tissues (low pH inactivates LA). Options: **a) Local infiltration** (around abscess - not into pus), **b) Nerve block** (away from infection), **c) General anesthesia** (severe cases, trismus, children). **PROCEDURE**: **1. Prepare site**: Antiseptic (povidone-iodine). **2. Incision**: **Stab incision** (scalpel #11 or #15 blade) at **point of maximum fluctuance** or most **dependent** area. **Adequate length** (1.5-2 cm minimum - allow drainage). **Intraoral**: Vertical incision (avoid nerves/vessels). **Extraoral**: Along skin tension lines or submandibular/submental approach. **3. Blunt dissection**: Insert **curved hemostat/sinus forceps** into abscess cavity. **Open** widely (break loculations, explore cavity), **Finger dissection** if large cavity (submandibular space). **4. Irrigation**: **Copious saline** irrigation (mechanical debridement). **5. Obtain culture**: Aspirate pus with **syringe** (before contamination) - send for **culture/sensitivity** (anaerobic + aerobic). **6. Drain placement**: (a) **Corrugated rubber drain** (Penrose) or **iodoform gauze** - prevents premature closure, continued drainage, (b) Insert through incision, **secure** with silk suture to prevent displacement, (c) **Leave** 2-3 days (remove when drainage minimal). **7. Dressing**: **Intraoral** - none or loose gauze. **Extraoral** - sterile gauze, pressure dressing (mild). **POST-OPERATIVE CARE**: **1. Antibiotics**: Continue oral antibiotics 5-7 days. **2. Analgesics**: Ibuprofen + Paracetamol. **3. Warm saline rinses** (intraoral) or **hot fomentation** (extraoral) - promotes drainage. **4. Drain removal**: **2-3 days** when drainage minimal/stopped. **5. Follow-up**: Daily initially (assess drainage, change drain if needed). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Inadequate drainage** - recurrence, **Damage to nerves/vessels** (facial nerve, IAN, lingual), **Scarring** (extraoral), **Hemorrhage**.

viva.question: What is osteomyelitis of the jaw? Describe types and management.
viva.model_answer: **Osteomyelitis** is **infection of bone and bone marrow**. **Mandible** more common than maxilla (less vascularity, denser cortical bone). **TYPES**: **1. ACUTE OSTEOMYELITIS**: **Sudden onset**, **severe pain**, **fever**, **swelling**. **Cause**: Odontogenic infection spreads to bone (periapical abscess, fracture, surgery). **Features**: **Pus formation**, **sequestrum** formation (dead bone), **Involucrum** (new bone shell around dead bone). **2. CHRONIC OSTEOMYELITIS**: **Persistent** (>1 month), **low-grade**, **intermittent** symptoms. **Primary chronic** (direct infection - rare) or **Secondary** (from untreated acute). **Features**: **Draining sinus** (intraoral/extraoral), **Pathological fracture** (weak bone), **Sequestra** in CT/radiograph. **3. CHRONIC SCLEROSING OSTEOMYELITIS** (Garré's): **Non-suppurative** (no pus), **young patients**, mandibular **body/angle**. **Periosteal bone formation** → **cortical thickening** → "**onion-peel**" appearance (radiograph). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **Acute**: Deep boring pain, fever, malaise, swelling, **paresthesia** (IAN involvement), **teeth mobile**, **pus discharge**, **trismus**. **Chronic**: Intermittent pain/swelling, **draining sinus**, **foul odor**, **sequestra** may be palpable/visible. **RADIOGRAPHIC**: **Early** - no changes (10-14 days). **Late**: **Irregular radiolucency**, **moth-eaten** appearance, **sequestra** (radiopaque), **involucrum**. **DIAGNOSIS**: **Clinical + Radiographic + Culture**. **Biopsy** if chronic (rule out malignancy). **MANAGEMENT**: **1. Antibiotics** (prolonged - **6-12 weeks**): **IV initially** (Clindamycin 600-900mg q8h or Ampicillin-Sulbactam), then **oral** (Clindamycin 300mg QID, Amoxicillin-Clavulanate). **Adjust** per culture/sensitivity. **2. Surgical**: (a) **Remove source** - extract infected teeth, (b) **Incision & drainage** - if abscess, (c) **Sequestrectomy** - remove dead bone (sequestra), (d) **Saucerization** - create bony cavity for drainage/granulation, (e) **Decortication** - remove sclerotic bone (chronic), (f) **Resection** - if severe/refractory (rare). **3. Supportive**: **Hyperbaric oxygen** (controversial - improves oxygenation, healing). **4. Prevent**: Good oral hygiene post-extraction, antibiotics for high-risk (irradiated bone, immunosuppressed).

viva.question: What is alveoloplasty? Describe indications and procedure.
viva.model_answer: **Alveoloplasty** is **surgical reshaping/recontouring** of alveolar ridge to create **optimal foundation** for prosthesis (denture). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Irregular/sharp bony ridge** - causes denture discomfort, sore spots. **2. Undercuts** - prevent denture insertion/seating. **3. Post-extraction** (multiple teeth) - smooth sharp interdental/interradicular septa. **4. Protruding/flared labial plate** - improper denture support. **5. Prominent/sharp mylohyoid ridge** - interferes with denture flange. **6. Genial tubercles enlargement** - mandibular midline prominence. **7. Maxillary tuberosity enlargement** - reduces interarch space. **TYPES**: **1. Simple/Intraseptal**: Removes **interdental/interradicular bone** only. Done **during/immediately after** multiple extractions. **No flap** raised. **2. Radical/Interseptal**: **Extensive reshaping** with **mucoperiosteal flap**. **Removes** labial/lingual cortical plates, sharp ridges. Done **separate procedure** or with extractions. **PROCEDURE (Simple - at time of extraction)**: (1) **Extract teeth**, (2) **Rongeur/bone file** - remove **sharp bone** (interdental septa), smooth labial/lingual plates, (3) **Digital compression** - compress alveolar bone plates together (reduces ridge width), (4) **Irrigation**, (5) **Primary closure** if possible (approximate socket margins with sutures). **PROCEDURE (Radical - separate surgery)**: (1) **Incision**: **Crestal incision** along ridge ± **vertical releasing incisions**, (2) **Flap elevation**: Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap (buccal and lingual/palatal), expose bone, (3) **Bone recontouring**: (a) **Rongeur** - remove irregular bone, sharp projections, (b) **Bone file** - smooth surface, (c) **Surgical bur** (with irrigation) - remove large undercuts, excessive bone, (d) Create **gradual, rounded contour**, (4) **Trim excess soft tissue**: Fibrous/redundant tissue excision, (5) **Flap repositioning**: Approximate flaps (tension-free), (6) **Sutures**: Interrupted 3-0 silk, primary closure, (7) **Immediate denture** or **surgical stent** (protects, applies pressure). **Denture fabrication**: **6-8 weeks** post-surgery (healing, resorption stabilization). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Excessive bone removal** (reduces ridge height - poor retention), **Perforation** into maxillary sinus/nasal cavity, **Nerve injury** (mental, IAN), **Hemorrhage**, **Delayed healing**.

viva.question: What is frenectomy? Describe types and indications.
viva.model_answer: **Frenectomy** is **surgical removal** of **frenum** (fold of mucous membrane attaching lip/tongue to underlying structures). **TYPES**: **1. LABIAL FRENECTOMY**: **Maxillary labial** (most common) - between **central incisors** (midline). **Indications**: (a) **Midline diastema** - frenum attachment on incisive papilla prevents closure, (b) **Denture instability** - thick, prominent frenum dislodges denture during lip movement, (c) **Periodontal problem** - frenum pull causes recession. **Procedure**: (a) **Anesthesia** - infiltration, (b) **Hemostat** placed along frenum (crush tissue), (c) **Incision** along both sides of hemostat (diamond/rhomboid shape), (d) **Excise** frenum (including fibrous tissue extending to periosteum), (e) **Blunt dissection** to bone if needed, (f) **Suture** - interrupted (approximate margins). **2. LINGUAL FRENECTOMY** (Frenuloplasty): **Indication**: **Ankyloglossia (tongue-tie)** - restricted tongue movement. **Features**: Inability to protrude tongue beyond incisors, V-shaped tongue tip, speech problems ("l", "r" sounds), difficulty breastfeeding (infants). **Procedure**: (a) **Elevate tongue** - retract upward/forward, (b) **Incision** - midline horizontal incision at frenum base, (c) **Release** attachment to floor of mouth, (d) **Suture** - reposition mucosa (prevent reattachment). **Alternative**: **Z-plasty** (more complex) - prevents scarring/reattachment. **3. BUCCAL FRENECTOMY**: Less common. **Indication**: Frenum pull causes denture instability or gingival recession. **TIMING**: **Labial** - after **orthodontic closure** of diastema (prevents recurrence) OR before denture fabrication. **Lingual** - infancy (if severe feeding problems) or childhood (before speech development). **POST-OPERATIVE**: Analgesics, soft diet, chlorhexidine rinses, suture removal 7 days.

viva.question: What is vestibuloplasty? When is it indicated?
viva.model_answer: **Vestibuloplasty** is **surgical deepening** of **vestibular sulcus** (space between alveolar ridge and lip/cheek) to **increase denture-bearing area** and improve **denture retention/stability**. **INDICATIONS**: **1. Resorbed alveolar ridge** - severe resorption (long-standing edentulous patient) causes **shallow vestibule**, (2) **Inadequate denture retention** - insufficient sulcus depth for peripheral seal, (3) **High muscle attachments** - muscles attached close to ridge crest (reduce denture stability), (4) **Before implant placement** - create adequate soft tissue coverage. **PRINCIPLES**: **Reposition muscles** (mentalis, buccinator, mylohyoid) **away from ridge crest** (apically/laterally). **Cover denuded bone** with **oral mucosa** or allow **epithelialization**. **TECHNIQUES**: **1. Submucosal vestibuloplasty**: **Incision** at depth of vestibule, **dissect** muscle attachments from bone, **reposition** apically, **secure** with sutures. **Surgical stent** maintains depth during healing. **2. Secondary epithelialization**: **Remove** mucosa from ridge area, expose bone, allow **spontaneous epithelialization** (6-8 weeks). **Stent** essential. **3. Skin/mucosal grafts**: **Split-thickness skin graft** (from thigh) or **palatal graft** to cover denuded area. Better stability. **4. Clark's vestibuloplasty** (mandible): Full-thickness mucoperiosteal flap reflected to depth, muscles repositioned, **stent** holds position. **POST-OPERATIVE**: **Surgical stent** worn **continuous 7-14 days** (holds soft tissue position, prevents relapse). **Analgesics**, soft diet. **Denture fabrication** after **6-8 weeks** (tissue maturation). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Loss of depth** (relapse - most common), **Nerve injury** (mental nerve), **Hemorrhage**, **Infection**, **Graft failure**. **ALTERNATIVE**: **Ridge augmentation** (bone grafting) - preferred in many cases (restores bone height rather than just sulcus depth).

viva.question: What is maxillary tuberosity reduction? When is it needed?
viva.model_answer: **Maxillary tuberosity reduction** is **surgical removal** of **excess bone/soft tissue** from **maxillary tuberosity** (posterior maxilla - distal to last molar). **INDICATIONS**: **1. Enlarged tuberosity**: Excessive vertical height. **2. Insufficient interarch space** - between maxillary tuberosity and mandibular ridge (prevents proper denture fabrication). **Minimum required**: **12-15 mm** for complete dentures. **3. Fibrous hyperplastic tissue** - redundant soft tissue. **4. Unilateral enlargement** - causes uneven occlusal plane. **TYPES OF ENLARGEMENT**: **1. Soft tissue hyperplasia** - fibrous tissue only (easier to remove). **2. Bony enlargement** - excess bone (requires bone removal). **3. Combination** - both bone and soft tissue. **PROCEDURE**: **1. Assessment**: **Radiograph** (PA/OPG) - determine if **bone** or **soft tissue** enlargement. **Measure interarch space** (calipers/models). **2. Anesthesia**: **PSA block** + **Greater palatine nerve block**. **3. Incision**: **Elliptical/wedge incision** on **crest** of tuberosity (remove wedge of soft tissue ± bone). OR **Circumferential incision** around tuberosity. **4. Tissue removal**: **a) Soft tissue only**: Excise with scalpel. **b) Bone involvement**: (i) **Elevate mucoperiosteal flap**, (ii) **Rongeur/bone file** - remove excess bone, (iii) **Smooth** sharp edges, (iv) **Replace flap**. **5. Hemostasis**: Pressure, electrocautery if needed. **6. Sutures**: Interrupted 3-0 silk (approximate margins). **7. Immediate denture/surgical stent**: Apply pressure, protect, guide healing. **PRECAUTIONS**: **1. Preserve blood supply**: Maintain **palatal soft tissue** (blood supply from greater palatine artery). **Avoid excessive removal** - risk necrosis. **2. Avoid maxillary sinus**: **Check radiograph** - sinus may extend into tuberosity. **Perforation** - close primarily, antibiotics. **3. Limit bone removal**: Excessive reduction causes **ridge resorption**, poor denture support. **COMPLICATIONS**: **Hemorrhage** (pterygoid plexus proximity), **Oro-antral communication**, **Excessive reduction**, **Flap necrosis**, **Delayed healing**.

viva.question: What is a dentigerous cyst? Describe clinical features and management.
viva.model_answer: **Dentigerous (Follicular) cyst** is a **developmental odontogenic cyst** arising from **separation** of **follicle** from **crown** of **unerupted tooth**. **2nd most common** odontogenic cyst (after radicular cyst). **PATHOGENESIS**: Fluid accumulation between **reduced enamel epithelium** and **enamel** surface of unerupted tooth crown. **ASSOCIATION**: **Mandibular 3rd molars** (most common), maxillary canines, maxillary 3rd molars, supernumerary teeth, odontomes. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Age**: **2nd-3rd decade** (eruption age). **2. Asymptomatic** - discovered on routine radiograph (unerupted tooth). **3. Painless swelling** - if large (slow-growing). **4. Egg-shell crackling** - thin bone over large cyst. **5. Tooth displacement** - adjacent teeth displaced by expanding cyst. **6. Tooth non-eruption** - involved tooth fails to erupt. **RADIOGRAPHIC**: **1. Well-defined unilocular radiolucency** - **symmetrically surrounding** crown of unerupted tooth. **2. Radiolucency attached** at **CEJ** (cementoenamel junction) - **pathognomonic**. **3. Sclerotic border** (corticated margin). **4. Tooth displacement** - impacted tooth pushed away. **5. Root resorption** - adjacent teeth (if large). **TYPES (by location)**: **Central** (most common - surrounds crown symmetrically), **Lateral** (along tooth root), **Circumferential** (surrounds entire tooth). **DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS**: **Odontogenic keratocyst** (more aggressive, recurrence), **Ameloblastoma** (multilocular, aggressive), **Hyperplastic dental follicle** (<5mm - not cystic). **HISTOPATHOLOGY**: **Cyst lining** - **non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium** (2-3 cells thick), **fibrous connective tissue** wall. **Chronic inflammation** if secondarily infected. **COMPLICATIONS**: **1. Pathological fracture** (large mandibular cysts). **2. Infection** (rare - becomes radicular cyst). **3. Malignant transformation** - **RARE** (ameloblastoma 0.1%, squamous cell carcinoma - extremely rare). **MANAGEMENT**: **SMALL CYSTS** (<2cm): **Enucleation** + **Extraction** of involved tooth. (1) **Flap** - mucoperiosteal, (2) **Bone removal** - expose cyst, (3) **Enucleate** cyst completely (intact if possible - for histopath), (4) **Extract** associated tooth (usually), (5) **Curettage** cyst cavity (remove lining completely), (6) **Irrigation**, (7) **Primary closure**, (8) **Histopathology** - confirm diagnosis. **LARGE CYSTS** (>2cm, extensive bone involvement): **Marsupialization** first (decompress, reduce size), then **Enucleation** later (staged). **Marsupialization**: Create **surgical window** into cyst, **suture** cyst lining to oral mucosa (wide opening), allows **drainage** and **shrinkage** (6-12 months), then enucleate. **PROGNOSIS**: **Excellent** with complete removal. **Recurrence rare** if properly enucleated.

viva.question: What is a radicular cyst? How does it differ from a dentigerous cyst?
viva.model_answer: **Radicular (Periapical/Apical) cyst** is an **inflammatory odontogenic cyst** arising from **chronic periapical granuloma** due to **pulp necrosis**. **Most common odontogenic cyst** (60%). **PATHOGENESIS**: **Chronic pulpitis** → **Pulp necrosis** → **Chronic periapical inflammation** → **Stimulation of epithelial rests of Malassez** (in PDL) → **Cyst formation**. **Associated with NON-VITAL tooth** (key difference from dentigerous). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Age**: **3rd-5th decade** (peak). **2. Location**: **Anterior maxilla** most common (especially **maxillary lateral incisor** - due to chronic trauma/pulp necrosis). **3. Asymptomatic** - usually discovered on radiograph. **4. Painless swelling** - if large (slow-growing over months/years). **5. Non-vital tooth** - involved tooth does NOT respond to pulp tests. **6. Discolored tooth** - grayish (necrotic pulp). **RADIOGRAPHIC**: **1. Well-defined round/oval radiolucency** at **root apex**. **2. Lamina dura** (white line around root) **lost**. **3. Size**: Usually <1.5 cm. Larger cysts (>1.5 cm) more likely true cysts. **4. Sclerotic margin** (corticated border). **HISTOPATHOLOGY**: **Non-keratinized stratified squamous epithelium** lining (thicker than dentigerous - 5-20 cells), **inflamed fibrous wall**, **cholesterol clefts** common (shiny crystals in histology). **DIFFERENTIAL FROM DENTIGEROUS CYST**: | Feature | Radicular Cyst | Dentigerous Cyst | |---------|----------------|------------------| | **Association** | NON-VITAL tooth (apex) | UNERUPTED tooth (crown) | | **Tooth vitality** | Non-responsive | Not applicable (unerupted) | | **Location** | Root apex | Surrounding crown at CEJ | | **Etiology** | Inflammatory (infection) | Developmental | | **Most common site** | Maxillary anterior | Mandibular 3rd molar | | **Histology** | Thicker epithelium, inflammation | Thin epithelium (2-3 cells) | **TYPES**: **Apical** (at apex - most common), **Lateral** (along root - lateral canal necrosis), **Residual** (remains after extraction without cyst removal). **MANAGEMENT**: **1. Small cysts** (<1 cm - likely granuloma): **Root canal treatment (RCT)** of involved tooth - **cyst may resolve** spontaneously (40-60% success). **Apicoectomy** if RCT fails. **2. Large cysts** (>1 cm - true cyst): **Enucleation + apicoectomy** ± **RCT** (if tooth retained) OR **Extraction + enucleation** (if tooth non-restorable). **3. Very large**: **Marsupialization** → **Enucleation** later. **PROGNOSIS**: Excellent. **Recurrence rare** if completely removed.

viva.question: What is odontogenic keratocyst (KCOT)? Why is it clinically significant?
viva.model_answer: **Odontogenic keratocyst (OKC)** / **Keratocystic odontogenic tumor (KCOT)** - **aggressive developmental** odontogenic cyst. **Reclassified** as **tumor** by WHO (2005) due to aggressive behavior (changed back to cyst 2017). **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **1. HIGH RECURRENCE** (25-60%) - **most aggressive** odontogenic cyst. **2. Local aggressiveness** - invades marrow spaces, difficult complete removal. **3. Association with syndromes** - **Gorlin-Goltz syndrome** (Nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome) - multiple OKCs + basal cell carcinomas + skeletal abnormalities. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Age**: **2nd-3rd decade** (young adults). **2. Location**: **Posterior mandible** (angle/ramus - 70%), maxilla (30%). **3. Often asymptomatic** - discovered on radiograph. **4. Slow expansion** - less swelling than dentigerous cyst (grows along bone - **anteroposterior** rather than lateral expansion). **5. Multiple cysts** - in Gorlin-Goltz syndrome. **RADIOGRAPHIC**: **1. Well-defined radiolucency** - **unilocular** (young) or **multilocular** (older/larger - "soap-bubble" appearance). **2. Scalloped margins** - irregular borders (grows into bone marrow spaces). **3. May surround unerupted tooth** - mimics dentigerous cyst. **4. **Root resorption** - adjacent teeth. **HISTOPATHOLOGY** (KEY diagnostic): **1. PARAKERATINIZED** stratified squamous epithelium (5-8 cell layers thick). **2. Corrugated/wavy surface** ("corrugated roofing iron"). **3. Palisaded basal cells** (columnar cells in picket-fence arrangement). **4. Thin fibrous wall**. **5. Satellite/daughter cysts** - in wall (explains high recurrence). **DIFFERENTIAL**: **Dentigerous cyst** (similar radiograph), **Ameloblastoma** (more aggressive, multilocular). **MANAGEMENT**: **CHALLENGE**: Balance **complete removal** (reduce recurrence) vs **preserve bone/teeth**. **OPTIONS**: **1. Enucleation alone**: **Simplest** but **highest recurrence** (60%). **2. Enucleation + Carnoy's solution**: Chemical cauterization of cyst cavity (**peripheral ostectomy**). **Reduces recurrence** to 10-15%. **Carnoy's solution** - fixes residual epithelium (ferric chloride, glacial acetic acid, chloroform). **3. Enucleation + peripheral ostectomy**: **Burr** removes 1-2mm bone from cavity wall (removes satellite cysts). **4. Marsupialization**: **Decompress** first (large cysts), then enucleate later. **Reduces recurrence**, preserves bone. **5. Resection**: Segmental mandibulectomy - **reserved** for large/recurrent (extensive bone involvement). **FOLLOW-UP**: **Annual radiographs** for **5 years** minimum (recurrences appear late). **Gorlin-Goltz syndrome**: **Lifelong surveillance**.

viva.question: What are the types of biopsy? When is each indicated?
viva.model_answer: **Biopsy** - removal of **tissue** for **histopathological examination** to establish definitive diagnosis. **TYPES**: **1. EXCISIONAL BIOPSY**: **Complete removal** of entire lesion with margin of normal tissue. **Indications**: (a) **Small lesions** (<1-1.5 cm), (b) **Pedunculated** lesions (stalked), (c) **Lesions with low malignancy suspicion** (clinical diagnosis benign), (d) **Therapeutic** - removes lesion completely (diagnosis + treatment). **Advantage**: **Single procedure** (diagnostic + curative). **Disadvantage**: If malignant, margins may be inadequate. **Examples**: Fibroma, mucocele, small benign tumors. **2. INCISIONAL BIOPSY**: **Removal of representative part** of lesion (wedge/punch). **Remaining lesion** left in situ. **Indications**: (a) **Large lesions** (>1.5 cm), (b) **High malignancy suspicion** - plan definitive surgery after confirmation, (c) **Multiple clinical appearances** - sample different areas, (d) **Deep-seated lesions**, (e) **Uncertain diagnosis**. **Technique**: **Wedge biopsy** - elliptical incision (includes normal + abnormal tissue), or **Punch biopsy** (4-6mm punch). **Advantage**: Less invasive, **plan definitive treatment** after diagnosis. **Examples**: Large oral ulcers, suspected malignancy, mucosal lesions. **3. ASPIRATION BIOPSY**: **Fine needle aspiration (FNA)** - aspirate cells/fluid with needle. **Cytology** (not histology). **Indications**: (a) **Cystic swellings** - differentiate cyst vs solid tumor, (b) **Salivary gland lesions**, (c) **Lymph nodes** (metastasis?). **Advantage**: **Minimally invasive**, quick, OPD procedure. **Limitation**: **Cytology** only (no tissue architecture) - less definitive than histology. **4. CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY**: **Larger bore needle** - obtain tissue core (histology possible). **Indications**: Deep tumors (salivary gland), bone lesions. **PRINCIPLES**: **1. Representative sample** - include normal + abnormal tissue (incisional), adequate size. **2. Avoid necrotic areas** - sample viable proliferating edge. **3. Handle gently** - avoid crush artifact. **4. Fix immediately** - **10% formalin** (tissue preservation). **5. Hemostasis** - sutures, pressure. **6. Label** - patient details, site, clinical diagnosis. **7. Inform patient** - results take 5-7 days. **CONTRAINDICATIONS**: **Vascular lesions** (hemangioma) - risk of severe bleeding (diagnose clinically/radiographically).

viva.question: Describe the temporomandibular joint (TMJ) anatomy and common disorders.
viva.model_answer: **TMJ ANATOMY**: **Ginglymoarthrodial joint** (hinge + gliding). **Components**: **1. Articular surfaces**: **Mandibular condyle** (ellipsoid) + **Glenoid fossa** of temporal bone + **Articular eminence** (tubercle). **2. Articular disc**: **Fibrocartilage** disc divides joint into **upper** (translation/gliding) and **lower** (rotation/hinge) compartments. **Biconcave** shape. **Attachments**: Anteriorly to **lateral pterygoid muscle** (superior head), posteriorly to **retrodiscal tissue** (bilaminar zone - vascular), medially/laterally to **condyle**. **3. Capsule**: Fibrous capsule surrounds joint. **4. Ligaments**: **Lateral (temporomandibular)** - limits posterior condyle movement. **Sphenomandibular, Stylomandibular** - accessory. **MOVEMENTS**: **Opening**: (1) **Rotation** (0-25mm) - condyle rotates in lower compartment (hinge), (2) **Translation** (>25mm) - condyle + disc glide forward along articular eminence (upper compartment). **Closing**: Reverse. **COMMON TMJ DISORDERS (TMD)**: **1. INTERNAL DERANGEMENT**: **Disc displacement**. **a) Disc displacement with reduction**: Disc **anteriorly displaced** (mouth closed), **clicks/pops** during opening (disc reduces back onto condyle), **full mouth opening**. **Reciprocal click** (opening + closing). **b) Disc displacement without reduction** (closed lock): Disc **permanently displaced anteriorly**, **cannot reduce**, **limited mouth opening** (<30mm), **deviation** to affected side, **no click** (disc stuck). **2. OSTEOARTHRITIS (Degenerative joint disease)**: **Crepitus** (grating sound), pain, limited movement, **radiographic changes** (flattening, osteophytes, sclerosis). **3. MYOFASCIAL PAIN DYSFUNCTION** (MPD): **Muscle pain** (masticatory muscles - masseter, temporalis, pterygoids), **tenderness** on palpation, **limited opening** (muscle spasm), **no joint sounds**. **Etiology**: Stress, bruxism, parafunctional habits. **4. ANKYLOSIS**: **Fusion** of joint - fibrous or bony. **No movement**, severely limited opening. **Causes**: Trauma, infection. **5. TMJ ARTHRITIS**: **Rheumatoid arthritis**, **Septic arthritis**. **DIAGNOSIS**: **Clinical** (history, examination - opening, palpation, sounds), **Radiographs** (OPG, lateral TMJ), **MRI** (gold standard - soft tissue/disc visualization), **CT** (bone changes).

viva.question: What is the management of temporomandibular disorders (TMD)?
viva.model_answer: **TMD management** - **conservative first**, surgery **last resort**. **90% improve** with non-surgical therapy. **CONSERVATIVE (First-line)**: **1. Patient education**: Explain condition (usually self-limiting), avoid aggravating factors. **2. Behavior modification**: (a) **Soft diet** - avoid hard, chewy foods, (b) **Avoid wide opening** - no yawning, large bites, (c) **Avoid parafunctional habits** - **no gum chewing**, nail biting, pencil biting, (d) **Stress reduction** - counseling, relaxation techniques. **3. Physical therapy**: (a) **Moist heat** - warm compress (20 min, 3-4x/day) - muscle relaxation, (b) **Ice packs** - acute pain (first 24-48 hrs), (c) **Jaw exercises** - gentle stretching (improve mobility, muscle strength), (d) **Massage** - masticatory muscles (masseter, temporalis). **4. Pharmacotherapy**: (a) **NSAIDs** - **Ibuprofen 400mg TDS** (pain, inflammation), (b) **Muscle relaxants** - **Diazepam 5mg** or **Chlorzoxazone** (muscle spasm, especially nighttime bruxism), (c) **Tricyclic antidepressants** - **Amitriptyline 10-25mg** (chronic pain, nocturnal bruxism). **5. Occlusal splint (Bite guard)**: **Stabilization splint** - **hard acrylic**, covers maxillary/mandibular teeth, worn **nighttime**. **Functions**: (a) Protects teeth from bruxism, (b) **Repositions** condyle-disc relationship (controversial), (c) **Muscle relaxation** (reduces hyperactivity), (d) **Cognitive awareness** (reminds patient not to clench). **Indications**: Bruxism, muscle pain, mild disc displacement with reduction. **MINIMALLY INVASIVE**: **6. Arthrocentesis**: **Lavage** of upper joint space (wash out inflammatory mediators, lyse adhesions). **Indications**: **Closed lock** (disc displacement without reduction - acute), limited opening, pain. **Procedure**: **2 needles** in superior joint space, irrigate with **Ringer's lactate** (200-300 mL). **Success**: 70-80% (improves opening, reduces pain). **7. Arthroscopy**: **Endoscopic** visualization + treatment (lysis of adhesions, disc repositioning). More invasive than arthrocentesis. **SURGICAL (Last resort - <5% cases)**: **8. Discectomy**: Remove damaged disc. **9. Condylotomy**: Osteotomy of condylar neck (repositions condyle). **10. Joint replacement**: Total joint prosthesis (severe ankylosis, end-stage DJD). **STEPWISE APPROACH**: Conservative (2-3 months) → Arthrocentesis/Arthroscopy → Surgery (if all fail).

viva.question: What is TMJ dislocation? How do you reduce it?
viva.model_answer: **TMJ dislocation** - **condyle displaced** anteriorly **beyond articular eminence**, **cannot return** to glenoid fossa. **Bilateral** more common than unilateral. **ETIOLOGY**: **Wide opening** - yawning, laughing, prolonged dental treatment, trauma, seizures. **Predisposing**: **Lax ligaments**, **shallow fossa**, **previous dislocations**. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Mouth wide open** - **cannot close**. **2. Drooling** - cannot swallow saliva. **3. Speech difficulty**. **4. Bilateral**: **Prognathic** appearance (chin protrudes forward). **Preauricular depressions** (empty fossa palpable). **5. Unilateral**: **Deviation** to non-dislocated side, **open bite** opposite side. **6. Muscles in spasm** - painful, difficult to reduce (time-dependent). **7. Preauricular pain**. **TYPES**: **Acute** (<24-48 hrs), **Chronic/Recurrent** (repeated episodes), **Chronic** (>1 month unreduced). **REDUCTION (Manual - Hippocratic method)**: **1. Position**: Patient **seated** (head supported), operator **in front** or **behind** patient (behind easier). **2. Muscle relaxation**: (a) **Reassure** patient (anxiety worsens spasm), (b) **Local anesthesia** - inject into lateral pterygoid muscle/joint capsule (optional), (c) **Sedation/GA** if severe spasm (chronic dislocation), (d) **Wait** 10-15 min (muscle relaxation/fatigue). **3. Thumb placement**: Operator places **thumbs** on **occlusal surfaces** of lower molars (wrap thumbs with gauze - prevent bite injury). **Fingers** grasp **inferior border** of mandible externally. **4. Downward pressure**: **Press down firmly** on molars (disimpacts condyle from anterior to eminence, moves condyle **inferiorly**). **5. Backward rotation**: While maintaining downward pressure, **push chin backward and upward** (guides condyle **posteriorly** into fossa). **6. Reduction**: **Palpable/audible click** - condyle relocates. **Mouth closes** (patient bites down). **Quickly remove thumbs** (reflex bite). **POST-REDUCTION**: (1) **Soft diet** 1 week, (2) **Avoid wide opening** 2 weeks, (3) **NSAIDs** - pain/inflammation, (4) **Jaw exercises** - prevent stiffness. **PREVENTION OF RECURRENCE**: **1. Patient education** - avoid wide opening, yawning with care. **2. Occlusal splint** (night guard). **3. Sclerosing injections** - into capsule (controversial). **4. Surgical** (recurrent cases): **a) Eminectomy** - flatten/remove articular eminence (condyle cannot lock anteriorly), **b) Capsulorrhaphy** - tighten capsule. **COMPLICATIONS**: **Fracture** (forced reduction), **Nerve injury**, **Recurrence** (most common).

viva.question: Classify mandibular fractures. What is favorable vs unfavorable fracture?
viva.model_answer: **ANATOMICAL CLASSIFICATION**: **1. Symphysis**: Midline (between mental foramina). **2. Parasymphysis**: Between midline and canine. **3. Body**: Between canine and angle. **4. Angle**: Junction of body and ramus (most common - 25-30%). **Tooth in fracture line**, thin bone. **5. Ramus**: Ascending vertical portion. **6. Condyle**: Condylar neck/head (2nd most common - 25%). **Subcondylar, condylar neck, intracapsular**. **7. Coronoid process**: Rare (<1%). **DIRECTION OF FRACTURE LINE**: **FAVORABLE vs UNFAVORABLE** (muscle forces on fracture stability): **FAVORABLE**: Fracture line oriented such that **muscle pull** tends to **CLOSE/APPROXIMATE** fragments (stable). Easier to reduce/maintain. **UNFAVORABLE**: Muscle pull **DISTRACTS/SEPARATES** fragments (unstable). Requires rigid fixation. **HORIZONTAL FAVORABLE**: **Anterior fragment** pulled **upward** by **suprahyoid muscles** (digastric, geniohyoid). **Posterior fragment** pulled **upward** by **elevators** (masseter, temporalis, medial pterygoid). **Fracture line** runs **anterior-inferior to posterior-superior** - fragments compress together. **Example**: Angle fracture with oblique line. **HORIZONTAL UNFAVORABLE**: **Fracture line** runs **anterior-superior to posterior-inferior** - muscle pull **separates** fragments (gap formation). **Example**: Body fracture with reverse oblique. **VERTICAL FAVORABLE**: **Medial pterygoid** pull (medial) on posterior fragment does NOT displace (fracture line orientation prevents). **VERTICAL UNFAVORABLE**: **Lateral pterygoid** + **medial pterygoid** pull causes **rotation/displacement** of posterior fragment **medially** and **anteriorly**. **OTHER CLASSIFICATIONS**: **1. Dentate vs Edentulous**: **Edentulous** - atrophic bone, higher fracture risk, different fixation. **2. Simple vs Compound**: **Compound** - communicates with external environment (oral cavity/skin) - higher infection risk, needs antibiotics. **Simple** - closed fracture. **3. Complete vs Incomplete** (greenstick). **4. Comminuted**: Multiple fragments. **5. Pathological**: Through diseased bone (cyst, tumor, osteomyelitis). **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Unfavorable** fractures need **ORIF** (open reduction, internal fixation). **Favorable** may respond to **closed reduction** (IMF).

viva.question: Describe Le Fort classification of maxillary fractures.
viva.model_answer: **Le Fort classification** - **midface fractures** involving **maxilla**. Based on **predictable lines of weakness**. **KEY FEATURE**: **ALL Le Fort fractures** involve **pterygoid plates** (posterior maxilla) - **pathognomonic**. **LE FORT I** (Horizontal/Floating palate): **Level**: **Low** (above roots of teeth). **Fracture line**: Transverse across **maxilla above apices** of teeth, through **lateral nasal walls**, across **maxillary sinuses**, through **pterygoid plates** (posterior). **Result**: **Entire maxilla** (palate + alveolus) **separates** as single unit ("floating palate"). **Mobility**: **Maxilla** mobile (grasp anterior teeth, move up-down - free-floating). **Nasal bones** intact. **LE FORT II** (Pyramidal): **Level**: **Mid-level**. **Fracture line**: **Pyramid-shaped** - starts at **nasal bridge** (nasofrontal suture), extends **laterally** through **medial orbital walls** (lacrimal bones), **inferior orbital rim**, across **maxillary sinuses**, through **pterygoid plates**. **Result**: **Central maxilla** + **nose** detach as **pyramid-shaped segment**. **Mobility**: **Nose** + **maxilla** move together (grasp nose, move - entire central midface mobile). **Orbital rim** fractured. **LE FORT III** (Craniofacial dysjunction): **Level**: **High** (complete separation of facial skeleton from cranium). **Fracture line**: Across **nasofrontal suture**, through **medial** and **lateral orbital walls** (both), across **zygomatic arches**, through **pterygoid plates**, through **ethmoid sinuses**. **Result**: **ENTIRE FACE** separates from skull base (craniofacial separation). **Mobility**: **Entire midface** mobile (grasp face, moves as unit - "dish-face"). **Most severe**. **CLINICAL SIGNS (Common to all)**: **1. Malocclusion** - teeth don't meet properly (open bite, crossbite). **2. Facial swelling/edema** - extensive. **3. Epistaxis** - nasal bleeding. **4. CSF rhinorrhea** (Le Fort II/III - cribriform plate involvement). **5. Ecchymosis** - periorbital ("raccoon eyes"), subconjunctival hemorrhage. **6. Mobility** - grasp maxilla, move (check level). **7. Step deformity** - palpable at fracture sites (infraorbital rim). **8. Anesthesia** - infraorbital nerve (V2) - cheek, upper lip, nose. **SPECIFIC SIGNS**: **Le Fort I**: Mobile maxilla, **nasal bones stable**. **Le Fort II**: **Bilateral periorbital edema**, **CSF leak** (may), **nasal bones mobile**. **Flattened nasal bridge**. **Le Fort III**: **Massive facial edema**, **"Dish-face"** deformity (flattened midface), **CSF leak** (high risk), **entire face mobile**. **Life-threatening** - airway compromise. **DIAGNOSIS**: **Clinical** + **CT scan** (gold standard - 3D reconstruction shows fracture lines). **MANAGEMENT**: **Stabilize airway** (priority - Le Fort III often needs intubation/tracheostomy), **ORIF** (open reduction, internal fixation with plates/screws), **IMF** (inter-maxillary fixation - 4-6 weeks).

viva.question: What are the principles of fracture management? Describe IMF and ORIF.
viva.model_answer: **FRACTURE MANAGEMENT PRINCIPLES**: **1. Airway** - ensure patent airway (priority in trauma). **2. Hemorrhage control** - stop bleeding. **3. Soft tissue management** - clean, debride, suture lacerations. **4. Reduction** - restore anatomic position. **5. Fixation/Immobilization** - maintain reduction during healing. **6. Nutrition** - adequate (liquid diet if IMF). **7. Function** - restore occlusion, jaw movement. **8. Esthetics** - restore facial contour. **METHODS OF FIXATION**: **1. CLOSED REDUCTION** (Conservative): **No surgical exposure** of fracture. **Indirect manipulation** to reduce, then immobilize. **INTERMAXILLARY FIXATION (IMF / Maxillomandibular Fixation MMF)**: **Principle**: **Wire/elastics** between **maxillary and mandibular teeth** to **immobilize mandible** using maxilla as stable base. **Indications**: (a) **Favorable fractures** (stable), (b) **Minimally displaced** fractures, (c) **Condylar fractures** (conservative management), (d) **Adjunct to ORIF** (maintain occlusion during healing). **Methods**: **a) Arch bars + wires**: **Arch bars** (metal bars with hooks) **bonded/wired** to teeth (maxilla + mandible). **Intermaxillary wires/elastics** between hooks (fixes jaws together). **Standard** method. **b) Ivy loops**: **Individual wire loops** around teeth (instead of arch bars). **Quicker** but less stable. **c) Ernst ligatures, Stout wires**: Variations. **Duration**: **4-6 weeks** (mandible), **6-8 weeks** (maxilla - slower healing). **Complications**: **Aspiration risk** (vomiting - cannot open mouth), **Weight loss**, **Poor oral hygiene**, **TMJ stiffness**, **Airway compromise**. **Wire cutters** kept with patient always (emergency release). **2. OPEN REDUCTION INTERNAL FIXATION (ORIF)**: **Surgical exposure** of fracture, **anatomic reduction**, **rigid fixation** with **plates and screws** (internal). **Indications**: (a) **Unfavorable** fractures (unstable), (b) **Displaced** fractures, (c) **Multiple** fractures, (d) **Comminuted** fractures, (e) **Edentulous** mandible (no teeth for IMF), (f) **Bilateral** fractures (both sides), (g) **Condylar fractures** with malocclusion/dislocation. **Advantages**: (1) **Rigid fixation** - immediate stability, (2) **Early function** - no/minimal IMF needed, (3) **Better alignment** - direct visualization, (4) **Shorter treatment** - faster return to normal. **Technique**: (a) **Approach** - intraoral or extraoral incision, (b) **Expose fracture** - elevate soft tissue, (c) **Reduce** - align fragments anatomically, (d) **Fix** - **Titanium miniplates + screws** across fracture line. **Champy's principles**: **Load-bearing** (body, angle) - 2 plates. **Tension zone** (superior border), **Compression zone** (inferior border). (e) **Closure** - layer by layer. **Complications**: **Infection** (plate exposure), **Malunion**, **Nerve injury**, **Hardware failure**, **Plate removal** (later if symptomatic). **3. EXTERNAL FIXATION**: **Pins** through skin into bone, connected externally. **Rare** in facial fractures (cosmetic concerns). Used in **comminuted**, **infected**, **gunshot wounds**. **CHOICE**: **Favorable/Simple** → **Closed + IMF**. **Unfavorable/Complex** → **ORIF** (± short IMF).

viva.question: What is a zygomatic complex (tripod) fracture? How is it diagnosed and managed?
viva.model_answer: **Zygomatic complex (ZMC) / Tripod fracture** - fracture of **zygoma** (cheekbone) with **separation** at **3 sutures**: (1) **Zygomaticofrontal** (lateral orbital rim), (2) **Zygomaticomaxillary** (inferior orbital rim), (3) **Zygomaticotemporal** (zygomatic arch). **Etiology**: **Direct trauma** to cheek - assault (fist/blunt object), RTA, sports. **Zygoma prominence** makes it vulnerable. **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Flattening of cheek** - **loss of malar prominence** (depressed zygoma). **2. Periorbital edema/ecchymosis** - swelling, black eye. **3. Subconjunctival hemorrhage** - blood in sclera (no posterior limit - differentiates from localized bruise). **4. Step deformity** - palpable at **infraorbital rim** and **lateral orbital rim** (fracture displacement). **5. Infraorbital nerve anesthesia** - **numbness** of cheek, upper lip, nose, upper teeth (nerve injury in infraorbital canal). **Pathognomonic sign**. **6. Enophthalmos** - **sunken eye** (if orbital floor fractured - orbital volume increased). **Late sign** (after edema subsides). **7. Diplopia** - **double vision** (extraocular muscle entrapment in orbital floor fracture, or edema). **8. Trismus** - limited mouth opening (if arch impinges on coronoid process). **9. Epistaxis** - nasal bleeding (maxillary sinus involvement). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic features. **2. Radiographs**: **Waters view** (occipitomental) - best for zygoma. **Shows**: (a) **Opacification** of maxillary sinus (blood), (b) **Fracture lines** at sutures, (c) **Depressed arch**. **3. CT scan** - **gold standard** (3D reconstruction - exact displacement, orbital floor involvement). **COMPLICATIONS**: **Persistent enophthalmos**, **Persistent diplopia** (muscle entrapment), **Facial asymmetry** (malunion), **Infraorbital anesthesia** (may be permanent). **MANAGEMENT**: **OBSERVATION** (No treatment): **Indications**: (a) **Minimal/no displacement**, (b) **No functional impairment** (vision, occlusion), (c) **No esthetic concern**. **Monitor** - most heal uneventfully. **SURGICAL REDUCTION**: **Indications**: (a) **Significant displacement** (cosmetic deformity), (b) **Enophthalmos** (>2mm), (c) **Persistent diplopia** (>2 weeks), (d) **Trismus**, (e) **Large orbital floor defect**. **Timing**: **Early** (within 7-14 days - before healing/fibrosis). **Techniques**: (a) **Closed reduction**: **Gillies approach** - temporal incision, **elevator** under zygomatic arch, **lift** zygoma (pop back into place). **No fixation**. Simple fractures only. (b) **Open reduction**: **Incisions** (intraoral + lower eyelid OR coronal), **expose fracture** sites, **reduce** anatomically, **fix** with **miniplates** at 2-3 points (zygomaticomaxillary buttress, infraorbital rim, zygomaticofrontal). **Orbital floor reconstruction** if large defect (implant - titanium mesh/porous polyethylene). **Post-op**: **Antibiotics** (sinus communication), **Decongestants** (nasal), **Avoid nose blowing**, **Eye care** (if eyelid swelling).

viva.question: What is the management of condylar fractures?
viva.model_answer: **Condylar fractures** - **2nd most common** mandibular fracture (25%). **Bilateral** common (falls on chin - force transmitted to both condyles). **TYPES**: **1. Intracapsular** - fracture within TMJ capsule (condylar head). **2. Condylar neck** - below head, above sigmoid notch. **3. Subcondylar** - below sigmoid notch (most common). **DISPLACEMENT**: **Non-displaced/minimally displaced** vs **Displaced** vs **Dislocated** (complete loss of contact). **GENERAL PRINCIPLE**: **MOST condylar fractures** managed **CONSERVATIVELY** (non-surgical). **INDICATIONS FOR CONSERVATIVE MANAGEMENT** (90%): **1. Non-displaced/minimally displaced** fractures. **2. Bilateral fractures** with **acceptable occlusion**. **3. Condylar neck fractures** (below head). **4. Children** (excellent remodeling potential - almost always conservative). **5. Edentulous patients**. **6. Unilateral** with normal occlusion. **CONSERVATIVE TREATMENT**: **1. Short-term IMF** (if needed): **7-14 days** only (prevent ankylosis - TMJ stiffness). **Training elastics** (light - guide occlusion, allow some movement). OR **No IMF** if occlusion acceptable. **2. Soft diet**: 4-6 weeks. **3. Early mobilization**: **Jaw exercises** start after **1-2 weeks** (crucial - prevent ankylosis, restore function). **Active** opening/closing, lateral movements. **4. Analgesics**: NSAIDs. **5. Follow-up**: Monitor occlusion, mouth opening, healing (radiographs). **OUTCOMES**: **Excellent** remodeling, functional recovery. **INDICATIONS FOR SURGICAL (ORIF)** (10%): **ABSOLUTE**: **1. Bilateral fractures** with **malocclusion** (anterior open bite - condyles telescoped). **2. Condyle displaced into middle cranial fossa** (intracranial). **3. Foreign body** in joint. **4. Lateral extracapsular dislocation** (condyle outside fossa laterally). **RELATIVE**: **1. Severe displacement** (>45° angulation, >1cm overlap). **2. Inability to achieve occlusion** conservatively. **3. Associated midface fractures** (need stable mandible for MMF). **SURGICAL APPROACHES**: **Preauricular**, **Submandibular**, **Retromandibular**, **Endoscopic**. **Fix** with miniplate. **COMPLICATIONS**: **1. Ankylosis** - especially if prolonged IMF (>2-3 weeks). **Prevention**: Early mobilization. **2. Malocclusion** - managed with occlusal adjustment, orthodontics. **3. Facial nerve injury** (surgical cases - temporary usually). **4. TMJ dysfunction** - pain, clicking (10-20% - usually resolves). **5. Growth disturbance** (children - rare with modern conservative management). **KEY**: **Children** - almost **NEVER** operate (excellent remodeling). **Adults** - **conservative** unless absolute indication.

viva.question: What is sialolithiasis? Describe clinical features and management.
viva.model_answer: **Sialolithiasis** - **salivary calculus (stone)** in ductal system of salivary gland. **Most common salivary gland disorder**. **LOCATION**: **Submandibular gland/duct** (80-90%) - **Wharton's duct**. **Reasons**: (1) **Upward flow** (against gravity), (2) **Thick alkaline** secretion, (3) **Higher calcium/phosphate** content, (4) **Long tortuous** duct (12 cm). **Parotid** (5-20%), **Sublingual** (rare). **CLINICAL FEATURES**: **1. Intermittent painful swelling** - **pathognomonic**. **Swelling** of gland **during meals** (saliva production), **subsides** after meal (1-2 hrs). **"Mealtime syndrome"**. **2. Palpable stone** - **bimanual palpation** (submandibular duct - floor of mouth). **Hard, immobile** mass along duct. **3. Reduced salivary flow** - thick/absent saliva from duct orifice (milk duct). **4. Tenderness** - over gland/duct. **5. Secondary infection** - if complete obstruction → **acute sialadenitis** (fever, swelling, pus from duct). **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - mealtime swelling + palpable stone. **2. Radiographs**: **Occlusal radiograph** (80% stones radiopaque - calcium). **Sialography** (contrast in duct - shows stone as **filling defect**) - **AVOID if acute infection** (spread infection). **3. Ultrasound** - detects radiolucent stones, ductal dilation. **4. Sialendoscopy** - endoscopic visualization + removal. **MANAGEMENT**: **MEDICAL** (Small stones <3mm, distal duct): **1. Conservative**: (a) **Hydration** - increase saliva flow, (b) **Sialogogues** - lemon juice, sour candies (stimulate saliva), (c) **Warm compresses** + **Massage** - milk stone distally, (d) **NSAIDs** - pain/inflammation, (e) **Antibiotics** (if infection) - **Amoxicillin-Clavulanate** (covers *S. aureus, Streptococcus*). **2. Stone milking** - **bimanual massage** (push stone anteriorly toward orifice, express). **SURGICAL**: **1. Intraoral removal** (Distal duct stones - palpable in floor of mouth): (a) **Anesthesia** (local), (b) **Dilate duct** orifice, (c) **Incision** over stone (longitudinal - along duct), (d) **Remove stone**, (e) **Marsupialize** duct (suture duct edges to mucosa - create wide opening, prevent stricture). **Do NOT suture duct** closed (stenosis risk). **2. Sialendoscopy** - endoscopic **basket retrieval** or **lithotripsy** (break stone). **Minimally invasive**. **3. Gland excision** - **Submandibular gland removal** if: (a) **Recurrent** sialolithiasis, (b) **Intraglandular** stone (within gland), (c) **Chronic sialadenitis** (gland damaged). **Risks**: **Lingual nerve injury**, **Hypoglossal nerve injury**, **Marginal mandibular nerve** (facial nerve branch - lip droop). **PREVENTION**: Hydration, good oral hygiene.

viva.question: What is a ranula? Describe types and management.
viva.model_answer: **Ranula** - **mucous extravasation pseudocyst** arising from **sublingual gland** (floor of mouth). **Bluish, translucent swelling** ("frog belly" appearance - Latin *rana* = frog). **TYPES**: **1. SIMPLE (Oral) RANULA**: **Confined** to **floor of mouth** (above mylohyoid muscle). **Clinical features**: (a) **Painless, soft, fluctuant** swelling in **floor of mouth** (one side of frenulum), (b) **Bluish/translucent** color (saliva visible through thin mucosa - pathognomonic), (c) **Displaces tongue** upward/laterally (if large), (d) **No pain** (unless infected). **2. PLUNGING (Cervical) RANULA**: **Extends** through mylohyoid muscle into **neck** (submandibular/submental space). **"Plunges"** below mylohyoid. **Clinical features**: (a) **Neck swelling** (submandibular region), (b) **Soft, fluctuant**, non-tender, (c) **May** have oral component (floor of mouth swelling) OR **only neck** swelling, (d) **Differential**: Dermoid cyst, lymphangioma, cystic hygroma, branchial cyst. **DIAGNOSIS**: **1. Clinical** - characteristic bluish oral swelling (floor of mouth). **2. Aspiration** - **thick, ropy, mucoid** fluid (saliva - viscous, egg-white consistency). **Differential from epidermoid** (cheesy material). **3. MRI/CT** - plunging ranula (extent, relation to mylohyoid). **4. NO histopathology** - **pseudocyst** (no epithelial lining - just mucus pool in connective tissue). **MANAGEMENT**: **SIMPLE RANULA**: **1. Observation** - may spontaneously resolve (especially small, recent). **2. Marsupialization**: (a) **Elliptical excision** of cyst roof (create wide opening into mouth), (b) **Suture** cyst lining edges to oral mucosa, (c) **Simple**, outpatient procedure. **Limitation**: **High recurrence** (50%) - gland continues secreting. **3. Excision of sublingual gland**: **Definitive treatment** - **remove entire sublingual gland** (source of saliva). (a) **Intraoral approach**, (b) **Excise sublingual gland** completely (along with ranula), (c) **Low recurrence** (5-10%). **Risks**: **Lingual nerve injury** (runs close - numbness of tongue), **Wharton's duct injury** (submandibular duct - runs through floor of mouth). **Preferred** for recurrent cases. **PLUNGING RANULA**: **Sublingual gland excision** via **intraoral** approach (addresses source). **Neck dissection** usually **not needed** (ranula collapses once gland removed). **Alternative**: **Combined intraoral + neck approach** if large cervical component. **RECURRENCE**: **Marsupialization** 50%, **Gland excision** 5-10%.

viva.question: How do you manage dental extractions in patients with bleeding disorders?
viva.model_answer: **PRE-OPERATIVE ASSESSMENT**: **1. History**: **Type of bleeding disorder**, **Severity**, **Previous bleeding episodes** (dental/surgical), **Medications** (anticoagulants, antiplatelets). **2. Laboratory tests**: **Hemophilia**: **Factor VIII** (Hemophilia A) or **Factor IX** (Hemophilia B) levels. **Anticoagulants**: **INR** (warfarin), **aPTT** (heparin). **General**: **Platelet count**, **PT**, **Bleeding time**. **3. Physician consultation**: Coordinate with hematologist/physician. **HEMOPHILIA**: **Factor replacement** essential. **1. Mild Hemophilia A** (Factor VIII 5-25%): **Desmopressin (DDAVP)** - releases stored Factor VIII (2-3x increase). **Dose**: 0.3 mcg/kg IV **1 hr before** procedure. **Alternative**: **Tranexamic acid** (antifibrinolytic) alone. **2. Moderate/Severe Hemophilia** (Factor VIII <5%): **Factor VIII concentrate** - raise level to **50-100%** before extraction (target depends on extent). **Dose**: **Loading**: 25-50 IU/kg pre-op, **Maintenance**: Continue post-op (7-10 days). **Hemophilia B**: **Factor IX concentrate** (similar protocol). **3. Local measures** (crucial): (a) **Atraumatic extraction** - minimize trauma, (b) **Sutures** - **primary closure** (figure-of-eight over socket), (c) **Hemostatic agents**: **Oxidized cellulose (Surgicel)**, **Gelfoam**, **Fibrin glue** - pack socket, (d) **Tranexamic acid** mouthwash - **5% TXA** rinse QID x 7 days (prevents clot lysis), (e) **Avoid** aspirin, NSAIDs (use paracetamol). **4. Hospital setting** - NOT outpatient (need monitoring, factor availability). **WARFARIN (Anticoagulation)**: **1. Check INR**: **Therapeutic range** (2-3) - **safe** for simple extractions with local measures. **INR 2.5-3.5** - acceptable for minor oral surgery. **INR >4** - **defer**, adjust warfarin (consult physician). **2. DO NOT STOP warfarin** (risk of thromboembolism - stroke, MI). **Exception**: Major surgery (consult physician - may bridge with heparin). **3. Local hemostatic measures**: (a) **Minimize trauma** - atraumatic technique, **limit extractions** (1-2 teeth per visit), (b) **Sutures** - resorbable, primary closure, (c) **Hemostatic agents** - **Surgicel**, **Gelfoam** pack socket, (d) **Tranexamic acid** mouthwash - **10 mL 5% TXA** rinse **QID x 5-7 days** (do NOT swallow - hold 2 min, spit). **VERY effective**. (e) **Pressure** - bite gauze 30 min. **4. Antibiotics**: **Amoxicillin** (reduce infection - infected socket bleeds more). **5. Post-op instructions**: **No rinsing** 24 hrs, **soft diet**, **avoid hot liquids**, **no alcohol** (interacts with warfarin). **6. Follow-up**: Day 1, day 3 (check bleeding). **ASPIRIN/CLOPIDOGREL**: **Continue medication** (DO NOT STOP - thrombotic risk). **Local measures** sufficient for simple extractions. **Clopidogrel** slightly higher bleeding risk than aspirin. **THROMBOCYTOPENIA**: **Platelet count <50,000** - **defer** elective extraction. **<50,000** - **platelet transfusion** pre-op (raise to >50,000). **LOCAL MEASURES** (universal - all bleeding disorders): Atraumatic technique, sutures, hemostatic packing, **tranexamic acid mouthwash** (CRITICAL), pressure, post-op care.

viva.question: Describe the phases of wound healing.
viva.model_answer: **Wound healing** - complex process restoring tissue integrity. **TYPES**: **Primary intention** - wound edges approximated (sutured) - minimal scarring. **Secondary intention** - wound left open (granulation fills defect) - more scarring. **Tertiary intention** (delayed primary) - initially open, closed later. **PHASES (Overlapping)**: **1. HEMOSTASIS (Immediate - minutes)**: **Vasoconstriction** → **Platelet aggregation** → **Clot formation** (fibrin mesh). **Prevents blood loss**, **scaffolding** for cell migration. **2. INFLAMMATORY PHASE (0-3 days)**: **a) Early (0-24 hrs)**: **Vasodilation** → **Increased permeability** → **Edema**, **erythema**, **pain**. **Neutrophils** arrive (phagocytose bacteria, debris) - peak 24 hrs. **b) Late (24-72 hrs)**: **Macrophages** arrive (phagocytose debris, **secrete growth factors** - crucial for healing progression). **Lymphocytes** (immune response). **Clinical**: **Swelling, redness, warmth, pain, loss of function** (cardinal signs of inflammation). **Prolonged inflammation** → **chronic wound**, **delayed healing**. **3. PROLIFERATIVE PHASE (3 days - 3 weeks)**: **Tissue formation**. **a) Granulation tissue formation** (3-5 days): **Fibroblasts** proliferate → **collagen synthesis** (Type III initially). **Angiogenesis** → **new capillaries** (pink, granular appearance). **Myofibroblasts** → **wound contraction**. **b) Epithelialization** (24-48 hrs onwards): **Keratinocytes** migrate across wound (from edges) → **cover defect**. **Faster** in **primary intention** (sutured - edges close). **c) Contraction**: **Myofibroblasts** pull wound edges together (reduces defect size - important in **secondary intention**). **4. REMODELING/MATURATION PHASE (3 weeks - 1 year)**: **Collagen remodeling**: **Type III collagen** replaced by **Type I** (stronger). **Collagen** reorganized along **tension lines**. **Scar maturation**: Initially **red, raised** → gradually **pale, flat** (12-18 months). **Tensile strength** increases (plateaus at **80%** of original - never 100%). **Excess collagen** breakdown → **scar softens**. **FACTORS AFFECTING HEALING**: **Local**: Infection, blood supply, foreign body, wound tension, hematoma. **Systemic**: **Diabetes** (impaired), **Immunosuppression** (steroids, chemotherapy), **Malnutrition** (protein, Vitamin C deficiency), **Age** (elderly slower), **Smoking** (vasoconstriction). **NORMAL TIMELINE**: **Epithelialization** complete: **7-10 days** (oral mucosa faster - 3-5 days). **Suture removal**: **7 days** (face/oral), **10-14 days** (skin elsewhere). **Scar maturation**: **12-18 months**.

viva.question: Describe principles of suturing and commonly used suture materials.
viva.model_answer: **PRINCIPLES OF SUTURING**: **1. Aseptic technique** - sterile instruments, gloves. **2. Tissue handling**: **Gentle** - avoid crushing (tissue forceps with teeth, not hemostats). **3. Hemostasis**: **Control bleeding** before closure (pressure, cautery). **4. Dead space elimination**: **Close in layers** (prevent hematoma/seroma). **5. Eversion**: Wound edges **slightly everted** (not inverted) - better healing, less scar depression. **6. Tension-free**: **No tension** on wound edges (undermining if needed). **7. Accurate approximation**: **Align tissue layers** properly. **8. Knot security**: **Square knots** (surgeon's knot if tension). **SUTURE MATERIALS**: **ABSORBABLE** (degraded by body - no removal needed): **1. Catgut** (Plain/Chromic): **Natural** (collagen from sheep/cattle intestine). **Rapid absorption** (plain 7-10 days, chromic 14-21 days). **High tissue reaction**. **Rarely used** now. **2. Polyglycolic acid (Vicryl)**: **Synthetic**. **Absorption**: 60-90 days. **Moderate tissue reaction**. **Uses**: **Deep layers**, muscle, subcutaneous (where removal difficult). **3. Polyglactin (Vicryl Rapide)**: **Faster absorption** (10-14 days). **Uses**: **Oral mucosa** (intraoral - no removal needed). **NON-ABSORBABLE** (must be removed): **1. Silk**: **Natural** (braided). **Easy handling**, good knot security. **High tissue reaction** (inflammation). **Uses**: **Intraoral/skin** (extraoral) - **MOST COMMON** in oral surgery. **Removal**: **7 days**. **2. Nylon (Ethilon)**: **Synthetic** (monofilament). **Low tissue reaction**. **Less memory** (harder to tie). **Uses**: Skin closure (face). **3. Polypropylene (Prolene)**: **Synthetic** (monofilament). **Very low reaction**. **High memory** (springs back). **Uses**: Skin, cardiovascular. **4. Stainless steel**: **Very strong**, **inert**. **Difficult handling**. **Uses**: Bone wiring, tendon repair. **NEEDLE TYPES**: **Cutting** (triangular - cuts through tissue) - **SKIN, oral mucosa** (tough tissue). **Reverse cutting** (preferred - strength at wound edge). **Round (Tapered)** - muscle, viscera (soft tissue). **SIZE**: **Oral surgery**: **3-0** or **4-0** silk (moderate thickness). **Finer** (5-0, 6-0) for delicate (facial skin). **Thicker** (2-0, 0) for heavy tissue. **SUTURING TECHNIQUES**: **1. Simple interrupted**: **Individual knots** - each suture independent. **Most common**, versatile. **Advantage**: One fails, others hold. **2. Continuous (Running)**: **Single thread**, multiple passes, **one knot** at each end. **Faster**. **Disadvantage**: One break, entire suture fails. **Uses**: Long incisions. **3. Horizontal mattress**: **Everting** suture (good for edges). **Distributes tension**. **4. Vertical mattress**: **Deep + superficial** bites (two-layer closure in one). **Eversion**. **5. Figure-of-eight**: **Hemostatic** (compresses tissue). **Uses**: Extraction sockets, bleeding. **REMOVAL**: **Oral/face**: **7 days**. **Elsewhere**: **10-14 days**. **Absorbable**: **No removal** (falls off/dissolves).

viva.question: Classify nerve injuries. How do you assess and manage paresthesia?
viva.model_answer: **NERVE INJURY CLASSIFICATION (Seddon)**: **1. NEUROPRAXIA**: **Mildest**. **Conduction block** (axon intact, myelin damaged). **No Wallerian degeneration**. **Recovery**: **Complete**, **spontaneous** (days-weeks). **Prognosis**: Excellent. **2. AXONOTMESIS**: **Moderate**. **Axon disrupted**, **endoneurium intact** (nerve sheath preserved). **Wallerian degeneration** occurs distal to injury. **Regeneration**: **Yes** (axons regrow along intact sheath - **1mm/day** / **1 inch/month**). **Recovery**: **Partial to complete** (weeks-months). **Prognosis**: Good (80-90% recovery). **3. NEUROTMESIS**: **Severe**. **Complete transection** (axon + endoneurium + perineurium). **Wallerian degeneration**. **Regeneration**: **Poor** (axons cannot find pathway - **neuroma** formation, misdirection). **Recovery**: **Minimal** without surgical repair. **Prognosis**: Poor unless **microsurgical repair**. **DENTAL SURGERY NERVE INJURIES**: **Inferior alveolar nerve (IAN)**: 3rd molar extraction, implants, mandibular fractures. **Lingual nerve**: 3rd molar extraction (lingual flap elevation), lingual anesthesia. **Incidence**: **Temporary** 0.5-5%, **Permanent** 0.1-1%. **ASSESSMENT OF PARESTHESIA**: **1. History**: **Onset** (immediate post-op vs delayed), **Duration**, **Progression** (improving/static/worsening), **Nature** (numbness, tingling, dysesthesia - abnormal sensation). **2. Clinical tests**: **a) Two-point discrimination**: **Normal**: 2-4mm (lower lip/tongue). **Impaired**: >6mm. **b) Light touch**: Cotton wisp/brush - **detect sensation**. **c) Pin-prick**: **Sharp/dull** discrimination. **d) Thermal**: Hot/cold discrimination. **3. Map extent**: Mark borders of anesthetic/paresthetic area (compare to normal side). **4. Baseline documentation**: **Immediate post-op** assessment (medico-legal). **5. Serial assessment**: **1 week, 1 month, 3 months, 6 months** (track recovery). **MANAGEMENT**: **IMMEDIATE (If injury suspected intra-op)**: **1. Inform patient** (document consent discussion). **2. Primary repair** (if complete transection visualized - rare) - **microsurgical** epineurial sutures. **POST-OPERATIVE**: **1. Reassurance**: **70-90%** spontaneous recovery (most are neuropraxia/axonotmesis). **Majority recover** in **3-6 months**. **2. Observation**: **First 3 months** - natural recovery expected. **Serial assessments**. **3. Pharmacotherapy** (controversial efficacy): **Vitamin B12** (Methylcobalamin 1500 mcg) - supports nerve regeneration. **Gabapentin/Pregabalin** - if neuropathic pain. **Corticosteroids** (Dexamethasone) - early (within 48 hrs) if severe injury (reduce edema) - **controversial**. **4. Microsurgical repair**: **Indications**: (a) **Complete transection** (known intra-op), (b) **No recovery** at **3-6 months** (likely neurotmesis), (c) **Worsening** paresthesia. **Timing**: **3-6 months** optimal (too early - may recover spontaneously, too late - distal nerve atrophy). **Technique**: **Direct repair** (if gap <5mm) or **Nerve graft** (if larger gap). **Prognosis**: Variable (30-70% improvement). **PREVENTION**: **Pre-operative CBCT** (assess IAN proximity in 3rd molar cases), **Careful technique**, **Lingual plate preservation**, **Avoid excessive force**.

viva.question: What are the indications for tracheostomy? Describe the procedure.
viva.model_answer: **Tracheostomy** - surgical creation of opening into **trachea** (through anterior neck) for airway access. **Temporary** (tube removable) or **Permanent**. **INDICATIONS**: **1. Upper airway obstruction**: (a) **Maxillofacial trauma** - bilateral mandibular fractures, midface fractures (tongue fall-back, bleeding), (b) **Ludwig's angina** - floor of mouth swelling → airway compromise, (c) **Tumors** - oral/pharyngeal/laryngeal (obstructing airway), (d) **Burns** - inhalational injury, facial burns, (e) **Angioedema** - severe allergic reaction. **2. Prolonged intubation**: **>7-10 days** mechanical ventilation (ICU patients) - **prevents laryngeal damage** from endotracheal tube. **3. Inability to intubate**: **Difficult airway** (limited mouth opening, anatomical anomalies). **4. Pulmonary toilet**: Facilitate **suctioning** of secretions (neuromuscular disorders, coma). **5. Prophylactic**: Before **major head-neck surgery** (airway edema expected post-op). **TYPES**: **Emergency** (cricothyrotomy - faster) vs **Elective** (tracheostomy - preferred if time permits). **PROCEDURE (Elective tracheostomy)**: **1. Position**: **Supine**, **neck extended** (shoulder roll), **head midline**. **2. Anesthesia**: **General** (preferred) or **Local** with sedation (awake tracheostomy). **3. Landmarks**: **Thyroid cartilage** (laryngeal prominence), **Cricoid cartilage** (below thyroid), **Tracheal rings** (below cricoid), **Sternal notch**. **4. Incision**: **Transverse** (2-3 cm) at level of **2nd-3rd tracheal ring** (midway between cricoid and sternal notch). **Alternative**: **Vertical** midline incision (faster - emergency). **5. Dissection**: (a) Divide **subcutaneous tissue**, **platysma** (muscle), (b) **Retract** strap muscles (sternohyoid, sternothyroid) **laterally**, (c) **Divide thyroid isthmus** (suture-ligate or retract superiorly) - exposes trachea. **6. Tracheal incision**: (a) **Identify tracheal rings** (palpate), (b) **Vertical incision** through **2nd-3rd rings** (inferiorly based flap OR cruciate incision OR window excision), (c) **Avoid 1st ring** (subglottic stenosis risk). **7. Tube insertion**: (a) **Dilate** tracheal opening (tracheal dilator), (b) Insert **tracheostomy tube** (cuffed, appropriate size - adult 7-9mm), (c) **Inflate cuff** (seal airway), (d) **Confirm**: Bilateral air entry (auscultation), **Capnography** (CO₂ detection). **8. Secure tube**: **Sutures** to skin (stay sutures through trachea for emergency re-insertion if displaced), **Ties** around neck (snug - 2 fingers space). **9. Dressing**: Sterile gauze around stoma. **COMPLICATIONS**: **Intra-operative**: Hemorrhage (thyroid vessels), **apnea** (hypercapnic patient - loss of respiratory drive), tube misplacement. **Early post-op**: **Tube obstruction** (secretions, blood), **accidental decannulation** (first 3-5 days dangerous - tract not formed, difficult re-insertion), **subcutaneous emphysema**, pneumothorax. **Late**: **Tracheal stenosis** (1st ring injury, high cuff pressure), **tracheoesophageal fistula**, granulation tissue. **POST-OP CARE**: **Humidified oxygen** (prevent secretion drying), **Regular suctioning**, **Tube change** (first change 5-7 days after tract established, then weekly), **Stoma care** (cleaning).

viva.question: Describe the extracranial course and branches of the facial nerve.
viva.model_answer: **Facial nerve (CN VII)** - **motor nerve** to muscles of **facial expression** + parasympathetic to salivary/lacrimal glands + taste (anterior 2/3 tongue via chorda tympani). **EXTRACRANIAL COURSE**: **1. Exit from skull**: Emerges from **stylomastoid foramen** (between styloid and mastoid processes). **2. Enters parotid gland**: Immediately enters **parotid gland** (runs through gland - **NOT supplies parotid** - supplied by glossopharyngeal CN IX). **3. Division**: Divides into **two main trunks** within parotid: **Temporofacial** (upper) and **Cervicofacial** (lower). **4. Further branching**: Forms **5 main terminal branches** (pes anserinus - "goose foot" pattern). **TERMINAL BRANCHES (memory: "**T**o **Z**anzibar **B**y **M**otor **C**ar")**: **1. TEMPORAL (Frontal)**: **Course**: Crosses **zygomatic arch** (superficial - vulnerable). **Supplies**: **Frontalis** (raises eyebrows), **orbicularis oculi** (upper part - eye closure), corrugator. **Injury**: **Inability to raise eyebrow**, wrinkle forehead. **At risk**: Trauma to temporal region, **parotidectomy**, **facelift surgery**. **2. ZYGOMATIC**: **Course**: Crosses **zygoma**. **Supplies**: **Orbicularis oculi** (lower part - eye closure). **Injury**: **Incomplete eye closure**, lagophthalmos (corneal exposure risk). **3. BUCCAL**: **Course**: Crosses **masseter muscle** (over buccal region). **Multiple branches** (interconnecting). **Supplies**: **Buccinator**, orbicularis oris (upper lip), muscles of nose. **Injury**: **Loss of smile** (upper lip), inability to puff cheeks (blow air). **At risk**: **Parotidectomy**, facial trauma. **LEAST serious** branch injury (multiple interconnections - compensation). **4. MARGINAL MANDIBULAR**: **Course**: Runs along **inferior border of mandible** (may dip **below** mandible up to 1 cm in 20% cases - danger zone). Crosses **facial vessels**. **Supplies**: **Lower lip** depressors (depressor labii inferioris, depressor anguli oris), mentalis. **Injury**: **Most CLINICALLY SIGNIFICANT** - **asymmetric smile** (lower lip doesn't move down on affected side - "**lip droop**"), inability to show lower teeth, deviation of mouth to normal side. **At risk**: **Submandibular gland surgery**, **neck dissections**, **mandibular angle/body fracture surgery**, facelift. **5. CERVICAL**: **Course**: Runs **inferiorly** into neck. **Supplies**: **Platysma** (neck muscle). **Injury**: **Minimal clinical significance** (platysma not essential). **CLINICAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Parotid gland surgery** - nerve runs through gland (risk of injury to all branches). **Mandibular fractures/surgery** - marginal mandibular branch vulnerable. **Temporal artery biopsy** - temporal branch at risk. **PROTECTION during surgery**: **Identify nerve**, gentle tissue handling, **avoid cautery** near nerve.

viva.question: What are the essential elements of informed consent for oral surgery?
viva.model_answer: **Informed consent** - **legal and ethical** requirement before any surgical procedure. Patient must **understand** and **voluntarily agree** to treatment after receiving adequate information. **ESSENTIAL ELEMENTS**: **1. COMPETENCE**: Patient must be **legally competent** (adult >18 years, sound mind). **Exceptions**: **Minors** (<18 years) - **parental consent** required. **Emergency** - implied consent (save life). **Incompetent patient** - guardian/next-of-kin consent. **2. DISCLOSURE OF INFORMATION**: **a) Diagnosis**: Patient's current condition (e.g., impacted tooth, abscess). **b) Proposed treatment**: Procedure planned (e.g., surgical extraction, I&D), **Alternative treatments** (e.g., observation, antibiotics), **Option of no treatment** (consequences). **c) Risks and complications**: **Common**: Pain, swelling, bleeding, infection, dry socket, trismus. **Serious** (even if rare): **Nerve injury** (IAN/lingual - numbness), **jaw fracture**, **sinus perforation**, **damage to adjacent teeth**, **TMJ problems**. **d) Benefits**: Expected outcome (pain relief, infection control, prevent complications). **e) Prognosis**: Expected recovery time, success rate. **3. UNDERSTANDING**: Patient must **comprehend** information (language appropriate, layman's terms - avoid medical jargon). **Verify understanding**: Ask patient to **repeat** key points. **Written + verbal** explanation (consent form + discussion). **4. VOLUNTARINESS**: Decision made **freely** without coercion, pressure, or undue influence. **5. AUTHORIZATION**: Patient **signs consent form** (written documentation). **DOCUMENTATION**: **1. Consent form**: **Signed**, **dated**, **witnessed**. **Contains**: Procedure name, risks disclosed, patient signature, doctor signature, witness. **2. Medical records**: **Note in records** - "Informed consent obtained, risks explained (nerve injury, bleeding, infection, dry socket, jaw fracture, sinus perforation), patient understood, all questions answered". **Specific to procedure** (e.g., for 3rd molar - mention IAN injury risk with percentage). **SPECIAL SITUATIONS**: **Emergency**: **Implied consent** (unconscious patient, life-threatening - no time for consent). **Document**: "Emergency treatment, unable to obtain consent, acted in patient's best interest". **Minors**: **Parental consent**. **Exception**: **Mature minor** (court-determined competence) or **emancipated minor** (married, self-supporting). **Language barrier**: **Interpreter** (document interpreter used). **WITHDRAWAL OF CONSENT**: Patient can **withdraw consent** at any time (even during procedure if conscious). Must **stop procedure** (unless emergency). **MEDICO-LEGAL SIGNIFICANCE**: **Failure** to obtain informed consent = **BATTERY** (illegal touching) - grounds for lawsuit even if treatment successful. **Inadequate disclosure** = **NEGLIGENCE**. **Protection**: **Proper consent** + **documentation** protects against litigation. **WHAT TO DOCUMENT**: "**Discussed**: Procedure, alternatives, risks (bleeding, infection, nerve injury, dry socket), benefits, prognosis. **Patient verbalized understanding**, **all questions answered**, **consent form signed**".

viva.question: Describe General.
viva.model_answer:
A suture is a strand or thread of material used to approximate tissues and
also to ligate blood vessels.
Thus, Suturing is the act of sewing or bringing tissues or flap edges together
and holding them in apposition until normal healing takes place.

viva.question: Classify General.
viva.model_answer: Sutures can be conveniently classified into 2 groups-

viva.question: Write a short note on Monofilament & Multifilament.
viva.model_answer:
1> Absorbable Sutures
These are suture materials that are digested or hydrotysed by the enzymes
present in the body. Thus they require no removal from the surgical site.
a> Natural - Catgut
b> Synthetic - Polyglycolic acid
2> Non-Absarbable Sutures
These materials can not be metabolised by the body's natural mechanism
therefore they should be removed by the surgeon at the end of healing.
a> Natural - Silk, linen
b> Synthetic - Nylon, polypropylene
3> Monofilament
They consist of single strand of suture material
Advantages - monofilament sutures are more smooth and strong
- they do not allow any bacteria to survive.
Disadvantages — monofilament sutures can't be handled well like
multifilament sutures
- They have to be handled properly and delicately
without any damage to the strand during surgical
procedures to avoid any breakage past operatively.
4> Multifilament
They consist of several filaments braided or twisted together.
Advantages - Multifilament sutures are easier to handle and tie
than monofilament sutures
Disadvantages - They can harbour bacteria and are not suitable
in the presence of contamination and infection.

viva.question: Write a short note on PRINCIPLES OF SUTURING.
viva.model_answer:
The needle should be grasped with the help of needle holders at
approximately 3/4 th of its distance from the tip of needle.
The needle should never be held at the suture end as it is the weakest
of breakage of the needle.
point of the needle and grasping at this point results in either bending
The needle should pierce the tissue perpendicular to its surface as
piercing the tissue obliquely may result in a tear.
The curved needles should be passed through the tissues following the
curvature of the needle to prevent tearing of the tissue.
The suture should be placed equidistant (2-3 mm) from the incision
line. The depth of the penetration should also be equal on both sides of the
line.
The needle should be passed from mobile tissue to fixed tissue
When one side of the tissue is thinner than the other side, then the needle
should pass from thinner to thicker side.
Similarly, when one side is deeper & other side is superficial, the needle
should pass through the deeper to the superficial line side.
