The suture should not be tied so tightly that it results in blanching
of the tissues. The suture should just approximate the wound margins
The knot should not be placed over the wound margins.
Each suture should be placed 3-4 mm apart. The spacing between the
sutures depends upon the type of Tissues which are approximated.
When sutures are placed upon areas of underlying muscular activity
they should be clase to each other.
When length of Tissue on one side of the suture wound is longer than
the other, suturing it would result in 'dog-ear' formation.
In order to avoid it, the excessive Tissue should be undermined and an
incision at 30° to original incision is directed at the undermined
tissue.
The extra tissue is pulled over the incision, the appropriate amount
is excised, and wound is clased.

viva.question: Write a short note on SUPPORTIVE MEASURES.
viva.model_answer:
1. Antibiotics - For prevention of secondary infection
- Selection of AB should be done on the basis of culture
and sensitivity testing
- Penicillin and its derivatives are the drug of choice.
- Penicillin V 250-500mg 6 hourly is adequate
[initially IV later switched to oral route]
- In case of penicillin resistance, a broad spectrum AB
2. Nasal decongestants - encourage drainage of pus and secretions
- Ephedrine nasal drops (0.5%), instilled intranasally
every 2-3 hours
The correct method of using this is the recumbent position
with the head inclined to the affected side.
- Benzoin and menthol inhalations
3. Analgesics - Aspirin 500 mg QDS
- Paracetamol 500 mg TDS
- Ibuprofen 400 mg TDS

viva.question: Describe SUPPORTIVE MEASURES.
viva.model_answer:
George Caldwell and Henri Luc described a new method of entering into the
maxillary sinus where 2 separate openings are made, one in the canine fassa
to gain access to the antrum and other in the nasoantral wall for drainage.
Hence. it is called as Caldwell- Luc procedure.

viva.question: Write a short note on It is used for.
viva.model_answer:
- removal of teeth/fragments of root/impacted teeth in the antrum
- treatment of chronic maxillary sinusitis with polyps and cystic
degeneration of mucosa
- excision of cysts and benign tumours of maxillary sinus
— management of hematoma or haemarrhage in the maxillary sinus
- to treat fractures involving floor of the orbit or anterior maxillary
sinus wall

viva.question: Describe the procedure for It is used for.
viva.model_answer:
Incision extends from apex of canine down to near the gingival margin &
posteriorly to the 2nd molar
In edentulous cases, it is done along the alveolar ridge
The incision should be larger than the window to be cut in the bone
The mucoperiostium is then detached and retracted till the infra orbital
ridge taking care to preserve the integrity of infraorbital nerve.
A small gouge is used to perforate centre of the canine fassa.
The hole is enlarged by means of rongeurs or backbiting forceps until
it is large enough to allow introduction of atleast the index fingers.
The finger is used to palpate the lining of the sinus.
Blood and pus contained in the sinus are removed by aspirator.
Bleeding may be arrested by inserting small sponges saturated with
adrenaline.
The antrum is inspected using small light such as laryngoscope to illuminate
the antral walls.
If the entire antral membrane is thickened, hyperplastic or altered by
cystic degeneration and filled with polyps, all the diseased tissue should
be removed.
Granulomatous tissue may be found in the floor of the sinus if an abscessed tooth
has been the cause of infection or if a perforation had been made while extracting
a tooth tissue. A curette may be useful for removing the inflammatory granula-
-tion.
slowing Albance burn
The socket should be allowed to fill with a healthy blood clot and finally
gingival margins may be approximated by a suture.

viva.question: Write a short note on ANAPHYLAXIS.
viva.model_answer:
Anaphylaxis is aclinical syndrome of severe hypersensitivity reaction
characterised by cardiovascular system collapse respiratory system
depression, skin reaction and smooth muscle contractions.

viva.question: Write a short note on PATHOPHYSIOLOGY.
viva.model_answer:
Expasure to certain drugs and fareign bodies
[penicillin, aspirin, lignocaine]

viva.question: Write a short note on Production of IgE antibodies.
viva.model_answer:
The antibodies fix to most cells
On exposure to same drug,
antigen-antibody reaction occurs on mast cell surface
Release of mediators [Histamine, Leukotriene, 5-HT]
clinical features of anaphylaxis

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of Production of IgE antibodies.
viva.model_answer:
Cutaneous manifestations — pruritus
- urticaria
- angioedema
Respiratory manifestations — wheezing
- coughing
- stridar
- dyspnoea
CVS manifestations — Tachycardia
- Shock
- Hypotension
- Cardiac dyerhythmia

viva.question: What is the treatment for Production of IgE antibodies?
viva.model_answer:
If the allergic reaction is confined to the skin, an antihistamine is administered
IV or IM.
If respiratory involvement is seen, position the patient in supine position
with legs elevated, administer O2 and perform CPR if required.
Inject Adr 0.5 mg [ 0.5 ml of 1:1000 solution] IM; repeat every 5-10
min in case the patient does not show improvement or improvement is
transient.
This is the only Life saving measure.
Adr should not be injected IV unless shock is life threatning
If Adr is to be injected IV; it should be diluted to 1:10000 to 1:100000 and
infused slowly with constant monitoring.
Administer a H₁-antihistamine [chlorpheniramine 10-20 mg ] IM/slow IV.
IV glucocorticoid I hydro cartisone sodium succinate 200mg ] should be added
in severe/recurrant cases.
In case of bronchaspasm, administer salbutamol, initiate 8/29/0 BLS and monitor
vital signs.
basic
Prepare the pt. for transport to emergency care.

viva.question: Write a short note on ENUCLEATION.
viva.model_answer:
Enucleation involves complete removal of cyst lining and its contents.
Intraaral approach is the method of choice for enucleation although rarely
extraoral approach through the submandibular skin may be excised.
It is also c/a cystectomy or PartischII procedure.

viva.question: Write a short note on Small cysts.
viva.model_answer:
Small or large cysts not endangering vital structures or risk of pathological
fracture
Cysts with high recurrance rate (OKC)

viva.question: What are the advantages of Pastoperative care is reduced?
viva.model_answer: Pathological jaw fractures can occur in case of enucleation of large cyst

In young people, tooth germ or unerupted teeth involved with the cyst'

viva.question: Write a short note on Mucoperiosteal flap elevation.
viva.model_answer:
Prepare bony window, if bone over the cyst is intact
If thin layer of bone is present, it is carefully peeled off with periosteal elevator
cyst should be removed entirely without tearing
cyst Lining is gently separated from cavity wall using
periosteal elevatar or spoon excavator
if lining of cyst is difficult to separate then insert a
rolled gauze b/w cavity & cyst lining.
Or else, the cyst can be aspirated so that the sac shrinks
The cavity is isorigated and inspected and closed with sutures

viva.question: Write a short note on ENLICLEATION WITH OPEN PACKING.
viva.model_answer:
It is preferred in cases of infected large cysts where primary clasure might lead
to breakdown of the wound and intergere with healing.
So, instead of primary closure, X the flap is turned into the bony cavity.
It is fixed with 1/2 inch medicated gauze pack for 10 days.
A change in pack is followed by construction of an acrylic plug as used in
marsupialisation.

Inflammation or infection, which contraindicates supraperiasteal injection

inferior to

Maximum penetration should be 2 cm; 0.9-1.2 ml soin. is deposited

viva.question: Write a short note on VASOCONSTRICTORS IN LA.
viva.model_answer:
Vasoconstrictors are chemical agents added to local anaesthetic solutions
a> to oppose vasodilation caused by the local anaesthetic agents
b> to achieve hemastasis (constrict the blood vessels and control tissue
perfusion)

viva.question: Write a short note on USES OF VASOCONSTRICTORS.
viva.model_answer:
The net effects caused by addition of vasoconstrictor to LA are-
a> It decreases the rate of absorption of local anaesthetic agent into
cardiovascular system
b> It lowers the plasma level of local anaesthetic agent, ,and, hence,
decreases the risk of systemic toxicity.
c> It increases the duration and depth of action, as higher volumes of
local araesthetic agent remain in and around the nerve for longer
time
d> It decreases bleeding at the site of injection
The pH of LA solution with vasoconstrictor is lower/mare acidic than
without vasoconstrictor. (5.5-6)
(3.3-4)
Because of less acidic property, the plain solutions will have less burning
during injection & have more rapid clinical action.

viva.question: What are the indications for USES OF VASOCONSTRICTORS?
viva.model_answer:
Patients with significant cardiovascular diseases such as
a> ischemic heart disease b> hypertension and c> cerebral strokes
Patients with thyrotoxicasis or hyperthyroid states & diabetes mellitus
Patients receiving non specific beta-blockers ,monoamine oxidase
inhibitors tricyclic antidepressants

viva.question: Write a short note on Pregnancy.
viva.model_answer:
on
Patients with ^ general anaesthesia with halogenated agents
REDUCING AGENTS OR ANTIOXIDANTS FOR VA50CONSTRICTORS
Vasoconstrictors are relatively unstable in solutions, and undergo
oxidation, when exposed to sunlight. The solution appears pink to
brown in color after oxidation.
To ensure a reasonable shelf life, it is necessary to include a
reducing agent or antioxidant in the LA soln.

thus prevents oxidation of vasoconstrictors. hienira rsval

viva.question: Write a short note on A PRINCIPLES OF USE OF ELEVATORS.
viva.model_answer:
There are 3 work principles applicable to the dental elevators.
1> Lever principle
2> Wedge principle
3> Wheel and axle principle
viva.topic: A PRINCIPLES OF USE OF ELEVATORS

viva.question: Write a short note on LEVER PRINCIPLE.
viva.model_answer:
most commonly used principle
The elevator is a Lever of first order. In this, the fulcrum is located
between the load and effort. In order to gain mechanical advantage,
the effort arm must be longer than the resistance/ load arm.
When elevators are used, the force is transmitted at Long power arm of the
handle, so that the MA is derived at the short weight arm i.e the working
blade, which engages the tooth. eg - coupland elevator.

viva.question: Write a short note on WEDGE PRINCIPLE.
viva.model_answer:
It consists of 2 movable inclined planes, which meet and form a sharp
angle. The effort is applied to the base of the plane & the resistance
has its effect on the slant side.
A wedge can be used to split, expand or displace the portion of a
substance that receives force. The sharper the angle of the wedge,
the less effort is required to overcome the resistance.
The MA of wedge is 2.5.
The wedge elevator is forced between the root & bone parallel to the
long axis of the tooth. Eg- A small apexo elevator can displace the roots
towards the occlusal plane out of socket, similarly a straight elevator is
applied between the tooth and interdental bone to separate periodontal
ligament attachment.
input force
output
output
force
force
V
WHEEL & AXLE МИТ
This mechanical device consists of a wheel attached to an arle, where a
light force applied to a crank handle onto the side of the wheel creates
a torque about the axle centreline to lift a heavy load.
The effort is applied to the circumjerence of a wheel, which turns the
axle to raise the weight.
Greater the diameter of the wheel, more is the mechanical advantage.
The principle is applicable to the crossbar and Cryer's elevators, shreets where M
mechanical advantage is 4.6. xo. brobib will
The working tip of the crassbar is introduced deep into the space between
the bone and the root and the handle is rotated in an arcto remove the
root. When the handle is rotated, the force created on the blade is
multiplied creating greater MA.

viva.question: Write a short note on LEFORT I FRACTURE.
viva.model_answer:
It is also called as Low bevel gractive
- Low level or, Subzygomatic fracture
- Harizontal fracture of maxilla
- Guerin's fracture
- It is also called as floating fracture, because there is a separation of
complete dentoalveolar part of the maxilla, and the fractured fragment
is held only by means of soft tissue.

viva.question: Write a short note on Fracture Line.
viva.model_answer:
Here the harizontal fracture line is seen above the apices of the teeth,
which detaches the tooth-bearing partion of the maxilla from the rest
of the facial skeleton.

viva.question: Write a short note on Lateral extension of fracture line.
viva.model_answer:
commences or the lateral margin of piriform aperture, passes
horizontally above the nasal floor and canine fassa, passes along
the antero lateral wall of maxillary sinus, runs below the zygomatic
and then runs upwards and posteriorly to fracture the pterygoid
& plate at the junction of lower third and upper 2/3 rds.

viva.question: Write a short note on Medial extension of fracture line.
viva.model_answer:
starts on the lateral margin of piriform aperture, passes along the
laterall wall of nase and then joins the lateral wall behind the
maxillary tuberasity.

viva.question: What is the etiology of Medial extension of fracture line?
viva.model_answer:
This type of fracture results from application of horizontal force just
above the apices of maxillary teeth.

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of Medial extension of fracture line.
viva.model_answer:
Slight swelling and edema of the lower part of the face along with
upper Lip swelling.
Ecchymasis in the Labial and buccal vestibule as well as contusion
of the skin of the upper lip.
Laceration of upper Lip and intraoral mucasa may be seen

viva.question: Write a short note on Bilateral epistaxis or nasal bleeding.
viva.model_answer:
Mobility of upper dentoalveolar partion of jaw, which is frequently
mobile to digital pressure.
Occlusion may be disturbed. Patient may not be able to masticate food
Pain while speaking or moving the jaw
Percussion of the maxillary teeth produces dull "cracked cup "sound.

viva.question: Write a short note on FRACTURE OF ZYGOMATIC COMPLEX.
viva.model_answer:
The zygomaticomaxillary complex plays a key role in the structure,
function and esthetics of the 100 facial region.
It provides normal check contour and separates the orbital contents
from the temporal fassa and maxillary sinus.
As the zygomatic bone is clasely associated with the maxilla, frontal
and temporal bones, and they are usually involved when a zygomatic
bone fracture occurs, therefore these type of fractures are called
zygomaticomazillary fractures or zygomatic complex fractures or
tripod fractures.

Any displacement of the fracture in this axis becomes stable

of frontozygomatic suture and reduction of these fractures is

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of THAMINM.
viva.model_answer:
Flattening of the injured cheek (passibly masked by swelling) - most
common displacement of the complex is inward.
Immediately following injury or after the acute phase of edema is
subsided, then an alteration of contour on the affected side is
apparent.

viva.model_answer:
Circumarbital ecchymasis will develop after few hours from
effusion of blood into the surrounding tissues.
Circumarbital edema can be quite grass. The examination of the
eye may not be possible till edema subsides.
Subconjunctival hemarrhage is observed at the outer canthus
Depression of ocular level / limitation of ocular movement may be seen
Proptasis of the eye may be seen due to retrobulbar hemorrhage
Patient may complain of diplopia/ blurring of vision
Anaesthesia of the cheek, nase and Lip may be present
Edema of the cheek and eyelids, traumatic emphysema canoftenbe

viva.question: What is the treatment for Unilateral epistaxis may be present?
viva.model_answer:
In majority of cases, early operation is advisable, provided that there
are no opthalmic or cranial complications.
Whenever there is a grass periorbital edema and ecchymosis,
pastponement of the operation for 3-5 days can be done, but it
should not be prolonged more than 2 weeks.

viva.model_answer:
Simple elevation will be sufficient, because of high degree of stability
due to integrity of temporal fascia and the interdigitation of the

viva.model_answer:

viva.question: Describe UNSTABLE FRACTURES -.
viva.model_answer:
Odontogenic sinusitis is the inflammation of mucasa of any of the paranasal
sinuses.
Inflammation of most or all of the paranasal air sinuses simultaneously is
known as pansinusitis.
The maxillary sinusitis is usually odontogenic in origin, because of its clase
proximity with the maxillary teeth.
Maxillary sixusitis can be classified into <1> Acute and <2> Chronic

viva.question: What is the etiology of UNSTABLE FRACTURES -?
viva.model_answer:
It may be due to a persistant dental focus,
chronic rhinitis
chronic infection in the frontal or ethmoidal sinus
allergic conditions

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of PATHOPHYSIOLOGY.
viva.model_answer:
Severe throbbing pain, when the pus remains confined within bone in apical
region. The intensity reduces after 12-24 hours as the pus is discharged into
antral cavity.
Unilateral foul discharge through pasterior nares
Cacasmia - a fetid odor with bad taste in the mouth.

viva.question: Write a short note on INCISIONS IN THE ORAL CAVITY.
viva.model_answer:
It is desirable to incise through attached gingiva and over a healthy
bone. The suture line should have adequate bone support underneath
for uneventful healing.
Incisions placed near the teeth for extractions should be made in the
gingival sulcus.
Incisions involving the reflection of mucoperiasteal flap are direct,
straight line or curvilinear taking the shortest distance vertically
through the tissues.
Indirect incisions are used to access the areas like soft palate, tongue,
cheeks, Lips, floor of the mouth etc.
Integrity of the interdental papilla should be maintained as far
as passible
Blood supply to the incision should be adequate
Incisions should be at right angles to mucasa to prevent shelfing
edges that might cause necrosis of the undermined part

- through incisive papilla/over buccal aspect of midcrown
- over bony lesions / pathologic defects - dehiscence
- over freni
- vertical incisions on the Lingual side of the mandibular arch

viva.question: Write a short note on GRAIN PLACEMENT.
viva.model_answer:
A soft yests Yeates or carrugated rubber drain is inserted into the depth of the
abscess cavity, and external part is secured to the wound margin
with the help of sutures.
Drain left for atleast 24 hours.
The purpose of the drain is to allow the discharge of tissue fluids and
pus from the wound by keeping it patent. clean
The drain allows for debridement of abscess cavity by irrigation.
hrs Tissue fluids flow along the external surface of a latex drain.
Hence, it is not always necessary to make perforations in the drain,
which could weaken and perhaps cause fragmentation within the tissues.
Drains should be removed when the drainage has nearly И.Г. аиога completely
ceased. Some form of drains, such as latex drains are Trritating
to the surrounding tissues and may stimulate exudate formation
on their own. Thus, drains are left in infected wounds for 2-7 days

viva.question: Describe the pathogenesis of GARRES OSTEOMYELITIS.
viva.model_answer:
The etiological agents can be a carious tooth, or the overlying soft tissue
infection. The infectious process. localises in periasteum or beneath the
periasteal covering of cartex and spreads slightly into the interior dal of bone.
In case of persistence of the causative agent, it causes bony thickening,
visible on radiograph.

viva.question: Write a short note on RADIOGRAPHY.
viva.model_answer:
A focal area of well calcified bone proliferation may be seen that is smooth
and that often has a laminated or onion skin appearance.
The radiographic appearance is typical. There is condensation of cortical
bone, and overgrowth of bony Tissue beneath the periasteum.
The increase in mass of bone is due to
- mild Toxic stimulation of fibroblasts by attenuated infection
- compensatory thickening of bone as a mechanical adaptation to
reinforce the area weakened through disease.

viva.question: Write a short note on MARSUPIALISATION.
viva.model_answer:
Marsupialisation refers To creating a surgical window in the wall of the
cyst, evacuating the contents of the cyst, and maintaining continuity
between the cyst and the oral cavity, maxillary sinus or nasal cavity.
The only partion that is removed is the piece removed to produce the
window. The remaining cystic lining is left in situ.
It is also called decompression or Partisch I technique or cystotomy.
This process decreases intracystic pressure and promotes shrinkage of
the cyst and bone fill.

viva.question: What are the indications for MARSUPIALISATION?
viva.model_answer:
Age - In a young child, with developing tooth germs, or when development
of displaced Teeth has not progressed enucleation would damage the
tooth buds.
In the elderly, debilitated patient, marsupialisation is less stressful
Proximity to vital structures - when proximity of the cyst to vital
structures could create an aronasal or aroantral fistula, injure
neurovascular bundle or damage vital teeth, then marsupialisation
should be considered.
Size of cyst - In very large cyst, where enucleation could result in a
pathological fracture, marsupialisation can be accomplished, through
a more limited bony opening.

viva.question: Write a short note on SALIVARY FISTULA.
viva.model_answer:
Salivary fistula is a chronic communication between the salivary
gland or duct and the skin through which saliva is discharged

viva.question: What are the types of SALIVARY FISTULA?
viva.model_answer:
Internal -opening into mucasa of buccal cavity
External - opening into the skin, ,saliva is discharged on skin
causing eczema, excoriation, inflammation

viva.question: What is the etiology of SALIVARY FISTULA?
viva.model_answer:
It is a rare condition caused by accidental trauma, surgery and
infection.
It may occur as a result of actinomycosis, syphillis and cancrum
oris.
Any surgery on the parotid gland -superficial parotidectomy, drainage
of abscess, surgery for carcinoma cheek

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of SALIVARY FISTULA.
viva.model_answer:
Discharging watery fluid, exaggerated by keeping time in the mouth
collection of saliva under the skin flap with drainage through the wound
Fistulogram confirms the diagnosis.
Sialography is performed to establish the exact site or origin of fistula.

viva.question: What is the treatment for SALIVARY FISTULA?
viva.model_answer:
Treatment consists of aspiration of fluid, compression dressings and
removal of salivary stimulants.
Currently the most commonly used treatment is botulinum neurotoxin
type A and systemic hyascine or propantheline bromide.
The problem usually resolves with this treatment regimen.

viva.question: Classify SALIVARY FISTULA.
viva.model_answer:
Ankylasis means stiff joint or the joint which cant move.
TMJ ankylosis refers to stiffening (immovability) or pathological
intracapsular fusion/union (either of fibrous tissue adhesion or bony
deposition) between the head of the condyle and glenoid fassa articular area.

viva.question: Write a short note on ExTrapatular.
viva.model_answer:
arti-
Extra-ouricular ankylasis or false ankylasis is due to pathological changes
in the structures surrounding the TMJ causing limitations in mouth opening.
Radiographic findings illustrate normal appearing TMJ.
Intra-articularar true ankylasis is caused by fibrous orbony adhesions
between the articular surfaces of the mandibular condyle and glenoid
fassa.

viva.question: Write a short note on Intraular.
viva.model_answer:
Type I - The condylar head is present without much distartion.
anteriors are
Fibrous adhesions make movement impossible.
Type II - Bony fusion of the mis-shaped head and articular surface.
No involvement of sigmoid notch or caronoid process.
Type III - A bony block bridging across the ramus and the zygomatic
arch. Medially an atrophic dislocated fragment of former
head of condyle is still found.
Type IV - The normal anatomy of the TMJ is totally destroyed by
complete bony block between the ramus and skull base.
This classification is applicable for primary ankylosis and not far the
cases involving reankylasis after gap arthroplasty.
C> HAKIM & METAWALLI CLASSIFICATION (2002)
Class I - Unilateral/Bilateral ankylosis, the condyle and glenoid
fassa retain their original shape, and the maxillary
artery is in normal anatomic relation to the ankylotic
mass. [Fibrous]

viva.question: Write a short note on Bony.
viva.model_answer:
Class II - Unilateral/Bilateral ankylasis, between the condyle and
glenoid fassa/temparal bone. and the maxillary artery is

viva.question: Describe the technique for To prevent recurrence - reankylasis.
viva.model_answer:
A> Condylectomy
B> Gap arthroplasty
c> Interpositional arthroplasty
Most surgical procedures can be done through a preauricular incision
alone.
A> CONDYLECTOMY
Advocated in cases of fibrous ankylasis, where the joint space is
obliterated with deposition of fibrous bands but anatomy of
condylar head is almost unchanged.
Preauricular incision is mostly used to expose the condylar region
A horizontal asteotomy cut is made at the level of condylar neck.
Vital structures on the medial surface of condylar neck should be
protected using special condylar retractor.
The condylar head is sectioned at the level of the neck, and it is
separated from superior fibrous attachments and removed.
The stump of the condyle at the neck is smoothened and thus a new
joint is created here.

viva.question: What are the complications of To prevent recurrence - reankylasis?
viva.model_answer:
a> Lass of vertical height of ramus
b> Bilateral condylectomy may create an anterior open bite
c> Unitateral condylectomy causes deviation of mandible towards
the operated side on oral opening
Therefore, when the site of the fused joint is mobilised via condylec-
tomy, then after recontouring by arthroplasty, an alloplastic
material can be used to maintain the jaw space.
B> GAP ARTHROPLASTY
bony
Commonly done in cases of complete ankylosis.
A
Gap arthroplasty involves creation of an anatomical gap in the ankylased
segment to form an artificial joint space.
A gap in the bone is created to separate the ramus from the ankylased
mass in the glenoid fassa.
2 horizontal asteotomy cuts are made and the wedge of the bone between
these 2 cuts is removed
Care should be taken while removing the bone from the medial aspect
as it is clase to the maxillary artery and carotid canal.
A gap of 1-1.5cm is created to prevent reankylosis and the gap should be
maintained by active physiotherapy of the joint.

arthroplasty
c> INTERPOSITIONAL ARTHROPLASTY
When gap arthroplasty is done to release ankylasis, there are chances of
contact between the bone ends to form a reankylasis.
So an interpasitional material is to be placed in between them to avoid
contact and minimise chances of reankylosis
Materials which can be used are either alloplastic or autogenous materials.

viva.question: Write a short note on DRY SOCKET.
viva.model_answer:
Gry socket is defined as a past-operative pain in and around the dental
alveolus, which increases in severity at some moment between the first
and third day after a dental extraction, , accompanied by partial or
total disintegration of the intraalveolar clot, causing foul smell.
Term dry socket was given by Crawford (1896)
It is also known as - alveolar asteitis
- Fibrinolytic alveolitis

- This theory relates the highcount of anaerobic bacteria in the

viva.model_answer:

The maxillary sinusitis is usually odontogenic in origin, because of its
clase proximity with the maxillary teeth.
Maxillary sinusitis can be classified into <1> Acute or <2> Chronic

viva.question: Write a short note on PLEOMORPHIC ADENOMA.
viva.model_answer:
Also called as Benign mixed tumors, they are the most common salivary
gland tumors, which predominantly affect the superficial lobe of the
parotid gland.

viva.question: Describe the pathogenesis of PLEOMORPHIC ADENOMA.
viva.model_answer:
According to Gardick, a neoplastically altered epithelial cell with the
potential for multidirectional differentiation might be histogenetically
responsible for pleomorphic adenoma.

viva.question: Write a short note on Female predilection.
viva.model_answer:
Most common site - parotid glands.
Occurance in sublingual gland is rare.
8% of pleomarphic adenomas involve minor salivary glands.
Among them palate is the most common site. (60-65%)
The tumor in major salivary glands is slow growing, painless and
does not show fixation to deeper tissues or overlying skin.
Though pain is not a common symptom, Local discomfort is always
present.
In intraoral accessory glands, the tumor is 1-2 cm in diameter.
Since this tumor causes the patient difficulties in mastication, talking
and breathing, ,it is detected and treated earlier than tumor of
major glands.
Recurrent lesions occur as multiple nodules and are less mobile
than original Tumor.
Deep Lobe tumors may not show grass swelling on the outer aspect
but as they grow, they pass through the stylomand ibular tunnel of
Patey and push the pharyngeal wall, tonsil & soft palate. Thus, these
tumors are called dumb bell tumors.

viva.question: What investigations are done for Female predilection?
viva.model_answer:
FNAC - to confirm diagnosis and rule out malignancy
CT scan - done when the tumor is arising from deep lobe.
It helps to define the extraglandular spread & the extent
of parapharyngeal disease.
FNAC of Lymph nodes which are palpable in neck in case of
malignancy of parotid gland.
X ray of bones (mandible and mastoid process) to see for bony
resorption.
MRI is a better investigation but expensive
Slow growing parotid tumors should not be subjected to biopsy for
2 reasons - injury to facial nerve
- seeding of tumor cells in the subcutaneous plane

viva.question: What is the treatment for Female predilection?
viva.model_answer:
A> Conservative superficial parotidectomy.
It is the standerd treatment for benign pleomorphic adenoma.
It means removal of the entire lobe containing the tumor which is
superficial to facial nerve. Facial nerve should always be preserved.
Enucleation should never be done as it causes recurrance.
It is difficult to remove a recurrent tumor.
Imp. points in superficial parotidectomy
Adequate exposure by an incision which starts in front of tragus of
pinna, vertically downwards, curves round the ear lobule up to the
mastoid process and is carried downwards in the neck [Lazy incision]
Recognising the facial nerve - Facial nerve lies 1 cm inferomedial to
the pointed end of the tragal cartilage of external ear.
Trace the pasterior belly of digastric up to the mastoid process.
Facial nerve is in between the muscle and tympanic plate.
Gentle handling good suction & perfect hemostasis helps in clear
with
recognition of the nerve.

viva.question: What are the complications of Female predilection?
viva.model_answer:
After a few years pleomorphic adenoma shows features of transformation
into malignancy. [carcinoma expleomorphic adenoma]

Salivary gland tumors are tumors that arise from the tissues of salivary
glands.
They make up 6% of all head and neck tumors.
They are known to arise from both major as well as intraoral minor
salivary glands.

viva.question: Write a short note on TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA.
viva.model_answer:
A disorder of trigeminal nerve that causes episodes of sharp, stabbing pain
in the cheek, Lips, gums or chin on one side of the face.
People with this pain often wince or twitch, which is where trigeminal neuralgia
gets its French name "Tic douloureux" meaning painful twitch.

viva.question: What is the etiology of TRIGEMINAL NEURALGIA?
viva.model_answer:
Usually caused by contact between a normal blood vessel and the trigeminal
nerve at the base of the brain
This contact puts pressure on the nerve and causes it to malfunction
Can occur as a result of ageing, or it can be related to multiple sclerasis
or a similar disarder that damages the myelin sheath protecting
certain nerves.
Less commonly, TN can be caused by a tumor compressing the nerve.
Anomalies of superior cerebellar artery - most recently blamed cause

viva.question: Write a short note on Pain is a characteristic symptom.
viva.model_answer:
Occurs in bouts or paroxysms, sharp or lancinating in character
Radiates to the territary of one or more divisions of TN.
The pain is nearly always unilateral, and if bilateral, is successive
rather than concomitant
The mandibular & maxillary divisions are more commonly involved
than the opthatmic.
In some instances ,these 2 divisions may be simultaneously affected.
The pain may be exoked by a touch, a draft of air, any movement of the
face as in talking, chewing or yawning or swallowing.
The first episodes are usually fairly mild and brief, and it may be
few minutes or hours before the next attack.
With age the episodes become more frequent and painful, until the
patient begins to live in constant fear of next attack.
The momentary bursts of pain usually begins from the same spot on
the face each time. They are called trigger zones.
They are common on the vermillion barder of the lips, ala of the nase,
the cheeks and around the eyes.
The pt. learns to avoid touching the skin over the trigger zones which
frequently makes him go unwashed or unshaven for days.

A> Peripheral Nerve procedures

viva.model_answer:

viva.question: Describe the pathogenesis of PERIAPICAL CYST.

ТИЗМТАЯТ
caries, trauma and periodontal disease

Age group prevalance 20-60 years

viva.question: Write a short note on Seldom associated with primary teeth.
viva.model_answer:
Maxillary anteriors are the most commonly affected
Large cysts may produce expansion of the bone, displacement of tooth roots,
and crepitus when palpating the expanded alveolar plate.
The discolaration of the non-vital tooth and negative response of the
affected tooth to electric pulp testing are the presenting signs.
In addition, infected radicular cysts are painful, the infected teeth is
sensitive to percussion, and there may be swelling of overlying soft
tissues and lymphadenopathy.
An intraoral sinus tract may be identified with discharging pus or
brownish fluid, when the cyst is infected.
The involved teeth will be found to be non vital, discolared, fractured
or with heavy restorations or a failed root canal B treatment.

viva.question: What are the radiographic features of Seldom associated with primary teeth?
viva.model_answer:
The cyst exhibits' a round, pear or ovoid shaped radiolucency, with
a thin, radiopaque line around the periphery of radiolucent area.
In case of very Large cysts' or infected cysts, the peripheral white line
is occassionally absent.
312 IN 1,0011

viva.question: What is the treatment for Seldom associated with primary teeth?
viva.model_answer:
Non vital teeth that are associated with the cyst' are either extracted
or retained, by endodontic treatment and apicoectomy.
External sinus tracts should always be excised to prevent epithelial
ingrowth.
The commonly employed surgical procedure for radicular cysts' is
enudeation, with primary clasure.
Very small cysts can be removed through tooth socket
Large periodontal cysts that encroach upon the maxillary antrum
or inferior alveolar neurovascular bundle or the nase may be
treated by marsupialisation.

viva.question: Write a short note on DENTIGEROUS CYST.
viva.model_answer:
Dentigerous cyst is a developmental odontogenic cyst that is formed by the
expansion of dental follicle and therefore always attached to the neck of
the impacted or unerupted teeth.
It is also called as follicular cyst.
Paget (1863) coined the term dentigerous cyst.

viva.question: Describe the pathogenesis of DENTIGEROUS CYST.
viva.model_answer:
A> INTRAFOLLICULAR THEORY
It is caused by fluid accumulation between the REE and the examel
surface, resulting is a cyst in which the crown is located within the lumen.
B> EXTRAFOL.L.ICULAR THEORY
It may also arise by proliferation and custic transformation of islands of
odontogenic epithelium in connective tissue wall of dental follicle or even
outside dental follicle and this transformed epithelium then unite with
lining follicular epithelium farming cystic cavity around tooth crown.

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of DENTIGEROUS CYST.
viva.model_answer:
Always associated with crown of an impacted, embedded or unerupted teeth
Most lesions are present in 2nd and 3rd decades with slight male predilec
-tion.
Most common site - mandibular and maxillary 3rd molar area and
maxillary cuspid areas.
Dentigerous cyst has the potential to become an aggressive lesion.
Expansion of bone with subsequent facial asymmetry, extreme displacement
of teeth, severe root resorption of adjacent teeth and pain are consequences
of cyst enlargement.
There is usually no pain or discomfort associated with the cyst unless it
becomes secondarily infected.
The thinned out bone covering the center of convexity becomes fragile
and ultimately gets fragmented. As a result, $ oup percussion over
the area produces a typical egg shell crackling sound, which is also
true for other large odontogenic cysts.

viva.question: What are the radiographic features of DENTIGEROUS CYST?
viva.model_answer:
Well defined radiolucency around the impacted or unerupted teeth.
Usually unilocular but at times it may be multilocular.
Cysts have a well-defined sclerotic margin, unless when they are infected,
then the margins are poorly defined.

viva.question: Write a short note on Three radiological variations are observed.
viva.model_answer: a> central b> lateral c> circumferential

viva.question: What is the treatment for Three radiological variations are observed?
viva.model_answer:
Treatment via an intraoral or extraoral approach is decided by the
size of the cyst, adequate access and whether it is desirable to save the
involved teoth.
УЯОЗНТ S/A INDI АЯТИГ
A> MARSUPIALISATION
It is indicated in children if the cyst is very large in size and the
involved teeth/tooth are to be maintained

viva.question: Write a short note on INDIA.
viva.model_answer:
The tooth may erupt into occlusion as the defect heals with normal bone,
or arthodontic forces may be used to bring the tooth into occlusion.
B> ENLICLEATION
Alternatively. the cyst can be enucleated together with the involved
tooth in adults, as the possibility of the tooth erupting is low

viva.question: Write a short note on MUCOCELE.
viva.model_answer:
Mucocele is the term used for swelling due to accumulation of saliva, as a
result of obstruction or trauma to the salivary gland ducts.
It is not a true cyst as it lacks an epithelial Lining.
There are 2 types of mucoceles -
A> extravasation type
B> retention type
These two types differ in the pathogenesis. The extravasation type is the
common one.

viva.question: Describe the pathogenesis of MUCOCELE.
viva.model_answer:
A> EXTRAVASATION TYPE
It is caused by trauma leading to laceration of the duct, commonly of the
minor salivary glands resulting in extravasation of the mucin into the
connective tissue.
The trauma may be caused by biting of the Lip, cheek, pinching of Lip by
extraction forceps etc.
The mucin, which extravasates into the connective tissue, leads to inflamm-
- ation, drawing the neutrophils and macrophages to the site, which in will turn
will result in the formation of the granulation tissue, which actually
forms the wall of these extravasation cysts.
B> RETENTION TYPE
This occurs due to obstruction of the salivary duct, commonly because of
a siabolith or may be due to periductal scar, ,or impinging tumor, resulting
in the accumulation of the saliva in the duct.
This leads to dilatation of the duct resulting in a cyst like lesion, These
cysts may show the presence of ductal epithelium histologically.

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of MUCOCELE.
viva.model_answer:
Mucoceles occur as painless swellings, which may be recurrent.
The common sites of occurrence are -
A> extravasation type - Lower Lip and tongue
B> retention type - palate, cheek, floor of mouth and maxillary sinus
A well-demarcated lesion of the upper lip could be a salivary gland
neoplasm than a mucocele.
The extravasation type occurs in the 2nd decade and also in children.
The retention type occurs in the middle-aged persons.
Based on the Location clinically, there are 2 types of mucoceles
a> Superficial
b> Deep type
The superficial mucocele resembles a vescicle or bulla.
Since they are placed superficially, the bluish translucency is seen.
They are fluctuant. Usually, these cysts' are 4mm to 1cm in size.
The wall is very thin and they rupture easily.
The deep mucoceles are covered by normal appearing mucasa thus
they are different from the superficial ones in the color.
They are firm, well- circumscribed lesions and are present for a
longer period.

viva.question: What is the treatment for MUCOCELE?
viva.model_answer:
212 ИЗФОН
Mucoceles are treated by surgical excision. It is common to see the
recurrence after excision. It can be minimised ,if the associated
salivary gland acini are also removed.

viva.question: Write a short note on SUBLINGUAL SPACE INFECTION.
viva.model_answer:
The sublingual space is a fascial space between the oral mucasa of floor
of the mouth and the myelohyoid muscle.

viva.question: Write a short note on BOUNDARIES.
viva.model_answer: Superiorly - mucasa of the floor of the mouth

viva.question: Write a short note on Inferiarly - myelohyoid muscle.
viva.model_answer:
Laterally - medial side of mandible, above the myelohyoid muscle
Medially - Hyoglassus, genioglassus and geniohyoid muscles

viva.question: Write a short note on CONTENTS.
viva.model_answer:
Majar contents include - geniohyoid and genioglassus muscles, and the
hyoglassus muscle complex.
It also contains - i> deep part of the submandibular salivary gland
and its ducts anteriorly
ii> sublingual salivary gland
iii> Lingual nerve and
iv> hypoglassal nerve

viva.question: Write a short note on INVOLVEMENT.
viva.model_answer:
The teeth which frequently give rise to involvement of sublingual space
are the mandibular incisors, canines, premolars and sometimes first
molars.
The apices of these teeth are superior to the myelohyoid muscle.
The infection perforates Lingual cartical plate below the Levelo of the mucasa
of the floor of the mouth and passes into the sublingual space.

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of INVOLVEMENT.
viva.model_answer:
A> EXTRAORAL
- There is Little or no swelling. The lymph nodes may be enlarged and
tender. Pain and discomfart on deglutition. Speech may be affected.
B> INTRAORAL
- Firm, painful swelling seen in the floor of the mouth on the affected
side. The floor of the mouth is raised. The tongue may be pushed
superiorly. It results in airway obstruction.
The ability to protrude the tongue beyond the vermillion border of upper
Lip is affected.

viva.question: Describe the management of INVOLVEMENT.
viva.model_answer:
A> INTRAORALLY
- An incision is made close to the lingual cartical plate, lateral to the
sublingual plica, as the important structure at this site is the
sublingual nerve which is deeply placed and less likely to be damaged
by this approach.
- The other important structures lie medial to the plica and include
the Wharton's duct, sublingual artery and veins and the lingual
nerve.
- The sinus forceps is then inserted and opened to evacuate the puse
B> EXTRAORALLY
- When both the submental and sublingual spaces contain pus, they
can be drained via a skin incision placed in the submental region,
pushing a closed sinus forceps through the myelohyoid muscle.
- Similarly, when the submandibular space is involved, a sublingual
space abscess can be approached and drained through an incision in
the skin overlying the submandibular space, via the submandibular
space.

viva.question: Write a short note on MODE OF ACTION OF LA.
viva.model_answer:
LA acts on voltage sensitive Na+ channels and block nerve conduction by
preventing /decreasing the entry of Na+ ions.
The Na+ channel has an activation gate near the extracellular mouth and
an inactivation gate at the intracellular mouth. In the resting state, the
activation gate is clased.
The LA receptor is located within the channel in its in atraceltular half.
The LA traverses the membrane in unionised form
reionises in the axoplasm and approaches the LA receptor
The receptor has higher affinity to the LA in its activated as
well as inactivedated state compared to resting state
Binding of LA to the receptor closes the Not channel &
prolongs the inactivated state
It prevents the entry of Na+ ions and thus action potential is not generated
conduction blocade k

viva.question: Write a short note on THERO THEORIES FOR MODE OF ACTION OF LA.
viva.model_answer:
1. ACETYLCHOLINE & THEORY - Acetylcholine is a neurotransmitter at the
nerve synapses is assumed to be involved in nerve conduction.
2. CALCIUM AISPLACEMENT THEORY - Depolarisation is prevented by
displacement of calcium from certain membrane sites that control the
permeability to sodium.
3. SURFACE CHARGE [REPULSION] THEORY - Local anaesthetic agents
binds to the nerve membrane and change the electrical potential at
the membrane surface.
buco LA molecules with net positive charge make the surface potential
at the nerve membrane more positive and reduce the excitability
of nerve by increasing threshold potential.
4. MEMBRANE EXPANSION THEORY - Local anaesthetic agents diffuse
to hydrophobic regions of excitable membrane and expand the
membrane, preventing the sodium permeability.
5. SPECIFIC RECEPTOR THEORY - LA agents act by binding to specific
receptors on sodium channels in the nerve membrane.
Once the receptor access is gained either on its external surface or
on the internal axoplasmic surface ,Nat permeability is decreased or
eliminated, interrupting the nerve conduction.

viva.question: Write a short note on VPOJOITE.
viva.model_answer:
Dentoalveolar injuries are defined as those in which avulsion, subluxation
or fracture of the teeth occurs in association with a fracture of the
alveolus. This type of injury may occur alone or in conjunction with
some other type of mandibular fracture.

viva.question: Classify VPOJOITE.

Class I - Fracture of crown confined to enamel only
Class II - Fracture of crown involving examel and substantial partion of
dentine but without pulpal exposure

viva.question: Write a short note on BELL'S PALSY.
viva.model_answer:
Bell's palsy is defined as acute onset of non-suppurative inflammation
of facial nerve above the stylomastoid foramen, producing an unilateral
Lower Motor Neuron facial palsy.

viva.question: Describe BELL'S PALSY.
viva.model_answer:
It accounts for 60-70% of all cases of unilateral peripheral facial palsy.
Affects with equal frequency on the right and left sides of the face.
Either sex is affected equally.
May occur at any age, the median age is 40 years.
Increased incidence in the elderly diabetics hypertensives, pregnant
women than in the common people.

viva.question: What is the etiology of BELL'S PALSY?
viva.model_answer:
Cause is unclear, precipitating factors may be -
<a> viral infection
<b> vascular ischaemia
<c> autoimmune inflammatory disarders
<d> Heredity
<e> reactivation of HSV in geniculate ganglion
[Ramsey Hunt syndrome]

viva.question: Write a short note on CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS.
viva.model_answer:
Onset of Bells palsy is acute
ТИЗМЭРАИАМ
1/2 of the cases attain maximum paralysis in 48 hours
All cases are clinically prominent by 5 days.
Pain behind the ear may precede paralysis by 1-2 days.
Impairment of taste is present to some degree in all cases.
- but rarely beyond 2nd week of paralysis.
Hyperacusis or distartion of sound in ipsilateral ear
- paralysis of stapedius muscle
Patient feels stiffness of face pulled to one side.

viva.question: Describe the management of Grade I - Normal.
viva.model_answer:
A> SYMPTOMATIC
- Lubricating eye drops
- Protectionof the eyes during sleep - eye patches

B> MEDICOLNAL

decreases possibility of permanent paralysis СИА

C> SURGICAL
- If electroneurography reveals a greater than 90% lass of compound
action potential within first 2 weeks following onset of paralysis, facial
nerve decompression is recommended. After this period, surgery is not
benefecial.
- Nerve decompression can be carried out internally or externally.
- Internal decompression - The nerve is exposed is exposed in the facial
canal pressure in the canal is relieved by exposing the nerve and the
epineural sheath is opened to visualise the nerve fibres and release adhesions
or re-establish continuity.
- External decompression - is done by releasing the epineural sheath
from surrounding scar tissue, bone or a foreign body.
- Nerve anastomosis - reanimation
- anastomasis of the central end of hypoglassal or spinal accessary
nerve with the distal end of the facial nerve is done
- Nerve grafting - whenever there is evidence of пеичота or loss of
portion of a nerve, nerve grafting can be considered.

viva.question: Write a short note on FRENECTOMY.
viva.model_answer:
A frenum is a thin band of fibrous tissue and a few muscle fibres
covered by mucus membrane connecting the Lips, cheek and tonque
to the jawbone.
Maxillary midline frenum is most commonly seen. Lingual frenum
is also seen. Maxillary and mandibular frena are also found in
premolar-molar area.
Frenectomy is the surgical procedure involving the complete surgical excision
of the frenum including its attachments.
A
Frenotomy is the surgical procedure involving incision and relocation
of the frenum.
It is mainly of 2 types - a> Labial Frenectomy
b> Lingual Frenectomy

viva.question: What are the indications for FRENECTOMY?
viva.model_answer: Abnormal frenal attachment which causes midline diastema

viva.question: Write a short note on Frenum causing gingival recession.
viva.model_answer:
Prior to denture fabrication where the frenum obstructs the denture
peripheries. Lilcerations at the frenal attachments due to impingement of the denture

B> Z plasty

viva.question: What are the advantages of Frenum causing gingival recession?
viva.model_answer:
- no need of sutures
- reduced surgical time
- less postsurgical pain & discomfort
- less intraoperative bleeding

viva.question: Describe the procedure for Frenum causing gingival recession.
viva.model_answer:
Whenever there is an inadequate vestibular depth present, to increase the
retention and stability of the denture, deepening of the vestibule is considered.
Thus vestibuloplasty is a procedure to increase the depth by uncovering the
existing basal bone of the jaws surgically and by repositioning the overlying
mucasa, muscle attachments to a Lower position in the mandible/superior
position in the maxilla.
Vestibuloplasty can be done in the maxilla or in the mandible or both jaws.
Maxillary vestibuloplasty are primarily done on labial/buccalside
while mandibular techniques are done via labial or lingual approach.

viva.question: Write a short note on CLOSED SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBILOPLASTY.
viva.model_answer:
First described by Macintosh and Obwegeser
Indications - unstable denture because of shallow vestibular depth
with good underlying bone height and contours.
A
vertical midline incision is made in labial vestibule and supra-
periosteal tunnel is made from premolar (R) to premolar (L) area.
In maxilla further incisions may be given in 1st molar region
for further advancement.
The intervening submucasal tissue is then excised or repositioned
superiorly and maintained by placement of preformed dentures
which can be fixed to the mandible with circummandibular 239VT wiring
and to the maxilla by per alveolar wiring.

TO

viva.question: Write a short note on OPEN SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBULOPLASTY.
viva.model_answer:
Wallenius advocated an open method to overcome the relapse of clased
vestibuloplasty.
A harizontal incision is made in the mucogingival junction.
A thin mucasal flap is elevated by submucasal dissection followed
by supraperiosteal dissection to the desired extent, excising muscle
and subcutaneous tissues.
Stay sutures are used to fix the flap to the periosteum deep in vestibule.

viva.question: Describe the technique for OPEN SUBMUCOSAL VESTIBULOPLASTY.
viva.model_answer:
Mucasal flap from inner aspect of lower lip is used to increase the
vestibular depth in anterior mandibular labial vestibule.
The mentalis muscle is detached from the periosteum to required
depth and the vestibule is deepened via supraperiesteal dissection.
The flap is then redirected & placed directly against the periasteum
and sutured at the new depth of the vestibule.
Raw area is left on the Lip side to be healed by secondary intention.
Periosteum of bone is left intact.
Drawback - scarring of mucosa with subsequent decreased flexibility
of lower lip.
ЭЦОГИНОНТ 29 ГИЦАЯТ

viva.question: Write a short note on MODIFICATION.
viva.model_answer:
Similar to Kazanjians technique in which a flap of Labial mucasa is raised,
[but the vestibule is deepened by subperiasteal stripping instead of supraperias-
leal dissection]
In this procedure, flap is reflected from the inner aspect of the Lip till the
alveolar crest and periosteum is reflected from crest of the ridge till the
desired depth of the vestibule.
The is periasteum is now sutured to the Lip mucosal margin and then Lip flap
is sutured ^ at the required vestibular depth. biowNalayM beritus

A stent or splint is used for adaptation.
Advantage less scaring of the lip mucasa

viva.question: Describe the technique for X pridut.
viva.model_answer:
It is the reverse of Kazanjian's technique.
Here flap is reflected from alveolar crest till vermilion border of the Lip.
Supraperiasteal dissection is done till desired vestibular depth & edge of
the mobilised flap is pushed into vestibular depth.
This flap is held in position with sutures passed through the chin area
extraorally and tied around the rubber catheter.
Here alveolar bone is covered by periosteum which heals quickly by
granulation.

viva.question: Write a short note on AUTOCLAVE.
viva.model_answer:
It is one of the most common methods of moist heat sterilisation.
Thereare 2 types of autoclaves available
a> Downward displacement steriliser - this is nonvacuum type autoclave.
b> Steam sterilisers with pre and past-vacuum processes

viva.question: Write a short note on GENERAL STRUCTURE.
viva.model_answer:
It consists of a horizontal or vertical cyclinder of gunmetal or stainless
steel in a supporting iron case.
The lid bears a discharge tap for air and steam, a pressure gauge and a
safety valve.

viva.question: Write a short note on WORKING PRINCIPLE.
viva.model_answer:
Water boils when its vapour pressure equals to that of surrounding atmas-
- phere. Hence, when pressure inside a closed ЮЯТИОЭ vessel no increases the temp.
at which water boils also increases.
This principle has been applied in autoclave.
Bacteria are more susceptible to moist heat as bacterial protein
coagulates rapidly. Saturated steam can penetrate porous material
easily. When steam comes in contact with a cooler surface it condenses
to water and liberates it Patent heat to that surface.
The Large reduction in volume sucks in more steam to the same site and
the process continues till the temperature of the article is raised to that of
steam. The condensed water produces moist conditions for killing the
microbes present.

viva.question: Write a short note on STERILISATION PROCESS AND CONDITIONS.
viva.model_answer:
The sterilisation process is composed of 3 main phases -

viva.question: Write a short note on HYPERBARIC OXYGEN THERAPY.
viva.model_answer:
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy for radiation damaged tissue was introduced
in 1973 by Greenwood, Gilchrist and Mainous.
Since there is a decrease in vascularity and cellularity of both hard and
soft tissues following irradiation of the bone, the hypoxic compromised
tissues when challenged by a traumatic episode fail to heal.
Delayed fibroblastic activity in neocollagen formation results in chronic
nonhealing wound. These wounds commonly get injected secondarily.
The goal of treating OML and asteoradionecrosis is to overcome hypoxia
& increase vascularity of tissues.
In order to increase tissue oxygenation, the patient is subjected to increa-
sed oxygen concentration and pressure.
HBO therapy consists of 100% oxygen administered at more than
1 absolute ospheric pressure. [ATA]
This 100% oxygen is a means of increasing the oxygen dissolved in plasma
and tissues that inturn induces angiogenesis in the compromised tissues.
formation

viva.question: Write a short note on METHODS OF DELIVERY.
viva.model_answer:
of blood
vessel
Hyperbaric oxygen therapy is administered in a chamber called the
hyperbaric chamber which may be monoplace or multiplace.
Each session ranges from go to 120 min. where patient breathes 100%
oxygen at 2 to 2.4 ATA.
Each session is termed as dive. A total of 20-30 dives spanning 5 days
a week is planned preoperatively.

viva.question: Write a short note on HBO therapy has shown to.
viva.model_answer:
- enhance the recruitment and function of leukocytes
- induces fibroblast growth, increased collagen formation
- stimulate angiogenesis in the healing tissue
- inhibits aerobic and anaerobic bacteria
- inhibit bacterial toxin formation
- hyperbaric oxygen increases the amount of oxygen in tissue fluids by
a 10-fold factor.

The most common site is submandibular gland and Whartons duct,
because its secretionis more alkaline, thicker, and the duct has a
tortuous course and a smaller orifice.
It usually occursin 3rd-5th decades, an with slight male predominance.
Simuttaneous sialdlithiasis in more than 1 salivary gland is rare.
Bilateral occurance of the same gland is also rare.
However, multiple sialoliths in the same duct or gland is common.

viva.question: Describe the clinical features of PREVALENCE.
viva.model_answer:
Most common salivary gland disease in adults and the second most
common salivary gland disease in children [after mumps]
gland swelling.
It is the major cause of unilateral diffuse widespread parotid or submandibular
Patients complain of pain and swelling during and after eating the food,
when saliva production is at its peak. There is gradual reduction of
pain and swelling, which occurs at the resting period.
It recurs again repeatedly, when flow is stimulated.
In some cases, the swelling is diffuse and resembles cellulitis.
Secondary infection often supervenes with fever
Tenderness, trismus, decreased or absence of salivary flow or purulent
discharge at the orifice of the duct may be present.

viva.question: Write a short note on TRACHEOSTOMY.
viva.model_answer:
Tracheastomy is defined as making an opening in anterior wall of trachea
and converting it into a stoma on skin surface.

viva.question: Write a short note on FUNCTIONS.
viva.model_answer:
Respiration through alternate pathway in case of obstruction above the stoma
Alveolar ventilation is improved due to reduction in dead space by 30-50%
[normal dead space 150 ml] and reducing the resistance to airflow.
Protection of airway by way of cuffed tracheastomy tube i.e protection
from aspiration of pharyngeal secretions in comatase patients and blood
due to injuries in pharynx, larynx, maxillofacial region.

viva.question: What is the treatment for Nerve excitability tests?
viva.model_answer:
Open injuries of the facial nerve should be repaired surgically as soon as
possible.
Immediate primary repair at the time of intentional or unintentional
transection [neurotmesis] gives the best result.
Delayed primary repair or early secondary repair is acceptable, when delay T209
is necessary for certain reasons Like contaminated wound ,compromised medical
status, extensive tissue destruction etc.
Because of progressive distal nerve degeneration and muscle atrophy 12
months is often considered as the Limit for successful facial nerve
repair.

part of the nerve loss then autogenous nerve grafting is done
- Hypoglassal nerve is the most commonly used nerve graft
- Sural nerve is an alternative

viva.question: Describe the technique for Nerve excitability tests.
viva.model_answer:

B> REANIMATION
On the non-paralysed side intact buccal branch can be connected to
the paralysed stem of the facial nerve with a sural nerve graft.
The graft is passed over the upperlip.
c> MASKING PROCEDURES
In long standing facial nerve palsy ,the results of micronerve
reconstruction are unfavorable due to atrophy of the facial muscles.
In these patients, regional muscle transpositions with temporal,
massetar or platysma muscles may be considered.
These muscles are transpased and passed through the incisions in the
Lip /cheek groove and then sutured to the dermis.
ТИЗМТАЗЯТ
Alternatively, the strips of fascia lata may be looped through the
cheek and anchored to the temporal and parotid fascia at one
end and to the angle of the mouth and lips at the other end.

viva.question: Write a short note on SUPPORTIVE CARE.
viva.model_answer:
A tarsarrhaphy or eye patch and methoylcellulose drops are provided
when eyelid closure is inadequate to protect the cornea from injury.
Application of heat to the face, massage to facial muscles and facial
exercises are helpful to maintain muscle tone and prevent muscle
atrophy.

viva.question: Write a short note on ODONTOMA.
viva.model_answer:
The term odontoma refers to any tumor of odontogenic origin in true
sense. This is the growth in which both epithelial and ectomesenchymal
cells exhibit complete or partial differentiation of tooth formation.
This is considered more as a developmental anomaly, and also as a composite
lesion, as it contains more than one tissue.
Radiographically it is of 2 types-
A> compound Odontoma
B> Complex odontoma

hemastasis. Surgical bleeding most of the times is caused by ineffective-
local hemastasis.

II TRISMUS of 10M)

INTRODUCTION Trismus is defined as a prolonged tetanic spasm of the
jaw muscles by which normal opening of the mouth is
restricted (lockedjaw).
CAUSES - 1) Trauma to muscles or blood vessels is the infratemporal fossa
most common causative factors.
2) local anesthetic solutions into which alcohol or cold sterilizing
solutions have diffused produce irritation of tissues (eg-mu-scle)
leading potentially to trismus-
3) Injection of LA solution intramuseularly or supramuscularly
leads to a rapidly progressive necrosis of exposed muscle fibres
a) Hemorrhage
5) Low-grade infection after infection can cause trismus.
6) Multiple Needle penetrations
7) withdeawal of the needle from tessue increased the likelehood
of involvement of lingual or inferior alvedar Nerve &
the development of rismus.
8) Excessive volumes of Local anesthetic solution deposited into
a restricted are produce distention oftissues, which may
lead to post injection trismus.
PREVENTION 1) Use shaep steile disposable needle,
2) Properly case for and handle dental LA cartridges.
3) use aseptic technique. contaminated needles should be
changed immediately
4) Practice atraumatec ensertion and injection technique
5) Avoid repeat injections & multiple ensertions into the
same are by gaining knowledge of anatomy and
proper technique
-use regional nerve blocks instead of local infiltration
wherever possible and rational
